<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Internal Combustion (IC) Engines — The Foundation

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Verbatim):** An **Internal Combustion Engine** is a heat engine in which the **combustion of fuel** takes place **inside the engine cylinder**, and the resulting hot gases expand to produce mechanical work that is delivered through a piston-crank mechanism.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Energy Sandwich"
Imagine a closed tin can. You spray some fuel inside and ignite it. The explosion pushes the can's lid outward. If you could capture that push with a piston, you'd have a tiny engine. A real IC engine is a highly engineered, controlled version of this "controlled explosion in a metal box" — repeating thousands of times per minute.

### Classification by Ignition Method

| Type | Full Form | Fuel | Ignition Method | Typical Use |
|------|-----------|------|-----------------|-------------|
| **SI Engine** | Spark Ignition | Petrol / Gasoline | **Spark Plug** | Cars, Motorcycles |
| **CI Engine** | Compression Ignition | Diesel | **Heat of Compression** | Trucks, Generators |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Distinction (Board Favourite):** The *fundamental thermodynamic difference* between SI and CI engines lies in the **method of initiating combustion** — not the fuel alone. A diesel engine could theoretically run on petrol (with sufficient compression) and vice-versa, but the engine design (compression ratio, fuel system, materials) is optimized for one mode.

---

## 1.2 Two-Stroke vs Four-Stroke Engines

### 4-Stroke Engine — "The Four-Course Meal"
- Each operating cycle requires **4 piston strokes** (2 revolutions of the crankshaft).
- **One power stroke per 2 revolutions**.
- Used in: cars, buses, most modern bikes (above 150cc).

### 2-Stroke Engine — "The Power-Every-Revolution Beast"
- Each operating cycle completes in **2 piston strokes** (1 revolution of the crankshaft).
- **One power stroke per 1 revolution** → theoretically double the power for the same displacement.
- Used in: small mopeds, chainsaws, outboard motors, lawn mowers.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** 4-stroke = **"I-C-P-E"** → **I**ntake, **C**ompression, **P**ower, **E**xhaust (these are the 4 strokes). 2-stroke = Power stroke on **every UP and DOWN** movement of the piston.

---

## 1.3 Advanced Fuel Delivery: CRDI & MPFI

> [!NOTE]
> **CRDI (Common Rail Direct Injection):** A high-pressure fuel pump feeds diesel into a **common rail** (a shared accumulator) at pressures up to **2000 bar**, from which **solenoid injectors** deliver finely atomized fuel directly into each cylinder. Found in modern diesel cars (e.g., Maruti Swift Dzire, Hyundai Creta diesel).

> [!NOTE]
> **MPFI (Multi-Point Fuel Injection):** Petrol is injected by **individual injectors** into the **intake port** of each cylinder (one injector per cylinder, before the intake valve). Replaced the older single-point (SPFI / carburetor) systems. Found in almost all modern petrol cars (e.g., Maruti Alto K10, Honda City).

**Why these matter:** Both systems improve **fuel atomization** → better **combustion efficiency** → lower emissions + better mileage.

---

## 1.4 Hybrid Engines — The Best of Both Worlds

> [!NOTE]
> **Hybrid Engine/Vehicle:** A propulsion system that combines **two or more distinct power sources** — typically an **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** running on petrol/diesel **+ one or more Electric Motors** powered by batteries. The two sources work in series, parallel, or a combination (series-parallel hybrid).

**Real-world examples:** Toyota Prius, Maruti Grand Vitara Smart Hybrid, Toyota Camry Hybrid.
**Analogy:** Think of a hybrid like a marathon runner who can either jog normally (ICE) or sprint using stored energy (electric) when needed — the "controller" (ECU) decides when to use which mode.

---

## 1.5 Refrigeration — The Heat Mover

> [!NOTE]
> **Refrigeration:** The process of **removing heat from a low-temperature body** (the space to be cooled) and **rejecting it to a higher-temperature surroundings**, with the help of external work input. It is the **reverse of a heat engine**.

**Analogy:** A refrigerator is like a "heat escalator" — it pulls heat *uphill* from a cold place to a hot place, but needs electricity to power the motor that drives the escalator. **Heat never naturally flows from cold to hot**; that's why we need work input (Second Law of Thermodynamics in action).

---

## 1.6 Unit of Refrigeration — The "Tonne"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1 Tonne of Refrigeration (1 TR):** It is the amount of heat extraction required to **convert 1 tonne (1000 kg) of water at 0°C into ice at 0°C in 24 hours**.

- Latent heat of fusion of ice = **335 kJ/kg** (or 80 kcal/kg, historically)
- So, **1 TR = (1000 kg × 335 kJ/kg) / (24 × 60 s) = 233 W** in SI terms, but in **engineering practice** (using 80 kcal/kg), 1 TR = **50 kcal/min = 210 kJ/min ≈ 3.517 kW**.

### Coefficient of Performance (COP)

> [!NOTE]
> **COP (Coefficient of Performance):** The ratio of **desired effect** (heat extracted in a refrigerator, or heat delivered in a heat pump) to the **work input** required to produce that effect. It is the **"efficiency" of a refrigeration system** — though it can exceed 1 (which is impossible for a heat engine efficiency).

**Analogy:** COP of 3 means "for every 1 unit of electricity you pay for, you get 3 units of cooling" — like a 3x return on your energy investment.

---

## 1.7 Reversed Carnot Cycle

> [!NOTE]
> **Reversed Carnot Cycle:** The Carnot heat engine cycle (the most efficient possible heat engine) **operated in reverse** — it becomes the most efficient possible refrigeration cycle between two given temperature reservoirs. It is the **theoretical benchmark** for all real refrigeration cycles.

**Key Property:** It consists of **two isothermal** and **two isentropic** processes, but now traversed in the opposite direction (compressor-receiver-expander-cooler sequence).

---

## 1.8 Vapour Compression Refrigeration (VCR) Cycle

> [!NOTE]
> **Vapour Compression Cycle (Description Only as per KTU 2024):** A practical refrigeration cycle in which a **refrigerant** (e.g., R-134a, R-22, ammonia) is **vaporized at low pressure** (absorbing heat from the cold space), then **compressed to high pressure**, **condensed by rejecting heat** to the surroundings, and finally **expanded back to low pressure** through a throttling device. It is the working principle of **domestic refrigerators, ACs, and cold storage**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** This topic is for **description only** — no detailed calculations on cycle parameters are required for ESE. Focus on identifying **components, state points, and the sequence of processes**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Strokes of a 4-Stroke SI Engine (Detailed Logic)

**Stroke 1 — Suction (Intake) Stroke:**
- Piston moves from **Top Dead Center (TDC)** to **Bottom Dead Center (BDC)**.
- The **intake valve** opens; the **exhaust valve remains closed**.
- A mixture of **air + petrol vapor** (in carbureted/MPFI systems) is drawn into the cylinder due to the partial vacuum created by the descending piston.
- Process: Approximately **constant pressure** (≈ atmospheric) intake.

**Stroke 2 — Compression Stroke:**
- Both valves are **closed**. Piston moves from **BDC → TDC**.
- The air-fuel mixture is **compressed** to a high pressure and temperature.
- Compression ratio **r = V₁/V₂**, where V₁ = clearance volume + swept volume, V₂ = clearance volume.
- For SI: **r ≈ 8 to 12**; for CI: **r ≈ 14 to 22**.
- Just before TDC, the **spark plug fires** (in SI engines) — this initiates combustion.

**Stroke 3 — Power (Expansion / Working) Stroke:**
- Combustion raises pressure rapidly (constant volume heat addition, in idealized Otto cycle).
- High-pressure gas **pushes the piston from TDC → BDC**, delivering useful work via the connecting rod and crankshaft.
- This is the **only stroke where the engine delivers work** to the crankshaft.

**Stroke 4 — Exhaust Stroke:**
- The **exhaust valve opens**; intake valve remains closed.
- Piston moves from **BDC → TDC**, **pushing out the burnt gases** to the atmosphere.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In 2-stroke engines, the **suction and exhaust** are achieved via **ports (not valves)** using the **blower/scavenging** action, and **intake + compression happen in one upward stroke**, while **power + exhaust happen in one downward stroke**.

---

## 2.2 SI vs CI Engine — The Comparative Analysis

| Parameter | SI Engine | CI Engine |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| Fuel | Petrol (gasoline) | Diesel |
| Ignition | Spark plug (external) | Heat of compression (auto) |
| Compression ratio | 8 – 12 | 14 – 22 |
| Idealized cycle | **Otto cycle** | **Diesel cycle** |
| Fuel injection | Intake manifold (MPFI) or carburetor | Direct into cylinder (CRDI) |
| Throttle | Yes (controls load) | No throttle (load controlled by fuel quantity) |
| Thermal efficiency | Lower | Higher (≈ 35-40%) |
| Weight | Lighter | Heavier (stronger construction) |
| Starting | Easy (no load) | Difficult (needs glow plugs in cold) |
| Application | Cars, bikes | Trucks, locomotives, ships |

---

## 2.3 The Two-Stroke Engine — Operation

| Phase | Piston Motion | Port/Valve Status | Process |
|-------|---------------|-------------------|---------|
| Stroke 1 (Up) | BDC → TDC | Transfer port closes at BDC, exhaust port opens slightly later | Compression of fresh charge + exhaust of burnt gases |
| Stroke 2 (Down) | TDC → BDC | Both ports open (transfer + exhaust) | Power + scavenging of fresh charge from crankcase |

**Key Concept: Crankcase Scavenging** — In a 2-stroke engine, the space below the piston (crankcase) acts as a **pump**. The downward movement compresses the fresh charge in the crankcase, and when ports open, this charge is pushed up into the cylinder, displacing exhaust gases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scavenging** = the process of **replacing exhaust gases with fresh air-fuel mixture** in a 2-stroke engine.

---

## 2.4 CRDI vs MPFI — Deep Comparison

| Feature | MPFI | CRDI |
|---------|------|------|
| Engine type | SI (Petrol) | CI (Diesel) |
| Injection location | Intake port (before valve) | Directly into combustion chamber |
| Injection pressure | 3 – 5 bar | 1000 – 2000 bar |
| Number of injections | 1 per cycle (per cylinder) | Multiple (Pilot + Main + After) |
| Atomization | Coarser | Ultra-fine |
| Emission | Moderate | Lower (especially with Pilot injection) |
| Noise | Quieter | Slightly noisier (combustion knock) |

---

## 2.5 Hybrid Engine Architectures

**Series Hybrid:**
- ICE drives a **generator** → electricity → battery → electric motor → wheels.
- ICE never directly drives wheels. (e.g., older Chevrolet Volt, range-extender EVs)

**Parallel Hybrid:**
- ICE **and** electric motor **both** can drive the wheels (via clutch/gearbox).
- (e.g., Honda Insight, mild hybrids in Maruti cars)

**Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid:**
- A **planetary gear set** acts as a power-split device; both ICE and motor can drive wheels directly or via generator.
- (e.g., Toyota Prius, Toyota Camry Hybrid)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** "Series = ICE generates, Motor drives. Parallel = Both can drive. Series-Parallel = Best of both (Toyota's signature)."

---

## 2.6 Refrigeration Concepts in Depth

### The Vapour Compression Cycle (VCR) — Four Key Processes

| Process | Component | What Happens | Thermodynamic State |
|---------|-----------|--------------|---------------------|
| 1 → 2 | **Compressor** | Low-pressure vapor compressed to high pressure | Superheated vapor |
| 2 → 3 | **Condenser** | High-pressure vapor rejects heat to surroundings → condenses to liquid | Saturated/subcooled liquid |
| 3 → 4 | **Throttle/Expansion Valve** | High-pressure liquid expands to low pressure (flash evaporation) | Liquid-Vapor mixture |
| 4 → 1 | **Evaporator** | Low-pressure mixture absorbs heat from cold space → vaporizes | Saturated vapor |

### Refrigerating Effect (RE) and Work Done

$$\text{RE per kg} = h_1 - h_4 \quad \text{(kJ/kg)}$$

$$\text{Work done per kg} = h_2 - h_1 \quad \text{(kJ/kg)}$$

$$\text{Heat rejected per kg} = h_2 - h_4 \quad \text{(kJ/kg)}$$

where $h_1, h_2, h_3, h_4$ are the enthalpies at the four state points (1 = compressor inlet, 2 = compressor outlet/condenser inlet, 3 = condenser outlet/expansion valve inlet, 4 = expansion valve outlet/evaporator inlet).

---

## 2.7 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is your EXAM WEAPON. Memorize the boxed formulas — they appear almost every ESE.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|------|
| 1 | **Compression Ratio** | $r = V_1 / V_2$ | $V_1$=total cyl. volume, $V_2$=clearance vol. | Dimensionless |
| 2 | **Otto Cycle Efficiency (SI Engine)** | $\eta_{otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | $r$=comp. ratio, $\gamma = C_p/C_v$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | **Diesel Cycle Efficiency (CI Engine)** | $\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$ | $\rho$=cut-off ratio $=V_3/V_2$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | **1 Tonne of Refrigeration** | $1 \text{ TR} = 50 \text{ kcal/min} = 210 \text{ kJ/min} \approx 3.517 \text{ kW}$ | Standard cooling rate | kW |
| 5 | **Mass of ice melted in 24 h** | $m = \dfrac{\text{TR} \times 210 \times 60 \times 24}{L_f} = \dfrac{\text{TR} \times 302400}{335}$ | $L_f = 335$ kJ/kg | kg |
| 6 | **Refrigerator COP** | $\text{COP}_{ref} = \dfrac{Q_2}{W} = \dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1 - Q_2}$ | $Q_2$=heat extracted, $W$=work in, $Q_1$=heat rejected | Dimensionless |
| 7 | **Heat Pump COP** | $\text{COP}_{hp} = \dfrac{Q_1}{W} = \dfrac{Q_1}{Q_1 - Q_2}$ | $Q_1$=heat delivered, $W$=work in | Dimensionless |
| 8 | **Relation between COPs** | $\text{COP}_{hp} = \text{COP}_{ref} + 1$ | Always true for any cycle | Dimensionless |
| 9 | **Reversed Carnot COP (Refrigerator)** | $\text{COP}_{carnot,ref} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1 - T_2}$ | $T_1$=hot reservoir (K), $T_2$=cold reservoir (K) | Dimensionless |
| 10 | **Reversed Carnot COP (Heat Pump)** | $\text{COP}_{carnot,hp} = \dfrac{T_1}{T_1 - T_2}$ | Same $T_1, T_2$ as above | Dimensionless |
| 11 | **Heat Rejected Relation** | $Q_1 = Q_2 + W$ | Energy balance of refrigerator | kW |
| 12 | **Refrigeration Load (in TR)** | $\text{TR} = \dfrac{m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}{210 \times 60}$ | $m$=kg/s, $c_p$=kJ/kg·K, $\Delta T$=K | TR |
| 13 | **VCR COP (practical)** | $\text{COP}_{vcr} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | $h_i$=enthalpy at state $i$ | Dimensionless |
| 14 | **Brake Power (4-stroke)** | $BP = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | $N$=rpm, $T$=torque (N·m) | Watts |
| 15 | **Indicated Power** | $IP = \dfrac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot n \cdot K}{60}$ | $P_m$=MEP, $L$=stroke, $A$=area, $n$=rpm/2 for 4S, $K$=# cyl. | Watts |

> [!NOTE]
> **For air-standard cycle analysis (Otto, Diesel):** $\gamma$ for air ≈ **1.4** (this is the ratio of specific heats at constant pressure to constant volume for diatomic gases at room temperature).

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **IC Engines** drive 95% of the world's transportation (road, rail, marine, aviation turbines).
- **CRDI/MPFI** enable compliance with **Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI)** emission norms in India.
- **Hybrid Vehicles** deliver **40-50% better fuel economy** in city driving (regenerative braking recaptures kinetic energy).
- **Refrigeration** is essential for: food preservation (cold storage chains), pharmaceuticals (vaccine storage at 2-8°C), air-conditioning (human comfort), cryogenics (LNG, medical oxygen), and industrial process cooling.
- **Vapour Compression Cycles** cool ~99% of all domestic and commercial refrigeration worldwide.
- **Reversed Carnot Cycle** provides the **theoretical maximum COP** — used as the benchmark to judge real cycles.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Otto Cycle Thermal Efficiency (SI Engine)

> [!NOTE]
> The **Otto cycle** is the idealized thermodynamic cycle for SI engines, consisting of:
> - Process 1→2: **Isentropic compression**
> - Process 2→3: **Constant volume heat addition** ($Q_{in}$)
> - Process 3→4: **Isentropic expansion**
> - Process 4→1: **Constant volume heat rejection** ($Q_{out}$)

### Step 1: Heat Added (at constant volume, 2→3)

For a closed system, heat added at constant volume:

$$Q_{in} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2)$$

**Reasoning:** At constant volume, no boundary work is done, so all heat goes into raising the internal energy of the gas. $C_v$ = specific heat at constant volume.

### Step 2: Heat Rejected (at constant volume, 4→1)

$$Q_{out} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$$

**Reasoning:** Similarly, heat rejected at constant volume lowers the internal energy.

### Step 3: Net Work Done

For any cyclic process, the net work equals net heat transfer (First Law):

$$W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out} = m C_v \left[ (T_3 - T_2) - (T_4 - T_1) \right]$$

### Step 4: Thermal Efficiency Definition

$$\eta = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{Q_{in} - Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$$

### Step 5: Substitute the Expressions

$$\eta = \frac{m C_v \left[ (T_3 - T_2) - (T_4 - T_1) \right]}{m C_v (T_3 - T_2)}$$

**The $m C_v$ cancels:**

$$\eta = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}$$

### Step 6: Apply Isentropic Relations

For **process 1→2** (isentropic compression of an ideal gas):

$$T_2 / T_1 = (V_1 / V_2)^{\gamma - 1} = r^{\gamma - 1}$$

For **process 3→4** (isentropic expansion):

$$T_3 / T_4 = (V_4 / V_3)^{\gamma - 1} = r^{\gamma - 1}$$

(Here we use $V_4 = V_1$ and $V_3 = V_2$ for the constant volume processes.)

### Step 7: Relate Temperature Ratios

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{T_3}{T_4}$$

Rearranging:

$$T_4 = T_3 \cdot \frac{T_1}{T_2}$$

Substitute into $T_4 - T_1$:

$$T_4 - T_1 = T_3 \cdot \frac{T_1}{T_2} - T_1 = T_1 \left( \frac{T_3}{T_2} - 1 \right) = T_1 \cdot \frac{T_3 - T_2}{T_2}$$

### Step 8: Substitute Back into Efficiency

$$\eta = 1 - \frac{T_1 \cdot \frac{T_3 - T_2}{T_2}}{T_3 - T_2} = 1 - \frac{T_1}{T_2}$$

### Step 9: Express in Terms of Compression Ratio

Since $T_2 / T_1 = r^{\gamma - 1}$, we have $T_1 / T_2 = 1 / r^{\gamma - 1}$:

$$\boxed{\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Board Killer Fact:** Higher compression ratio → Higher efficiency. But SI engines are limited to $r \approx 10$ due to **knocking** (auto-ignition of fuel-air mixture). CI engines can have $r \approx 20$ → higher efficiency.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Diesel Cycle Efficiency (CI Engine)

> [!NOTE]
> The **Diesel cycle** differs from Otto in that **heat addition is at constant pressure** (2→3), not constant volume. This is because fuel is injected and burns at nearly constant pressure as the piston moves down.

### The Four Processes of the Diesel Cycle:
- 1→2: Isentropic compression
- 2→3: **Constant pressure heat addition** (fuel injection + combustion)
- 3→4: Isentropic expansion
- 4→1: Constant volume heat rejection

### Step 1: Heat Added (at constant pressure, 2→3)

At constant pressure, use $C_p$:

$$Q_{in} = m C_p (T_3 - T_2)$$

### Step 2: Heat Rejected (at constant volume, 4→1)

$$Q_{out} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$$

### Step 3: Net Work & Efficiency

$$\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{m C_v (T_4 - T_1)}{m C_p (T_3 - T_2)} = 1 - \frac{1}{\gamma} \cdot \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}$$

### Step 4: Express in Terms of Compression Ratio $r$ and Cut-off Ratio $\rho$

Where:
- $r = V_1 / V_2$ (compression ratio)
- $\rho = V_3 / V_2$ (cut-off ratio — how much the volume increases during heat addition at constant pressure)

Using isentropic relations and the ideal gas law at the constant-pressure process:

$$\boxed{\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \frac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma (\rho - 1)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Comparison:** Notice that the Otto efficiency is a **special case** of the Diesel efficiency when $\rho \to 1$ (heat addition at constant volume). The Diesel cycle also has the term $\frac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$, which **reduces efficiency** if cut-off ratio is large — meaning a longer combustion duration is less efficient.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Reversed Carnot COP (Refrigerator)

### Setup
A refrigerator operating between a **cold reservoir at $T_2$ (K)** and a **hot reservoir at $T_1$ (K)**, where $T_1 > T_2$, with the **Reversed Carnot cycle** as the ideal cycle.

### Step 1: Heat Transfer Relations for Reversed Carnot

- Heat extracted from cold space: $Q_2$ (at $T_2$, isothermal)
- Heat rejected to hot space: $Q_1$ (at $T_1$, isothermal)
- The two isentropic processes transfer heat only with no entropy change.

### Step 2: Apply Entropy Balance

For a **cyclic, reversible** process, the net change in entropy of the universe is zero (Second Law for reversible cycle):

$$\Delta S_{universe} = \frac{Q_1}{T_1} - \frac{Q_2}{T_2} = 0$$

**Reasoning:** $Q_1$ leaves the system at $T_1$ (positive heat into hot reservoir → positive entropy), $Q_2$ enters the system from the cold reservoir (positive heat out of cold reservoir → negative entropy change for system, but positive for the cold reservoir). For a reversible cycle, these must balance.

### Step 3: Rearrange the Entropy Equation

$$\frac{Q_1}{T_1} = \frac{Q_2}{T_2}$$

$$Q_1 = Q_2 \cdot \frac{T_1}{T_2}$$

### Step 4: Apply First Law (Energy Conservation)

$$W = Q_1 - Q_2$$

**Reasoning:** Work input equals the difference between heat rejected and heat extracted.

### Step 5: Substitute $Q_1$ in Terms of $Q_2$

$$W = Q_2 \cdot \frac{T_1}{T_2} - Q_2 = Q_2 \left( \frac{T_1}{T_2} - 1 \right) = Q_2 \cdot \frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_2}$$

### Step 6: Form the COP (Refrigerator)

$$\text{COP}_{ref} = \frac{Q_2}{W} = \frac{Q_2}{Q_2 \cdot \frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_2}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_{carnot,ref} = \frac{T_2}{T_1 - T_2}}$$

### Step 7: Derive the Heat Pump COP

For a **heat pump**, the desired effect is $Q_1$ (heat delivered), not $Q_2$:

$$\text{COP}_{hp} = \frac{Q_1}{W}$$

Using $Q_1 = Q_2 \cdot \frac{T_1}{T_2}$ and $W = Q_2 \cdot \frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_2}$:

$$\text{COP}_{hp} = \frac{Q_2 \cdot T_1 / T_2}{Q_2 \cdot (T_1 - T_2)/T_2} = \frac{T_1}{T_1 - T_2}$$

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_{carnot,hp} = \frac{T_1}{T_1 - T_2}}$$

### Step 8: Verify the Universal Relation

$$\text{COP}_{hp} - \text{COP}_{ref} = \frac{T_1}{T_1 - T_2} - \frac{T_2}{T_1 - T_2} = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1 - T_2} = 1$$

$$\therefore \boxed{\text{COP}_{hp} = \text{COP}_{ref} + 1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This relation holds for ANY refrigeration cycle, not just Carnot.** It is a fundamental identity that examiners love to test.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example — KTU Typical Problem

**Problem:** A cold storage plant is to be maintained at $-10°C$. The surrounding temperature is $30°C$. Calculate the **COP of a Reversed Carnot refrigerator** and the **power required to maintain the cold storage** if the refrigeration load is **5 TR**.

### Step 1: Convert Temperatures to Kelvin

$$T_1 = 30 + 273 = 303 \text{ K}$$

$$T_2 = -10 + 273 = 263 \text{ K}$$

### Step 2: Calculate COP

$$\text{COP}_{carnot} = \frac{T_2}{T_1 - T_2} = \frac{263}{303 - 263} = \frac{263}{40} = 6.575$$

### Step 3: Convert 5 TR to kW

$$Q_2 = 5 \times 3.517 = 17.585 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 4: Calculate Power Required

$$W = \frac{Q_2}{\text{COP}} = \frac{17.585}{6.575} = 2.674 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 5: Interpretation

> A real refrigerator between the same temperatures would have a **COP of about 2-3** (due to irreversibilities), and would need **~6-9 kW** of power. The Reversed Carnot is the **theoretical best-case scenario**.

---

## 3.5 Refrigerating Effect (RE) from VCR Cycle — Analytical Steps

For a **practical VCR cycle** with refrigerant (state points 1-2-3-4):

### Step 1: RE per kg of refrigerant

$$RE = h_1 - h_4 \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}$$

Where:
- $h_1$ = enthalpy at compressor inlet (saturated vapor)
- $h_4$ = enthalpy at evaporator inlet (after throttling)

### Step 2: Work of compression per kg

$$W_c = h_2 - h_1 \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}$$

Where $h_2$ = enthalpy at compressor outlet (superheated vapor).

### Step 3: Heat rejected in condenser per kg

$$Q_1 = h_2 - h_3 \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}$$

### Step 4: Mass flow rate of refrigerant

$$\dot{m} = \frac{\text{Refrigeration Load (kW)}}{RE \text{ (kJ/kg)}}$$

If the load is given in TR, first convert to kW: $\text{kW} = \text{TR} \times 3.517$.

### Step 5: COP of VCR

$$\text{COP}_{vcr} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Note:** Detailed P-h and T-s diagrams for the VCR cycle are **NOT** required for ESE per syllabus ("Description Only"). Focus on **enumerating components and processes**.

## 4.1 Block-Level Schematic of a 4-Stroke SI Engine — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```


## 4.7 Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1[EVAPORATOR LOW PRESSURE COLD SIDE] -->|SATURATED VAPOR STATE 1| G2[COMPRESSOR]
    G2 -->|SUPERHEATED VAPOR STATE 2| G3[CONDENSER HIGH PRESSURE HOT SIDE]
    G3 -->|LIQUID STATE 3| G4[EXPANSION VALVE THROTTLING DEVICE]
    G4 -->|LIQUID VAPOR MIXTURE STATE 4| G1
    G1 -. HEAT ABSORBED FROM COLD SPACE Q2 .-> G1
    G3 -. HEAT REJECTED TO SURROUNDINGS Q1 .-> G3
    G2 -. WORK INPUT W .-> G2
```

## 4.8 Reversed Carnot Cycle — Block Flow (Refrigerator)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H1[COLD SPACE AT T2] -->|ISOTHERMAL HEAT Q2 AT T2| H2[EXPANDER ISENTROPIC 4 TO 3]
    H2 -->|ISENTROPIC PROCESS| H3[HOT SPACE AT T1]
    H3 -->|ISOTHERMAL HEAT REJECTED Q1 AT T1| H4[COMPRESSOR ISENTROPIC 1 TO 2]
    H4 -->|ISENTROPIC PROCESS| H1
    H4 -. WORK INPUT W .-> H4
    H1 -. DESIRED EFFECT Q2 .-> H1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For P-h and T-s Diagrams of VCR and Reversed Carnot cycle:** The KTU 2024 syllabus marks these topics as **"Description Only"** — meaning students should be able to **describe the processes in sequence and identify state points** verbally, but detailed graphical plotting is not assessed. The block diagrams above serve this purpose.

## 4.9 Cycle Sequencing Comparison Table (Pictorial Reference)

| Cycle | Heat Addition | Heat Rejection | Compression | Expansion |
|-------|---------------|----------------|-------------|-----------|
| **Otto (SI)** | Constant Volume (2→3) | Constant Volume (4→1) | Isentropic (1→2) | Isentropic (3→4) |
| **Diesel (CI)** | Constant Pressure (2→3) | Constant Volume (4→1) | Isentropic (1→2) | Isentropic (3→4) |
| **Reversed Carnot (Ref.)** | Isothermal (3→4) | Isothermal (1→2) | Isentropic (2→3) | Isentropic (4→1) |
| **VCR (Practical)** | Evaporation (4→1) at low P | Condensation (2→3) at high P | Actual Compression (1→2) | Throttling (3→4) |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Question Bank Notes:** Questions are tagged with simulated KTU past-year tags, mapped to Course Outcomes (CO1-CO2 for this module) and Bloom's Taxonomy Levels (L1-Remember, L2-Understand, L3-Apply, L4-Analyze, L5-Evaluate). Part A carries 3 marks each; Part B carries 14 marks each (with sub-parts of 7+7 marks).

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – December 2023 | CO1 | Bloom's L1-Remember]
**Differentiate between SI and CI engines based on (a) fuel used, (b) ignition method, and (c) typical compression ratio.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | SI Engine | CI Engine |
|---------|-----------|-----------|
| (a) Fuel used | Petrol (gasoline) | Diesel |
| (b) Ignition method | Spark plug provides external ignition | Heat of compression ignites fuel (auto-ignition) |
| (c) Compression ratio | 8 to 12 (limited by knocking) | 14 to 22 (higher → higher efficiency) |

SI engines are used in cars and two-wheelers; CI engines in trucks, buses, and heavy equipment.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 | CO2 | Bloom's L2-Understand]
**Define Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigerator. How is it different from the efficiency of a heat engine?**

**Model Answer:**

**COP of a refrigerator** is defined as the ratio of the heat extracted from the cold space (desired effect) to the work input required to accomplish it:

$$\text{COP}_{ref} = \frac{Q_2}{W} = \frac{Q_2}{Q_1 - Q_2}$$

where $Q_2$ = heat extracted from cold body, $Q_1$ = heat rejected to hot body, $W$ = work input.

**Difference from heat engine efficiency:**

| Heat Engine Efficiency | Refrigerator COP |
|------------------------|------------------|
| Ratio of work output to heat input | Ratio of heat extracted to work input |
| $\eta = W/Q_{in}$, always **< 1** | COP can be **> 1** (often 2–6) |
| Limited by Second Law (η ≤ 1) | Not bounded by 1; bounded by Carnot COP |

The COP represents the **"return on energy invested"** — a higher COP means more cooling per unit of work.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU ESE pattern, students are given **internal choice** between two questions of 14 marks each. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – December 2023 | CO1 | Bloom's L3-Apply + L4-Analyze]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a 4-stroke SI petrol engine. State the function of each part.**

**Model Answer:**

A **4-stroke SI engine** completes one working cycle in **four piston strokes** (two revolutions of the crankshaft). The four strokes are:

**1. Suction (Intake) Stroke:**
- Piston moves from TDC to BDC.
- Intake valve opens, exhaust valve remains closed.
- Air-fuel mixture drawn in from the carburetor/MPFI system.
- Pressure in cylinder ≈ atmospheric (slightly below).

**2. Compression Stroke:**
- Both valves closed.
- Piston moves from BDC to TDC.
- The mixture is compressed to a high pressure and temperature.
- Compression ratio r = V₁/V₂ ≈ 8 to 12.
- Just before TDC, the **spark plug fires**, initiating combustion.

**3. Power (Working) Stroke:**
- Combustion gases expand rapidly, pushing the piston from TDC to BDC.
- This is the **only stroke** where the engine delivers work.
- Both valves are closed.

**4. Exhaust Stroke:**
- Exhaust valve opens; intake valve closed.
- Piston moves from BDC to TDC, expelling burnt gases.

**Function of Key Parts:**
- **Cylinder:** Houses the piston and combustion.
- **Piston:** Receives gas pressure and transmits force to the connecting rod.
- **Connecting Rod:** Links piston to crankshaft, converts linear to rotary motion.
- **Crankshaft:** Converts reciprocating motion to rotary, delivers output torque.
- **Flywheel:** Stores rotational kinetic energy, ensures smooth running.
- **Valves (Intake & Exhaust):** Control the flow of gases in/out of cylinder.
- **Camshaft & Timing Belt/Chain:** Synchronizes valve opening with piston position.
- **Spark Plug:** Provides the spark to ignite the air-fuel mixture.
- **Cylinder Head:** Closes the top of the cylinder; houses valves, spark plug, injectors.
- **Crankcase:** Houses the crankshaft and lubricating oil.
- **Lubrication System:** Reduces friction between moving parts.
- **Cooling System (water/air):** Removes excess heat from the cylinder.

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to SECTION 4.1 and 4.3 above for the working cycle flow and parts architecture.)

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine develops a brake power of 30 kW at 3000 rpm. The mean effective pressure is 8 bar and the mechanical efficiency is 80%. Calculate: (i) the brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC) if the fuel consumption is 10 kg/h, and (ii) the brake thermal efficiency if the calorific value of fuel is 43 MJ/kg.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Number of cylinders, $K = 4$
- Brake power, $BP = 30$ kW
- Speed, $N = 3000$ rpm
- BMEP (Brake Mean Effective Pressure) related to BP via: not directly given; use $BP$ for BSFC.
- Mechanical efficiency, $\eta_{mech} = 80\% = 0.8$
- Fuel consumption, $m_f = 10$ kg/h
- Calorific value, $CV = 43$ MJ/kg = 43,000 kJ/kg

**Step 1: Convert fuel consumption to kg/s**

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{10}{3600} = 2.778 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate BSFC**

$$\text{BSFC} = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{BP} = \frac{2.778 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}}{30 \text{ kW}} = 9.26 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/kJ}$$

Converting to standard form (kg/kWh):

$$\text{BSFC} = \frac{10 \text{ kg/h}}{30 \text{ kW}} = 0.333 \text{ kg/kWh}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Brake Thermal Efficiency**

Heat input rate:

$$Q_{in} = \dot{m}_f \times CV = 2.778 \times 10^{-3} \times 43{,}000 = 119.44 \text{ kW}$$

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP}{Q_{in}} = \frac{30}{119.44} = 0.2512 = \mathbf{25.12\%}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) BSFC = **0.333 kg/kWh** (or 333 g/kWh)
- (ii) Brake thermal efficiency = **25.12%**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 | CO2 | Bloom's L3-Apply + L5-Evaluate]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**A refrigeration system operates on the Vapour Compression Cycle (VCR). Describe the cycle with a block diagram and explain the function of each component.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **"Description Only"** topic per KTU 2024 syllabus, so the answer should focus on the **sequence of processes and component functions**, not numerical calculations.

The **Vapour Compression Refrigeration (VCR) cycle** is the most widely used practical refrigeration cycle. It uses a refrigerant (e.g., R-134a, R-22, ammonia) that undergoes **phase change** (vaporization and condensation) to absorb and reject heat.

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to SECTION 4.7 above for the functional flow diagram.)

**The four main components and their functions:**

**1. Compressor (State 1 → State 2):**
- Compresses the low-pressure, low-temperature refrigerant vapor (from evaporator) to high pressure and high temperature.
- The compression is approximately **isentropic** in an ideal cycle (practically slightly superheated).
- **Types:** Reciprocating, rotary, scroll, screw, centrifugal.
- Work input **W = h₂ - h₁** is supplied here (electric motor driven).

**2. Condenser (State 2 → State 3):**
- The high-pressure, high-temperature vapor rejects heat **Q₁** to the surroundings (atmosphere or cooling water).
- The refrigerant **condenses** to a high-pressure liquid (saturated or subcooled).
- **Types:** Air-cooled (in domestic fridges), water-cooled (in industrial systems).

**3. Expansion Valve / Throttling Device (State 3 → State 4):**
- The high-pressure liquid passes through a throttling device (capillary tube, thermostatic expansion valve, or float valve).
- Pressure and temperature **drop suddenly** due to the **throttling (isenthalpic)** process: **h₃ = h₄**.
- A small fraction of liquid **flash-evaporates**, cooling the rest further.
- **No work done, no heat transfer** during throttling.

**4. Evaporator (State 4 → State 1):**
- The low-pressure, low-temperature liquid-vapor mixture absorbs heat **Q₂** from the space to be cooled (refrigeration effect).
- The liquid **completely vaporizes** to saturated vapor (state 1).
- **Q₂ = h₁ - h₄** is the desired refrigeration effect.

**Energy Balance:**

$$W + Q_2 = Q_1 \quad \text{or} \quad Q_1 = W + Q_2$$

**COP of the VCR Cycle:**

$$\text{COP}_{vcr} = \frac{Q_2}{W} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$

**Advantages of VCR:** Compact, reliable, high COP (2-4 typical), suitable for all capacities from domestic to industrial.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A refrigerator operates between $-5°C$ (cold space) and $40°C$ (surroundings). If the actual COP achieved is 2.5, calculate: (i) the COP of a Reversed Carnot refrigerator operating between the same temperatures, (ii) the ratio of the actual work to the Carnot work for the same refrigeration effect, and (iii) the relative efficiency.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $T_2 = -5 + 273 = 268$ K (cold reservoir)
- $T_1 = 40 + 273 = 313$ K (hot reservoir)
- $\text{COP}_{actual} = 2.5$

**Part (i): Reversed Carnot COP**

$$\text{COP}_{carnot} = \frac{T_2}{T_1 - T_2} = \frac{268}{313 - 268} = \frac{268}{45}$$

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_{carnot} = 5.956}$$

**Part (ii): Ratio of actual work to Carnot work**

Let the refrigeration effect be $Q_2$ (same for both).

For **actual refrigerator:**

$$W_{actual} = \frac{Q_2}{\text{COP}_{actual}} = \frac{Q_2}{2.5}$$

For **Carnot refrigerator:**

$$W_{carnot} = \frac{Q_2}{\text{COP}_{carnot}} = \frac{Q_2}{5.956}$$

**Ratio:**

$$\frac{W_{actual}}{W_{carnot}} = \frac{Q_2 / 2.5}{Q_2 / 5.956} = \frac{5.956}{2.5}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{W_{actual}}{W_{carnot}} = 2.382}$$

**Interpretation:** The actual refrigerator requires **2.382 times more work** than the ideal Carnot refrigerator for the same cooling effect. This shows the **irreversibility penalty**.

**Part (iii): Relative Efficiency (also called Coefficient of Performance Ratio)**

$$\eta_{relative} = \frac{\text{COP}_{actual}}{\text{COP}_{carnot}} = \frac{2.5}{5.956}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{relative} = 0.4198 = 41.98\%}$$

**Interpretation:** The actual refrigerator achieves about **42% of the ideal Carnot performance** — typical for real VCR systems which are about 40-50% of the Carnot limit.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in this module:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin** in Carnot COP calculations. The KTU examiner deducts **1-2 marks** for using $T_1 - T_2$ in °C. ALWAYS add 273 (or 273.15 for higher precision).
> 2. **Confusing Otto and Diesel cycles.** Otto = constant volume heat addition (SI). Diesel = constant pressure heat addition (CI). Drawing the wrong P-V diagram = 0 marks for that part.
> 3. **Mixing up "swept volume" with "clearance volume."** Compression ratio $r = V_1/V_2$ where $V_1$ = swept volume + clearance volume; $V_2$ = clearance volume only.
> 4. **Writing COP = efficiency** — they are NOT the same. COP can exceed 1; efficiency cannot.
> 5. **Forgetting to describe the VCR cycle components** when asked — students often draw the diagram but forget to list all 4 components explicitly. Always list: **Compressor, Condenser, Expansion Valve, Evaporator**.
> 6. **In MPFI vs CRDI questions:** Students write "MPFI is for diesel" — this is **WRONG**. MPFI is for petrol, CRDI is for diesel. **1 mark lost for this confusion.**
> 7. **Saying "hybrid = electric car"** — hybrid uses BOTH IC engine and electric motor. Pure electric vehicles (BEV) are NOT hybrids. The definition is strict: **two or more distinct power sources**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Save this section for last-minute revision before ESE. Read it twice, and you'll be 80% exam-ready.**

### Internal Combustion Engines
- ☐ **IC Engine** = combustion inside cylinder, converts chemical → mechanical energy.
- ☐ **SI Engine** = petrol, spark plug, r = 8–12, Otto cycle.
- ☐ **CI Engine** = diesel, compression ignition, r = 14–22, Diesel cycle.
- ☐ **4-stroke** = 4 strokes, 2 revolutions, 1 power stroke per 2 revs.
- ☐ **2-stroke** = 2 strokes, 1 revolution, 1 power stroke per 1 rev.
- ☐ **Otto efficiency:** $\eta = 1 - 1/r^{\gamma-1}$ (γ = 1.4 for air).
- ☐ **Diesel efficiency:** $\eta = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \frac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$
- ☐ **Key parts:** Cylinder, Piston, Connecting Rod, Crankshaft, Flywheel, Camshaft, Valves, Spark Plug, Crankcase.
- ☐ **Higher r → higher efficiency** (limited by knocking in SI).

### Modern Fuel Systems
- ☐ **MPFI** = petrol + injectors at intake port + 3-5 bar pressure.
- ☐ **CRDI** = diesel + common rail + 1000-2000 bar + solenoid injectors + ECU-controlled.
- ☐ **CRDI gives better atomization → better combustion → lower emissions.**

### Hybrid Engines
- ☐ **Hybrid = IC engine + Electric motor** (two distinct power sources).
- ☐ **Series:** ICE → generator → motor → wheels.
- ☐ **Parallel:** Both ICE and motor can drive wheels.
- ☐ **Series-Parallel (Power-Split):** Planetary gear set, Toyota Prius style.
- ☐ **Regenerative braking** recaptures kinetic energy into battery.

### Refrigeration
- ☐ **Refrigeration = heat moved from cold to hot** using work input (reverse of heat engine).
- ☐ **1 TR = 50 kcal/min = 210 kJ/min ≈ 3.517 kW** (engineering convention).
- ☐ **1 TR melts 1000 kg of ice at 0°C in 24 hours** (definition).
- ☐ **COP of refrigerator** = $Q_2 / W$ (desired effect / work input).
- ☐ **COP of heat pump** = $Q_1 / W$ (heat delivered / work input).
- ☐ **$\text{COP}_{hp} = \text{COP}_{ref} + 1$** (universal relation).
- ☐ **Reversed Carnot COP** (Refrigerator) = $T_2 / (T_1 - T_2)$ ← remember **Kelvin**.
- ☐ **Reversed Carnot COP** (Heat Pump) = $T_1 / (T_1 - T_2)$.
- ☐ **Reversed Carnot is the theoretical BEST** — all real cycles have lower COP.
- ☐ **Relative efficiency = Actual COP / Carnot COP** (typically 0.4 to 0.5 for VCR).

### Vapour Compression Cycle (VCR)
- ☐ **Four components:** Compressor → Condenser → Expansion Valve → Evaporator.
- ☐ **State points:** 1 (compressor inlet, sat. vapor) → 2 (compressor outlet, superheated) → 3 (condenser outlet, liquid) → 4 (evaporator inlet, mixture).
- ☐ **Throttling** is isenthalpic: $h_3 = h_4$.
- ☐ **VCR is "description only"** per KTU — no P-h or T-s numerical problems.

### Universal Memory Aids
- ☐ **"I-C-P-E"** for 4-stroke SI engine strokes.
- ☐ **"Every revolution = power"** for 2-stroke engines.
- ☐ **"MPFI = Petrol Port"**, **"CRDI = Diesel Direct"**.
- ☐ **"COP = Reward for Energy"** (reward = cooling delivered, energy = work in).
- ☐ **"Reversed Carnot = Reversed Engine"** (same processes, opposite direction).

### Critical Constants & Values
- ☐ **γ (air) = 1.4** (for all Otto & Diesel cycle calculations).
- ☐ **1 TR ≈ 3.517 kW** (use 3.5 for quick mental math).
- ☐ **Latent heat of fusion of ice = 335 kJ/kg** (or 80 kcal/kg).
- ☐ **0°C = 273.15 K** (use 273 in exam unless specified).
- ☐ **BSFC typical range: 0.2 – 0.4 kg/kWh** for SI engines; 0.15 – 0.25 for CI.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** Read the question **carefully** — note whether it asks for **Otto or Diesel**, **Refrigerator or Heat Pump**, **Carnot or Actual**. These are the **single biggest sources of mark loss** in GDEST203 Module 1.

---

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDEST203 - BASIC MECHANICAL AND CIVIL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Introduction to Psychrometrics and Air Conditioning

**Psychrometrics** is the science that studies the thermodynamic properties of moist air (a mixture of dry air and water vapour) and the changes in those properties under varying conditions. It is the foundational language used to analyse every air-conditioning (AC) system, refrigeration cycle, and HVAC design problem.

In any air-conditioned space, the "air" we feel is never pure dry air. It is a binary mixture of:
- **Dry Air (D.A.)** — a fixed gas mixture (≈ 78% N₂, 21% O₂, 1% other gases by mass).
- **Water Vapour (W.V.)** — the moisture content that determines human comfort.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Almost every numerical problem in this module is built on the properties **DBT, WBT, DPT, Specific Humidity (ω), Relative Humidity (φ), and Enthalpy (h)**. Master these definitions word-for-word as they appear in 3-mark questions.

---

### 1.2 Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT)

- **Formal Definition:** The Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT) is the temperature of the moist air as measured by a standard mercury-in-glass thermometer whose bulb is **exposed directly to the air** but shielded from radiation and moisture. It represents the *true thermodynamic temperature* of the air.
- **Symbol:** $T_{db}$ or $t_{db}$
- **Unit:** °C or K
- **Measured by:** Ordinary thermometer (Dry Bulb Thermometer).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine leaving a regular kitchen thermometer on your study table for 10 minutes. The reading you get *is* the DBT. It tells you the "actual" hotness or coldness of the air around you.

---

### 1.3 Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT)

- **Formal Definition:** The Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT) is the temperature a parcel of air would have if it were **cooled adiabatically to saturation by evaporation of water into it**. Practically, it is measured by wrapping a muslin cloth (cotton wick) around a thermometer bulb, saturating it with distilled water, and exposing it to a strong airflow.
- **Symbol:** $T_{wb}$ or $t_{wb}$
- **Unit:** °C or K
- **Key Property:** $T_{wb} \leq T_{db}$ (with equality only when the air is fully saturated, i.e., φ = 100%).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** The classic "swamp cooler" in summer. As water evaporates from a wet cloth, it absorbs heat from the cloth, making the cloth feel cooler. The lowest temperature it can reach by this evaporation trick is the WBT. Lower WBT = drier the air. Higher WBT (closer to DBT) = more humid the air.

---

### 1.4 Dew Point Temperature (DPT)

- **Formal Definition:** The Dew Point Temperature (DPT) is the temperature **at which the water vapour present in the air becomes saturated (i.e., starts condensing into liquid droplets)**, when the air is cooled at **constant pressure and constant moisture content (specific humidity)**. Below this temperature, the air cannot hold all its water vapour, and condensation (dew, fog, or rain) occurs.
- **Symbol:** $T_{dp}$ or $t_{dp}$
- **Unit:** °C or K
- **Key Properties:**
  - $T_{dp} \leq T_{wb} \leq T_{db}$
  - $T_{dp} = T_{wb} = T_{db}$ only at saturation (φ = 100%).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Take a cold drink bottle out of the fridge in summer. Tiny water droplets (dew) form on its outer surface. The temperature of the bottle's surface at the moment droplets first appear is the **Dew Point Temperature** of the surrounding air. Notice the bottle was *not* cooled to room temperature — only up to the DPT did condensation begin.

---

### 1.5 Specific Humidity (Absolute Humidity / Humidity Ratio)

- **Formal Definition:** Specific Humidity (ω) is the **mass of water vapour (m_v) per unit mass of dry air (m_a)** present in the moist air mixture. It is also called the **Humidity Ratio** in ASHRAE terminology.
- **Symbol:** $\omega$ (omega) or $W$
- **Unit:** kg of water vapour / kg of dry air (dimensionless ratio).
- **Mathematical Expression:**

$$\omega = \frac{m_v}{m_a} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{p_v}{p_{atm} - p_v}$$

where $p_v$ = partial pressure of water vapour, $p_{atm}$ = total atmospheric pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a packed lunchbox with rice and curry. The "curry" represents water vapour, and the "rice" represents dry air. Specific Humidity tells you the ratio of curry to rice. If you add more curry (more moisture) without changing the rice (dry air), specific humidity rises.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** Specific Humidity is **measured along the dry air mass** (the denominator is always dry air, never total moist air). This is a common exam trap — students mistakenly use total mass as the denominator.

---

### 1.6 Relative Humidity (RH)

- **Formal Definition:** Relative Humidity (φ) is the **ratio of the partial pressure of water vapour (p_v) actually present in the air to the saturation pressure of water vapour (p_vs) at the same DBT**, usually expressed as a percentage.
- **Symbol:** $\phi$ (phi) or RH
- **Unit:** % (dimensionless ratio expressed as percentage).
- **Mathematical Expression:**

$$\phi = \frac{p_v}{p_{vs}(T_{db})} \times 100\%$$

- **Key Property:** $0 \leq \phi \leq 1$ (or 0% to 100%).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** A sponge has a maximum capacity to absorb water. The sponge's current wetness is the **actual vapour pressure (p_v)**, and its total absorption capacity is the **saturation pressure (p_vs)**. Relative Humidity is simply **how full the sponge is** at the current temperature. As temperature rises, the sponge can hold more water, so the same moisture level gives a *lower* RH.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Misconception:** RH is *not* a measure of actual moisture content. A hot dry desert can have a low RH but a "comfortable" cool coastal region with the same RH may feel muggy because the actual vapour pressure differs. Always judge moisture with **Specific Humidity (ω)**, not RH alone.

---

### 1.7 Psychrometric Chart (A Brief Overview)

- **Formal Definition:** A Psychrometric Chart is a **graphical representation of the thermodynamic properties of moist air**, plotted with **Dry Bulb Temperature (X-axis)** and either **Specific Humidity or Moisture Content (Y-axis)**. All other properties (WBT, DPT, RH, enthalpy, specific volume) can be read directly from the chart for a given state point.
- **Type:** Mollier-type (h-ω) and Carrier-type (DBT-ω) charts are the two most common variants used in KTU problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** A psychrometric chart is the "map" of moist air, just like a road map guides a driver. Every air-conditioning process (heating, cooling, humidification, dehumidification, mixing) becomes a "journey" along this map — you start at a point and end at another point following a specific line (process path).

---

### 1.8 Cooling and Dehumidification (Process Overview)

- **Formal Definition:** Cooling and Dehumidification is the process of **simultaneously reducing the temperature and removing moisture** from the air. It is achieved by passing the warm humid air over a **cooling coil** whose surface temperature is **below the Dew Point Temperature** of the incoming air. The moisture condenses on the coil and drips away.
- **Key Parameter:** Apparatus Dew Point (ADP) — the temperature of the cooling coil surface, also called the **Coil Surface Temperature (CST)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Take a cold Coca-Cola can out of the fridge on a hot, humid day. The can's surface is at a temperature below the DPT of the room air. As warm air touches the cold can, it cools down (temperature drops) and water condenses on the can (moisture is removed). That is exactly what an AC's cooling coil does — just on a much larger scale!

---

### 1.9 Central Air Conditioning System (Layout Overview)

- **Formal Definition:** A Central Air Conditioning System is an HVAC system where the air is **conditioned centrally in an equipment room (plant room)** and then **distributed to multiple conditioned spaces (rooms/zones)** through a network of ducts, fans, and diffusers. It is used in large buildings like malls, hospitals, airports, and offices.
- **Major Components:** Air Handling Unit (AHU), Chiller, Cooling Tower, Chilled Water Pump, Condenser Water Pump, Ductwork, Dampers, Filters, Grilles, and Diffusers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of the "central kitchen" of a hotel. Food is prepared in one large kitchen (the AHU/Chiller room) and delivered to all the rooms (zones) via a complex network of corridors and lifts (the ductwork). The Central AC works exactly the same way.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Insight:** For 14-mark questions, you are expected to draw a clean **schematic block diagram** of a Central AC system and label every component. Diagrams with unlabelled lines will lose up to 3-4 marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Relationship Between DBT, WBT, DPT, and RH — The "Why" Behind the Order

The three temperatures follow a strict ordering rule, which is fundamental to every psychrometric problem:

$$T_{dp} \leq T_{wb} \leq T_{db}$$

**Why this order holds:**

1. **DBT is the "real" air temperature** — it never gets reduced by evaporation or condensation.
2. **WBT < DBT** — because WBT accounts for the *evaporative cooling effect* on the wick. Evaporation requires latent heat, which is drawn from the air, lowering its temperature below the true DBT.
3. **DPT < WBT** — because at the DPT, the air is cooled *further* to the point where condensation just begins. The air must first reach saturation (DPT), and only then can it release moisture. The WBT, in contrast, is the limit of evaporative cooling in unsaturated air.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "Dew is the Deepest":** When air cools, it first crosses DBT → WBT (evaporation limit) → DPT (saturation point). The Dew Point is the "deepest" cold the moisture-laden air can reach before it releases water.

---

### 2.2 Theoretical Basis of Each Process Line on the Psychrometric Chart

| Process | Property That Stays Constant | Path on Psychrometric Chart |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensible Heating** | ω (moisture content) | Horizontal line moving right (DBT ↑) |
| **Sensible Cooling** | ω (moisture content) | Horizontal line moving left (DBT ↓) |
| **Cooling + Dehumidification** | Coil surface temp (ADP) constant | Oblique line sloping down-left, ω ↓ |
| **Heating + Humidification** | Steam added, RH rises | Line sloping up-right, ω ↑ |
| **Adiabatic Saturation** | WBT constant (for ideal process) | Line along constant WBT (≈ constant h) |
| **Mixing of Two Air Streams** | Mass-weighted average | Straight line between two state points |
| **Chemical Dehumidification** | DBT changes, ω ↓ drastically | Steep line downward |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Strategy:** The question will often describe a process in words — you must translate it into a **process line direction** on the chart, then read the new state point coordinates. Master the table above — it is worth at least one guaranteed 7-mark question.

---

### 2.3 Enthalpy of Moist Air — The Hidden Property

While not always asked directly, the **enthalpy of moist air per kg of dry air** is the property used during cooling/heating calculations and the psychrometric chart's WBT lines are essentially constant-enthalpy lines (for low-velocity adiabatic processes).

$$h = h_a + \omega \cdot h_v \quad [\text{kJ/kg of dry air}]$$

A simplified KTU-friendly form using DBT ($t$ in °C):

$$h = 1.005 \cdot t + \omega \cdot (2500 + 1.88 \cdot t) \quad [\text{kJ/kg d.a.}]$$

where:
- $1.005$ = specific heat of dry air, kJ/kg·K
- $1.88$ = specific heat of water vapour, kJ/kg·K
- $2500$ = latent heat of vaporisation at 0°C, kJ/kg

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Property / Formula | Symbol | Expression | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specific Humidity | $\omega$ | $\omega = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{p_v}{p_{atm} - p_v}$ | kg w.v. / kg d.a. |
| 2 | Relative Humidity | $\phi$ | $\phi = \dfrac{p_v}{p_{vs}(T_{db})} \times 100$ | % |
| 3 | Saturation Pressure Ratio | — | $p_{vs} = \phi \cdot p_{vs}(T_{db})$ | kPa or bar |
| 4 | Degree of Saturation | $\mu$ | $\mu = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_s}$ | — |
| 5 | Specific Volume of Moist Air | $v$ | $v = \dfrac{R_a \cdot T_{db}}{p_{atm} - p_v}$ | m³/kg d.a. |
| 6 | Enthalpy of Moist Air | $h$ | $h = 1.005\,t + \omega\,(2500 + 1.88\,t)$ | kJ/kg d.a. |
| 7 | DBT-WBT relation (approx.) | — | $p_v = p_{vs}(T_{wb}) - (p_{atm} - p_{vs}(T_{wb}))\cdot \dfrac{(T_{db}-T_{wb})}{1530}$ | kPa |
| 8 | By-Pass Factor (BPF) | BPF | $BPF = \dfrac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}}$ | — |
| 9 | Contact Factor (1 − BPF) | CF | $CF = 1 - BPF$ | — |
| 10 | Cooling Coil Load | $Q_s$ | $Q_s = \dot{m}_a \cdot (h_1 - h_2)$ | kW |
| 11 | Sensible Heat Factor | SHF | $SHF = \dfrac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_l}$ | — |
| 12 | Dehumidified Moisture | $\Delta\omega$ | $\Delta\omega = \omega_1 - \omega_2$ | kg/kg d.a. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Notation Convention:** In all derivations, point **1** denotes the *entering* (inlet) air condition to the coil, and point **2** denotes the *leaving* (outlet) air condition. ADP = Apparatus Dew Point = Cooling coil surface temperature.

---

### 2.5 Why These Concepts Matter in Real Engineering

- **Hospital OTs & Pharma Cleanrooms:** Demand strict control of DBT (22 ± 1°C) and RH (45 ± 5%) to prevent microbial growth and static charge.
- **Data Centres:** RH must be held between 40–55% to avoid electrostatic discharge damage to servers.
- **Textile & Paper Mills:** Specific Humidity (not RH) is the actual parameter that affects fibre moisture regain, which determines yarn strength and paper curl.
- **Cold Storage & Warehouses:** DPT and coil frost formation depend on this psychrometric behaviour; ignoring it can ice up evaporator coils.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Every AC, refrigeration, and HVAC load calculation in a building project begins with a psychrometric analysis. Architects and MEP engineers size cooling coils, chillers, and ducts using these exact formulas and the psychrometric chart.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Specific Humidity Formula

We begin with the ideal gas law applied separately to dry air and water vapour in the moist air mixture.

**Step 1 — Apply Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures**

$$p_{atm} = p_a + p_v$$

where:
- $p_{atm}$ = total atmospheric (barometric) pressure
- $p_a$ = partial pressure of dry air
- $p_v$ = partial pressure of water vapour

**Step 2 — Apply Ideal Gas Equation to Each Component**

For dry air:

$$p_a \cdot V = m_a \cdot R_a \cdot T$$

For water vapour:

$$p_v \cdot V = m_v \cdot R_v \cdot T$$

**Step 3 — Take the Ratio to Eliminate V and T**

$$\frac{p_v}{p_a} = \frac{m_v \cdot R_v}{m_a \cdot R_a}$$

**Step 4 — Use the Known Gas Constant Ratio**

The specific gas constants are:
- $R_a = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K
- $R_v = 0.461$ kJ/kg·K

So:

$$\frac{R_v}{R_a} = \frac{0.461}{0.287} \approx 1.608 \approx \frac{1}{0.622}$$

**Step 5 — Express in Terms of Specific Humidity**

Recall that $\omega = \dfrac{m_v}{m_a}$. Substituting:

$$\frac{p_v}{p_a} = \omega \cdot \frac{1}{0.622} \Rightarrow \omega = 0.622 \cdot \frac{p_v}{p_a}$$

**Step 6 — Replace $p_a$ Using Dalton's Law**

$$p_a = p_{atm} - p_v$$

Therefore, the final form:

$$\boxed{\;\omega = 0.622 \cdot \frac{p_v}{p_{atm} - p_v}\;}$$

This is the **master equation** for specific humidity. Every KTU problem that gives you DBT and RH (and thus $p_v$ via saturated steam tables) ends with this formula.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the DBT–WBT Relationship

This is the **empirical relationship** that lets you compute the partial pressure of water vapour using only DBT and WBT — no need for RH explicitly.

**Step 1 — Energy Balance on the Wet Bulb Thermometer**

The dry bulb reads $T_{db}$, and the wet bulb reads $T_{wb}$. The heat transferred from the air to the wick equals the heat required to evaporate the water from the wick.

Heat given up by air (per kg of dry air):

$$Q_{lost} = (h_a)_{T_{db}} - (h_a)_{T_{wb}} = c_{p,a} \cdot (T_{db} - T_{wb})$$

Heat gained by evaporating water (using $m_v$ from the wick):

$$Q_{gained} = m_v \cdot h_{fg}(T_{wb})$$

**Step 2 — Equate the Two (Steady State)**

$$c_{p,a} \cdot (T_{db} - T_{wb}) = m_v \cdot h_{fg}(T_{wb})$$

**Step 3 — Replace $m_v$ Using the Mass Transfer Analogy**

Forced convection mass transfer gives:

$$m_v = h_m \cdot A \cdot (\rho_{vs}(T_{wb}) - \rho_v)$$

where $h_m$ is the mass transfer coefficient and $\rho_{vs}$, $\rho_v$ are vapour densities.

**Step 4 — Combine and Simplify**

Using the **Lewis Relation** ($h / h_m \approx c_{p,a}$), after considerable simplification and linearisation (valid for moderate temperatures), the classical Carrier formula emerges:

$$\boxed{\;p_v = p_{vs}(T_{wb}) - (p_{atm} - p_{vs}(T_{wb})) \cdot \frac{(T_{db} - T_{wb})}{1530}\;}$$

where the constant **1530** is in °C and arises from the psychrometric constant and gas properties at standard atmospheric pressure.

**Step 5 — Special Case Check (Saturation)**

If air is fully saturated, $p_v = p_{vs}(T_{db}) = p_{vs}(T_{wb})$, and $T_{db} = T_{wb}$. Substituting gives:

$$p_v = p_{vs}(T_{wb}) - (p_{atm} - p_{vs}(T_{wb})) \cdot 0 = p_{vs}(T_{wb}) \;\checkmark$$

The equation self-validates — a powerful KTU-style derivation result.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Bypass Factor (BPF) Equation

The Bypass Factor is **THE most important concept** for any 14-mark numerical on cooling coils.

**Step 1 — Define the Three Temperatures on the Chart**

- $T_1$ = DBT of air entering the cooling coil
- $T_2$ = DBT of air leaving the cooling coil
- $T_{ADP}$ = Apparatus Dew Point (cooling coil surface temperature)

**Step 2 — Define BPF as the Ratio of "Un-contacted" Air to Total Air**

Out of every 1 kg of dry air entering the coil:
- A fraction **(1 − BPF)** is in *perfect* thermal contact with the coil and exits at $T_{ADP}$.
- A fraction **BPF** *bypasses* the coil (slips through fin gaps) and exits at $T_1$ (unchanged).

**Step 3 — Energy Balance on the Outlet Air Stream**

The mixed outlet temperature $T_2$ is the mass-weighted average:

$$T_2 = (1 - BPF) \cdot T_{ADP} + BPF \cdot T_1$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to Isolate BPF**

$$T_2 - T_{ADP} = BPF \cdot (T_1 - T_{ADP})$$

Dividing both sides:

$$\boxed{\;BPF = \frac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Interpreting BPF Values**

| BPF Value | Meaning | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| $BPF = 0$ | All air fully contacted (ideal) | Infinite coil rows — impractical |
| $BPF = 1$ | No air contacted (coil dead) | Coil broken or fully bypassed |
| $BPF \approx 0.1$–$0.3$ | Normal range | Good coil design, 4–8 rows typical |

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Path Equations

| Process | Constraint | Equation Relating Inlet & Outlet | Use in KTU Numerical |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sensible Heating | $\omega_1 = \omega_2$ | $h_2 - h_1 = 1.005 \cdot (T_2 - T_1)$ | Heating coil load |
| Sensible Cooling | $\omega_1 = \omega_2$ | $Q_s = \dot{m}_a \cdot 1.005 \cdot (T_1 - T_2)$ | Dry coil load |
| Cooling + Dehumidification | $T_{ADP}$ constant along process line | $Q_{total} = \dot{m}_a \cdot (h_1 - h_2)$ | AC cooling coil |
| Heating + Humidification | Steam injection | $\omega_2 = \omega_1 + \dfrac{\dot{m}_{steam}}{\dot{m}_a}$ | Winter AC |
| Adiabatic Evaporative Cooling | $h_1 \approx h_2$ (constant) | $T_2 < T_1$, $\omega_2 > \omega_1$ | Desert coolers |
| Mixing of Two Streams | Mass conservation | $h_{mix} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_1 h_1 + \dot{m}_2 h_2}{\dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_2}$ | Recirculated air + fresh air |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Air at DBT 35°C and WBT 25°C is cooled to DBT 20°C using a cooling coil with ADP at 10°C. The mass flow rate of dry air is 1.2 kg/s. Atmospheric pressure = 101.325 kPa. Calculate: (a) the bypass factor, (b) the contact factor, and (c) the total cooling load in kW.

Assume $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$ from standard steam tables and approximate $h_1$, $h_2$ using the formula $h = 1.005\,t + \omega\,(2500 + 1.88\,t)$.

**Solution Outline:**

**Part (a) — Bypass Factor:**

$$BPF = \frac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}} = \frac{20 - 10}{35 - 10} = \frac{10}{25} = 0.4$$

**Part (b) — Contact Factor:**

$$CF = 1 - BPF = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6$$

**Part (c) — Total Cooling Load:**

Using $h = 1.005\,t + \omega\,(2500 + 1.88\,t)$ with $\omega_1 \approx 0.0156$ kg/kg (from DBT 35°C, WBT 25°C) and $\omega_2 \approx 0.0088$ kg/kg (from DBT 20°C, RH ≈ 60%):

$$h_1 = 1.005(35) + 0.0156(2500 + 1.88 \cdot 35) = 35.175 + 0.0156 \cdot 2565.8 \approx 75.2 \text{ kJ/kg d.a.}$$

$$h_2 = 1.005(20) + 0.0088(2500 + 1.88 \cdot 20) = 20.10 + 0.0088 \cdot 2537.6 \approx 42.4 \text{ kJ/kg d.a.}$$

$$Q_{total} = \dot{m}_a \cdot (h_1 - h_2) = 1.2 \cdot (75.2 - 42.4) = 1.2 \cdot 32.8 = 39.36 \text{ kW}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Insight:** The cooling load is calculated from **enthalpy difference**, not just DBT difference. Using $1.005 \cdot \Delta T$ alone gives only the *sensible* part and loses 3 marks in 14-mark questions.
