## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Psychrometric Chart — Functional Block Architecture Flow

The psychrometric chart's coordinate system and process lines are best explained as a functional block diagram mapping property-to-property relationships.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Moist Air Sample] --> B[Measure DBT with Dry Bulb Thermometer]
    A --> C[Measure WBT with Wet Wick Thermometer]
    A --> D[Measure DPT via Dew Cup Method]
    B --> E[Plot Point on X-axis of Psychrometric Chart]
    C --> F[Follow Constant WBT Line]
    D --> G[Follow Constant omega Line]
    E --> H[Locate State Point 1]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Read RH from Curved phi Lines]
    H --> J[Read omega from Y-axis]
    H --> K[Read Enthalpy h from Inclined h Lines]
    H --> L[Read Specific Volume v from Inclined v Lines]
    I --> M[Property Set of Moist Air]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Apply AC Process - Heating Cooling Dehumidification]
    N --> O[Locate State Point 2 on Chart]
    O --> P[Compute Loads: Sensible Latent Total]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The diagram shows the workflow of any psychrometric analysis — measure temperatures, plot the state point, read remaining properties from the chart, then apply a process line to get the new state and compute loads.

---

### 4.2 Cooling and Dehumidification Process — Block-Level Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Return Air from Room - Warm and Humid] --> B1[Filter Section - Removes Dust]
    B1 --> C1[Cooling Coil - Below DPT]
    C1 --> D1[Condensate Drains Out]
    C1 --> E1[Cooled and Dehumidified Air]
    E1 --> F1[Fan Section - Provides Airflow]
    F1 --> G1[Heating Coil - Optional Reheat]
    G1 --> H1[Humidifier - Optional Steam Injection]
    H1 --> I1[Supply Air to Conditioned Space]
    I1 --> J1[Room Gains Heat and Moisture]
    J1 --> A1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Note:** The **ADP (Apparatus Dew Point)** is the temperature of the cooling coil surface. The leaving air (point 2) is *warmer* than the ADP because some air bypasses the coil (Bypass Factor > 0). Always label points 1, 2, and ADP on the diagram.

---

### 4.3 Layout of Central Air Conditioning System — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PLANT_ROOM[Plant Room - Central AC Equipment]
        CH[Chiller - Water Chilling Unit] --> CHWP[Chilled Water Pump]
        CHWP --> CWP[Condenser Water Pump]
        CWP --> CT[Tower - Cooling Tower]
        CT --> CH
        CHWP --> AHU
    end
    AHU[AHU - Air Handling Unit] --> SF[Supply Fan]
    SF --> DW[Supply Ductwork with Dampers]
    DW --> DG[Diffusers and Grilles in Rooms]
    DG --> ZONES[Conditioned Zones - Multiple Rooms]
    ZONES --> RA[Return Air Ductwork]
    RA --> RAF[Return Air Fan]
    RAF --> EXH[Exhaust Air to Outside]
    RAF --> MA[Mixing Air Dampers]
    MA --> FA[Fresh Air Intake from Outside]
    FA --> AHU
    subgraph AHU_INSIDE[Inside AHU]
        FLT[Pre Filters] --> CL[Cooling Coil] --> ELM[Eliminator] --> HC[Heating Coil] --> HUM[Humidifier] --> BFW[Blower Fan]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Diagram Must Include:**
> 1. **Chiller** + **Cooling Tower** + **Chilled Water Pump** + **Condenser Water Pump** (forming the refrigeration cycle).
> 2. **AHU** with **Filter → Cooling Coil → Heating Coil → Humidifier → Fan** (in that order).
> 3. **Supply Duct → Room → Return Duct → Mixing Chamber → Fresh Air Intake** (the air-side loop).
> 4. **Dampers** at the mixing chamber for fresh air / return air proportion control.
> 5. **Condensate drain** line from the cooling coil.

---

### 4.4 Schematic Flow — Moisture and Air Path Through the System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fresh Air + Return Air] --> B[Mixing Plenum with Dampers]
    B --> C[Filter Bank]
    C --> D[Pre-heating Coil]
    D --> E[Spray Washer or Cooling Coil]
    E --> F[Eliminator Plates]
    F --> G[Reheating Coil]
    G --> H[Humidifier with Steam Injection]
    H --> I[Supply Fan]
    I --> J[Supply Duct]
    J --> K[Room Terminal Devices]
    K --> L[Return Grille]
    L --> B
    E -.Condensate Drain.-> M[Drain Pan and Pipe]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to draw a layout, always include the **reheat coil** after the cooling coil if the process is "cooling with controlled RH" — this prevents the air from becoming too dry and uncomfortable. The reheat coil **does not** change moisture content (ω), only the temperature.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the following terms with their SI units: (i) Dry Bulb Temperature, (ii) Dew Point Temperature, and (iii) Specific Humidity.**

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT):** The temperature of moist air as measured by an ordinary (dry) thermometer whose bulb is directly exposed to the air, shielded from radiation. **Unit:** °C or K. It is the true thermodynamic temperature of the air.

(ii) **Dew Point Temperature (DPT):** The temperature at which the water vapour in the air becomes saturated (begins to condense) when cooled at constant pressure and constant specific humidity. **Unit:** °C or K. Below this temperature, condensation occurs as dew or fog.

(iii) **Specific Humidity (ω):** The mass of water vapour present per unit mass of dry air in the mixture. **Unit:** kg of water vapour per kg of dry air (dimensionless). Formula: $\omega = 0.622 \cdot p_v / (p_{atm} - p_v)$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Distinguish between Relative Humidity and Specific Humidity. Why is specific humidity preferred in engineering calculations?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Relative Humidity (φ) | Specific Humidity (ω) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Ratio of actual vapour pressure to saturation vapour pressure at DBT | Mass of water vapour per unit mass of dry air |
| Formula | $\phi = p_v / p_{vs}(T_{db}) \times 100\%$ | $\omega = 0.622 \cdot p_v / (p_{atm} - p_v)$ |
| Unit | Percentage (%) | kg w.v. / kg d.a. |
| Depends on | Both moisture content AND temperature | Only moisture content |
| Changes with heating at constant moisture? | **Yes**, decreases as T rises | **No**, remains constant |

**Why specific humidity is preferred in engineering:**
Specific humidity is based on a *mass* of dry air, which is invariant during simple heating/cooling processes (no moisture added or removed). RH depends on both temperature and moisture, so it changes even when the actual moisture is unchanged. For air-conditioning load calculations and process line tracing, ω is the property that stays constant during sensible heating/cooling — making it the engineer's natural choice.

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

---

## Question A (14 Marks)

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **psychrometer** (dry and wet bulb thermometer). How is the relative humidity determined from its readings?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Moist air at a DBT of 38°C and WBT of 26°C enters a cooling coil with ADP at 12°C and leaves at DBT 22°C. Calculate: (i) the bypass factor of the coil, (ii) the contact factor, and (iii) the total cooling load in kW, if 2.0 kg/s of dry air is being handled. Use the standard psychrometric chart approximations.

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Construction and Working of a Psychrometer (7 marks):**

A **psychrometer** (or sling psychrometer) consists of two identical mercury-in-glass thermometers mounted side by side on a wooden or metal frame.

- **Dry Bulb Thermometer:** The bulb is exposed directly to the ambient air. It records the **DBT**.
- **Wet Bulb Thermometer:** The bulb is wrapped with a muslin cloth (cotton wick), which is wetted with distilled water before use. A small container of water may be attached, or the wick may be manually wetted.

**Working Principle:** The instrument is either **whirled** (sling-type) or exposed to a **forced air current** (aspirated-type). As air flows over the wet bulb:
1. Water evaporates from the wick.
2. The latent heat required for evaporation is drawn from the bulb, cooling it below DBT.
3. The wet bulb temperature stabilises at $T_{wb}$ when the heat transfer rate equals the evaporative mass transfer rate.

**Determining RH from DBT and WBT:**
- The psychrometric chart is entered with the DBT on the X-axis.
- The constant-WBT line (which is also approximately a constant-enthalpy line) is followed up to its intersection with the vertical DBT line.
- From this state point, the curved **relative humidity line** is read — that gives φ.
- Alternatively, the empirical Carrier equation is used: 
$$p_v = p_{vs}(T_{wb}) - (p_{atm} - p_{vs}(T_{wb})) \cdot \frac{(T_{db} - T_{wb})}{1530}$$
and then $\phi = (p_v / p_{vs}(T_{db})) \times 100\%$.

**Block Diagram of the Sling Psychrometer:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Wooden Handle] --> B[Sling Psychrometer Frame]
    B --> C[Dry Bulb Thermometer]
    B --> D[Wet Bulb Thermometer with Muslin Wick]
    D --> E[Distilled Water Reservoir]
    F[User Whirls Instrument] --> B
    B --> G[Read DBT and WBT]
    G --> H[Compute RH from Chart or Formula]
```

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $T_1 = 38°C$ (DBT entering)
- $T_2 = 22°C$ (DBT leaving)
- $T_{ADP} = 12°C$ (Apparatus Dew Point)
- $\dot{m}_a = 2.0$ kg/s of dry air
- WBT₁ = 26°C (given in problem for chart reading)

**Step 1 — Bypass Factor:**

$$BPF = \frac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}} = \frac{22 - 12}{38 - 12} = \frac{10}{26} = 0.3846$$

**Step 2 — Contact Factor:**

$$CF = 1 - BPF = 1 - 0.3846 = 0.6154$$

**Step 3 — Cooling Load (using psychrometric chart approximations):**

From the psychrometric chart at DBT 38°C, WBT 26°C: $h_1 \approx 79.5$ kJ/kg d.a., $\omega_1 \approx 0.0165$ kg/kg d.a.

At DBT 22°C (assuming ~85% RH due to dehumidification): $h_2 \approx 47.5$ kJ/kg d.a., $\omega_2 \approx 0.0140$ kg/kg d.a.

**Total Cooling Load:**

$$Q_{total} = \dot{m}_a \cdot (h_1 - h_2) = 2.0 \cdot (79.5 - 47.5) = 2.0 \cdot 32.0 = 64.0 \text{ kW}$$

**Sensible Heat Load:**

$$Q_s = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot (T_1 - T_2) = 2.0 \cdot 1.005 \cdot (38 - 22) = 32.16 \text{ kW}$$

**Latent Heat Load:**

$$Q_l = Q_{total} - Q_s = 64.0 - 32.16 = 31.84 \text{ kW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Do NOT compute cooling load using only $1.005 \cdot \Delta T$ — that gives *sensible* load only. The **total** load requires **enthalpy difference**, since dehumidification is part of the process. Losing this distinction typically costs 3-4 marks out of 7.

---

## Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the process of **cooling and dehumidification** with the help of a psychrometric chart. Define the term **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)** and **Bypass Factor**. How does the BPF affect the performance of a cooling coil?

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat schematic layout, describe the working of a **Central Air Conditioning System** used for a multi-storey commercial building. Label all major components.

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Cooling and Dehumidification (7 marks):**

**Process Description:**
Cooling and dehumidification is the simultaneous reduction of air temperature and removal of moisture. It is performed in an AHU by passing warm humid air over a **cooling coil** whose surface temperature is **below the DPT** of the incoming air.

**Process Steps:**
1. Warm, humid room air (state 1: high DBT, high ω) enters the cooling coil section.
2. As air contacts the cold coil fins (at temperature $T_{ADP}$):
   - Air temperature drops (sensible cooling).
   - Moisture condenses on the coil surface because the coil is below DPT (latent cooling).
3. The condensate drips into the drain pan and is removed.
4. Air leaves the coil (state 2: lower DBT, lower ω, higher RH).
5. The process line on the psychrometric chart is a **straight line from state 1 to a point on the saturation curve at $T_{ADP}$**, with the slope determined by BPF.

**Key Definitions:**

- **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP):** The temperature of the cooling coil surface, equivalent to the temperature at which the *ideal* leaving air (assuming 100% contact) would be saturated. It is the intersection of the process line with the saturation curve (φ = 100%).

- **Bypass Factor (BPF):** The ratio of air that *escapes* contact with the coil to the total air passing through it. It quantifies coil inefficiency.

$$BPF = \frac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}}$$

**Effect of BPF on Coil Performance:**

| BPF | Effect |
|---|---|
| High BPF (≈ 1) | Coil ineffective — most air passes through unchanged |
| Low BPF (≈ 0) | Ideal coil — all air reaches coil temperature |
| Higher BPF | Smaller Δω (less dehumidification), higher leaving DBT |
| Lower BPF | More rows of coil, larger coil surface, more pumping power |

**Part (b) — Central Air Conditioning System Layout (7 marks):**

A central AC system conditions air centrally and distributes it to multiple zones. **Major components:**

1. **Chiller:** Refrigeration unit producing chilled water (typically 6–8°C).
2. **Chilled Water Pump:** Circulates chilled water from chiller to AHU cooling coil.
3. **Cooling Tower:** Rejects condenser heat to atmosphere.
4. **Condenser Water Pump:** Circulates water between chiller condenser and cooling tower.
5. **Air Handling Unit (AHU):** Contains filter, cooling coil, heating coil, humidifier, and fan.
6. **Supply Ductwork:** Distributes conditioned air to rooms via dampers, grilles, diffusers.
7. **Return Air Ductwork:** Returns room air back to AHU mixing chamber.
8. **Mixing Dampers:** Blend fresh outdoor air with return air (typically 10–20% fresh air).
9. **Exhaust Fan / Damper:** Vents a portion of return air outside to maintain fresh air supply.
10. **Automatic Controls:** Sensors for DBT, RH, CO₂, and actuators for dampers and valves.

**Schematic Layout (Block Diagram):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    OA[Outside Air - Fresh] --> MD[Mixing Dampers]
    RA[Return Air from Rooms] --> MD
    MD --> FLT[Filter Section]
    FLT --> CC[Cooling Coil - Chilled Water]
    CC --> HC[Heating Coil - Hot Water]
    HC --> HUM[Humidifier - Steam]
    HUM --> FAN[Supply Fan]
    FAN --> SD[Supply Duct]
    SD --> RM[Conditioned Rooms]
    RM --> RAD[Return Air Duct]
    RAD --> RA
    CC -.Drain.-> COND[Condensate Drain]
    CH[Chiller] -.Chilled Water.-> CC
    CH -.Hot Gas.-> CT[Cooling Tower]
    CT -.Cool Water.-> CH
```

**Working Sequence:**
- Chiller produces chilled water → sent to AHU cooling coil via pump.
- Outside + return air → mixed in plenum → filtered → cooled (and dehumidified) → optionally reheated → humidified (if needed) → blown by fan → supplied via ducts → enters rooms.
- Warm stale air from rooms returns to AHU through return ducts.
- A portion is exhausted to outside; the rest mixes with fresh air.
- The chiller rejects heat via the cooling tower to the atmosphere.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Students often omit either the **reheat coil** (needed to prevent overcooling after dehumidification) or the **fresh air intake** (essential for IAQ — Indoor Air Quality). Forgetting these costs 2-3 marks. Always mention the **purpose** of each component, not just its name.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ✅ **DBT (T_db):** True air temperature measured by ordinary thermometer. *Y-axis of chart = none, X-axis = DBT.*
- ✅ **WBT (T_wb):** Measured by wet-wick thermometer. $T_{wb} \leq T_{db}$. WBT line ≈ constant enthalpy line on chart.
- ✅ **DPT (T_dp):** Saturation temperature at constant ω. $T_{dp} \leq T_{wb} \leq T_{db}$. Read DPT by following constant-ω line down to φ = 100% curve.
- ✅ **Specific Humidity (ω):** $\omega = 0.622 \cdot p_v / (p_{atm} - p_v)$. **Y-axis of chart.** Constant in sensible heating/cooling.
- ✅ **Relative Humidity (φ):** $\phi = (p_v / p_{vs}(T_{db})) \times 100\%$. Curved lines on chart (100% = saturation curve).
- ✅ **Enthalpy (h):** $h = 1.005\,t + \omega(2500 + 1.88\,t)$ kJ/kg d.a. Inclined lines on chart.
- ✅ **ADP (Apparatus Dew Point):** Coil surface temperature. Where process line meets the φ = 100% curve.
- ✅ **Bypass Factor (BPF):** $BPF = (T_2 - T_{ADP}) / (T_1 - T_{ADP})$. Indicates coil inefficiency. Typical: 0.1–0.4.
- ✅ **Contact Factor (CF):** $CF = 1 - BPF$. Fraction of air in perfect contact with coil.
- ✅ **Cooling Load:** Use **enthalpy difference** for total load: $Q = \dot{m}_a \cdot (h_1 - h_2)$.
- ✅ **Sensible Load:** $Q_s = \dot{m}_a \cdot 1.005 \cdot \Delta T$ (when ω is constant).
- ✅ **Process Line Slopes:**
  - Sensible heating/cooling: **horizontal** (ω constant).
  - Cooling + dehumidification: **sloped down-left** (T ↓, ω ↓).
  - Heating + humidification: **sloped up-right** (T ↑, ω ↑).
  - Evaporative cooling: along constant WBT line.
- ✅ **Central AC Components (must draw):** Chiller, Cooling Tower, Chilled Water Pump, Condenser Water Pump, AHU (with filter, cooling coil, heating coil, humidifier, fan), Supply Duct, Return Duct, Mixing Dampers, Fresh Air Intake, Exhaust.
- ✅ **Remember the Order:** Fresh Air + Return Air → Mixing → Filter → Cooling Coil → Eliminator → Heating Coil → Humidifier → Fan → Supply Duct.
- ✅ **Number to Memorise:** 0.622 (gas constant ratio), 1.005 (Cp of dry air), 1.88 (Cp of vapour), 2500 (latent heat at 0°C), 1530 (Carrier's psychrometric constant).
- ✅ **Common Traps:**
  - RH ≠ moisture content (use ω for moisture content).
  - Cooling load is NOT $1.005 \cdot \Delta T$ alone.
  - DPT requires constant ω, not constant pressure alone.
  - At saturation, all three temperatures become equal.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark questions, always **draw the process line on the psychrometric chart**, label points 1, 2, and ADP, write the formulas used, and show all units. This visual + structured approach is what KTU examiners reward with full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sand Casting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDEST203 - BASIC MECHANICAL AND CIVIL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sand Casting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Sand Casting

## 1.1 Formal KTU Academic Definition

**Sand Casting** is a versatile, time-tested *expendable mold* metal casting process in which molten metal is poured into a single-use (disposable) cavity formed within a sand-based mold, allowed to solidify, and then the mold is broken away to retrieve the finished casting. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, it is classified under the broader domain of **foundry engineering** and is recognized as the most widely used casting process globally, accounting for over **60%** of all metal castings produced.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (GDEST203 – Module 2):**
> Sand casting is taught as a *primary manufacturing process* that bridges the gap between raw metal and finished engineering components. Students must know the mold constituents, the sequence of operations, and the role of each tool/equipment in producing dimensionally accurate castings.

The fundamental classification parameters are:
- **Mold Material:** Silica-based sand (SiO₂), bonded with clay, chemical binders, or oil.
- **Mold Reusability:** *Expendable* (single-use) — the mold is destroyed after each casting.
- **Process Family:** *Gravity-fed* casting (no external pressure is applied to force the metal).
- **Tolerance Grade (as per IS 13779):** Typically **CT 10 – CT 13** (relatively coarse compared to die casting).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Ice-Cube Tray Method"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy for First-Time Learners:**
> Imagine you have a tray in which you press a small toy soldier into wet beach sand. You carefully remove the toy, leaving a perfect soldier-shaped cavity. You then pour melted chocolate into that cavity, let it cool, and dig it out — you now have a chocolate soldier. **Sand casting works exactly this way**, except:
> - The "wet sand" is **molding sand** (sand + clay + water).
> - The "toy" is the **pattern** (a replica of the final part, slightly oversized).
> - The "chocolate" is **molten metal** (iron, aluminum, bronze, etc.).
> - The "tray" is the **flask**, which holds the sand together.
> - The "tray dividers" are the **cope** (top half) and **drag** (bottom half).

This analogy makes the entire process intuitive: **a disposable sand mold takes the shape of a pattern, receives molten metal, and is broken to release the solidified part.**

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Molding Sand Grain Size (AFS No.)** | **50 – 100** | Finer grains → smoother surface, lower permeability |
| **Green Compression Strength** | **0.5 – 0.9 kg/cm²** | Sand must hold shape under molten metal pressure |
| **Moisture Content (Green Sand)** | **4% – 8%** | Critical for clay bonding activation |
| **Pouring Temperature (Cast Iron)** | **1300 °C – 1400 °C** | ~150 °C above melting point (superheat) |
| **Shrinkage Allowance (Iron)** | **1% – 1.5%** | Pattern is oversized to compensate |
| **Draft Angle** | **1° – 3°** | Allows clean pattern withdrawal |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constituents of Molding Sand

A typical **green sand** mold is a carefully engineered mixture. Each constituent has a specific engineering function:

1. **Silica Sand (SiO₂) — 85% to 95%**
   - The bulk refractory base.
   - Must withstand molten metal temperatures (**up to 1600 °C** for steel).
   - High thermal stability and chemical inertness.
   - **Why?** Provides the structural skeleton of the mold.

2. **Clay (Bentonite) — 5% to 15%**
   - Acts as the **binder**.
   - Bentonite (a montmorillonite mineral) swells when wetted, coating sand grains and holding them together.
   - **Why?** Provides green strength (strength when moist) and dry strength (strength after baking).

3. **Water (Moisture) — 4% to 8%**
   - Activates the bonding action of clay.
   - Too little water → brittle mold; too much water → steam explosions and porosity defects.
   - **Why?** Clay needs water to develop its cohesive (bonding) properties.

4. **Additives (Coal Dust, Wood Flour, Cereals) — 1% to 5%**
   - **Coal dust:** Burns on contact with hot metal, forming a reducing gas (CO) that prevents sand-metal reaction and improves surface finish (called **peel**).
   - **Wood flour:** Increases collapsibility — the mold collapses slightly as the casting cools, preventing hot tears.
   - **Why?** Tailors sand properties to specific metal/alloy and casting complexity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Sands Used in Foundries:**
> - **Facing Sand:** A fine, high-quality sand applied directly against the pattern for a smooth casting surface.
> - **Backing Sand:** Coarse, reused sand that fills the rest of the flask for economy.
> - **Parting Sand:** Dry, unbonded sand dusted on the parting plane to prevent the cope and drag from sticking together.
> - **Core Sand:** Mixed with binders (oil, resin) to form **cores** that create internal cavities.
> - **Loam Sand:** A thick mixture of sand + clay + water, used for large castings (e.g., turbine housings) built over brick or scaffolding.

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Sand Mold — Component Diagram Logic

A complete sand mold consists of multiple precisely positioned components. Each plays a distinct engineering role:

- **Flask:** A rigid steel/wooden frame that holds the sand. It has two halves:
  - **Cope (Upper Flask):** Forms the top half of the mold.
  - **Drag (Lower Flask):** Forms the bottom half of the mold.
- **Pattern:** A wooden, metal, or plastic replica of the final casting, oversized by the *shrinkage allowance* and provided with a *draft angle*.
- **Core:** A separate sand shape placed inside the mold cavity to form hollow sections or internal features.
- **Core Prints:** Extensions on the pattern that create seats (recesses) in the mold to hold the core in position.
- **Sprue:** The vertical channel through which molten metal enters the mold.
- **Runner:** The horizontal channel distributing metal from the sprue to the gate.
- **Gate:** The final entry point into the mold cavity.
- **Riser (Feeder):** A reservoir of molten metal that feeds the casting as it shrinks during solidification — prevents **shrinkage porosity**.
- **Vents:** Thin channels that allow gases and air to escape from the mold cavity.
- **Parting Line:** The interface between the cope and drag.

## 2.3 Pattern — The Heart of Sand Casting

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Takeaway:** The pattern determines the *shape, size, and surface finish* of the casting. Mastering pattern allowances and types is essential for board exam marks.

### 2.3.1 Pattern Allowances (Critical for KTU Numerical/Descriptive Questions)

A pattern is **never** made the exact size of the desired casting. It is intentionally altered with the following allowances:

| Allowance | Definition | Typical Value (Cast Iron) |
|---|---|---|
| **Shrinkage Allowance** | Pattern is *larger* than the casting to compensate for metal contraction on cooling. | **1.0 % to 1.5 %** |
| **Machining Allowance (Finish)** | Extra material left on surfaces that will be machined later. | **1.5 mm to 6 mm** |
| **Draft (Taper) Allowance** | Taper on vertical surfaces to enable clean pattern withdrawal. | **1° to 3°** |
| **Distortion (Camber) Allowance** | Made for castings likely to warp on cooling (e.g., long flat plates). | **0.5 % to 2 %** opposite to warp direction |
| **Shake Allowance** | Negative allowance to compensate for the slight pattern enlargement from ramming. | **0.1 % to 0.3 %** |

### 2.3.2 Common Pattern Types

1. **Single Piece (Solid) Pattern** — Simplest; pattern made in one piece. Used for flat, simple shapes.
2. **Split (Two-Piece) Pattern** — Most common; pattern split at the parting line, one half in cope, the other in drag.
3. **Multi-Piece Pattern** — Used for complex shapes requiring more than one parting surface.
4. **Match Plate Pattern** — Pattern halves mounted on opposite sides of a single metal plate (used in machine molding for high-volume production).
5. **Cope and Drag Pattern** — Similar to match plate but each half is mounted on separate plates.
6. **Gated Pattern** — Multiple patterns mounted on a common runner system to pour several castings at once.
7. **Skeleton Pattern** — A wooden frame (skeleton) with a sand contour — used for very large castings.
8. **Sweep Pattern** — A wooden/metal template ("sweep") that is revolved around a fixed axis to sweep the mold shape. Used for circular shapes like pulleys, gears, and bowls.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Memorize the shrinkage formula — it appears in nearly every KTU board paper either directly or as a sub-part.

$$
\boxed{
D_{\text{pattern}} \;=\; D_{\text{casting}} \;\times\; \left(1 \;+\; \dfrac{S}{100}\right)
}
$$

Where:
- $D_{\text{pattern}}$ = Dimension on the pattern (mm)
- $D_{\text{casting}}$ = Required final dimension on the casting (mm)
- $S$ = Shrinkage allowance (%)

**Auxiliary formula for draft angle linear allowance:**

$$
L \;=\; H \,\tan(\theta)
$$

Where $L$ is the taper length on the pattern side, $H$ is the vertical height, and $\theta$ is the draft angle.

**Total allowance (combined effect on a dimension requiring both shrinkage and machining):**

$$
D_{\text{pattern}} \;=\; \left(D_{\text{casting}} + M\right) \times \left(1 + \dfrac{S}{100}\right)
$$

Where $M$ is the machining allowance in mm.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Sand casting is indispensable in industries that demand **low-to-medium production volumes** and **large, complex parts**:
- **Automotive:** Engine blocks, cylinder heads, transmission housings.
- **Agriculture:** Tractor parts, ploughshares, pump housings.
- **Railways:** Couplers, brake components, rail wheel centers.
- **Power Generation:** Turbine casings, valve bodies, pump impellers.
- **General Engineering:** Manhole covers, machine tool beds, pipe fittings.

> The choice of sand casting over die casting or investment casting is driven by **cost-effectiveness for low volumes, material flexibility (almost any metal can be cast), and the ability to produce very large parts** (weighing several tons).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Sand Casting Process (Twelve Engineering Operations)

The complete sand casting sequence — frequently asked in KTU 14-mark questions — is presented below in its exhaustive operational form:

**Step 1 — Pattern Preparation**
A pattern is selected or manufactured with all required allowances (shrinkage, machining, draft). Material selection depends on accuracy, life, and cost:
- **Wood (softwood/teak):** Low cost, used for < 100 castings.
- **Metal (aluminum/cast iron):** High accuracy, used for > 1000 castings.
- **Plastics/epoxy:** Medium-volume, excellent surface finish.
- **Plaster:** Used for prototype/lost patterns.

**Step 2 — Flask and Sand Preparation**
The drag (lower flask) is placed on a molding board. Facing sand is prepared by sieving and mulling (intensive mixing).

**Step 3 — Drag Molding**
The pattern is placed on the board (pattern face down). Drag is placed around it. Facing sand is sprinkled over the pattern, then backing sand is filled in layers. Each layer is **rammed** (compacted) using a hand or pneumatic rammer. Excess sand is struck off (leveled) using a strike-off bar (slick).

**Step 4 — Drag Inversion and Cope Placement**
The drag is rotated 180° (inverted) and placed on the floor. The parting surface is smoothed, and **parting sand** is dusted over it to prevent the cope sand from sticking.

**Step 5 — Cope Molding**
The cope is positioned on the drag, and **sprue pins** and **riser pins** are placed vertically. Cope sand is filled and rammed in the same manner as the drag.

**Step 6 — Sprue and Riser Pin Removal**
The sprue pin is withdrawn carefully, leaving a vertical channel. Riser pin is similarly removed, leaving a riser cavity.

**Step 7 — Parting and Pattern Withdrawal**
The cope is lifted off the drag using a roll-over/lift-off mechanism. The pattern remains in the drag and is withdrawn with **draw spikes** (rap the pattern gently) — the draft angle ensures clean release. Hollow cavities (for cores) are carved using a cutter.

**Step 8 — Core Making and Placement**
A core is separately prepared using a core box and baked (if dry sand core) or used as-is (if green sand core). The core is positioned in the core prints created in the drag.

**Step 9 — Mold Assembly and Clamping**
The cope is reassembled over the drag, and the flask is clamped or weighted (copes weights / clamps) to resist the **metallostatic pressure** of the poured metal.

**Step 10 — Melting and Pouring**
The metal is melted in a cupola, induction furnace, or crucible furnace. It is poured into the sprue at the correct **pouring temperature** (typically 100 °C – 200 °C above the metal's liquidus) to ensure complete filling.

**Step 11 — Solidification and Cooling**
The metal is allowed to solidify. The riser supplies liquid metal to compensate for shrinkage. Cooling time depends on section thickness (governed approximately by **Chvorinov's Rule**):

$$
t_s \;=\; B \,\left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^{\!2}
$$

Where:
- $t_s$ = solidification time (seconds)
- $B$ = mold constant (depends on metal and mold material)
- $V$ = volume of the casting section
- $A$ = surface area through which heat is lost

**Step 12 — Shakeout, Cleaning, and Inspection**
The flask is opened, sand is shaken off (shakeout), and the casting is freed. The riser and sprue are cut off (by sawing, grinding, or torch cutting). The casting is then shot-blasted, fettled, and inspected for defects.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Pattern Dimension Calculation (KTU Board Standard)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A cast iron flange is to be sand cast. The final required outer diameter is **300 mm**. The shrinkage allowance for cast iron is **1.2%**, and a machining allowance of **3 mm** is required on the diameter. Calculate the dimension to be made on the wooden pattern.

**Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify given data.**
- Required final outer diameter: $D_{\text{casting}} = 300 \text{ mm}$
- Shrinkage allowance: $S = 1.2\%$
- Machining allowance: $M = 3 \text{ mm}$ (added *before* applying shrinkage)

**Step 2 — Recall the governing formula.**

$$
D_{\text{pattern}} \;=\; \left(D_{\text{casting}} + M\right) \times \left(1 + \dfrac{S}{100}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**

$$
D_{\text{pattern}} \;=\; \left(300 + 3\right) \times \left(1 + \dfrac{1.2}{100}\right)
$$

$$
D_{\text{pattern}} \;=\; 303 \times \left(1 + 0.012\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the parenthesis.**

$$
D_{\text{pattern}} \;=\; 303 \times 1.012
$$

**Step 5 — Perform the multiplication.**

$$
D_{\text{pattern}} \;=\; 306.636 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Final result (rounded to practical precision).**

$$
\boxed{D_{\text{pattern}} \;\approx\; 306.64 \text{ mm} \;\approx\; 307 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 7 — Engineering interpretation.**
The pattern is made approximately **7 mm larger** in diameter than the final casting. This extra material compensates for two physical phenomena:
- **3 mm (machining):** Surface cleanup after casting.
- **3.636 mm (shrinkage):** Thermal contraction of cast iron during cooling from pouring temperature (~1400 °C) to room temperature.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Casting Defects vs. Causes (KTU Frequently Tested)

> This matrix is a *high-yield* revision tool — it appears in nearly every KTU 2024 board exam as a 7-mark sub-question.

| Defect | Description | Primary Cause | Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Blowholes** | Smooth, round cavities just below the surface | Entrapped gases from sand moisture / low permeability | Reduce moisture; improve venting |
| **Pinholes** | Tiny, dispersed surface pores | High hydrogen content in melt | Degassing, dry sand, controlled moisture |
| **Shrinkage Cavity** | Internal void in last-to-solidify region | Inadequate risering | Proper riser design (Chvorinov's rule) |
| **Cold Shut** | A discontinuity or seam on the surface | Two metal streams meet but don't fuse | Increase pouring temperature, improve gating |
| **Misrun** | Casting is incomplete — metal solidifies early | Low pouring temperature / poor fluidity | Higher superheat, better sprue design |
| **Hot Tear (Crack)** | Crack formed during cooling | Sand mold is too rigid; no collapsibility | Add wood flour; use softer sand |
| **Sand Inclusion** | Sand particle embedded in casting | Erosion of mold walls by metal stream | Reduce pouring velocity; better facing sand |
| **Scab** | Buckled or lifted layer of sand on casting | Excessive thermal expansion of sand | Use proper sand mix; control moisture |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sand Casting Process Flow — Functional Architecture

The Mermaid diagram below maps the **block-level functional architecture** of the sand casting process, illustrating the sequential flow of material, information, and operations from pattern creation to finished casting.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1 Pattern Design] --> B[Step 2 Pattern Making]
    B --> C[Step 3 Drag Molding]
    C --> D[Step 4 Drag Inversion]
    D --> E[Step 5 Cope Molding with Sprue and Riser Pins]
    E --> F[Step 6 Pattern Withdrawal]
    F --> G[Step 7 Core Making and Placement]
    G --> H[Step 8 Mold Assembly and Clamping]
    H --> I[Step 9 Melting and Pouring]
    I --> J[Step 10 Solidification and Cooling]
    J --> K[Step 11 Shakeout and Fettling]
    K --> L[Step 12 Inspection and Dispatch]
    L --> M[Finished Casting]
    M --> N{Defect Check}
    N -->|Accept| O[Ship to Customer]
    N -->|Reject| P[Re-melt and Recycle]
```

## 4.2 Sand Mold Component Architecture

This block-level schematic illustrates the **internal architecture of an assembled sand mold**, showing how the pattern, cores, gating system, and risers interact spatially.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph A[Cope Upper Half]
        A1[Sprue Vertical Channel]
        A2[Pouring Cup]
        A3[Vent Channels]
        A4[Upper Sand Layer]
    end
    subgraph B[Drag Lower Half]
        B1[Runner Horizontal Channel]
        B2[Gate Entry to Cavity]
        B3[Mold Cavity Shape from Pattern]
        B4[Core Print Seat for Core Support]
        B5[Lower Sand Layer]
    end
    subgraph C[Core Assembly]
        C1[Core Sand Body with Binder]
        C2[Core Print Extensions]
    end
    subgraph D[Feeding System]
        D1[Riser Reservoir]
        D2[Atmosphere]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> B4
    B3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
```

## 4.3 Pattern Allowance Architecture

The diagram below maps how a single casting dimension is mathematically transformed into the corresponding pattern dimension by sequentially applying each allowance.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Required Casting Dimension] --> B[Add Machining Allowance]
    B --> C[Apply Shrinkage Allowance 1 to 1.5 percent]
    C --> D[Add Draft Taper]
    D --> E[Compensate Shake Allowance]
    E --> F[Final Pattern Dimension]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this architecture:** Each block represents a sequential engineering decision. Skipping any step leads to dimensional inaccuracy in the final casting. This is the same logical chain examiners expect students to write out in 14-mark descriptive answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define sand casting. List any four constituents of molding sand with their functions.**

**Model Answer:**
Sand casting is a metal forming process in which molten metal is poured into a cavity formed in a sand-based *expendable mold*, allowed to solidify, and retrieved by breaking the mold.

The four principal constituents of molding sand are:

1. **Silica Sand (SiO₂):** Forms the refractory base that withstands high molten metal temperatures.
2. **Clay (Bentonite):** Acts as the binder, holding sand grains together when moistened.
3. **Water:** Activates the clay's bonding action, providing the required green strength.
4. **Additives (e.g., coal dust, wood flour):** Improve surface finish, collapsibility, and prevent sand-metal reaction.

### **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Distinguish between a *pattern* and a *core* in sand casting.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Pattern | Core |
|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Forms the *external* shape of the casting | Forms the *internal* cavity or hollow feature |
| **Location** | Placed in the mold box before sand ramming | Placed inside the mold cavity after pattern withdrawal |
| **Material** | Wood, metal, plastic, or plaster | Sand mixed with binder (oil, resin, sodium silicate) |
| **Reusability** | Reusable for many castings | Usually single-use (expendable) |
| **Shape Source** | Direct replica of the final part | Shaped using a *core box* |

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Always draw a *labeled* sand mold diagram in pattern/cope/drag questions. Marks are explicitly allotted for diagrams in KTU 2024 scheme answer scripts.
> - Do **not** confuse *shrinkage allowance* (oversize the pattern) with *machining allowance* (extra material for finishing). Examiners check this distinction.
> - In process-sequence questions, students frequently miss **mold drying, core placement, and shakeout**. Mention all twelve steps for full marks.

### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**A. (a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure of sand casting with a neat sketch. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Detailed):**

The sand casting process is a sequence of twelve engineering operations:

1. **Pattern preparation** with required allowances.
2. **Drag molding** — pattern placed on the board, drag positioned, sand filled and rammed.
3. **Drag inversion** — drag is turned over.
4. **Parting and cope molding** — parting sand is dusted, cope placed, sprue and riser pins positioned, sand filled and rammed.
5. **Pattern and pin withdrawal** — pins pulled out, pattern withdrawn using draw spikes.
6. **Core making and placement** — core is separately prepared and placed in core prints.
7. **Mold assembly** — cope placed on drag and clamped.
8. **Melting** — metal melted in cupola or induction furnace.
9. **Pouring** — molten metal poured into the sprue at the correct pouring temperature.
10. **Solidification** — metal solidifies; riser feeds shrinkage.
11. **Shakeout** — mold is broken, casting removed.
12. **Cleaning and inspection** — sprue and riser cut off, casting fettled and inspected.

> *A neat labeled diagram of a sand mold showing cope, drag, sprue, runner, gate, riser, core, pattern cavity, and parting line should be drawn. (2 marks for diagram, 5 marks for explanation.)*

**A. (b)** List and explain the various allowances provided on a pattern. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The five major pattern allowances are:

1. **Shrinkage Allowance** — Pattern is oversized to compensate for metal contraction during solidification and cooling. For cast iron ≈ 1.0–1.5%, for aluminum ≈ 1.5–2.0%, for steel ≈ 1.5–2.0%.

2. **Machining Allowance (Finish Allowance)** — Extra material left on surfaces that will be machined to achieve final dimensions and finish. Typically 1.5 to 6 mm.

3. **Draft (Taper) Allowance** — Slight taper on all vertical surfaces of the pattern (1° to 3°) so that the pattern can be withdrawn from the sand without damaging the mold cavity.

4. **Distortion (Camber) Allowance** — Provided in castings prone to warping (e.g., long flat castings) by making the pattern curved in the *opposite* direction to the expected warp.

5. **Shake Allowance** — A small negative allowance (~0.1 to 0.3%) to compensate for the slight enlargement of the mold cavity caused by ramming (sand expands slightly when compacted).

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**B. (a)** Describe the various types of patterns used in sand casting. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The major pattern types are:

1. **Single Piece (Solid) Pattern** — Made in one piece. Used for simple, flat castings.
2. **Two-Piece (Split) Pattern** — Most common; split at the parting line, one half in cope, one in drag.
3. **Multi-Piece Pattern** — For complex castings requiring multiple parting surfaces.
4. **Match Plate Pattern** — Both halves mounted on a metal plate; used in machine molding.
5. **Cope and Drag Pattern** — Similar to match plate, but on separate plates.
6. **Gated (Follow Board) Pattern** — Multiple patterns joined by a common runner for mass production.
7. **Skeleton Pattern** — A wooden frame; sand contour built over it. Used for very large castings.
8. **Sweep Pattern** — A template revolved to form the mold. Used for circular shapes.

**B. (b)** What are the common casting defects? Explain any four with causes and remedies. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*(Refer to Section 3.3 Analytical Matrix for the full table.)* Four major defects:

1. **Blowholes** — Gas pockets just below the surface. Cause: low permeability / high moisture. Remedy: lower moisture content, add vents.
2. **Shrinkage Cavity** — Internal void. Cause: inadequate riser. Remedy: proper riser sizing using Chvorinov's rule.
3. **Cold Shut** — Discontinuity on the surface. Cause: low pouring temperature. Remedy: increase superheat, redesign gating.
4. **Hot Tear** — Crack formed during cooling. Cause: mold too rigid. Remedy: increase collapsibility with wood flour.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rapid-Revision Checklist (Sand Casting):**

- ✅ **Mold Type:** Expendable, gravity-fed, sand-based.
- ✅ **Most Common Sand:** Green sand (sand + clay + water + additives).
- ✅ **Shrinkage Order:** Cast iron (1–1.5%) < Aluminum (1.5–2%) < Steel (1.5–2%) < Bronze/Brass (1.5–2.5%).
- ✅ **Pattern Allowances to Memorize:** Shrinkage, Machining, Draft, Distortion, Shake — **5 total**.
- ✅ **Pattern Formula:** $D_p = (D_c + M)(1 + S/100)$.
- ✅ **Cope = Top half**, **Drag = Bottom half** (Frequent MCQ trap).
- ✅ **Riser** supplies molten metal to compensate for *solidification shrinkage* (NOT pattern shrinkage).
- ✅ **Core** forms *internal* cavities; placed *after* pattern withdrawal; held in *core prints*.
- ✅ **Draft angle 1°–3°** is mandatory on vertical pattern surfaces.
- ✅ **Pouring temperature** is 100–200 °C above the metal's melting point.
- ✅ **Chvorinov's Rule** governs solidification time: $t_s = B(V/A)^2$.
- ✅ **Sand Casting Share:** ~60% of all metal castings produced globally.
- ✅ **Tolerance Grade (IS 13779):** CT 10 – CT 13 (coarse).
- ✅ **Common Defects:** Blowholes, Pinholes, Shrinkage cavity, Cold shut, Misrun, Hot tear, Sand inclusion, Scab.

> **Final Exam Mantra:** "If you can draw the *labeled sand mold sketch* and list the *12 process steps* with the *5 pattern allowances* and the *8 common defects*, you have secured at least **10 out of 14 marks** in any sand casting 14-mark KTU question."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDEST203 - BASIC MECHANICAL AND CIVIL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Forging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Forging** is a primary metal forming manufacturing process in which compressive forces are applied to a solid metallic billet, ingot, or preform (workpiece) to plastically deform it into a desired shape, geometry, and internal grain structure. The deformation is achieved using localized compressive stresses generated between two dies (tooling surfaces), hammers, or presses, without allowing the material to melt.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 2):**
> *Forging is a metal working process in which the metal is heated and shaped by applying compressive forces using hammers, presses, or specialized dies. The process refines the grain structure, improves mechanical strength, and produces components with superior toughness and fatigue resistance.*

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, forging is studied as one of the fundamental **bulk deformation processes** alongside rolling, extrusion, drawing, and sheet metal operations. It occupies a critical position in manufacturing engineering because forged components are used in safety-critical applications such as **automotive crankshafts, connecting rods, turbine shafts, aerospace landing gear, railway axles, and structural fasteners**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Conceptual Distinction:**
> - In *casting*, the metal is in a **liquid state**.
> - In *machining*, the metal is in a **solid state** but **material is removed**.
> - In *forging*, the metal is in a **solid (plastic) state** and **material is reshaped** without removal.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you have a **lump of clay**. You cannot cut a perfect shape out of it easily, but if you **press it between your two palms**, or **pound it on a table with your fist**, the clay flows and takes the shape of whatever is pressing it. That is forging in its simplest form.

Now, replace:
- The **clay** → a heated steel billet (typically heated to **0.5 to 0.75 of its recrystallization temperature**, making it soft and ductile).
- Your **palms/fist** → industrial hammers or forging dies made of hardened tool steel.
- The **squeezing action** → the compressive forging force, often ranging from a few **tonnes to several thousand tonnes**.

The heat makes the metal "obedient" — it flows plastically under pressure rather than cracking. As the metal cools, it **"remembers"** its new shape, and the internal grain structure becomes aligned along the contour of the finished part, giving forged components exceptional strength.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy Summary:**
> Think of forging like **making flat bread (chapati/roti)** — you start with a ball of dough, press it, stretch it, and shape it under compressive force of your palms or a rolling pin. The dough never melts, but it flows into shape. Forging follows the same physics.

---

## 1.3 Classification Snapshot

Forging processes are classified by the KTU syllabus into two broad categories:

| Classification | Description | Common Examples |
|----------------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Hot Forging** | Performed above recrystallization temperature (typically **950 °C – 1250 °C** for steel) | Hammer forging, Press forging, Drop forging |
| **Cold Forging** | Performed at room temperature (below **recrystallization temperature**); requires higher force but yields better surface finish | Cold heading, Cold extrusion-forging, Coining |
| **Warm Forging** | Performed between room temperature and recrystallization (typically **650 °C – 850 °C** for steel) | Warm heading of bolts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Hot Forging?**
> When metal is heated above its recrystallization temperature, new strain-free grains continuously form during deformation. This prevents strain hardening, lowers the yield strength, and allows the metal to flow easily under lower forces. **Above the recrystallization temperature → the metal behaves almost like plasticine.**

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Forging Reference Values (for Carbon Steel):**
> - **Hot forging temperature range:** 900 °C to 1250 °C
> - **Recrystallization temperature (steel):** ≈ 0.4 × Melting Point (in Kelvin) = ~450 °C
> - **Forging force (open die):** 5 – 50 MPa compressive stress
> - **Forging force (closed die):** 200 – 1000 MPa
> - **Typical forging strain (ε):** 0.3 – 2.0
> - **Furnace atmospheres used:** Neutral or slightly oxidizing

---

## 1.5 Historical & Engineering Context

Forging is one of the **oldest manufacturing processes** in human history — dating back to **3500 BCE** when blacksmiths shaped iron and bronze using hand hammers and anvils. The legendary **Damascus steel swords** were forged products whose layered grain structure gave them legendary sharpness and flexibility.

In modern industry, forging is the **backbone of the heavy engineering sector**:
- **Aerospace:** Jet engine discs, turbine blades
- **Automotive:** Crankshafts, connecting rods, gears, knuckles
- **Power generation:** Rotor shafts for turbines
- **Defence:** Gun barrels, armor plates, missile casings
- **Oil & Gas:** Valve bodies, flanges, drilling components
- **Railways:** Axles, wheels, couplings

> [!TIP]
> **Fun Fact (For Examiners):**
> A forged crankshaft in a Formula 1 car is roughly **30 % stronger** than a machined-from-bar-stock equivalent — because forging aligns the grain flow with the principal stress directions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Working Principle of Forging

The science behind forging is governed by the **plastic deformation** of metals. When a compressive load exceeds the **yield strength (σy)** of the material, it deforms plastically (permanently) without fracturing. The basic sequence of events is:

1. **Heating** the workpiece to the plastic working temperature.
2. **Positioning** the workpiece between the die surfaces.
3. **Application** of compressive force (via hammer impact or press stroke).
4. **Plastic flow** of material into the die cavity.
5. **Cooling** and ejection of the forged component.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plastic Deformation Trigger Condition:**
> For forging to occur, the applied stress **σ ≥ σy** (yield stress of the material at the working temperature). For hot forging, σy is significantly lower than at room temperature, hence lower force suffices.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Theory of the Forging Process

The forging process follows these structured logical steps:

### **Step 1: Workpiece Preparation**
- The starting material is a **billet, ingot, or bar stock** with known volume and cross-section.
- Surface scale, cracks, and impurities are removed by **descaling** (water jets, brushing, or acid pickling).
- The material is heated uniformly in a **gas furnace, oil furnace, or induction heater**.

### **Step 2: Pre-forming (Drawing Out / Upsetting)**
- **Drawing out (Cogging):** The cross-section is reduced by lengthwise hammering to increase length.
- **Upsetting:** The length is reduced to increase cross-sectional area — useful before closed-die forging to distribute material.

### **Step 3: Die Forging**
- The preform is placed in a die cavity.
- The upper die (ram) descends, applying the forging force.
- Material flows **laterally (sideways)** into the die cavity to fill the shape.

### **Step 4: Flash Formation (in Closed Die Forging)**
- Excess material squeezes out as a thin **flash** along the parting line.
- The flash acts as a **pressure intensifier** — its restricted flow increases cavity pressure, ensuring complete filling.

### **Step 5: Trimming**
- The flash is removed in a separate **trimming press operation**.

### **Step 6: Heat Treatment & Finishing**
- Normalizing, quenching, or annealing may be performed.
- Final machining, grinding, or surface finishing is carried out as required.

---

## 2.3 Types of Forging (KTU High-Yield Classification)

### **A. Hammer Forging**
- Uses **impact energy** of a falling hammer or ram.
- Hammers can be: **Board hammers, Drop hammers, Steam hammers, Air hammers**.
- **Forging energy** is supplied by gravity (drop hammer) or by steam/air pressure.
- **Typical strain rate:** 10 – 100 s⁻¹ (high strain rate).

### **B. Press Forging**
- Uses a **slow continuous compressive stroke** from a hydraulic or mechanical press.
- **Typical strain rate:** 0.05 – 0.5 s⁻¹ (low strain rate).
- Produces more uniform deformation than hammer forging.
- Suitable for **large components** like turbine rotors.

### **C. Open-Die Forging (Smith Forging)**
- The workpiece is deformed between **flat or simple shaped dies**.
- No enclosing cavity; the metal is free to flow laterally.
- Used for **large shafts, rings, discs, and cylinders**.
- Operator skill is critical.
- Tolerances are loose (±3 mm to ±6 mm).

### **D. Closed-Die Forging (Impression-Die Forging)**
- The workpiece is enclosed within a **die cavity** that defines the final shape.
- Produces **near-net-shape** parts with high accuracy.
- Requires precise control of **billet volume, flash design, and draft angles**.
- Tolerances are tight (±0.2 mm to ±0.8 mm).

### **E. Impression-Die Forging Variants**
- **Conventional closed die** — flash-based.
- **Flashless forging** — uses a die with a stepped punch to avoid flash (very precise billet volume).
- **Split-die forging** — die splits open to release the forging.

### **F. Upset Forging**
- The length of the billet is compressed to **increase its cross-section**.
- Used to produce heads on bolts, fasteners, and rail axles.

### **G. Swaging (Rotary Swaging)**
- A workpiece is fed axially between **reciprocating dies** that hammer radially.
- Used to **reduce the cross-section** of bars and tubes.

### **H. Roll Forging**
- Performed in a **rolling mill** with contoured rolls.
- Used to produce tapered shafts, axles, and leaf springs.

### **I. Ring Rolling**
- A thick ring is rolled between a **main roll and a mandrel**.
- Used to produce large seamless rings for bearings and gears.

### **J. Cold Forging**
- Performed at room temperature.
- High precision, excellent surface finish, increased strength due to **strain hardening**.
- Used for **nuts, bolts, screws, automotive fasteners**.

### **K. Hot Forging (Standard Process)**
- Performed at elevated temperatures for most ferrous metals.
- Lower force, better material flow, less cracking.

### **L. Warm Forging**
- Intermediate temperature range; combines benefits of hot and cold forging.

### **M. Isothermal Forging**
- Dies are heated to the same temperature as the workpiece.
- Used for difficult-to-form materials like **titanium and superalloys**.

---

## 2.4 Forging Operations (KTU Syllabus Specific)

The KTU module lists the following standard forging operations:

| S.No. | Operation | Description | Typical Use |
|-------|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Drawing Out (Cogging)** | Reducing cross-section, increasing length | Shafts, bars |
| 2 | **Upsetting** | Increasing cross-section, reducing length | Bolt heads, gear blanks |
| 3 | **Bending** | Shaping the workpiece along an axis | Hooks, links, brackets |
| 4 | **Punching** | Producing a hole through the workpiece | Eyebolts, links |
| 5 | **Piercing** | Similar to punching, removes a slug | Hollow cylinders |
| 6 | **Swaging** | Reducing cross-section at one end | Tapered shafts, needle tips |
| 7 | **Heading** | Forming a head on a wire or rod | Bolts, nails, rivets |
| 8 | **Coining** | Precision forging with closed dies for fine detail | Coins, medals, jewelry |
| 9 | **Extrusion (Forging-type)** | Forcing material through a die orifice | Tubes, complex profiles |
| 10 | **Cut-off (Setting Down)** | Local reduction to allow blanking | Blanks for next operation |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are the highest-yield for KTU Board Examinations.**

### **A. Forging Force Equations**

| S.No. | Formula | Description | Units |
|-------|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Compressive forging stress | Pa (N/m²) |
| 2 | $F = \sigma_y \cdot A$ | Maximum forging force (ideal case) | N |
| 3 | $F = K_f \cdot \sigma_y \cdot A$ | Real forging force (with form factor) | N |
| 4 | $K_f = 1 + 0.4 \cdot \mu \cdot \dfrac{d}{h}$ | Forging form factor (Lubrication factor) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $A = w \cdot L$ | Projected area in plane-strain forging | m² |
| 6 | $V_{before} = V_{after}$ | **Volume constancy** (key principle) | m³ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:**
> - $\sigma_y$ = Yield strength of material at forging temperature
> - $K_f$ = Form factor (depends on friction $\mu$, die width $d$, and workpiece height $h$)
> - $\mu$ = Coefficient of friction at die-workpiece interface
> - $A$ = Projected area of contact

### **B. Strain and Energy Equations**

| S.No. | Formula | Description |
|-------|---------|-------------|
| 7 | $\varepsilon = \ln \left( \dfrac{h_0}{h_f} \right)$ | True strain in compression (forging) |
| 8 | $E_{forging} = F_{avg} \cdot \Delta h$ | Forging energy (work done) |
| 9 | $P_{hammer} = E_{blow} \times n$ | Hammer power = energy per blow × blows per second |
| 10 | $F \cdot h = 0$ (approx) | First law for plastic deformation — kinetic energy converts to plastic work |

### **C. Closed Die Forcing Parameters**

| S.No. | Formula | Description |
|-------|---------|-------------|
| 11 | $V_{billet} = V_{cavity} + V_{flash}$ | Billet volume distribution |
| 12 | $A_{flash} = w \cdot t$ | Flash cross-section (width × thickness) |
| 13 | $t_{flash} \approx 0.05 \sqrt{A_{plan}}$ | Empirical flash thickness rule |

### **D. Die Wear & Lubrication**

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|-----------|---------------|
| Die material (hot forging) | H11 / H13 tool steel (die steel) |
| Die hardness | 45 – 55 HRC |
| Lubricant (hot forging) | Graphite-based, water-soluble oils |
| Lubricant (cold forging) | Phosphate + soap coating |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why Forging is Indispensable in Modern Industry:**

1. **Superior Mechanical Properties:** Forging refines the **grain flow** to follow the contour of the part. This gives higher **fatigue strength** and **impact resistance** than cast or machined parts.
2. **Structural Integrity:** No internal voids or porosity (unlike casting). Hence preferred for **safety-critical components**.
3. **Material Economy:** Very little scrap — typically only the **flash** is removed.
4. **Versatility:** Suitable for a wide range of sizes — from **grams (jewelry)** to **hundreds of tonnes (ship propeller shafts)**.
5. **High Production Rate:** Especially with mechanical presses and automatic hammers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation of Forging:**
> - **High initial die cost** (especially for closed-die forging).
> - **Limited to ductile metals** — brittle materials cannot be forged.
> - **Part geometry restrictions** — deep recesses, sharp internal corners are difficult.

---

## 2.7 Comparison Table: Forging vs Other Manufacturing Processes

| Property | Forging | Casting | Machining |
|----------|---------|---------|-----------|
| Starting state | Solid | Liquid | Solid |
| Material wastage | Low (flash only) | Nil (but risers) | High (chips) |
| Grain structure | Continuous, directional | Random | Cut from raw stock |
| Strength | **Highest** | Moderate | Lower (grain cut) |
| Dimensional accuracy | Good (closed die) | Moderate | Excellent |
| Surface finish | Moderate | Poor | Excellent |
| Tooling cost | High | Moderate | Low (for simple parts) |
| Production rate | High | High | Slow for complex parts |

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Forging Force in Plane-Strain Compression

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:**
> A cylindrical billet of initial height $h_0$ and diameter $d_0$ is compressed between two flat dies. The coefficient of friction at the die-workpiece interface is $\mu$. The yield strength of the material at the forging temperature is $\sigma_y$. Derive the forging force required for a given reduction in height.

### **Given:**
- Initial height: $h_0$
- Final height after forging: $h_f$
- Initial diameter: $d_0$
- Yield stress: $\sigma_y$
- Coefficient of friction: $\mu$

### **Find:**
- The forging force $F$ as a function of $h_f$.

### **Derivation:**

**Step 1: Apply Volume Constancy**
Since forging is plastic deformation (material does not change density), the volume remains constant:

$$V_{before} = V_{after}$$

For a cylinder:
$$A_0 \cdot h_0 = A_f \cdot h_f$$

Therefore, the final area:
$$A_f = \dfrac{A_0 \cdot h_0}{h_f}$$

For a cylinder with initial diameter $d_0$:
$$A_0 = \dfrac{\pi d_0^2}{4}$$

Hence:
$$A_f = \dfrac{\pi d_0^2}{4} \cdot \dfrac{h_0}{h_f}$$

**Step 2: Apply Force Equilibrium with Friction**

When the material is compressed, it tends to flow **laterally** (sideways). However, friction at the die-workpiece interface **restrains** this flow. This creates a non-uniform pressure distribution across the workpiece.

Using **Slab Analysis (elementary plasticity theory)**, consider a thin slab at distance $z$ from the centerline. The differential equation governing the pressure is:

$$\dfrac{d\sigma}{dz} = \dfrac{2 \mu \sigma}{h}$$

Wait — this is for plane strain. For the **axisymmetric (cylindrical) case**, the friction shear acts on the lateral surface area. The simplified form-factor approach is preferred for KTU-level derivations:

**Step 3: Use the Form Factor (Geometric Constraint) Approximation**

The forging force is:
$$F = \sigma_y \cdot A_f \cdot K_f$$

where $K_f$ is the **form factor** accounting for friction:
$$K_f = 1 + \dfrac{\mu \cdot d_0}{3 \cdot h_f}$$

(The factor $1/3$ comes from the average friction contribution over the cylinder's lateral surface.)

**Step 4: Substitute $A_f$**

$$F = \sigma_y \cdot \left( \dfrac{\pi d_0^2}{4} \cdot \dfrac{h_0}{h_f} \right) \cdot \left( 1 + \dfrac{\mu \cdot d_0}{3 \cdot h_f} \right)$$

Expanding:

$$\boxed{F = \dfrac{\pi d_0^2 \sigma_y h_0}{4 h_f} \left( 1 + \dfrac{\mu d_0}{3 h_f} \right)}$$

**Step 5: Physical Interpretation**

- As $h_f \to h_0$ (no deformation), $F \to \sigma_y \cdot A_0$ — the bare yield force.
- As $h_f$ decreases, the **second term grows** → friction requires additional force.
- A high $\mu$ (no lubrication) drastically increases forging force.
- This is why **graphite lubrication** is critical in hot forging.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: True Strain in Forging

**Given:** Initial height $h_0$, Final height $h_f$ (after compression).

**Step 1: Definition of True Strain**

True strain in compression:
$$\varepsilon = \int_{h_0}^{h_f} \dfrac{dh}{h}$$

**Step 2: Integrate**

$$\varepsilon = \left[ \ln h \right]_{h_0}^{h_f} = \ln h_f - \ln h_0$$

**Step 3: Final Form**

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = \ln \left( \dfrac{h_f}{h_0} \right)}$$

**Note:** Since $h_f < h_0$ in compression, the value is **negative**. By convention, the magnitude is used:

$$\varepsilon_{mag} = \ln \left( \dfrac{h_0}{h_f} \right)$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Forging Energy (Work Done)

**Step 1: Work done by the forging force over a small displacement $dh$**

$$dW = F \cdot \vert dh \vert$$

**Step 2: Substitute $F$ from above**

$$dW = \sigma_y \cdot A \cdot K_f \cdot \vert dh \vert$$

**Step 3: Use Volume Constancy $A \cdot h = V$ (constant)**

$$A = \dfrac{V}{h}$$

$$dW = \sigma_y \cdot \dfrac{V}{h} \cdot K_f \cdot dh$$

**Step 4: Integrate from $h_0$ to $h_f$ (taking the negative sign to make work positive)**

$$W = \sigma_y \cdot V \cdot K_f \cdot \int_{h_f}^{h_0} \dfrac{dh}{h}$$

$$W = \sigma_y \cdot V \cdot K_f \cdot \ln \left( \dfrac{h_0}{h_f} \right)$$

**Step 5: Substitute $V = A_0 h_0$**

$$\boxed{W = \sigma_y \cdot A_0 \cdot h_0 \cdot K_f \cdot \ln \left( \dfrac{h_0}{h_f} \right)}$$

**Step 6: For the case with no friction ($K_f = 1$):**

$$W_{ideal} = \sigma_y \cdot V \cdot \ln \left( \dfrac{h_0}{h_f} \right) = \sigma_y \cdot V \cdot \varepsilon$$

This shows the **linear relation between forging energy and true strain**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example: Forging Force Calculation**

**Problem:**
A cylindrical steel billet of initial diameter $d_0 = 50$ mm and initial height $h_0 = 100$ mm is forged in an open-die forging operation at a temperature where the yield strength $\sigma_y = 75$ MPa. The coefficient of friction at the die-workpiece interface is $\mu = 0.3$. The final height after forging is $h_f = 60$ mm. Calculate:
1. The final diameter $d_f$
2. The forging force required
3. The true strain
4. The forging energy

### **Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $d_0 = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m
- $h_0 = 100$ mm $= 0.1$ m
- $h_f = 60$ mm $= 0.06$ m
- $\sigma_y = 75 \text{ MPa} = 75 \times 10^6$ N/m²
- $\mu = 0.3$

**Step 1: Final Diameter using Volume Constancy**

$$\dfrac{\pi d_0^2}{4} \cdot h_0 = \dfrac{\pi d_f^2}{4} \cdot h_f$$

$$d_0^2 \cdot h_0 = d_f^2 \cdot h_f$$

$$d_f^2 = \dfrac{d_0^2 \cdot h_0}{h_f}$$

$$d_f^2 = \dfrac{(50)^2 \cdot 100}{60} = \dfrac{2500 \cdot 100}{60} = \dfrac{250000}{60} = 4166.67 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$d_f = \sqrt{4166.67} = 64.55 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{d_f \approx 64.55 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 2: Forging Force**

First, compute the final projected area:
$$A_f = \dfrac{\pi d_0^2}{4} \cdot \dfrac{h_0}{h_f} = \dfrac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4} \cdot \dfrac{0.1}{0.06}$$

$$A_f = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 0.0025}{4} \cdot 1.6667 = 0.001963 \cdot 1.6667 = 0.003273 \text{ m}^2$$

Form factor:
$$K_f = 1 + \dfrac{\mu d_0}{3 h_f} = 1 + \dfrac{0.3 \cdot 0.05}{3 \cdot 0.06} = 1 + \dfrac{0.015}{0.18} = 1 + 0.0833$$

$$K_f = 1.0833$$

Forging force:
$$F = \sigma_y \cdot A_f \cdot K_f$$

$$F = 75 \times 10^6 \cdot 0.003273 \cdot 1.0833$$

$$F = 75 \times 10^6 \cdot 0.003546$$

$$\boxed{F \approx 265{,}945 \text{ N} \approx 266 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 3: True Strain**

$$\varepsilon = \ln \left( \dfrac{h_0}{h_f} \right) = \ln \left( \dfrac{100}{60} \right) = \ln(1.6667)$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon \approx 0.5108}$$

**Step 4: Forging Energy**

First, compute the volume:
$$V = A_0 \cdot h_0 = \dfrac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4} \cdot 0.1 = 0.001963 \cdot 0.1 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

Energy:
$$W = \sigma_y \cdot V \cdot K_f \cdot \ln \left( \dfrac{h_0}{h_f} \right)$$

$$W = 75 \times 10^6 \cdot 1.9635 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 1.0833 \cdot 0.5108$$

$$W = 75 \times 10^6 \cdot 1.0876 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{W \approx 8157 \text{ J} \approx 8.16 \text{ kJ}}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Forging Defects

| S.No. | Defect | Cause | Prevention |
|-------|--------|-------|------------|
| 1 | **Cold shut** | Material flows meet but don't fuse | Improve die design, increase temperature |
| 2 | **Cracking** | Excessive strain, low temperature | Increase temperature, gradual reduction |
| 3 | **Laps** | Material folds back on itself | Proper die fillet radius |
| 4 | **Incomplete filling** | Insufficient material, low pressure | Proper billet volume, higher force |
| 5 | **Flash too thick** | Excessive material in cavity | Trim excess before forging |
| 6 | **Scale pits** | Surface oxide scale embeds in metal | Descaling before forging |
| 7 | **Burst** | Internal cracking from tensile stresses | Use proper draft angles, slower deformation |
| 8 | **Die wear** | High temperature, abrasion | Die lubrication, preheating |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are direct conceptual questions typical of KTU Module 2 Part A tests. Each answer is 3–4 sentences long, precisely tuned for a **3-mark valuation** (definition + key point + example).

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define forging. Differentiate between open-die forging and closed-die forging.**

**Model Answer:**

Forging is a primary metal forming process in which a solid metallic workpiece is plastically deformed under compressive forces into a desired shape, generally at elevated temperatures.

| Feature | Open-Die Forging | Closed-Die Forging |
|---------|------------------|---------------------|
| Die shape | Flat or simple | Complex cavity |
| Material flow | Free lateral flow | Confined in cavity |
| Tolerance | Loose (3 to 6 mm) | Tight (0.2 to 0.8 mm) |
| Production rate | Low | High |
| Typical use | Large shafts, rings | Automotive, aerospace components |

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**What is hot forging? State any four advantages of hot forging over cold forging.**

**Model Answer:**

Hot forging is a forging process carried out at temperatures above the recrystallization temperature of the metal (typically 0.5 to 0.75 of its melting point, in Kelvin).

**Advantages of hot forging:**
1. **Lower force required** — yield strength decreases with temperature.
2. **Improved ductility** — metal flows easily without cracking.
3. **Larger deformations possible** — high strain values without fracture.
4. **Refined grain structure** — recrystallization eliminates strain hardening.
5. **Reduced residual stresses** in the finished component.

---

## 5.2 KTU Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, every Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice (OR). Each question typically has two parts: **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer EITHER the full Question A OR the full Question B.

---

### **Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the classification of forging processes with neat sketches. List any four forging operations with their applications.**

**Model Answer:**

Forging processes can be classified based on **temperature, equipment, die type, and workpiece geometry**.

**1. Based on Temperature:**
- **Hot forging:** Performed above recrystallization temperature (950 °C – 1250 °C for steel). Produces refined grain, requires lower force.
- **Cold forging:** Performed at room temperature. Produces high precision and surface finish.
- **Warm forging:** Performed at intermediate temperature (650 °C – 850 °C).

**2. Based on Equipment:**
- **Hammer forging:** Uses impact energy (drop, steam, air hammers).
- **Press forging:** Uses continuous stroke (hydraulic, mechanical presses).

**3. Based on Die Type:**
- **Open-die forging (Smith forging):** Flat dies; material flows freely.
- **Closed-die (Impression-die) forging:** Enclosed cavity; near-net shape.

**Four Forging Operations:**

| S.No. | Operation | Description | Application |
|-------|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Drawing out (Cogging)** | Cross-section reduced, length increased | Long shafts, axles |
| 2 | **Upsetting** | Cross-section increased, length reduced | Bolt heads, gear blanks |
| 3 | **Bending** | Workpiece bent along an axis | Hooks, links, levers |
| 4 | **Punching** | Hole punched through the workpiece | Eyebolts, links, chain links |
| 5 | **Swaging** | Radial hammering reduces section | Tapered shafts, needle points |

**(b) [7 Marks] A steel billet of 80 mm diameter and 150 mm height is hot forged in an open-die press. The final height is 75 mm. The yield strength of steel at forging temperature is 90 MPa and the coefficient of friction is 0.25. Calculate: (i) Final diameter, (ii) Forging force, (iii) True strain, (iv) Forging energy.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $d_0 = 80$ mm $= 0.08$ m
- $h_0 = 150$ mm $= 0.15$ m
- $h_f = 75$ mm $= 0.075$ m
- $\sigma_y = 90 \text{ MPa} = 90 \times 10^6$ N/m²
- $\mu = 0.25$

**(i) Final Diameter:**

By volume constancy:
$$d_0^2 \cdot h_0 = d_f^2 \cdot h_f$$
$$d_f^2 = \dfrac{d_0^2 \cdot h_0}{h_f} = \dfrac{(80)^2 \cdot 150}{75} = \dfrac{6400 \cdot 150}{75} = 12800 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$\boxed{d_f = \sqrt{12800} \approx 113.14 \text{ mm}}$$

**(ii) Forging Force:**

$$A_f = \dfrac{\pi d_0^2}{4} \cdot \dfrac{h_0}{h_f} = \dfrac{\pi (0.08)^2}{4} \cdot \dfrac{0.15}{0.075} = 0.005027 \cdot 2 = 0.01005 \text{ m}^2$$

$$K_f = 1 + \dfrac{\mu d_0}{3 h_f} = 1 + \dfrac{0.25 \cdot 0.08}{3 \cdot 0.075} = 1 + \dfrac{0.02}{0.225} = 1 + 0.0889 = 1.0889$$

$$F = \sigma_y \cdot A_f \cdot K_f = 90 \times 10^6 \cdot 0.01005 \cdot 1.0889$$

$$\boxed{F \approx 985{,}000 \text{ N} \approx 985 \text{ kN}}$$

**(iii) True Strain:**

$$\varepsilon = \ln \left( \dfrac{h_0}{h_f} \right) = \ln \left( \dfrac{150}{75} \right) = \ln(2)$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon \approx 0.6931}$$

**(iv) Forging Energy:**

$$V = \dfrac{\pi d_0^2}{4} \cdot h_0 = 0.005027 \cdot 0.15 = 7.54 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

$$W = \sigma_y \cdot V \cdot K_f \cdot \ln \left( \dfrac{h_0}{h_f} \right)$$

$$W = 90 \times 10^6 \cdot 7.54 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 1.0889 \cdot 0.6931$$

$$\boxed{W \approx 51{,}200 \text{ J} \approx 51.2 \text{ kJ}}$$

---

### **Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Describe the working principle of a drop hammer forging machine. List its advantages and limitations.**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle of a Drop Hammer:**

A drop hammer is a forging machine in which a heavy ram is **lifted to a fixed height and then released** to fall under gravity onto the workpiece placed on the lower die (anvil). The kinetic energy of the falling ram is transferred to the workpiece as **impact energy**, causing plastic deformation.

**Components:**
1. **Ram (Hammer head):** Heavy block of steel, typically 500 kg to 25 tonnes.
2. **Lift mechanism:** Wedge lift, belt lift, or chain lift.
3. **Anvil (Lower die bed):** Supports the lower die and absorbs recoil.
4. **Die holder:** Holds upper and lower dies in alignment.
5. **Frame:** Rigid steel structure supporting the entire assembly.

**Working Sequence:**
1. The heated workpiece is placed on the lower die.
2. The operator positions it manually or with a manipulator.
3. The ram is lifted to a preset height by the lift mechanism.
4. The ram is released, falls under gravity, and strikes the workpiece.
5. The impact energy is sufficient to cause plastic flow.
6. The ram is lifted, the workpiece is repositioned, and the cycle repeats.

**Forging Energy:**
$$E_{blow} = m \cdot g \cdot h$$

where $m$ = mass of ram, $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $h$ = drop height.

**Advantages:**
1. **Simple design** and low initial cost.
2. **High impact energy** for large deformations.
3. Suitable for **small batch production**.
4. Flexible — can perform a **variety of operations** (drawing, upsetting, bending, punching).
5. Easy to **change dies** for different products.

**Limitations:**
1. **Low production rate** — manual operation between blows.
2. **Operator skill dependent** — quality varies.
3. **Limited die control** — flash management is difficult.
4. **High noise and vibration.**
5. **Not suitable for deep cavities** or complex shapes.

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain the concept of flash in closed-die forging. Why is the flash necessary? How does it act as a pressure intensifier?**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept of Flash:**

In closed-die forging, the die cavity is divided by a **parting line** into an upper and lower die. A thin groove is provided along the parting line called the **flash gutter** or **flash land**. When the upper die descends and the metal fills the cavity, the **excess material** is forced into this groove, forming a thin lip of metal called the **flash**.

**Flash Geometry:**
- **Flash thickness (t):** Typically 1 – 3 mm.
- **Flash width (w):** Typically 5 – 15 mm.
- **Flash volume:** About 20 – 45 % of total billet volume.

**Why is the Flash Necessary?**

1. **Accommodates volume variation:** Real billets have ±2 to 5 % volume variation due to cutting tolerances and scale. The flash absorbs this excess.
2. **Controls cavity pressure:** Acts as a resistance to material flow, ensuring the cavity is fully filled.
3. **Acts as a pressure intensifier:** The thin flash gap is harder to push material through, so material in the cavity is pressurized to very high levels.
4. **Prevents incomplete filling:** Without flash, cavities at the extremes of the die may not fill.

**How does the Flash act as a Pressure Intensifier?**

The flash has a much **smaller cross-section** than the cavity. According to the **continuity principle**, for the same volume of material to flow:

$$V_{cavity} \gg V_{flash} \text{ (in terms of flow path resistance)}$$

Since the flash land is narrow, the **flow stress required** to push metal into the flash is very high. This high resistance means that once the cavity is full, the remaining forging pressure is "spent" pushing metal into the flash. This **maintains high cavity pressure**, ensuring:
- All intricate details of the die are filled.
- The forging is **fully dense** with no voids.
- Final shape is accurate.

**Key Equations:**

The pressure in the flash can be estimated as:
$$P_{flash} = 2 \sigma_y \left( 1 + \dfrac{2 \mu w}{t} \right)$$

The cavity pressure equals the flash pressure (in equilibrium):
$$P_{cavity} = P_{flash}$$

The **thinner the flash, the higher the pressure intensifier effect** — but too thin causes excess die wear.

**Flash Removal:**
The flash is removed in a separate **trimming press** operation using a die set with a sharp cutting edge along the parting line contour.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**

1. **Confusing True Strain with Engineering Strain in Forging:**
   - *Engineering strain:* $e = \dfrac{h_0 - h_f}{h_0} = \dfrac{\Delta h}{h_0}$
