   - *True strain:* $\varepsilon = \ln \left( \dfrac{h_0}{h_f} \right)$
   - Many students write the engineering strain formula and lose **1–2 marks** for not using the natural logarithm. The KTU Board strictly expects **$\ln$ (true strain)** for forging problems.

2. **Forgetting the Form Factor ($K_f$):**
   - The simplified formula $F = \sigma_y \cdot A_f$ is for **frictionless ideal forging**.
   - For real problems, students MUST include $K_f = 1 + \dfrac{\mu d_0}{3 h_f}$.
   - Missing this loses **2–3 marks** easily.

3. **Unit Conversion Errors:**
   - $\sigma_y$ in MPa → must convert to **N/m²** (multiply by $10^6$).
   - Dimensions in mm → must convert to **m** (divide by 1000).
   - Forgetting the $10^6$ factor leads to answers off by 6 orders of magnitude.

4. **Mixing Up $h_0/h_f$ Direction:**
   - In the true strain formula, the **larger value (initial) goes in the numerator**.
   - $\varepsilon = \ln(h_0/h_f)$ — NOT $\ln(h_f/h_0)$.
   - Wrong direction gives a **negative strain**, which is a sign of an incorrect understanding.

5. **Not Mentioning Volume Constancy:**
   - Every forging derivation must start with $V_{before} = V_{after}$ as the **governing principle**.
   - Examiners look for this explicitly; missing it costs the first step's marks.

6. **Confusing Hot vs Cold Forging Temperature Range:**
   - Hot forging: Above recrystallization temperature (e.g., **above 727 °C for steel**).
   - Cold forging: **At room temperature (~25 °C)**.
   - Mixing these up shows a lack of conceptual clarity.

7. **Skipping Units in Final Answer:**
   - KTU board valuation insists on **units** in every numerical answer.
   - Force in **N or kN**, energy in **J or kJ**, strain is **dimensionless**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 High-Density Revision Checklist for Forging (Module 2):**

### **A. Core Concepts (Must Know)**
- Forging is a **solid-state plastic deformation** process using **compressive forces**.
- Forging refines **grain flow**, giving **superior mechanical properties** vs casting/machining.
- **Hot forging** (above recrystallization T) is most common; yields lower force and better ductility.
- **Cold forging** offers precision and surface finish; requires higher force.
- **Volume constancy** is the fundamental governing equation: $V_{before} = V_{after}$.

### **B. Key Equations (Memorize)**
- True strain: $\varepsilon = \ln(h_0/h_f)$
- Forging force: $F = \sigma_y \cdot A_f \cdot K_f$
- Form factor: $K_f = 1 + \dfrac{\mu d_0}{3 h_f}$
- Forging energy: $W = \sigma_y \cdot V \cdot K_f \cdot \ln(h_0/h_f)$
- Volume constancy (cylindrical): $d_0^2 h_0 = d_f^2 h_f$

### **C. Forging Operations (KTU Module Specific)**
1. Drawing out (cogging) 2. Upsetting 3. Bending 4. Punching 5. Swaging 6. Heading 7. Coining 8. Piercing 9. Setting down (cut-off) 10. Extrusion

### **D. Forging Process Types (Remember All 6)**
1. **Hammer forging** (Drop, Steam, Air, Board) — impact-based.
2. **Press forging** (Hydraulic, Mechanical, Screw) — continuous stroke.
3. **Open-die forging** (Smith forging) — flat dies, free flow.
4. **Closed-die forging** (Impression die) — cavity dies with flash.
5. **Special processes** (Roll forging, Ring rolling, Swaging, Isothermal).
6. **Cold/Warm forging** — temperature variants.

### **E. Flash Knowledge (Critical for Board)**
- Flash = thin metal layer at **parting line** of closed-die forging.
- Purpose: absorbs **volume variation**, ensures **cavity filling**, acts as **pressure intensifier**.
- Flash thickness affects die wear and final product accuracy.
- Flash is removed in a **trimming press**.

### **F. Die Knowledge**
- Die material: **H11 / H13 hot-work tool steel** (for hot forging).
- Die hardness: **45–55 HRC**.
- Lubricant: **Graphite-based** for hot forging; **phosphate + soap** for cold forging.

### **G. Defects to Mention in Long Answers**
- Cold shut, laps, cracks, scale pits, incomplete filling, die wear, burst, flash irregularities.

### **H. Industrial Applications (Mention for Context)**
- Automotive: **crankshafts, connecting rods, gears, knuckles**.
- Aerospace: **turbine discs, landing gear**.
- Defence: **gun barrels, missile casings**.
- Power: **rotor shafts, valve bodies**.
- General: **bolts, nuts, fasteners, railway axles, hand tools**.

### **I. Numerical Problem Strategy (Time Saving)**
1. List all given data in **SI units** first.
2. Apply volume constancy to find **final dimensions**.
3. Compute **form factor** $K_f$ if friction data given.
4. Use the **master equation** $F = \sigma_y \cdot A_f \cdot K_f$.
5. Compute strain and energy in **separate sub-parts**.
6. **Box all final answers** with units.

### **J. Mnemonics for Quick Recall**
- **"HFPCSI"** for forging process types: **H**ammer, **F**orging-types, **P**ress, **C**old, **S**pecial, **I**sothermal.
- **"DDUBPSC"** for forging operations: **D**rawing, **D**rawing out, **U**psetting, **B**ending, **P**unching, **S**waging, **C**oining.

### **K. Examiner's Pet Topics (High-Frequency in KTU 2024 Scheme)**
- Forging force derivation.
- Flash and its role.
- Hot vs Cold forging.
- Hammer vs Press forging.
- Open-die vs Closed-die comparison.
- Worked numerical on forging force and energy.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:**
> For a 14-mark question, structure your answer in **3 parts**:
> (1) Diagram / schematic (2 marks)
> (2) Explanation with bullet points (5 marks)
> (3) Numerical with all steps (7 marks)
> This guarantees full marks if every step is shown clearly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rolling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDEST203 - BASIC MECHANICAL AND CIVIL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Rolling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rolling** is a **metal forming process** in which the metal stock (billet, bloom, slab, or sheet) is passed through one or more pairs of **rotating rolls** to reduce its cross-sectional area, achieve uniform thickness, and/or improve mechanical properties such as grain structure and surface finish.

It is classified as a **bulk deformation (compressive) process** in manufacturing technology, governed by the principles of plastic deformation under compressive normal forces combined with tangential friction forces at the roll–workpiece interface.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Rolling Pin Analogy:** Imagine a chef using a *belan* (rolling pin) to flatten a ball of dough into a thin chapati. The dough enters thick, gets squeezed between the rolling pin and the platform, and exits thinner and longer. This is *exactly* what industrial rolling does — but on heated steel ingots weighing several tons, between massive steel rolls driven by **motors rated in megawatts**.

- The **dough** = the metal stock (ingot, slab, billet)
- The **rolling pin** = the work rolls
- The **thickness reduction** = the *draft* ($\Delta h$)
- The **elongation** = the *spread* (negligible in width, significant in length)

## 1.3 Classification of Rolling Processes

| Classification Basis | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **By Temperature** | Hot Rolling | Performed **above the recrystallization temperature** (typically $> 0.6\,T_m$). |
| | Cold Rolling | Performed **below the recrystallization temperature**. |
| **By Product Geometry** | Flat Rolling | Produces plates, sheets, strips (rectangular cross-section). |
| | Shape Rolling | Produces I-beams, rails, channels, T-sections. |
| **By Roll Configuration** | Two-High, Three-High, Four-High, Cluster Mills | Defined by the number and arrangement of rolls. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Students must remember that the **recrystallization temperature for steel is approximately $0.6 \times T_m$**, where $T_m$ is the melting point in Kelvin.

## 1.4 Direction of Rolling Terminology (for flat rolling)

- **Longitudinal Direction** — Direction of roll rotation / material feed.
- **Transverse Direction** — Direction across the width of the sheet.
- **Normal Direction** — Direction through the thickness.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric Parameters of Rolling — The "Why" and "How"

When a workpiece of initial thickness $h_0$ enters a pair of rolls of radius $R$ and exits with reduced thickness $h_1$, several critical geometric parameters arise.

### Step-by-Step Logic:

1. **Draft ($\Delta h$):** The reduction in thickness per pass.
   - $\Delta h = h_0 - h_1$

2. **Coefficient of Friction ($\mu$):** Required at roll–workpiece interface to *pull* the workpiece into the roll gap. This is the **fundamental condition** for rolling to occur.

3. **Angle of Bite / Contact Angle ($\alpha$):** The angle subtended at the roll center by the arc of contact.
   - Derived from geometry of two intersecting circles.

4. **Projected Length of Contact ($L$):** The horizontal projection of the arc of contact on the roll surface.

5. **Neutral Point / No-Slip Point (N):** The point on the contact arc where roll surface velocity equals workpiece velocity.

6. **Forward Slip ($s$):** The workpiece exits *faster* than the roll's tangential velocity at the exit, due to volume constancy.

7. **Backward Slip:** Below the neutral point, the workpiece moves *slower* than the roll surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The friction between the rolls and the metal is the *driving force* that pulls the metal into the rolls. If $\mu$ is too low, the rolls will simply *spin* without grabbing the metal — a phenomenon called **"slipping out"** or **"refusal to bite."**

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **examination-critical** and appear repeatedly in KTU past papers.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Draft | $\Delta h = h_0 - h_1$ | mm | $h_0 > h_1$ |
| 2 | True Strain | $\varepsilon = \ln(h_0 / h_1)$ | dimensionless | — |
| 3 | Reduction | $r = \Delta h / h_0$ | dimensionless | Often in % |
| 4 | Angle of Bite | $\cos\alpha = 1 - \dfrac{\Delta h}{2R}$ | radians | Exact relation |
| 5 | Angle of Bite (small $\alpha$ approx.) | $\alpha \approx \sqrt{\Delta h / R}$ | radians | Use when $\alpha < 15^\circ$ |
| 6 | Projected Contact Length | $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$ | mm | $L \approx R\alpha$ |
| 7 | Maximum Draft (no-slip condition) | $\Delta h_{\max} = \mu^2 R$ | mm | From $\alpha_{\max} = \tan^{-1}\mu$ |
| 8 | Maximum Angle of Bite | $\alpha_{\max} = \tan^{-1}(\mu)$ | radians | Frictional limit |
| 9 | Forward Slip | $s = \dfrac{v_1 - v_0}{v_0}$ | dimensionless | $v_1 > v_0$ at exit |
| 10 | Forward Slip (approx.) | $s \approx \dfrac{R \gamma^2}{2 h_1}$ | dimensionless | $\gamma$ = neutral angle |
| 11 | Roll Pressure (mean) | $\bar{p} = Y_f \left(1 + \dfrac{\mu L}{2 \bar{h}}\right)$ | MPa | $Y_f$ = flow stress |
| 12 | Separating Force | $F = \bar{p} \cdot w \cdot L$ | N | $w$ = width |
| 13 | Rolling Torque (per roll) | $T = 0.5 \cdot F \cdot L$ | N·m | Approx. uniform pressure |
| 14 | Rolling Power | $P = 2 \pi N T$ | W | $N$ = rpm of roll |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Substitution Rule:** Whenever you see a problem asking for the **angle of bite** or **contact length**, always check whether the small-angle approximation is justified. For typical industrial rolling, $\alpha$ is between $3^\circ$ and $15^\circ$, so the approximation $\alpha \approx \sqrt{\Delta h / R}$ is **usually valid**.

## 2.3 Rolling Mills — Classification (KTU High-Yield)

### Two-High Rolling Mill
- Two rolls, rotating in opposite directions.
- May be **non-reversing** (sheet for large production) or **reversing** (heavy slabs).
- **Limitation:** Roll deflection increases with width → poor flatness on wide sheets.

### Three-High Rolling Mill
- Three rolls stacked vertically; the middle roll rotates in one direction, top and bottom rotate oppositely.
- Material is passed between top–middle and middle–bottom alternately.
- **Used for:** Heavy sections, rails, beams.

### Four-High Rolling Mill
- Two **small work rolls** supported by two **larger backup rolls**.
- Backup rolls prevent deflection → excellent flatness for thin sheets.

### Cluster Mill (Sendzimir / Z-Mill)
- One work roll supported by **2 to 20 backup rolls** arranged in a cluster.
- Used for very thin, hard, high-strength sheets (stainless steel, silicon steel for electrical laminations).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** Cluster mills (Sendzimir) are the workhorse of the **electrical steel industry** — they roll silicon steel as thin as **0.1 mm** for transformer cores. Four-high mills dominate the **automotive sheet** industry. The 4-high configuration was the breakthrough that enabled mass production of car body panels with tight thickness tolerances ($\pm 0.01$ mm).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Angle of Bite and Contact Length

**Statement:** Prove that the angle of contact $\alpha$ between the work roll (radius $R$) and the workpiece satisfies $\cos\alpha = 1 - \dfrac{\Delta h}{2R}$ and that the projected contact length is $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$.

### Setup

Let the two roll centers be separated by a horizontal distance. Place the origin at the center of the *right* roll. The roll surface has equation $x^2 + y^2 = R^2$. The entry plane is at $y = +h_0/2$ and exit plane at $y = +h_1/2$ (using upper half symmetry).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let the bite point } P \text{ lie on the roll surface at angle } \alpha \text{ from the roll center line.}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Vertical coordinate of point } P &: y_P = R \sin\alpha \\
\text{Since } P \text{ lies on the entry plane of the workpiece: } y_P &= h_0 / 2 \\
\text{Since } P \text{ lies on the exit plane of the workpiece: } y_P &= h_1 / 2
\end{aligned}
$$

> Using the **mid-thickness approximation** (most common KTU method), we relate the bite point to the average of entry and exit thickness.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{By symmetry, the roll surfaces meet at the centerline.} \\
\text{Let } h_m = \dfrac{h_0 + h_1}{2} \text{ be the mean thickness.} \\
\text{Then the vertical gap between rolls at the bite point} &: h_m = 2R(1 - \cos\alpha) \\
\Rightarrow \cos\alpha &= 1 - \dfrac{h_m}{R} = 1 - \dfrac{h_0 + h_1}{2R}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **standard KTU textbook form**, the draft $\Delta h = h_0 - h_1$ is used directly. Since the rolls are tangent at the centerline and the *workpiece thickness changes* from $h_0$ to $h_1$, the more useful form is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Approximation: } \dfrac{h_0 + h_1}{2} &\approx h_0 - \dfrac{\Delta h}{2} \\
\text{This gives the classical form:} \quad \cos\alpha &= 1 - \dfrac{\Delta h}{2R}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{For small } \alpha: \cos\alpha &\approx 1 - \dfrac{\alpha^2}{2} \\
\Rightarrow 1 - \dfrac{\alpha^2}{2} &= 1 - \dfrac{\Delta h}{2R} \\
\Rightarrow \alpha^2 &= \dfrac{\Delta h}{R} \\
\Rightarrow \alpha &= \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta h}{R}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Projected contact length:} \quad L &= R \sin\alpha \approx R\alpha \\
L &= R \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta h}{R}} = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** $\boxed{\cos\alpha = 1 - \dfrac{\Delta h}{2R}, \quad \alpha \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta h}{R}}, \quad L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}}$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Forward Slip (Simplified — Ekelund Approximation)

**Statement:** Show that forward slip $s = \dfrac{R \gamma^2}{2 h_1}$ where $\gamma$ is the angle to the neutral point and $h_1$ is the exit thickness.

### Setup

- $v_0$ = entry velocity of the strip (horizontal).
- $v$ = tangential velocity of the roll surface = $\omega R$.
- $v_1$ = exit velocity of the strip (horizontal).
- At the **neutral point N**, the roll surface moves *with* the strip → $v_N = v_{\text{strip, horizontal}}$.
- Let $\beta$ = angle to neutral point measured from the roll center.
- Let $\gamma$ = angle subtended between the exit plane and the neutral point. ($\gamma = \alpha - \beta$)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At the entry point (angle } \alpha \text{ from center):} \\
\text{Horizontal component of roll velocity} &= v \cos\alpha \\
\text{Strip entry velocity} &= v_0 \\
\text{Since strip is slower at entry, } v_0 &= v \cos\alpha \quad \text{(velocity continuity)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At the neutral point N (angle } \beta \text{ from center):} \\
\text{Strip velocity equals horizontal roll velocity} \\
v_N &= v \cos\beta
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At the exit point:} \\
\text{Horizontal roll velocity at exit} &= v \cos 0 = v \\
\text{Strip exit velocity} &= v_1 \\
\text{By volume constancy (entry volume = exit volume, neglecting spread):} \\
h_0 \cdot w \cdot v_0 &= h_1 \cdot w \cdot v_1
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Forward slip definition:} \quad s &= \dfrac{v_1 - v}{v} \\
\text{Using } v_1 = v \cos\beta / \cos(\beta - \mu) \text{ from slip-line theory:} \\
s &\approx \dfrac{R \gamma^2}{2 h_1} \quad \text{(for small } \gamma \text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The exact derivation involves the **friction hill** concept (Ekelund's analysis) and is beyond KTU's typical scope. The simplified formula $s = \dfrac{R \gamma^2}{2 h_1}$ is what KTU examiners expect for Module 2 problems.

> **Result:** $\boxed{s = \dfrac{v_1 - v}{v} \approx \dfrac{R \gamma^2}{2 h_1}}$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Maximum Draft Condition

**Statement:** Show that the maximum possible draft per pass is $\Delta h_{\max} = \mu^2 R$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Condition for the rolls to "bite" the workpiece:} \\
\alpha &\leq \tan^{-1}(\mu) \quad \text{(friction limit)} \\
\text{For maximum bite, } \alpha_{\max} &= \tan^{-1}(\mu) \\
\text{Taking tangent of both sides:} \quad \tan\alpha_{\max} &= \mu
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{From the small-angle geometry: } \tan\alpha &\approx \alpha = \sqrt{\Delta h / R} \\
\text{Setting } \sqrt{\Delta h_{\max} / R} &= \mu \\
\Rightarrow \Delta h_{\max} &= \mu^2 R
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** $\boxed{\Delta h_{\max} = \mu^2 R}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Consequence:** To increase draft, engineers either (a) **increase roll radius** (why cluster mills have very small work rolls *but* the friction coefficient is hard to change), or (b) **increase friction** by roughening the roll surface (e.g., ground rolls for hot rolling, knurled surfaces, or applying friction-enhancing lubricants that paradoxically act as grip agents in hot rolling). In cold rolling, the friction coefficient is too low → multiple passes are required.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Rolling Process Geometry (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **functional flow** of the rolling process — from material entry through the roll bite to the output of the rolled product.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Material Entry] --> B[Initial Thickness h0]
    B --> C[Approach Zone: Roll Surface Exerts Normal Force]
    C --> D{Friction mu Greater than tan alpha}
    D -->|Yes| E[Successful Bite: Material Enters Roll Gap]
    D -->|No| F[Rolls Spin Without Gripping: Process Fails]
    E --> G[Deformation Zone: Plastic Flow Occurs]
    G --> H[Neutral Point N: v_roll equals v_strip]
    H --> I[Backward Slip Zone: v_roll greater than v_strip]
    H --> J[Forward Slip Zone: v_roll less than v_strip]
    J --> K[Exit Thickness h1]
    K --> L[Rolled Product with Reduced Cross Section]
    F --> M[Increase mu or R or Decrease Delta h]
    M --> B
```

## 4.2 Rolling Mill Classification (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RM[Rolling Mills] --> A1[Two High Mill]
    RM --> A2[Three High Mill]
    RM --> A3[Four High Mill]
    RM --> A4[Cluster Mill Sendzimir]
    A1 --> B1[Used for Slabs and Plates]
    A1 --> B2[Simple Design Low Cost]
    A2 --> C1[Used for Heavy Sections and Rails]
    A2 --> C2[Material Reversed Between Top and Bottom]
    A3 --> D1[Used for Sheet and Strip Production]
    A3 --> D2[Small Work Rolls Backed by Large Backup Rolls]
    A4 --> E1[Used for Thin Hard Stainless Steel]
    A4 --> E2[Multiple Backup Rolls in Cluster Arrangement]
```

## 4.3 Force and Velocity Analysis in the Roll Bite

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Entry: v0 Strip Velocity] --> F2[Entry: v cos alpha Roll Velocity Component]
    F2 --> F3[Neutral Point N: v cos beta]
    F3 --> F4[Exit: v1 Strip Velocity]
    F3 --> F5[Exit: v Tangential Roll Velocity]
    F4 --> F6[Forward Slip s equals v1 minus v divided by v]
    F5 --> F6
    F1 --> G1[Horizontal Force from Friction]
    G1 --> G2[Vertical Force from Roll Pressure]
    G2 --> G3[Net Separating Force F]
    G3 --> G4[Moment Arm equals L divided by 2]
    G4 --> G5[Rolling Torque T equals 0.5 F L]
    G5 --> G6[Power P equals 2 pi N T]
```

## 4.4 Hot Rolling vs Cold Rolling — Process Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Required Product Properties] --> Q2{Critical Surface Finish}
    Q2 -->|Yes| R1[Choose Cold Rolling]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Large Thickness Reduction}
    Q3 -->|Yes| R2[Choose Hot Rolling First]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4[Choose Cold Rolling for Tight Tolerances]
    R2 --> S1[Cool Down to Room Temperature]
    S1 --> R1
    R1 --> T1[Final Smooth Surface Improved Mechanical Properties]
    T1 --> U1[Automotive Panels Appliances Precision Strips]
    R2 --> V1[Railroad Rails I Beams Plates]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams above are **functional flowcharts** because the physical schematics of rolling (with curvature, force vectors, etc.) are not natively renderable in Mermaid. These block diagrams map every conceptual interaction required by the KTU syllabus at the process and force-flow level.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define rolling. List the differences between hot rolling and cold rolling.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Rolling** is a metal forming process in which the metal stock is passed between two rotating rolls to reduce its cross-sectional thickness and improve its mechanical properties.

| Parameter | Hot Rolling | Cold Rolling |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | Above recrystallization temperature ($> 0.6\,T_m$) | Below recrystallization temperature |
| Surface Finish | Rough, scaled | Smooth, bright |
| Ductility Required | Lower (workpiece is soft) | Higher (workpiece is strain-hardened) |
| Dimensional Accuracy | Poor | Excellent |
| Grain Structure | Recrystallized, coarse | Strained, fine, directional |
| Forces Required | Lower | Higher (due to work hardening) |

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Explain the term "angle of bite" and derive the condition for the rolls to bite the workpiece.**

**Model Answer:**

The **angle of bite** ($\alpha$) is the angle subtended at the roll center by the arc of contact between the roll and the workpiece. It is the angle that defines the entry geometry of the deformation zone.

**Derivation of Bite Condition:**

For the rolls to successfully pull the workpiece into the roll gap, the horizontal component of the friction force at the roll surface must be greater than or equal to the horizontal component of the normal (compressive) reaction:

$$
\mu \geq \tan\alpha
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \alpha \leq \tan^{-1}(\mu)
$$

The **maximum permissible angle of bite** is therefore $\alpha_{\max} = \tan^{-1}(\mu)$.

The corresponding **maximum draft** is derived as:

$$
\Delta h_{\max} = \mu^2 R
$$

If the actual angle of bite $\alpha > \alpha_{\max}$, the rolls will spin without gripping the metal.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working of a **four-high rolling mill** and state its advantages over a two-high mill. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A four-high rolling mill consists of **two small diameter work rolls** in direct contact with the metal, supported by **two larger diameter backup rolls**. The backup rolls prevent elastic deflection of the work rolls under the high separating force, ensuring the rolled product has uniform thickness across its width.

**Key Components:**
- **Work rolls:** Small diameter (typically 50–200 mm), made of hardened tool steel, in direct contact with the workpiece.
- **Backup rolls:** Large diameter (typically 400–1500 mm), provide rigidity to work rolls.
- **Roll chocks / bearings:** Allow rolls to rotate freely.
- **Screwed-down mechanism:** Adjusts the gap between the work rolls to set the exit thickness.

**Advantages over two-high mill:**

1. **Reduced roll deflection** — backup rolls resist bending, giving flat sheets even at widths of 2 m.
2. **Smaller contact area** — smaller work rolls mean lower rolling force for the same draft.
3. **Better thickness control** — uniform pressure across the width.
4. **Suitable for thin, hard materials** — can roll stainless steel, silicon steel.
5. **Longer work roll life** — work rolls see less bending fatigue.

---

**(b)** A steel slab of initial thickness $h_0 = 25$ mm is rolled in a two-high mill with roll radius $R = 250$ mm to a final thickness $h_1 = 20$ mm. The coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.08$. The width of the slab is $w = 300$ mm, and the mean flow stress $Y_f = 250$ MPa. Determine: **(i)** Angle of bite, **(ii)** Projected contact length, **(iii)** Maximum possible draft, **(iv)** Rolling force, **(v)** Torque per roll, **(vi)** Power if the rolls rotate at 60 rpm. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $h_0 = 25$ mm, $h_1 = 20$ mm, $R = 250$ mm, $\mu = 0.08$, $w = 300$ mm, $Y_f = 250$ MPa, $N = 60$ rpm.

**Step 1: Draft**
$$
\Delta h = h_0 - h_1 = 25 - 20 = 5 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2: (i) Angle of Bite**
$$
\cos\alpha = 1 - \dfrac{\Delta h}{2R} = 1 - \dfrac{5}{2 \times 250} = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99
$$
$$
\alpha = \cos^{-1}(0.99) = 0.1413 \text{ rad} = 8.10^\circ
$$

**Step 3: (ii) Projected Contact Length**
$$
L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{250 \times 5} = \sqrt{1250} = 35.36 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4: (iii) Maximum Possible Draft**
$$
\Delta h_{\max} = \mu^2 R = (0.08)^2 \times 250 = 0.0064 \times 250 = 1.6 \text{ mm}
$$

> **Note:** The actual draft (5 mm) **far exceeds** the maximum draft (1.6 mm), which means rolling cannot occur in a single pass with this friction. The student should reduce the draft per pass or use a lubricant with higher friction.

**Step 5: (iv) Rolling Force (mean pressure method)**
$$
\bar{h} = \dfrac{h_0 + h_1}{2} = \dfrac{25 + 20}{2} = 22.5 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
\bar{p} = Y_f \left(1 + \dfrac{\mu L}{2\bar{h}}\right) = 250 \left(1 + \dfrac{0.08 \times 35.36}{2 \times 22.5}\right)
$$
$$
\bar{p} = 250 \left(1 + \dfrac{2.829}{45}\right) = 250 \times 1.0629 = 265.7 \text{ MPa}
$$
$$
F = \bar{p} \cdot w \cdot L = 265.7 \times 300 \times 35.36 = 2{,}819{,}142 \text{ N} \approx 2819 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 6: (v) Torque per Roll**
$$
T = 0.5 \cdot F \cdot L = 0.5 \times 2{,}819{,}142 \times 0.03536 = 49{,}843 \text{ N·m} \approx 49.84 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 7: (vi) Power Required**
$$
P = 2\pi N T = 2\pi \times \dfrac{60}{60} \times 49{,}843 = 2\pi \times 49{,}843 = 313{,}135 \text{ W} \approx 313.14 \text{ kW}
$$

---

### Question B (Alternative to Question A) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a velocity diagram, explain the concept of **neutral point** and **forward slip** in rolling. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

When a workpiece passes through the roll gap, there exists a point on the arc of contact where the **horizontal velocity of the workpiece equals the tangential (horizontal) velocity of the roll surface**. This point is called the **Neutral Point (N)** or **No-Slip Point**.

**Velocity Zones in the Roll Bite:**

1. **Entry Zone (Approach Side):** From the entry plane to the neutral point.
   - The roll surface moves **faster** horizontally than the workpiece.
   - Friction acts on the workpiece in the direction of rolling (forward).
   - This friction **pulls** the workpiece into the rolls.

2. **At the Neutral Point N:**
   - Roll surface horizontal velocity = Workpiece velocity.
   - No relative motion → no slip.

3. **Exit Zone (Back Side, from N to exit):**
   - The roll surface moves **slower** horizontally than the workpiece.
   - Friction acts on the rolls in the direction of rolling (workpiece drives the roll).
   - The workpiece pushes the roll, leading to a small speed-up relative to the roll's nominal tangential velocity.

**Forward Slip Definition:**

$$
s = \dfrac{v_1 - v}{v}
$$

where $v_1$ is the exit velocity of the strip and $v$ is the tangential velocity of the roll. Since volume is conserved ($h_0 v_0 = h_1 v_1$) and the workpiece gets thinner, $v_1 > v_0$. At the exit, $v_1$ is typically **3 to 10% greater** than $v$, hence the term *forward slip*.

**Geometric Relation (Ekelund Approximation):**

$$
s \approx \dfrac{R \gamma^2}{2 h_1}
$$

where $\gamma$ is the angle from the roll center to the neutral point, measured from the exit plane.

> **Engineering Significance:** Forward slip is critical for the design of **guards**, **loopers**, and **tension control systems** in continuous rolling mills. Improper tension control due to forward slip causes strip breakage and thickness variations.

---

**(b)** A 40 mm thick aluminum slab is reduced to 30 mm in one pass using rolls of 350 mm radius. The rolls rotate at 100 rpm. The coefficient of friction is 0.12 and the mean flow stress is 180 MPa. The width is 500 mm. Calculate: **(i)** The angle of bite, **(ii)** The check whether the rolling is possible, **(iii)** The roll separating force, **(iv)** The torque and power required. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $h_0 = 40$ mm, $h_1 = 30$ mm, $R = 350$ mm, $\mu = 0.12$, $Y_f = 180$ MPa, $w = 500$ mm, $N = 100$ rpm.

**Step 1: Draft**
$$
\Delta h = h_0 - h_1 = 40 - 30 = 10 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2: (i) Angle of Bite**
$$
\cos\alpha = 1 - \dfrac{\Delta h}{2R} = 1 - \dfrac{10}{2 \times 350} = 1 - 0.01429 = 0.9857
$$
$$
\alpha = \cos^{-1}(0.9857) = 0.1692 \text{ rad} = 9.70^\circ
$$

**Step 3: (ii) Rolling Possibility Check**

For rolling to occur: $\alpha \leq \tan^{-1}(\mu)$

$$
\tan^{-1}(0.12) = 6.84^\circ = 0.1194 \text{ rad}
$$

Since $\alpha = 9.70^\circ > 6.84^\circ$, **rolling is NOT possible in a single pass** with the given parameters. The student must reduce the draft per pass.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students will compute $\alpha$ and stop. They will not check the **frictional bite condition** and lose 2 marks. **Always verify $\alpha \leq \tan^{-1}(\mu)$ before proceeding with force calculations.**

**Step 4: (iii) Roll Separating Force (for academic completeness)**

Even though the bite condition fails, the *force* calculation is often required by examiners for practice:

$$
L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{350 \times 10} = \sqrt{3500} = 59.16 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
\bar{h} = \dfrac{40 + 30}{2} = 35 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
\bar{p} = Y_f \left(1 + \dfrac{\mu L}{2\bar{h}}\right) = 180 \left(1 + \dfrac{0.12 \times 59.16}{2 \times 35}\right)
$$
$$
\bar{p} = 180 \times 1.1014 = 198.25 \text{ MPa}
$$
$$
F = \bar{p} \cdot w \cdot L = 198.25 \times 500 \times 59.16 = 5{,}866{,}005 \text{ N} \approx 5866 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5: (iv) Torque and Power**
$$
T = 0.5 \cdot F \cdot L = 0.5 \times 5{,}866{,}005 \times 0.05916 = 173{,}511 \text{ N·m} \approx 173.5 \text{ kN·m}
$$
$$
P = 2\pi N T = 2\pi \times \dfrac{100}{60} \times 173{,}511 = 1{,}816{,}870 \text{ W} \approx 1816.87 \text{ kW} \approx 1.82 \text{ MW}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Points:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mm to m** in force → torque calculations. Always keep units consistent (N, m, Pa) when computing torque and power.
> 2. **Confusing $\Delta h/2R$ with $\Delta h/R$** in the angle-of-bite formula. The correct form is $\cos\alpha = 1 - \Delta h/(2R)$.
> 3. **Skipping the bite condition check.** This is a 2-mark freebie — always verify $\alpha \leq \tan^{-1}(\mu)$.
> 4. **Using $h_0$ or $h_1$ instead of $\bar{h}$** in the mean roll pressure formula.
> 5. **Forgetting the factor 2** in the power equation $P = 2\pi NT$ — this accounts for **two rolls** rotating.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam — Rolling**

- **Rolling** is a **bulk forming process** using two counter-rotating rolls to reduce thickness.
- **Hot rolling** = above $0.6\,T_m$; **Cold rolling** = below $0.6\,T_m$ (better finish, tighter tolerance).
- **Draft** $\Delta h = h_0 - h_1$.
- **Angle of Bite (Contact Angle):** $\cos\alpha = 1 - \dfrac{\Delta h}{2R}$, approximated as $\alpha \approx \sqrt{\Delta h / R}$.
- **Contact Length (Projected):** $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$.
- **Bite Condition (Friction Limit):** $\alpha \leq \tan^{-1}(\mu)$.
- **Maximum Draft per pass:** $\Delta h_{\max} = \mu^2 R$.
- **Mean Roll Pressure (for thin workpiece):** $\bar{p} = Y_f \left(1 + \dfrac{\mu L}{2\bar{h}}\right)$.
- **Separating Force:** $F = \bar{p} \cdot w \cdot L$.
- **Torque per roll:** $T = 0.5 \cdot F \cdot L$.
- **Power required:** $P = 2\pi N T$ (where $N$ is in rps, not rpm).
- **Forward Slip:** $s = (v_1 - v)/v \approx R\gamma^2 / (2h_1)$.
- **Rolling Mill Hierarchy:** 2-high (basic) → 3-high (heavy) → 4-high (sheet) → cluster (thin foil/hard alloys).
- **Units Discipline:** Always work in **N, m, Pa** for force-torque-power conversions.
- **Volume Constancy:** $h_0 v_0 = h_1 v_1$ (neglecting spread).
- **Neutral Point:** Roll surface velocity = Workpiece velocity; separates forward-slip and backward-slip zones.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extrusion, Metal Joining Processes: Types of Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDEST203 - BASIC MECHANICAL AND CIVIL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Extrusion, Metal Joining Processes: Types of Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Extrusion — Formal Definition

**Extrusion** is a compressive metal forming process in which a metal billet (raw material) is forced to flow plastically through a shaped die opening, producing a continuous product of uniform cross-section. The cross-section of the product matches the shape of the die orifice. Extrusion is a bulk deformation process analogous to squeezing toothpaste out of a tube.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Extrusion is covered as a primary metal forming process along with forging, rolling, and drawing. Understand the classification (Hot/Cold, Direct/Indirect) and the governing extrusion ratio/pressure relationship.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Toothpaste Tube

Imagine squeezing a tube of toothpaste. The paste (metal) flows continuously out of the nozzle (die) and takes the shape of the nozzle opening. The harder you press (higher ram force), the faster the flow. The shape of the opening decides the cross-section of the product — round, square, or any complex profile. This is exactly how an extrusion press operates on a metal billet.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** Extrusion can produce *very complex hollow or solid cross-sections* in a single operation — a capability that rolling or drawing cannot match.

### 1.3 Metal Joining Processes — Formal Definition

**Metal Joining Processes** are manufacturing methods used to permanently bond two or more metal parts into a single, unified assembly. The major categories are:

1. **Welding** — Joining by heat (with or without pressure) and often a filler material, producing a metallurgical bond between the parent metals.
2. **Brazing** — Joining by heating above **450 °C** but below the melting point of the base metals, using a filler metal with a lower melting point.
3. **Soldering** — Joining by heating below **450 °C** using a filler metal (solder) with a melting point below that of the base metals.
4. **Adhesive Bonding** — Joining using non-metallic adhesives (e.g., epoxy).
5. **Mechanical Fastening** — Joining using rivets, bolts, screws, etc.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Focus:** The syllabus emphasizes **Welding** under metal joining. Mastering the classification of welding processes (Arc, Gas, Resistance, Solid-State) is a high-weightage topic.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — Welding = "Metal Stitching"

Think of welding as *stitching* two pieces of cloth together — but instead of thread, we use molten metal (or solid-state diffusion/pressure) to create a continuous joint. The "stitches" can be made in different ways: using an electric arc (like a spark), a gas flame, friction heat, or even ultrasonic vibrations. The strength of the joint depends on the technique and the skill of the operator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Distinction:** In **fusion welding**, the base metal at the joint actually *melts* and solidifies. In **solid-state welding**, no melting occurs — joining happens through diffusion, deformation, or atomic bonding at temperatures below the melting point.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Extrusion Processes

Extrusion is broadly classified on two independent axes:

#### A. Based on Direction of Metal Flow (Direct vs. Indirect)

| Type | Description | Key Feature |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Direct (Forward) Extrusion** | Billet and ram move in the *same* direction; metal flows forward through the die. | Friction between billet and container wall increases ram force. |
| **Indirect (Backward) Extrusion** | Die is mounted on the ram; metal flows *opposite* to the ram motion through a hollow ram. | Friction is virtually eliminated; lower ram force required. |

#### B. Based on Temperature

| Type | Temperature | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|------|-------------|------------|---------------|
| **Hot Extrusion** | Above recrystallization temperature | Lower force, less cracking, suitable for hard metals (e.g., steel, titanium) | Surface oxidation, lower dimensional accuracy |
| **Cold Extrusion** | Room temperature | Better surface finish, higher strength (strain hardening), tight tolerances | Higher force, limited to ductile metals (e.g., Al, Cu) |

#### C. Special Variants

- **Impact Extrusion:** A punch strikes the billet at high speed; used for collapsible tubes (toothpaste, cosmetics) and cartridge cases.
- **Hydrostatic Extrusion:** Billet is surrounded by a high-pressure fluid; fluid pressure forces the billet through the die. Friction is eliminated completely.

### 2.2 Extrusion Process — Why and How

**Why Extrusion?**
- Produces complex cross-sections (window frames, aircraft structural parts, heat sinks).
- Excellent for both brittle and ductile metals.
- Continuous length products possible.

**How it Works (Direct Hot Extrusion):**
1. A heated cylindrical billet is loaded into a container.
2. A hydraulic ram pushes the billet toward the die.
3. The metal flows plastically through the die orifice.
4. The extruded product exits with the die's cross-sectional shape.

### 2.3 Classification of Welding Processes

Welding processes are systematically classified as follows:

#### A. Fusion Welding (Base metal melts)

| Process | Heat Source | Typical Application |
|---------|------------|---------------------|
| **Arc Welding (SMAW)** | Electric arc between electrode and workpiece | Structural steel, pipelines, shipbuilding |
| **Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW/MIG)** | Continuous wire electrode + shielding gas | Automotive, fabrication |
| **Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW/TIG)** | Non-consumable tungsten electrode + inert gas | Stainless steel, aluminum, aerospace |
| **Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)** | Arc buried under granular flux | Heavy plate welding, pressure vessels |
| **Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)** | Constricted arc plasma | Precision welding, tube manufacturing |
| **Oxy-Acetylene Gas Welding** | Combustion of acetylene in oxygen | Thin sheets, repair work, plumbing |
| **Resistance Spot Welding** | Resistance heating + pressure | Sheet metal joining (car bodies) |
| **Resistance Seam Welding** | Continuous roll electrodes | Tanks, containers |
| **Projection Welding** | Pre-formed projections concentrate current | Nuts to plates, cross-wire welding |
| **Flash Butt Welding** | Arc flashing between parts | Rails, chains, rings |
| **Thermit Welding** | Exothermic reaction of Al + Fe₂O₃ | Rail joint repair (in-situ) |
| **Electron Beam Welding (EBW)** | High-velocity electron beam | Aerospace, nuclear industries |
| **Laser Beam Welding (LBW)** | Focused laser beam | High-speed automation, electronics |

#### B. Solid-State Welding (No melting)

| Process | Mechanism | Typical Application |
|---------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Forge Welding** | Heating + hammering/pressing | Historical (blacksmithing), modern die forging |
| **Friction Welding** | Mechanical friction heats the interface | Shafts, valves, dissimilar metals |
| **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** | Rotating tool stirs the metal | Aluminum ship panels, aerospace |
| **Ultrasonic Welding** | High-frequency vibrations | Thin foils, electrical connections |
| **Explosion Welding** | Controlled detonation bonds plates | Clad plates, bimetallic tubes |
| **Diffusion Welding** | Atomic diffusion under heat & pressure | Aerospace turbine blades, nuclear parts |
| **Cold Roll Welding** | Pressure + slight deformation | Clad sheets, bimetallic strips |
| **Hot Pressure Welding** | Heat + pressure simultaneously | Thermit rail welding |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical for KTU Numerical Problems:** Memorize the extrusion pressure equations and the welding heat-input formula.

| Concept | Formula | Description |
|---------|---------|-------------|
| Extrusion Ratio | $R = \dfrac{A_0}{A_f}$ | Ratio of billet cross-sectional area to final product area |
| Reduction in Area | $r = 1 - \dfrac{A_f}{A_0} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{R}$ | Fractional reduction |
| Ideal Extrusion Pressure | $p = \sigma_f \cdot \ln(R)$ | $\sigma_f$ = average flow stress |
| Direct Extrusion Pressure (with friction) | $p_d = \sigma_f \left( \ln R + \dfrac{2L}{D} \right)$ | $L$ = billet length, $D$ = billet diameter |
| Indirect Extrusion Pressure (no friction) | $p_i = \sigma_f \cdot \ln R$ | Lower than direct by friction term |
| Extrusion Force | $F = p \cdot A_0$ | Ram force needed |
| Welding Heat Input | $H = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ kJ/mm | $V$ = voltage (V), $I$ = current (A), $S$ = travel speed (mm/min) |
| Arc Energy (per unit length) | $E = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{v}$ | $v$ = welding speed (mm/s) |

> [!NOTE]
> **For tables, the divider $\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}$ is written using `\dfrac` to avoid breaking the markdown table. Likewise, never use the `|` symbol inside table cells — use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.**

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Extrusion:** Aluminum window frames, heat sinks for electronics, aircraft fuselage stringers, and lead pipes for radiation shielding all rely on extrusion. The process gives designers near-limitless freedom in profile shape.
- **Welding:** Every automobile has **thousands of spot welds**; every skyscraper is held together by arc welds; every ship hull is welded by SAW; jet engines use EBW and diffusion welding. Welding is the **backbone of modern manufacturing**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Ideal Extrusion Pressure

Consider a cylindrical billet of initial cross-sectional area $A_0$ being extruded through a die to a final area $A_f$. Assume no friction, no redundant work, and uniform deformation.

**Step 1 — Define the Extrusion Ratio**

$$R = \frac{A_0}{A_f}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Principle of Conservation of Volume**

For an incompressible metal undergoing plastic deformation, the volume remains constant. As the billet moves a small distance $dx$ in the container, an equal volume of metal must exit the die.

$$A_0 \cdot dx = A_f \cdot dL$$

Here, $dx$ is the billet's displacement in the container, and $dL$ is the length of extruded product produced.

**Step 3 — Express the True Strain**

The true (logarithmic) strain in the extrusion direction is the natural log of the area ratio:

$$\varepsilon = \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = \ln(R)$$

**Step 4 — Relate Pressure to Flow Stress**

In an ideal deformation (no friction, no redundant work), the extrusion pressure equals the flow stress multiplied by the true strain. This is a direct consequence of the constant-volume plastic work balance:

$$p \cdot V = \sigma_f \cdot V \cdot \varepsilon$$

Cancelling the volume $V$ from both sides:

$$p = \sigma_f \cdot \varepsilon$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the Strain**

$$p = \sigma_f \cdot \ln(R)$$

This is the **Ideal Extrusion Pressure (also called Flow Pressure)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the theoretical *minimum* pressure. In actual practice, friction and redundant work add terms, making the actual pressure higher.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Direct Extrusion Pressure (with Friction)

In **direct extrusion**, the billet slides along the container walls, generating shear friction. The frictional shear stress is $\tau_f = \mu \cdot p_a$ (Coulomb friction), where $p_a$ is the average pressure and $\mu$ is the friction coefficient.

**Step 1 — Friction Force on the Billet Wall**

The frictional force opposing the ram motion acts on the lateral surface of the billet in contact with the container.

$$F_f = \tau_f \cdot \pi D \cdot L$$

Where:
- $D$ = billet diameter
- $L$ = current length of billet remaining in container

**Step 2 — Equivalent Frictional Pressure**

This frictional force contributes an extra pressure that must be overcome by the ram:

$$\Delta p_f = \frac{F_f}{A_0} = \frac{\tau_f \cdot \pi D \cdot L}{\frac{\pi}{4} D^2} = \frac{4 \tau_f L}{D}$$

**Step 3 — Add Friction to Ideal Pressure**

The total direct extrusion pressure becomes:

$$p_d = \sigma_f \cdot \ln R + \frac{4 \tau_f L}{D}$$

Assuming sticking friction (worst case, $\tau_f = \dfrac{\sigma_f}{2}$):

$$p_d = \sigma_f \left( \ln R + \frac{2L}{D} \right)$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Indirect Extrusion Pressure

In **indirect extrusion**, the die is on the ram itself. The billet does *not* move relative to the container walls — the die moves instead. Therefore, the frictional term is **eliminated**, and:

$$p_i = \sigma_f \cdot \ln R$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why indirect extrusion requires a **lower ram force** than direct extrusion for the same extrusion ratio and billet size.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Direct Extrusion | Indirect Extrusion |
|-----------|------------------|---------------------|
| Metal Flow Direction | Same as ram | Opposite to ram |
| Friction with Container | **Present** (high) | Negligible |
| Ram Force | Higher | Lower (≈ 25–30% less) |
| Die Position | Fixed at container end | Mounted on hollow ram |
| Product Length | Limited by billet length | Limited by ram stroke |
| Billet Wastage | Discard (butt) remains | Minimal discard |
| Application | Most common industrial method | Specialized (e.g., seamless tubes) |

### 3.5 Welding Heat Input Derivation

For arc welding, the **heat input per unit length** of weld is critical because it controls the weld bead size, HAZ (heat-affected zone) width, and distortion.

**Step 1 — Electrical Power in the Arc**

$$P = V \cdot I$$

Where $V$ is arc voltage (volts) and $I$ is welding current (amperes).

**Step 2 — Energy per Unit Time**

Power is energy per second. Over a time $t$, the energy delivered is:

$$E = V \cdot I \cdot t$$

**Step 3 — Energy per Unit Weld Length**

If the welding travel speed is $v$ (mm/s) and the arc travels a distance $d = v \cdot t$ (mm), then the heat input per mm is:

$$H = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot t}{v \cdot t} = \frac{V \cdot I}{v} \quad \text{(J/mm)}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Standard kJ/mm**

If $V$ is in volts, $I$ in amperes, and $v$ in mm/min, we convert units:

$$H = \frac{V \cdot I}{v \times 1000} \quad \text{(kJ/mm, when } v \text{ is in m/min)}$$

Or equivalently, the KTU-standard form:

$$H = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \quad \text{(kJ/mm)}$$

Where $S$ is travel speed in mm/min.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter?** Excessive heat input causes grain growth, distortion, and reduced toughness. Insufficient heat input causes lack of fusion and porosity. Heat input is a key control parameter in WPS (Welding Procedure Specification).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Direct (Forward) Extrusion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hydraulic Ram] --> B[Container with Heated Billet]
    B --> C[Die with Shaped Orifice]
    C --> D[Extruded Product Continuous Length]
    B -. Friction along walls .-> E[Discard Butt Left Behind]
    F[Heating Furnace] --> B
    G[Hydraulic Pressure applied] --> A
```

**Functional Reading of the Diagram:**
- The hydraulic ram pushes the heated billet axially.
- Metal flows plastically through the die.
- A discard "butt" remains in the container after the billet is exhausted.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Indirect (Backward) Extrusion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hollow Ram with Die] --> B[Stationary Billet in Container]
    B --> C[Extruded Product flows backward through hollow ram]
    D[Hydraulic Pressure] --> A
    A -. No relative motion .-> E[No Wall Friction]
```

**Functional Reading:** The die is mounted on a hollow ram. The die moves into the stationary billet, and metal flows *backward* through the die's orifice and out through the hollow ram. Friction is essentially zero.

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Classification of Welding Processes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W[Welding Processes] --> FW[Fusion Welding]
    W --> SW[Solid State Welding]
    
    FW --> AW[Arc Welding]
    FW --> GW[Gas Welding]
    FW --> RW[Resistance Welding]
    FW --> BW[Beam Welding]
    FW --> TW[Thermit Welding]
    
    AW --> A1[SMAW]
    AW --> A2[GMAW MIG]
    AW --> A3[GTAW TIG]
    AW --> A4[SAW]
    AW --> A5[PAW]
    
    GW --> G1[Oxy Acetylene]
    GW --> G2[Oxy Hydrogen]
    
    RW --> R1[Spot Welding]
    RW --> R2[Seam Welding]
    RW --> R3[Projection Welding]
    RW --> R4[Flash Butt Welding]
    
    BW --> B1[Electron Beam EBW]
    BW --> B2[Laser Beam LBW]
    
    SW --> S1[Friction Welding]
    SW --> S2[Friction Stir Welding]
    SW --> S3[Ultrasonic Welding]
    SW --> S4[Explosion Welding]
    SW --> S5[Diffusion Welding]
    SW --> S6[Forge Welding]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Resistance Spot Welding Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Squeeze] --> S2[Step 2: Weld Current ON]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Hold Forge Pressure]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Off Time Cooling]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Electrode Retraction]
    
    E1[Copper Electrodes] --> S1
    W1[Workpieces Clamped] --> S1
    H1[Heat generated at interface] --> S2
    N1[Nugget forms] --> S3
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram — Functional Flow of an Arc Welding Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Power Source AC or DC] --> C[Cables]
    C --> E[Electrode Holder]
    E --> ARC[Electric Arc Plasma]
    ARC --> WP[Workpiece]
    WP --> G[Ground Cable]
    G --> P
    SHG[Shielding Gas CO2 or Argon] --> ARC
    FM[Filler Wire Consumable] --> ARC
    ARC --> WB[Weld Pool Solidifies into Bead]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` Define extrusion. Differentiate between direct and indirect extrusion.**

**Model Answer:**
Extrusion is a metal forming process in which a billet is forced through a die of the desired cross-section to produce a continuous product of uniform cross-section.

| Aspect | Direct Extrusion | Indirect Extrusion |
|--------|------------------|--------------------|
| Metal flow direction | Same as ram | Opposite to ram |
| Friction | Present (high) | Negligible |
| Ram force | Higher | Lower |
| Billet wastage | Discard butt left | Minimal |

**(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for comparison)**

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` List any four types of welding processes. Briefly explain gas welding.**

**Model Answer:**
Four types of welding:
1. Arc welding
2. Gas welding
3. Resistance welding
4. Solid-state welding (e.g., friction welding)

**Gas Welding (Oxy-Acetylene):** It uses the combustion of acetylene gas with oxygen to produce a flame temperature of about **3200 °C**. The flame melts the base metal and filler rod at the joint, forming a molten weld pool that solidifies on cooling. It is widely used for thin sheets, pipes, and repair work.

**(3 marks: 1 for list + 2 for explanation)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Extrusion Problems:**
> Students frequently *forget to convert units* of the extrusion ratio when billet and product have different units. They also confuse the *direct vs. indirect* friction term. Always state clearly whether the problem is direct or indirect extrusion before applying the formula.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the direct extrusion process. State its advantages and limitations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cylindrical aluminum billet of diameter **100 mm** and length **200 mm** is extruded directly at room temperature. The flow stress of aluminum is **150 MPa** and the coefficient of friction is **0.1**. Calculate:
1. The extrusion ratio
2. The ram force required (assume sticking friction).

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**
Direct extrusion: The billet is placed in a container and a hydraulic ram pushes it toward a stationary die. The metal flows forward through the die, taking the shape of the die orifice. Friction between the billet and container wall opposes the ram motion.

*Advantages:*
- Simple equipment
- Suitable for brittle metals
- Can produce very complex cross-sections
- Long product lengths possible

*Limitations:*
- High ram force due to friction
- Discard butt is wasted material
- Surface oxidation in hot extrusion

**Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Calculate Areas**

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (100)^2 = 7853.98 \text{ mm}^2$$

The problem states the product is to be calculated. Assuming the product is to be drawn to a final diameter where the extrusion ratio is given numerically — let us *assume* the final area $A_f$ corresponds to extrusion ratio $R = 4$ (as a typical exam value). Then:

$$A_f = \frac{A_0}{R} = \frac{7853.98}{4} = 1963.5 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Extrusion Ratio**

$$R = \frac{A_0}{A_f} = 4$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Direct Extrusion Pressure Formula (with sticking friction)**

For sticking friction, the friction shear stress $\tau_f = \dfrac{\sigma_f}{2}$:

$$p_d = \sigma_f \left( \ln R + \frac{2L}{D} \right)$$

Compute each term:

$$\ln R = \ln(4) = 1.386$$

$$\frac{2L}{D} = \frac{2 \times 200}{100} = 4.0$$

$$p_d = 150 \times (1.386 + 4.0) = 150 \times 5.386 = 807.9 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Ram Force**

$$F = p_d \cdot A_0 = 807.9 \times 7853.98 = 6.345 \times 10^6 \text{ N} \approx 6345 \text{ kN}$$

> [!NOTE]
> If the problem gives a different $R$, the same procedure applies: compute $\ln R$, add $\frac{2L}{D}$, multiply by $\sigma_f$, and finally multiply by $A_0$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify welding processes. Explain the working principle of **arc welding** with a neat sketch.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is **heat input** in arc welding? An arc welding operation uses a voltage of **25 V**, a current of **200 A**, and a travel speed of **5 mm/s**. Calculate the heat input in **kJ/mm**.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**
Welding processes are classified into:

**A. Fusion Welding** (base metal melts): Arc welding, Gas welding, Resistance welding, Thermit welding, Electron beam welding, Laser beam welding.

**B. Solid-State Welding** (no melting): Friction welding, Ultrasonic welding, Explosion welding, Diffusion welding, Forge welding.

**Arc Welding Working Principle:**
An electric arc is struck between an electrode and the workpiece. The arc temperature (around **4000–6000 °C**) melts the base metal, forming a weld pool. A filler rod may be added. As the arc moves along the joint, the molten pool solidifies into a continuous weld bead. A shielding gas or flux protects the weld from atmospheric contamination.

*Sketch Description:* Power source → Cables → Electrode holder → Arc → Workpiece (connected to ground cable). Filler rod fed into the arc. Shielding gas flows around the arc.

**Part (b):**

**Definition:** Heat input is the amount of thermal energy supplied per unit length of weld. It is a key parameter that controls weld bead geometry, HAZ width, and distortion.

**Given:**
- $V = 25$ V
- $I = 200$ A
- $v = 5$ mm/s

**Step 1 — Power in the Arc**

$$P = V \cdot I = 25 \times 200 = 5000 \text{ W} = 5 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Heat Input per Unit Length**

$$H = \frac{P}{v} = \frac{5000 \text{ J/s}}{5 \text{ mm/s}} = 1000 \text{ J/mm}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kJ/mm**

$$H = 1.0 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting to convert W·s to kJ. Also, students often use **5 mm/min** as 5 mm/s and get a wrong answer. Always check the units of travel speed and convert to **mm/s** for this formula.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (Must Memorize for KTU Exams):**

- **Extrusion** is a compression forming process where a billet is forced through a die. It is the most versatile process for complex cross-sections.
- **Direct extrusion** has friction with the container wall; **indirect extrusion** has negligible friction.
- **Hot extrusion** is for high-melting / brittle metals; **cold extrusion** gives better surface finish and dimensional accuracy.
- The **extrusion ratio** $R = \dfrac{A_0}{A_f}$ controls the deformation severity.
- **Ideal extrusion pressure:** $p = \sigma_f \cdot \ln(R)$ — this is the *theoretical minimum*.
- **Direct extrusion pressure (sticking friction):** $p_d = \sigma_f \left( \ln R + \dfrac{2L}{D} \right)$.
- **Indirect extrusion pressure:** $p_i = \sigma_f \cdot \ln R$ — always lower than direct.
- **Welding** is classified into **Fusion** (melting occurs) and **Solid-State** (no melting).
- **Arc welding** uses an electric arc as the heat source; **gas welding** uses combustion of fuel gas + oxygen.
- **Resistance welding** uses $I^2R$ heat from passing a large current; includes spot, seam, projection, and flash butt welding.
- **EBW and LBW** are high-energy, high-precision beam welding processes for aerospace/nuclear.
- **Friction welding** is solid-state; ideal for joining dissimilar metals.
- **Heat input formula:** $H = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{v}$ in J/mm (when $v$ in mm/s), or the KTU standard form $H = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ in kJ/mm (when $S$ in mm/min).
- **Always** state the units in the final answer and **always** show the conversion steps in numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Description with Sketches of Arc Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDEST203 - BASIC MECHANICAL AND CIVIL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Description with Sketches of Arc Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Arc Welding

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Arc Welding** is a fusion welding process in which coalescence (metallic bonding) of metals is achieved by heating the workpiece to the melting point using the **intense heat of an electric arc** struck between an electrode and the base metal (or between two electrodes). The arc temperature typically ranges from **6,000 °C to 7,000 °C** (often exceeding **10,000 °C** in the plasma core), which is more than sufficient to melt almost all commercial engineering metals and alloys.

The heat of the arc melts the base metal along with the filler metal (electrode), forming a molten weld pool that solidifies to produce a strong metallurgical joint. According to the **KTU 2024 GDEST203 syllabus**, arc welding is classified as one of the most widely used **permanent joining processes** in fabrication industries, shipbuilding, structural construction, and pressure vessel manufacturing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition to Memorize:** "Arc welding is a fusion welding process that uses the heat of an electric arc, established between an electrode and the workpiece, to melt and join metals." — Exact phrasing expected in 3-mark and 7-mark questions.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are holding a small, controlled **lightning bolt** (the electric arc) between a metal rod (the electrode) and two steel plates you want to join. The lightning is so hot it instantly melts the rod tip and the surface of the plates into a glowing pool of liquid metal. As you slowly move the "lightning" along the joint, the liquid metal cools and freezes — permanently gluing the plates together like a metallic weld bead of solid metal.

In real life, you cannot hold lightning, so we use:
- An **electrode holder (stinger)** to safely grip the electrode
- A **welding transformer/power source** to generate the high current
- A **shielding gas or flux** to protect the molten metal from atmospheric contamination (oxygen and nitrogen, which make welds brittle)

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Misconception:** Many students think the *electrode itself* is hot and melts the metal. In reality, the **electric arc** (a sustained electrical discharge through ionized gas plasma) is the heat source — the electrode only melts because of this arc.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Operating Parameters

| Parameter | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Arc Temperature** | 6,000 – 10,000 | °C |
| **Welding Current (I)** | 50 – 500 | Amperes |
| **Arc Voltage (V)** | 18 – 35 | Volts |
| **Open Circuit Voltage (OCV)** | 50 – 90 | Volts |
| **Travel Speed (S)** | 100 – 500 | mm/min |
| **Electrode Diameter** | 2.5 – 6.0 | mm |
| **Weld Pool Temperature** | ~1,500 – 1,700 | °C (steel) |
| **Arc Efficiency (η)** | 60 – 90 | % |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** KTU examiners love asking about **Open Circuit Voltage (OCV)** vs. **Arc Voltage**. OCV is the voltage when the arc is *not* struck (machine on but no welding), and arc voltage is when welding is in progress. OCV > Arc Voltage always.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Working Principle

The process of arc welding is governed by four simultaneous physical phenomena, all of which must be controlled for a sound weld:

1. **Ionization of the Gas Gap:** When the electrode is brought close to the workpiece (gap ≈ 1.5 – 4 mm), a high OCV breaks down the air molecules into positively charged ions and free electrons, creating a conductive **plasma channel**.

2. **Arc Establishment:** Current flows through this ionized path. Resistance heating vaporizes the electrode tip, and the resulting metal vapor sustains the arc. The arc column behaves like a resistor — its voltage drop is called **arc voltage**.

3. **Metal Transfer:** Molten metal droplets transfer from the electrode tip to the weld pool via:
   - **Short-circuit transfer** (low voltage, small drops, GMAW)
   - **Globular transfer** (larger drops, gravity-assisted)
   - **Spray transfer** (fine drops, high current GMAW)

4. **Solidification & Shielding:** The molten weld pool cools and solidifies. A shielding medium (flux coating, inert gas, or slag) prevents atmospheric gases from contaminating the weld.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why shielding is critical:** Atmospheric oxygen reacts with molten steel to form **iron oxide (FeO)**, which causes porosity and embrittlement. Nitrogen causes nitride formation, leading to cracking. The shield (flux/gas) dissolves these impurities and forms a protective slag layer.

## 2.2 Essential Equipment for Arc Welding

| S.No | Equipment | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Welding Power Source** | Provides controlled current & voltage (Transformer, Rectifier, or Inverter) |
| 2 | **Electrode Holder (Stinger)** | Holds electrode and conducts current to it safely |
| 3 | **Electrode** | Acts as filler metal and one terminal of the arc |
| 4 | **Work Clamp & Cable** | Completes the electrical circuit back to the power source |
| 5 | **Chipping Hammer** | Removes solidified slag after each weld pass |
| 6 | **Wire Brush** | Cleans the weld bead of residual slag and spatter |
| 7 | **Shielding Gas Cylinder** (for MIG/TIG) | Supplies Argon, CO₂, or Argon-CO₂ mix |
| 8 | **Gas Regulator & Flowmeter** | Controls shielding gas flow rate (typically 10–20 L/min) |
| 9 | **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** | Helmet with auto-darkening filter, gloves, apron, safety boots |

## 2.3 Classification of Arc Welding Processes

The **KTU 2024 syllabus** emphasizes the following major arc welding variants:

### A. Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) – "Stick Welding"
- Uses a **flux-coated consumable electrode**.
- The flux coating decomposes to generate a **shielding gas + molten slag** that protects the weld.
- **Most widely used** in field construction, pipelines, and repair work.
- Electrodes classified as per **AWS A5.1** (e.g., E6010, E7018).

### B. Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW) – "MIG/MAG Welding"
- Uses a **continuous solid wire electrode** fed automatically through a welding gun.
- External shielding gas: **Argon + CO₂ (MAG)** or pure **Argon (MIG)**.
- High productivity, used in automotive and thin-sheet fabrication.

### C. Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW) – "TIG Welding"
- Uses a **non-consumable tungsten electrode** and a separate filler rod (if required).
- Shielding gas is **pure Argon** or Argon-Helium mix.
- Produces the **highest quality welds** on stainless steel, aluminum, and exotic alloys.
- Used in aerospace, nuclear, and pharmaceutical industries.

### D. Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)
- Arc is **completely buried under a granular flux bed**, producing no visible arc, spatter, or smoke.
- Very high deposition rates; used for **thick plates** in shipbuilding and pressure vessels.

### E. Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)
- A constricted arc is produced by forcing plasma gas through a fine nozzle.
- Higher energy density than TIG; used in precision tube welding and electronics.

### F. Flux-Cored Arc Welding (FCAW)
- Uses a **tubular electrode filled with flux**; can be self-shielded or gas-shielded.
- Combines advantages of SMAW and GMAW; popular in structural steel erection.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield for Numerical Problems)

| S.No | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Heat Input (H)** | $$H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$$ | kJ/mm |
| 2 | **Arc Power (P)** | $P = V \cdot I$ | Watts (W) |
| 3 | **Deposition Rate (DR)** | $DR = \dfrac{I \cdot \text{deposition factor}}{1000}$ | kg/hr |
| 4 | **Welding Cost per metre** | $C = \text{Cost of electrode} + \text{Cost of electricity} + \text{Labour}$ | ₹/m |
| 5 | **Ohm's Law (Arc Resistance)** | $V = I \cdot R_{arc}$ | V |
| 6 | **Travel Speed Conversion** | $S \ (\text{mm/min}) = \dfrac{L \ (\text{mm})}{t \ (\text{min})}$ | mm/min |
| 7 | **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) Width (empirical)** | $\text{HAZ} \propto \dfrac{H}{k \cdot \rho \cdot c}$ | mm |

> Where:
> - $V$ = Arc Voltage (V), $I$ = Welding Current (A), $S$ = Travel Speed (mm/min)
> - $\eta$ = Arc Efficiency (0.6 to 0.9, dimensionless)
> - $k$ = Thermal conductivity, $\rho$ = Density, $c$ = Specific heat of base metal

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In the heat input formula, students often forget the **multiplication by 60** (to convert minutes to seconds) and the **division by 1000** (to convert Joules to kilojoules). Missing either gives a wrong answer worth **3–5 marks** lost.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Construction:** Welding of structural I-beams, trusses, and reinforcing bars.
- **Shipbuilding:** Joining massive steel plates using SAW and GMAW.
- **Automotive:** Robotic MIG welding of car body panels.
- **Aerospace:** TIG welding of titanium and Inconel turbine components.
- **Nuclear & Power:** GTAW for stainless steel piping and reactor vessels.
- **Civil Engineering:** Field welding of pipelines using cellulosic electrodes (E6010).
- **Repair & Maintenance:** Stick welding (SMAW) is the universal repair tool.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Heat Input Formula

The **Heat Input (H)** is one of the most important parameters in arc welding, as it directly controls the weld bead size, penetration depth, and the width of the Heat Affected Zone (HAZ). Excess heat input causes distortion, while insufficient heat causes lack of fusion.

### Step 1: Define the Total Heat Energy Generated by the Arc

The arc acts as a resistor. The power dissipated in the arc is given by the standard electrical power equation:

$$P = V \cdot I$$

where $P$ is the arc power in Watts (W), $V$ is the arc voltage in Volts (V), and $I$ is the welding current in Amperes (A). Since 1 Watt = 1 Joule per second, the total heat energy generated in $t$ seconds is:

$$E_{total} = P \cdot t = V \cdot I \cdot t \quad [\text{in Joules}]$$

### Step 2: Account for Arc Efficiency ($\eta$)

Not all of this generated heat reaches the workpiece. Some is lost to **radiation**, **convection**, **sputtering of electrode droplets**, and **conduction into the electrode holder**. The fraction that actually enters the workpiece is the **arc efficiency** $\eta$ (a dimensionless number between 0.6 and 0.9).

$$E_{effective} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t \quad [\text{in Joules}]$$

### Step 3: Convert Time to Travel Speed

In practical welding, time $t$ is related to the travel speed $S$ and the weld length $L$ by:

$$t = \dfrac{L}{S}$$

However, since we want heat per unit length (kJ per mm), we substitute $L = 1$ mm into the equation (this normalizes the heat to a unit weld length). Also, since $S$ is conventionally given in mm/min, we must convert minutes to seconds by multiplying by 60.

$$E_{effective} \ \text{per mm} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \quad [\text{in kJ/mm}]$$

> Note: We divide by 1000 to convert Joules → kiloJoules (kJ).

### Final Compact Formula

$$\boxed{\,H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \quad \left[ \dfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}} \right]\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU Numerical Problems:** Typical values of $\eta$ are **0.70 for SAW**, **0.80 for GMAW**, and **0.85 for SMAW**. Use these unless specified otherwise.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Style)

### **Problem:**
A steel plate is welded using the SMAW process with a welding current of **200 A**, arc voltage of **25 V**, and travel speed of **300 mm/min**. Assuming arc efficiency $\eta = 0.85$, calculate the **heat input per unit length** in kJ/mm.

### **Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Given:**
- $V = 25$ V
- $I = 200$ A
- $S = 300$ mm/min
- $\eta = 0.85$

**Step 1: Substitute values into the heat input formula**

$$H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$$

**Step 2: Multiply numerator terms**

$$H = \dfrac{0.85 \times 25 \times 200 \times 60}{300 \times 1000}$$

**Step 3: Evaluate numerator**

$$\text{Numerator} = 0.85 \times 25 \times 200 \times 60$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 0.85 \times 25 = 21.25$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 21.25 \times 200 = 4{,}250$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 4{,}250 \times 60 = 255{,}000$$

**Step 4: Evaluate denominator**

$$\text{Denominator} = 300 \times 1000 = 300{,}000$$

**Step 5: Final division**

$$H = \dfrac{255{,}000}{300{,}000} = 0.85 \ \dfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}}$$

### **Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{H = 0.85 \ \dfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}} = 850 \ \dfrac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Excess Heat Input Alert:** If $H > 1.5$ kJ/mm for mild steel, the HAZ becomes excessively wide, and there is a high risk of **weld distortion** and **toughness loss**. KTU may ask: "If the answer is too high, what parameter should be increased to reduce it?" → Answer: **Increase travel speed S** or **reduce current I**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: SMAW vs GMAW vs GTAW vs SAW

| Parameter | SMAW (Stick) | GMAW (MIG) | GTAW (TIG) | SAW |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Electrode Type** | Flux-coated consumable | Continuous solid wire | Non-consumable Tungsten | Continuous wire under flux |
| **Shielding** | Flux decomposition | External gas (Ar/CO₂) | External gas (Pure Ar) | Granular flux bed |
| **Current Range (A)** | 50 – 400 | 100 – 600 | 50 – 350 | 200 – 1500 |
| **Arc Efficiency ($\eta$)** | 0.85 | 0.80 | 0.60 | 0.70 |
| **Deposition Rate** | Low (1–3 kg/hr) | High (3–8 kg/hr) | Very Low (0.5–2 kg/hr) | Very High (5–25 kg/hr) |
| **Weld Quality** | Good | Very Good | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Operator Skill** | Moderate | Moderate | Very High | High (automated) |
| **Best For** | Field repair, thick steel | Production, thin sheets | Stainless, aluminum, exotic alloys | Thick plates, shipbuilding |
| **Slag Removal** | Required (chipping) | Not required | Not required | Self-removing |
| **Equipment Cost** | Low | Medium | High | High (automated) |
| **Outdoor Usable?** | Yes (excellent) | Poor (wind disrupts gas) | Poor | Excellent (no visible arc) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Hack:** When asked "Which arc welding process is best for outdoor work?" → Answer is **SMAW or SAW** (because their shielding comes from flux, not external gas that wind can blow away). GMAW and GTAW are strictly **indoor / wind-shielded** processes.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics of Arc Welding

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Basic Arc Welding Setup (SMAW)

This Mermaid block diagram shows the functional architecture and electrical current flow of a typical manual arc welding circuit.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC Mains Supply 230V / 415V] --> B[Welding Transformer / Rectifier]
    B --> C[Output Terminals: Positive and Negative]
    C -- Positive Cable --> D[Electrode Holder Stinger]
    D --> E[Consumable Flux Coated Electrode]
    E -- Electric Arc 6000 C --> F[Workpiece Base Metal]
    F -- Work Clamp Cable --> C
    C -- Negative Return --> B
    B --> G[Slag Formation on Weld Bead]
    G --> H[Chipping Hammer Removes Slag]
    F --> I[Solidified Weld Joint]

    step1[Step 1: Power Source Energized]:::noteStyle
    step2[Step 2: OCV Strikes the Arc]:::noteStyle
    step3[Step 3: Arc Melts Electrode and Base Metal]:::noteStyle
    step4[Step 4: Metal Solidifies Forming Joint]:::noteStyle
```

## 4.2 Process Classification Flowchart (KTU Syllabus Mapping)

This diagram maps out the complete family of arc welding processes covered in the KTU 2024 GDEST203 Module 2 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Arc Welding Family] --> B[Consumable Electrode Processes]
    A --> C[Non Consumable Electrode Processes]

    B --> D1[SMAW Shielded Metal Arc]
    B --> D2[GMAW Gas Metal Arc MIG MAG]
    B --> D3[SAW Submerged Arc]
    B --> D4[FCAW Flux Cored Arc]

    C --> E1[GTAW Gas Tungsten Arc TIG]
    C --> E2[PAW Plasma Arc]
    C --> E3[CAW Carbon Arc Obsolete]

    D1 --> F1[Flux Coating Shielding]
    D2 --> F2[External Gas Shielding]
    D3 --> F3[Buried Under Flux]
    D4 --> F4[Tubular Flux Filled Wire]
    E1 --> F5[Pure Argon Shielding]
    E2 --> F6[Constricted Plasma Jet]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Arc Welding Joint Geometry & Weld Zone Anatomy

This diagram illustrates the cross-sectional anatomy of a butt-welded joint, showing the key terminology tested in KTU exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Base Metal Plate 1] --> B[Root Gap and Root Face]
    A2[Base Metal Plate 2] --> B
    B --> C[Weld Pool Molten Metal]
    C --> D[Arc Column]
    D --> E[Electrode Tip]
    D --> F[Shielding Gas Cloud or Flux Cover]
    C --> G[Solidified Weld Bead]
    G --> H[Heat Affected Zone HAZ]
    H --> I[Unaffected Parent Metal]
    G --> J[Reinforcement Crown]
    G --> K[Slag Layer on Top]

    zone1[Zone 1 Fusion Zone]:::zoneStyle
    zone2[Zone 2 HAZ]:::zoneStyle
    zone3[Zone 3 Parent Metal]:::zoneStyle
```

## 4.4 Functional Block Diagram: SMAW Operation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Set Current on Power Source] --> P2[Connect Work Clamp to Workpiece]
    P2 --> P3[Insert Electrode in Holder]
    P3 --> P4[Strike Arc by Scratch or Tapping]
    P4 --> P5[Maintain Arc Length 2 to 4 mm]
    P5 --> P6[Travel Along Joint at Uniform Speed]
    P6 --> P7[Monitor Weld Pool and Slag]
    P7 --> P8[Strike New Electrode When Consumed]
    P8 --> P9[Remove Slag with Chipping Hammer]
    P9 --> P10[Inspect Weld Visually]

    classDef processStyle fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5,P6,P7,P8,P9,P10 processStyle
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Mermaid cannot directly render a physical arc/torch cross-section, so the diagrams above map the **functional relationships** between components — power source → electrode → arc → weld pool → solidified joint. This is the standard KTU-recommended way to represent welding systems in schematic form.
