# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define arc welding. Mention any four advantages of arc welding over gas welding.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Arc welding is a fusion welding process in which the heat required for joining metals is obtained from an electric arc struck between an electrode and the workpiece.

**Four Advantages over Gas Welding:**

1. **Higher temperature:** The arc temperature (6,000–10,000 °C) is much higher than the oxy-acetylene flame (3,200 °C), enabling faster welding and joining of thick sections.

2. **Faster travel speed:** Arc welding can deposit metal at 1–25 kg/hr, far exceeding gas welding (0.5–1 kg/hr).

3. **Better mechanical properties:** Arc welds have superior strength and ductility due to deeper penetration and finer grain structure.

4. **Wider material range:** Suitable for ferrous, non-ferrous, and exotic alloys (stainless steel, aluminum, titanium), whereas gas welding is limited mainly to mild steel and copper.

> **Marks Distribution:** Definition (1) + 4 advantages × 0.5 (2) = 3 marks

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is the function of flux coating in a shielded metal arc welding (SMAW) electrode?**

**Model Answer:**

The flux coating on an SMAW electrode performs **four critical functions** during welding:

1. **Shielding gas generation:** The flux decomposes in the arc heat to release a protective cloud of CO₂ and other gases that displace atmospheric oxygen and nitrogen from the weld zone.

2. **Slag formation:** The flux melts and forms a molten slag layer that floats on top of the weld pool, protecting it from atmospheric contamination and slowing the cooling rate (improving bead shape).

3. **Arc stabilization:** Certain flux ingredients (e.g., potassium and sodium compounds) ionize easily, making the arc smoother and easier to maintain.

4. **Alloying and deoxidation:** The flux adds beneficial alloying elements (Mn, Si) to the weld metal and contains deoxidizers (Fe-Mn, Fe-Si) that remove impurities.

> **Marks Distribution:** 4 functions × 0.75 = 3 marks

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks – Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Rule:** Part B Module 2 questions carry **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) for **7 marks** and (b) for **7 marks**. Two alternative questions are provided; answer ANY ONE.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) With the help of a neat sketch, describe the working principle of Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) process. List any four advantages and two limitations of SMAW. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle of SMAW:**

In SMAW (also called stick welding or manual metal arc welding), an electric arc is struck between a **flux-coated consumable electrode** and the workpiece. The arc melts both the electrode tip and the base metal, forming a molten weld pool. As the electrode melts, droplets transfer across the arc into the pool. Simultaneously, the flux coating decomposes to release shielding gas and forms a slag layer that protects the solidifying weld. The welder manually moves the electrode holder along the joint at a uniform travel speed.

**Functional Sequence:**
1. Power source is set to the required current (50–400 A).
2. The electrode is clamped in the electrode holder.
3. The electrode tip is touched to the workpiece and withdrawn slightly (1.5–4 mm gap) to strike the arc.
4. The arc melts the electrode and base metal → weld pool forms.
5. The flux decomposes → shielding gas + slag protect the pool.
6. The welder moves along the joint at uniform speed.
7. When the electrode is consumed, the slag is chipped off and a new electrode is struck.

**Four Advantages of SMAW:**
1. Low equipment cost and highly portable — ideal for field work.
2. Works in outdoor/windy conditions (shielding comes from flux, not external gas).
3. Versatile — can weld most ferrous metals and many non-ferrous alloys.
4. Simple to learn and well-suited for repair and maintenance work.

**Two Limitations of SMAW:**
1. Low deposition rate (1–3 kg/hr) compared to GMAW or SAW.
2. Slag must be chipped off between passes, reducing productivity.
3. (Optional third) Not suitable for reactive metals like titanium.

**Sketch:** Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram of the SMAW setup.

> **Marks Distribution:** Working principle (3) + Sketch (2) + Advantages (1) + Limitations (1) = 7 marks

---

**(b) Explain the Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW / TIG) process with a neat sketch. State its four main applications and two disadvantages. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**GTAW / TIG Welding:**

Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW), commonly called **TIG (Tungsten Inert Gas) welding**, uses a **non-consumable tungsten electrode** held in a special torch. The arc is struck between the tungsten electrode and the workpiece. A separate filler rod (if needed) is fed manually into the weld pool by the welder's other hand. The arc zone, weld pool, and hot tungsten tip are protected from atmospheric contamination by an inert shielding gas (typically **pure Argon**, sometimes Argon-Helium mixtures) flowing through the torch nozzle.

**Working Steps:**
1. Pure Argon flows through the torch nozzle at 8–15 L/min.
2. The tungsten electrode (negative polarity) strikes an arc on the workpiece.
3. The arc melts the base metal (and filler rod, if added) to form a weld pool.
4. The inert gas envelope prevents oxidation of the hot metal.
5. The welder moves the torch along the joint with precise control.

**Four Applications of GTAW:**
1. Welding of **stainless steel** pipes and tanks in the food, dairy, and pharmaceutical industries.
2. Fabrication of **aerospace components** using aluminum, titanium, and Inconel alloys.
3. Precision welding of **thin sheets** (0.5–3 mm) in instrumentation and electronics.
4. Nuclear reactor component fabrication and high-purity pipe welding.

**Two Disadvantages of GTAW:**
1. Very low deposition rate (0.5–2 kg/hr) — slow and expensive.
2. Requires high operator skill (two-handed coordination: one for torch, one for filler).
3. (Optional) Not suitable for outdoor windy conditions (inert gas is easily disturbed).

**Sketch:** Functional flow — Argon cylinder → Regulator → Torch → Tungsten electrode → Arc → Weld pool.

> **Marks Distribution:** Working principle (3) + Sketch (2) + Applications (1) + Disadvantages (1) = 7 marks

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a) Describe the Submerged Arc Welding (SAW) process with a neat sketch. List any three advantages of SAW over SMAW. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Submerged Arc Welding (SAW):**

In SAW, a continuous bare wire electrode is fed automatically through a welding head into the joint. Ahead of the weld, a layer of **granular flux** is deposited on the workpiece. The arc is **struck beneath this flux blanket** — so no arc, spatter, smoke, or UV radiation is visible from outside. The flux melts partially to form a protective molten slag + shielding gas envelope around the arc, while un-melted flux can be recovered and reused.

**Working Steps:**
1. Flux is poured from a hopper onto the joint ahead of the torch.
2. The wire electrode is fed continuously through the flux bed.
3. Current flows from the contact tip to the wire → arc is struck under the flux.
4. The arc melts the wire, the base metal, and the surrounding flux.
5. Molten flux forms a slag that covers the weld bead; un-melted flux is vacuum-recovered.
6. SAW is usually **fully mechanized or automated** for high productivity.

**Three Advantages of SAW over SMAW:**
1. **Very high deposition rate (5–25 kg/hr)** — much higher than SMAW (1–3 kg/hr).
2. **No visible arc, spatter, or fumes** — improves operator safety and comfort.
3. **Excellent weld quality** with deep penetration and uniform beads, suitable for thick plates.
4. (Optional bonus) Flux is recovered and reused, reducing consumable cost.

**Sketch:** Power source → Wire feeder → Welding head → Granular flux blanket covering the arc → Weld pool under the flux.

> **Marks Distribution:** Description (3) + Sketch (2) + Advantages (2) = 7 marks

---

**(b) A steel plate is welded using GMAW with current 250 A, voltage 28 V, and travel speed 400 mm/min. If the arc efficiency is 0.80, calculate the heat input in kJ/mm. Also comment on whether the heat input is acceptable for mild steel. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Welding Current, $I = 250$ A
- Arc Voltage, $V = 28$ V
- Travel Speed, $S = 400$ mm/min
- Arc Efficiency, $\eta = 0.80$

**Step 1: Write down the heat input formula**

$$H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the given values**

$$H = \dfrac{0.80 \times 28 \times 250 \times 60}{400 \times 1000}$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the numerator step by step**

$$0.80 \times 28 = 22.4$$

$$22.4 \times 250 = 5{,}600$$

$$5{,}600 \times 60 = 336{,}000$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the denominator**

$$400 \times 1000 = 400{,}000$$

**Step 5: Final calculation**

$$H = \dfrac{336{,}000}{400{,}000} = 0.84 \ \dfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{H = 0.84 \ \dfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}} = 840 \ \dfrac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}}}$$

**Comment on Acceptability:**

The recommended heat input range for **mild steel** is **0.8 to 1.5 kJ/mm**. Since $H = 0.84$ kJ/mm falls within this range, the heat input is **acceptable** for welding mild steel. The weld will have:
- Adequate penetration
- No excessive HAZ width
- Good mechanical properties
- Minimal distortion risk

If $H$ had exceeded 1.5 kJ/mm, the welder would have been advised to **increase travel speed** or **reduce current** to prevent overheating.

> **Marks Distribution:** Formula (1) + Substitution (1) + Step-by-step calculation (3) + Final answer (1) + Comment (1) = 7 marks

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Heat Input Formula:** Forgetting to multiply by **60** (sec → min conversion) or divide by **1000** (J → kJ conversion) — loses **2 marks** instantly.
>
> 2. **Mixing Up OCV and Arc Voltage:** Examiners deliberately swap these in numerical problems. OCV is **always higher** than arc voltage. SMAW OCV ≈ 50–80 V; arc voltage ≈ 18–25 V.
>
> 3. **Confusing MIG with TIG:** MIG = consumable wire + external gas. TIG = non-consumable tungsten + external gas + separate filler rod. Students often mix up which process uses which.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to Draw the Sketch:** KTU rules state that a 7-mark question **without a sketch** gets a maximum of **5 marks**. Always include a functional block diagram or labeled sketch.
>
> 5. **Writing "Stick Welding" without expanding SMAW:** Examiners deduct **0.5 marks** for not using the full technical name. Always write: "Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)".
>
> 6. **Saying "Flux protects weld" without specifying HOW:** Be specific — flux decomposes to release **shielding gas** AND forms a **molten slag** layer. Both are required.
>
> 7. **Numerical Errors:** In heat input problems, write units at every step (A, V, mm/min, kJ/mm). Skipping units loses **1 mark** even if the number is correct.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Pin This to Your Wall!**

- ✅ **Arc Welding Definition:** Fusion welding using heat of an electric arc between an electrode and workpiece.

- ✅ **Arc Temperature:** 6,000–10,000 °C (more than 2× hotter than oxy-acetylene).

- ✅ **Key Processes (must-know):** SMAW, GMAW/MIG, GTAW/TIG, SAW, FCAW, PAW.

- ✅ **SMAW:** Flux-coated stick electrode — outdoor friendly, slag removal required.

- ✅ **GMAW/MIG:** Continuous wire + external shielding gas — indoor, high productivity.

- ✅ **GTAW/TIG:** Non-consumable tungsten + pure Argon — highest quality, aerospace.

- ✅ **SAW:** Arc buried under granular flux — highest deposition rate, thick plates, no UV.

- ✅ **Heat Input Formula (MUST memorize):**
  $$H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \quad [\text{kJ/mm}]$$

- ✅ **Typical Arc Efficiency Values:** SMAW = 0.85, GMAW = 0.80, SAW = 0.70, GTAW = 0.60.

- ✅ **OCV vs. Arc Voltage:** OCV > Arc Voltage. OCV used to *strike* the arc; arc voltage is the *operating* voltage.

- ✅ **Flux Functions (4):** Shielding gas, slag formation, arc stabilization, deoxidation/alloying.

- ✅ **Shielding Gases:** Argon (TIG, MIG of Al/Cu), CO₂ (MIG of steel), Ar + CO₂ mix (MAG).

- ✅ **Common Defects to Know:** Porosity (gas entrapment), undercut (excess current), lack of fusion (low heat), slag inclusion (poor cleaning), cracks (high H).

- ✅ **Acceptable Heat Input for Mild Steel:** 0.8 to 1.5 kJ/mm.

- ✅ **Equipment Checklist:** Power source + Electrode holder + Work clamp + Cables + Electrode + Flux/Gas + PPE.

- ✅ **Always draw a labeled sketch or block diagram** in 7-mark and 14-mark answers — it is **mandatory** for full marks.

- ✅ **Safety First:** Always wear an **auto-darkening welding helmet** (shade #10–13), flame-resistant gloves, leather apron, and safety boots.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SMAW
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GDEST203 - BASIC MECHANICAL AND CIVIL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** SMAW # SMAW — Shielded Metal Arc Welding ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)** — also historically referred to as **Manual Metal Arc Welding (MMAW)** or colloquially known as **"Stick Welding"** — is a manual arc welding process in which coalescence (joining) of metals is produced by heating the workpiece with an electric arc established between a **flux-coated consumable electrode** and the base metal. The electrode serves a dual purpose: it acts as one of the electrodes for the arc and simultaneously supplies filler metal to the weld pool. The flux coating, upon decomposition, generates a **shielding gas atmosphere** and forms a **molten slag** that protects the weld pool from atmospheric contamination (oxygen, nitrogen, hydrogen). > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Verbatim)**: SMAW is an *arc welding process* that uses a *manually operated, consumable electrode* covered with a *flux coating*. The heat of the arc melts both the electrode tip and the base metal, producing a weld joint. ### 1.2 AWS Standard Designation In American Welding Society (AWS) terminology, SMAW is given the standardized process designation: $$\text{AWS Process Number} = \textbf{1}$$ The digit **"1"** universally represents Shielded Metal Arc Welding in any weld procedure specification (WPS) documentation used in industry. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!TIP] > **Real-world Analogy — "The Pencil Sharpener of Joining"** > > Imagine you are trying to join two metal plates. In SMAW, you hold a *pencil-like rod* (the stick electrode) in a holder. When you touch the tip of this rod to the metal surface and then slightly lift it, a brilliant electric spark (the **arc**) is created — this is similar to how a spark lighter works, except temperatures exceed **6,000 °C to 7,000 °C** at the arc core. > > The pencil "lead" (the inner metal core wire) literally *melts* and drips into the joint — it is being *consumed* just like a candle burns down. Around the electrode is a special coating (the **flux**) — think of it as the candle's wax — that vaporizes and forms a protective "umbrella" of gas, plus a thin layer of molten rock (the **slag**) that floats on top of the liquid weld metal like ice on water, shielding it from the air. > > Once the metal cools, you chip off the brittle slag crust, leaving behind a solid metallic joint. ### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics Used in SMAW | Parameter | Typical Range / Value | |---|---| | Arc Temperature | **6,000 °C – 7,000 °C** at the core | | Open Circuit Voltage (OCV) | **50 V – 90 V** (AC) / **60 V – 80 V** (DC) | | Arc Voltage (working) | **20 V – 30 V** | | Current Range | **30 A – 600 A** (depending on electrode size) | | Electrode Diameter | **1.6 mm – 12 mm** (most common: 2.5, 3.15, 4, 5 mm) | | Power Source Type | AC Transformer or DC Rectifier | | Polarity Types | DCEN (Direct Current Electrode Negative) / DCEP (Direct Current Electrode Positive) / AC | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner Watch-Point:** Always remember that **SMAW is a *manual* process**. If a question describes an *automatic* or *semi-automatic* stick electrode process with continuous wire feed, it is **NOT** SMAW — it is likely **SAW (Submerged Arc Welding)** or **FCAW (Flux-Cored Arc Welding)**. ### 1.5 Why SMAW is Taught First in Engineering Curricula SMAW is universally chosen as the *introductory welding process* in nearly all engineering institutions (including KTU's GDEST203) for three pedagogical reasons: 1. **Equipment Simplicity** — The setup requires only a power source, electrode holder, and consumable stick — making it ideal for laboratory demonstrations. 2. **Portability** — The system is lightweight, requires no shielding gas cylinder, and is the *most commonly used* field-welding process globally (pipelines, shipyards, construction sites). 3. **Universal Material Compatibility** — Can weld carbon steel, low-alloy steel, stainless steel, cast iron, and even non-ferrous metals with the right electrode choice. # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2. Working Principle — The Physics of the Arc ### 2.1 Electrical Circuit Formation The SMAW circuit is a *low-voltage, high-current* closed electrical loop. The fundamental components form a series circuit: $$\text{Power Source} \rightarrow \text{Electrode Cable} \rightarrow \text{Electrode Holder} \rightarrow \text{Electrode Tip} \rightarrow \text{ARC GAP} \rightarrow \text{Workpiece} \rightarrow \text{Ground Cable} \rightarrow \text{Back to Power Source}$$ The "arc" is essentially a **plasma column** formed when the high electric field ionizes the air gap between the electrode tip and the workpiece. The breakdown voltage of the air gap is approximately **30 V per centimeter**, but once ionized, the plasma becomes highly conductive and the voltage drops to the working arc voltage. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Sequence > [!NOTE] > **The "Strike-Arc-Advance-Cool-Slag" Five-Stage Cycle** — This is the exact sequence KTU examiners expect in a 7-mark descriptive answer. 1. **STRIKING the Arc** — The welder drags or scratches the electrode tip against the workpiece momentarily to create a short circuit. Upon slight withdrawal (≈ 2–4 mm gap), the arc is initiated. 2. **Arc Stabilization** — The arc reaches a steady length; a characteristic *crackling/hissing sound* (DC) or *buzzing* (AC) is heard. The arc voltage stabilizes around **20–30 V**. 3. **Metal Transfer** — Droplets of molten metal from the electrode tip are transferred across the arc into the weld pool. Three transfer modes exist in SMAW: *globular*, *spray*, and *short-circuit* (though globular dominates in stick welding). 4. **Weld Pool Solidification** — As the welder moves the electrode along the joint at a controlled **travel speed**, the molten pool solidifies behind the arc, forming the weld bead. 5. **Slag Formation & Removal** — The flux coating melts and floats as a protective slag. After cooling, this slag is **chipped off with a chipping hammer** and wire-brushed away to inspect the weld. ### 2.3 Functions of the Flux Coating (The "Heart" of SMAW) The flux coating is *not decorative* — it performs **at least five critical functions** simultaneously: | # | Function | Mechanism | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Shielding Gas Generation** | Flux decomposes → releases CO₂, H₂, H₂O vapor → displaces atmospheric N₂ and O₂ | | 2 | **Slag Formation** | Flux oxides (SiO₂, TiO₂, Al₂O₃) melt and float over the weld pool → block oxygen ingress | | 3 | **Arc Stabilization** | Certain flux ingredients (e.g., **potassium titanate, feldspar**) ionize easily → stabilize arc column | | 4 | **Alloying Element Addition** | Powdered Fe, Mn, Cr may be added in flux → alloy the weld metal | | 5 | **Weld Pool Cleaning** | Flux oxides (FeO, MnO) react with impurities → form slag → floats away | ### 2.4 Power Sources — The Two Options > [!IMPORTANT] > **DC vs AC in SMAW — A KTU Favourite Question** **A. DC Power Source (DCEN / DCEP)** - **DCEN — Direct Current Electrode Negative** (Electrode is Cathode). Produces *deep penetration* (~ 70% electrode heat goes into workpiece). Used for: thin plates, hard-facing, high-alloy electrodes. - **DCEP — Direct Current Electrode Positive** (Electrode is Anode). Produces *shallow penetration, fast melt-off*. Used for: low-hydrogen electrodes, surfacing. **B. AC Power Source (Alternating Current)** - Frequency = 50 Hz (India) or 60 Hz. Current alternates direction 100/120 times per second. - **No fixed polarity** — arc is re-ignited every half-cycle. - Used for: **general-purpose work**, where arc blow is a problem, and where magnetized workpieces are encountered (e.g., in ship repair). ### 2.5 Electrode Classification (AWS E-System) — KTU Critical The American Welding Society (AWS) classifies stick electrodes using the **EXXXXX** code. This is *high-yield* KTU content. $$\text{Format: } \textbf{E} \, \underbrace{\text{XX}}_{\text{Tensile Strength (ksi)}} \, \underbrace{\text{X}}_{\text{Position}} \, \underbrace{\text{X}}_{\text{Current/Coating Type}} \, \underbrace{\text{XX}}_{\text{Optional — Impact/Weld Metal Hydrogen)}$$ **Example:** **E7018** - **E** = Electrode - **70** = Minimum tensile strength = **70,000 psi (70 ksi) ≈ 482 MPa** - **1** = Welding position: **1 = All positions** (flat, horizontal, vertical, overhead) - **8** = Current type + coating: **8 = DCEP, low-hydrogen (iron powder) coating** - *(no suffix)* = Standard hydrogen control ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Heat Input (per unit length) | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S}$ | J/mm or kJ/mm | $\eta$ = arc efficiency (≈ **0.7–0.85** for SMAW), V = arc voltage (V), I = current (A), S = travel speed (mm/s) | | Electrode Consumption | $m_e = \rho \cdot \pi \cdot \dfrac{d^2}{4} \cdot L$ | kg | $\rho$ = density of steel core wire (≈ 7.85 g/cm³), d = core wire dia, L = length consumed | | Deposition Rate | $D = \dfrac{m_e}{t}$ | kg/hr | Mass of weld metal deposited per hour | | Power Consumed | $P = V \cdot I$ | Watts (W) or kVA | — | | Energy Consumed | $E = P \cdot t$ | Joules (J) or kWh | For billing / cost calculation | | Penetration Depth (approx.) | $p \approx k \cdot \dfrac{I}{v}$ | mm | Empirical — depends on joint, position, polarity | | Voltage Drop (cable loss) | $V_{drop} = I \cdot R_{cable}$ | Volts | Cable resistance R (Ω) at operating length | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Most Tested Formula in KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 2)**: The **Heat Input Formula** $H = \dfrac{\eta V I}{S}$ — It is asked in nearly every past paper of GDEST203, often as a 3-mark numerical sub-part. ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Construction Industry**: SMAW is the *dominant* process for on-site structural welding of I-beams, girders, and bridges. - **Shipbuilding**: Hull plate joining (DC stick electrodes with cellulose coating). - **Pipeline Construction**: Cross-country oil/gas pipelines (often using *cellulosic E6010 / E7010* electrodes for downhill welding). - **Repair & Maintenance**: Almost all in-field repair jobs because no shielding gas cylinder is required. - **Heavy Equipment Manufacturing**: Cranes, mining dump trucks, earthmovers. - **Civil Infrastructure**: Reinforcement bar (rebar) splicing in reinforced concrete. # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3. Exhaustive Derivation of Heat Input (The KTU Favourite) ### 3.1 Defining the Problem In KTU exam problems, students are typically given the following data and asked to find **heat input per unit length of weld**: > **Given:** Arc Voltage = **25 V**, Welding Current = **200 A**, Travel Speed = **4 mm/sec**, Process Efficiency (η) = **0.80** (typical for SMAW). > **Find:** Heat input $H$ in J/mm. ### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Identify the governing equation** The heat input in arc welding is defined as the *arc energy* delivered to the workpiece per unit length of weld travel. Mathematically: $$H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S}$$ where: - $\eta$ = thermal efficiency of the process (dimensionless) - $V$ = arc voltage in volts - $I$ = welding current in amperes - $S$ = travel speed in mm/s (or mm/min — must be consistent with units of H) > **Textual Logic:** Why multiply by η? Because not all electrical power is converted into useful heat in the workpiece — some is lost as radiation, spatter, and convective losses from the weld pool. For SMAW, η lies in the range **0.7 to 0.85**, and **0.8** is a typical engineering value. **Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values** $$H = \frac{0.80 \times 25 \text{ V} \times 200 \text{ A}}{4 \text{ mm/s}}$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the numerator** $$0.80 \times 25 \times 200 = 0.80 \times 5{,}000 = 4{,}000 \text{ W}$$ **Step 4 — Divide by travel speed** $$H = \frac{4{,}000 \text{ W}}{4 \text{ mm/s}} = 1{,}000 \text{ J/mm}$$ **Step 5 — Convert to kJ/mm (standard reporting unit in welding codes)** $$H = 1{,}000 \text{ J/mm} = 1.0 \text{ kJ/mm}$$ > **Final Answer:** The heat input for this SMAW operation is **$H = 1.0 \text{ kJ/mm}$**. ### 3.3 Conversion Variants — Exam-Style Conversions In some KTU questions, the travel speed is given in **mm/min** (instead of mm/s). The formula is *identical* but the units of H change: $$H \text{ (J/mm)} = \frac{\eta V I}{S_{\text{mm/s}}} \quad ; \quad H \text{ (kJ/mm)} = \frac{\eta V I \times 60}{S_{\text{mm/min}} \times 1000}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Watch out:** A common student error is *forgetting to convert* the units consistently. If S is in mm/min, then numerator must yield J/min, and then you divide by 1,000 to get kJ/mm. Always do a unit check. ### 3.4 Derivation of Electrode Consumption Rate **Given:** E7018 electrode, core wire diameter = **4 mm**, density of steel = **7.85 g/cm³**, length of electrode consumed in 1 minute = **120 mm**. **Find:** Mass of electrode consumed per minute (g/min) and per hour (kg/hr). **Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of the core wire** $$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (4 \text{ mm})^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 16}{4} = 4\pi \text{ mm}^2 \approx 12.566 \text{ mm}^2$$ **Step 2 — Volume consumed in 1 minute** $$V = A \times L = 12.566 \text{ mm}^2 \times 120 \text{ mm} = 1{,}507.96 \text{ mm}^3$$ **Step 3 — Convert volume to cm³** $$V = 1{,}507.96 \text{ mm}^3 \times \frac{1 \text{ cm}^3}{1{,}000 \text{ mm}^3} = 1.50796 \text{ cm}^3$$ **Step 4 — Mass consumed in 1 minute** $$m = \rho \times V = 7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3 \times 1.50796 \text{ cm}^3 \approx 11.84 \text{ g/min}$$ **Step 5 — Mass per hour** $$m_{hr} = 11.84 \text{ g/min} \times 60 \text{ min/hr} = 710.4 \text{ g/hr} = 0.7104 \text{ kg/hr}$$ > **Final Answer:** Electrode consumption rate = **0.71 kg/hr** (approximately). ### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — SMAW vs Other Arc Processes > [!NOTE] > This matrix is *highly probable* to appear as a 7-mark "compare" question in KTU 2024 Scheme. | Parameter | SMAW | GMAW (MIG/MAG) | GTAW (TIG) | SAW | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Process No. (AWS)** | 1 | 131/135 (MIG) / 136 (Flux-core) | 141 | 121 | | **Electrode Type** | Stick (consumable) | Continuous wire (consumable) | Tungsten (non-consumable) | Continuous wire (consumable) | | **Shielding Source** | Flux coating (no cylinder) | External inert/active gas | External inert gas (Ar/He) | Granular flux bed | | **Skill Level Required** | Moderate–High | Low–Moderate | Very High | Low (mechanized) | | **Portability** | **Excellent** (highest) | Poor (gas cylinder needed) | Poor | Poor (flux hopper) | | **Welding Speed** | Slow | Fast | Very Slow | **Fastest** | | **Weld Quality** | Good | Very Good | **Excellent** | Excellent | | **Typical Current** | 30–600 A | 50–600 A | 5–500 A | 200–2000 A | | **Typical Voltage** | 20–30 V | 18–32 V | 10–20 V | 28–40 V | | **Outdoor Use** | **Yes (best for field)** | Poor (wind disrupts gas) | Poor | Excellent (submerged) | | **Suitability for Thick Plates** | Moderate | Good | Limited | **Excellent** | | **Cost of Equipment** | **Lowest** | Moderate | High | High | ### 3.6 Advantages, Limitations, and Applications (KTU-Relevant) #### Advantages of SMAW 1. **Equipment is simple, rugged, and cheap** — power source + holder + electrode is the entire setup. 2. **Highly portable** — no gas cylinders, no bulky wire feeders. 3. **Works in all welding positions** — flat, horizontal, vertical-up, vertical-down, overhead. 4. **Suitable for outdoor/windy conditions** — flux coating self-generates shielding gas. 5. **Wide range of metals and thicknesses** — from thin gauge (1.5 mm) to heavy plates (≥ 25 mm). 6. **Tolerant to dirty/rusty surfaces** — flux deoxidizes the weld pool. 7. **Easy to teach and learn** — the manual process builds foundational welding skill. #### Limitations of SMAW 1. **Lower deposition rate** compared to GMAW, FCAW, and SAW. 2. **Intermittent welding** — the operator must *stop and replace* the electrode every **300–450 mm** of burn-off. 3. **Frequent slag removal** required between passes. 4. **Lower duty cycle** — welder fatigue, heat exposure. 5. **Not suitable for highly reactive metals** (e.g., titanium, aluminium) — flux chemistry is incompatible. 6. **Lower precision** — manual nature makes automation difficult. #### Major Application Areas | Sector | Use Case | |---|---| | **Construction** | Structural steel, I-beams, columns, trusses | | **Shipbuilding** | Hull plates, deck plates | | **Oil & Gas** | Cross-country pipelines, field welding | | **Power Plants** | Boiler tubes, pressure vessel repair | | **Civil Works** | Rebar splicing, bridge girder joints | | **Repair Workshops** | General fabrication, farm equipment, automotive frames | # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 SMAW Setup — Physical Schematic > [!NOTE] > The diagram below illustrates the *physical arrangement* of the SMAW process. In a real laboratory, the welder would hold the electrode holder in one hand (with a welding helmet) and the ground clamp would be bolted to the workpiece. ``` +------------------------------------------------------------+ | SHIELDED METAL ARC WELDING (SMAW) | | Equipment Layout | +------------------------------------------------------------+ [POWER SOURCE] (AC Transformer or DC Rectifier) +-------------------+ | OCV: 50-80 V | | Current: Variable| +----+----+----------+ | | | | (Ground Cable) | | +-----------------------------------+ | | | | | | | v | | | +---------------+ | | | | WORKPIECE | | | | | (Base Metal) | | | | | | | | | | =^^^= | | | | | (weld bead) | | | | +---------------+ | | | | +--|----- GROUND CLAMP --------+ | | | (Electrode Cable) | | v +----------------+ | ELECTRODE | | HOLDER (Stinger)| +-------+--------+ | | (Welder grips here) | v [Stick Electrode] Flux Coating (outer) ============== <-- Core wire (inner metal rod) Flux Coating | | <-- Approx 2-4 mm arc gap v * ARC * <-- Electric arc (6000-7000 C) * * * * | v ~~~~~~~~ WELD POOL ~~~~~~ <-- Molten metal + molten slag ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ Shielding Gas Cloud: CO2, H2, H2O vapor Slag Layer: floats on top of weld pool ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow — Process Block Diagram > [!NOTE] > This Mermaid block diagram illustrates the *functional flow* of inputs, energy, and outputs in the SMAW process. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Power Source
AC or DC] --> B[Electrode Cable
Current Carrier] B --> C[Electrode Holder
Stinger] C --> D[Stick Electrode
Consumable Flux Coated] D --> E[ARC INITIATION
Ionized Plasma Column] E --> F[Heat Generation
6000-7000 degC] F --> G[Weld Pool Formation
Base Metal Melting] F --> H[Filler Metal Transfer
Electrode Consumed] D --> I[Flux Decomposition] I --> J[Shielding Gas
CO2 H2 H2O] I --> K[Slag Formation
Floats on Weld Pool] J --> G K --> L[Solidified Weld Metal
Bead Formation] G --> L H --> L L --> M[Slag Chip-off
Post-Weld Cleaning] L --> N[Final Weld Joint] A --> O[Ground Cable
Return Path] O --> P[Workpiece
Base Metal] P --> E ``` ## 4.3 Electrode Cross-Section Anatomy (Mermaid Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Stick Electrode
Cylindrical Rod] --> B[Outer Layer
Flux Coating] A --> C[Inner Core
Solid Metal Wire] B --> B1[Cellulose
Generates Gas] B --> B2[Titanium Dioxide
Arc Stabilizer] B --> B3[Iron Powder
Increases Deposition] B --> B4[Silicates
Slag Former] C --> C1[Low Carbon Steel
or Alloyed Core] C --> C2[Provides Filler Metal] C --> C3[Provides Carrying Current] ``` ## 4.4 Position Diagram — All Welding Positions (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[1G / 1F
Flat Position
Groove / Fillet] P2[2G / 2F
Horizontal Position] P3[3G / 3F
Vertical Position] P4[4G / 4F
Overhead Position] P5[5G
Multiple Position Pipe] P6[6G
Inclined Position Pipe] P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 P1 -.->|SMAW Suitable?| YES[YES - All Positions] P2 -.-> YES P3 -.-> YES P4 -.-> YES P5 -.-> YES P6 -.-> YES ``` ## 4.5 Decision Tree — Electrode Selection (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Start: Choose SMAW Electrode] --> Q1{Base Material?} Q1 -->|Carbon Steel| CS[Use E60xx or E70xx series] Q1 -->|Low Alloy Steel| LAS[Use E80xx E90xx E100xx series] Q1 -->|Stainless Steel| SS[Use E308 E316 E347 series] Q1 -->|Cast Iron| CI[Use ENi-CI or ESt series] CS --> Q2{Welding Position?} Q2 -->|All Positions| Q3{Service Conditions?} Q3 -->|General Purpose| E6013[E6013: Rutile Coating AC DC] Q3 -->|High Strength Low H2| E7018[E7018: Low Hydrogen DCEP] Q3 -->|Deep Penetration Pipe| E6010[E6010: Cellulosic DCEP] Q2 -->|Flat Horizontal Only| E7024[E7024: Iron Powder High Deposition] ``` # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW). Mention any two of its advantages. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW), also known as manual metal arc welding, is an arc welding process in which the heat for welding is produced by an electric arc struck between a **flux-coated consumable electrode** and the **workpiece**. The flux coating decomposes to produce a shielding gas and forms a slag that protects the weld pool from atmospheric contamination. **Two Advantages (1 mark):** 1. Equipment is **simple, cheap, and highly portable** — no shielding gas cylinder is required. 2. Can be used in **all welding positions** (flat, horizontal, vertical, overhead) and in **outdoor/windy conditions**. --- ### Q2. List the functions of the flux coating in a stick electrode. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** The flux coating performs **five primary functions**: 1. **Generation of shielding gas** — The flux decomposes to release CO₂, H₂, and water vapour which displace atmospheric oxygen and nitrogen. 2. **Slag formation** — Flux components (SiO₂, TiO₂, Al₂O₃) form a molten slag that floats on the weld pool, protecting it from oxidation. 3. **Arc stabilization** — Ionizable ingredients like potassium titanate maintain a stable arc column. 4. **Alloying addition** — Powdered alloying elements (Fe, Mn, Cr) may be added via the flux. 5. **Deoxidation / cleaning** — Flux oxides react with impurities in the weld pool and float them away as slag. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Student Mistakes in SMAW Questions:** > 1. **Confusing SMAW with GTAW or GMAW** — Many students incorrectly state that "shielding gas is supplied externally from a cylinder" — this is wrong. In SMAW, shielding comes from the **flux coating** itself. > 2. **Forgetting the heat input formula's efficiency factor (η)** — Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you write $H = VI/S$ without including η. > 3. **Wrong unit conversion in heat input** — A unit mismatch (mixing mm/s with mm/min) is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction. > 4. **Confusing DCEN with DCEP** — Always state "**Electrode Negative**" or "**Electrode Positive**" — do not just say "DC". > 5. **Skipping slag removal** — Any welding process description without mentioning post-weld slag chipping is incomplete. --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` #### Q A(a). With a neat sketch, describe the working principle of the Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) process. Also explain the functions of the flux coating. (7 marks) **Model Answer:** **Introduction (1 mark):** Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) is a manual arc welding process in which the weld is produced by the heat of an electric arc between a **flux-coated consumable stick electrode** and the workpiece. The process is governed by AWS Process Number **1**. **Working Principle (4 marks):** 1. The welder connects the workpiece to one terminal of the power source (via ground cable) and the electrode holder (containing the stick electrode) to the other terminal. 2. The electrode is momentarily touched to the workpiece (called *scratch start* or *tap start*), creating a short circuit. Upon slight withdrawal (2–4 mm), an **electric arc** is struck, ionizing the air gap. 3. The arc temperature reaches **6,000–7,000 °C**, melting both the electrode tip and the base metal surface to form a molten weld pool. 4. As the arc progresses, droplets of molten electrode metal transfer across the arc into the weld pool, providing filler material. 5. The welder moves the electrode along the joint at a controlled travel speed; the molten pool solidifies behind the arc forming a continuous weld bead. 6. After each pass, the hardened **slag** is chipped off using a chipping hammer and brushed clean with a wire brush. **Neat Sketch (1 mark):** (Refer to the schematic in Section 4.1 — power source, electrode holder, electrode, arc, weld pool, ground cable, workpiece). **Functions of Flux Coating (2 marks):** (Refer to Section 2.3 — five functions: shielding gas, slag formation, arc stabilization, alloying, deoxidation/cleaning). **Conclusion (1 mark):** SMAW is the most widely used manual welding process due to its simplicity, portability, and versatility across a wide range of materials and positions. --- #### Q A(b). A single-pass SMAW weld is made on a 10 mm thick low-carbon steel plate with the following parameters: Arc Voltage = 24 V, Welding Current = 180 A, Travel Speed = 3.5 mm/s, Thermal Efficiency = 0.85. Calculate: (i) Heat input per unit length in kJ/mm, and (ii) Power consumed during welding in kW. (7 marks) **Model Answer:** **Given Data:** - Arc Voltage, $V = 24$ V - Welding Current, $I = 180$ A - Travel Speed, $S = 3.5$ mm/s - Thermal Efficiency, $\eta = 0.85$ **Part (i) — Heat Input per unit length (4 marks):** The formula for heat input is: $$H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S}$$ Substituting the values: $$H = \frac{0.85 \times 24 \times 180}{3.5}$$ Calculating the numerator first: $$0.85 \times 24 \times 180 = 0.85 \times 4{,}320 = 3{,}672 \text{ W}$$ Dividing by travel speed: $$H = \frac{3{,}672}{3.5} = 1{,}049.14 \text{ J/mm}$$ Converting to kJ/mm: $$H = 1.049 \text{ kJ/mm} \approx 1.05 \text{ kJ/mm}$$ **Part (ii) — Power Consumed in kW (3 marks):** The electrical power consumed (input to the power source, ignoring efficiency) is: $$P = V \times I = 24 \text{ V} \times 180 \text{ A} = 4{,}320 \text{ W}$$ Converting to kW: $$P = 4.32 \text{ kW}$$ **Final Answers:** - Heat input, $H = \textbf{1.05 kJ/mm}$ - Power consumed, $P = \textbf{4.32 kW}$ --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` #### Q B(a). Explain the AWS classification of SMAW electrodes with a suitable example. Compare the characteristics of E6010, E6013, and E7018 electrodes. (7 marks) **Model Answer:** **AWS Classification System (3 marks):** The American Welding Society (AWS) classifies SMAW electrodes using a **four-digit (or five-digit) code** of the form **E XXXX**: $$\text{E} \, \text{XX} \, \text{X} \, \text{X} \, (\text{XX})$$ - **E** = Electrode (always first letter). - **First Two Digits (XX)** = Minimum tensile strength of the deposited weld metal in **kilo-pounds per square inch (ksi)**. - E.g., "60" = 60 ksi ≈ 414 MPa; "70" = 70 ksi ≈ 482 MPa. - **Third Digit (X)** = Welding position in which the electrode can be used. - "1" = All positions (flat, horizontal, vertical, overhead). - "2" = Flat and horizontal only. - **Fourth Digit (X)** = Type of current, coating chemistry, and penetration characteristics. - "0" = Cellulosic, DCEP, deep penetration. - "1" = Cellulosic, AC/DC, deep penetration. - "3" = Rutile (TiO₂), AC/DC, medium penetration. - "8" = Low hydrogen, iron powder, DCEP. **Example (1 mark):** **E7018** - Minimum tensile strength: 70 ksi (≈ 482 MPa). - Welding position: All positions. - Coating: Low-hydrogen iron powder, DCEP current. **Comparison Table (3 marks):** | Parameter | E6010 | E6013 | E7018 | |---|---|---|---| | Tensile Strength | 60 ksi (414 MPa) | 60 ksi (414 MPa) | 70 ksi (482 MPa) | | Position | All positions | All positions | All positions | | Coating Type | Cellulosic (high gas) | Rutile (TiO₂) | Low-hydrogen iron powder | | Current/Polarity | DCEP | AC / DC (both) | DCEP | | Penetration | **Deep** | Medium | Medium–Deep | | Slag Quantity | Less | Moderate | High | | Bead Appearance | Coarse, rippled | Smooth, glossy | Smooth, slightly convex | | Typical Use | Pipeline, root pass | Sheet metal, general fabrication | Critical structures, pressure vessels, bridges | | Moisture Sensitivity | Low | Low | **High** (must be oven-dried) | --- #### Q B(b). Differentiate between AC and DC power sources used in SMAW. When is each preferred? An E7018 electrode of 4 mm core diameter is consumed at the rate of 120 mm/min. Calculate the mass of electrode consumed per hour. Take density of steel core = 7.85 g/cm³. (7 marks) **Model Answer:** **Difference between AC and DC Power Sources (3 marks):** | Aspect | AC (Alternating Current) | DC (Direct Current) | |---|---|---| | **Waveform** | Sinusoidal, 50 Hz | Constant polarity | | **Polarity** | Alternates 100 times/sec | Fixed (DCEP or DCEN) | | **Arc Stability** | Re-ignition needed every half-cycle | Steady, smooth arc | | **Cost** | Cheaper (transformer) | Costlier (rectifier) | | **Suitability for low-hydrogen electrodes** | Not suitable | **Mandatory** for E7018 etc. | | **Arc Blow Problem** | Less prone | More prone on magnetized parts | | **Electrode Compatibility** | Limited to AC-rated electrodes | Works with all electrode types | **Preference (2 marks):** - **DC is preferred** for: low-hydrogen electrodes (E7018), thin plates, critical welds, non-ferrous metals, and when arc blow is not a problem. - **AC is preferred** for: general-purpose work, welding magnetized workpieces (e.g., ship hull repair), and in equipment where a cheaper transformer can be used. **Numerical Part (4 marks):** **Given:** Electrode core diameter, $d = 4$ mm; consumption rate = 120 mm/min; density, $\rho = 7.85$ g/cm³. **Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of the core wire** $$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (4)^2}{4} = 4\pi \approx 12.566 \text{ mm}^2$$ **Step 2 — Length consumed per hour** $$L = 120 \text{ mm/min} \times 60 \text{ min} = 7{,}200 \text{ mm} = 7.2 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3 — Volume consumed per hour** $$V = A \times L = 12.566 \text{ mm}^2 \times 7{,}200 \text{ mm} = 90{,}475.2 \text{ mm}^3$$ Converting to cm³: $$V = 90{,}475.2 \text{ mm}^3 \times \frac{1 \text{ cm}^3}{1{,}000 \text{ mm}^3} = 90.475 \text{ cm}^3$$ **Step 4 — Mass consumed per hour** $$m = \rho \times V = 7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3 \times 90.475 \text{ cm}^3 = 710.23 \text{ g}$$ Converting to kg: $$m = 0.710 \text{ kg/hr} \approx \textbf{0.71 kg/hr}$$ **Final Answer:** Mass of electrode consumed per hour ≈ **0.71 kg/hr**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **SMAW — Rapid Revision Checklist (Print-Friendly)** - ✅ **SMAW** = Shielded Metal Arc Welding = Manual Metal Arc Welding = Stick Welding. **AWS Process No. = 1**. - ✅ **Three essentials**: Power source + Electrode holder + Flux-coated consumable electrode. **No external shielding gas required.** - ✅ **Arc temperature**: 6,000–7,000 °C. **Arc voltage**: 20–30 V. **Open circuit voltage**: 50–90 V. - ✅ **Heat input formula**: $\displaystyle H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S}$ — Always include the efficiency factor η (0.7–0.85 for SMAW). - ✅ **Five functions of flux**: Shielding gas + Slag + Arc stabilization + Alloying + Deoxidation. - ✅ **DCEN = Electrode is Cathode** → **Deep penetration**, 70% heat in workpiece. **DCEP = Electrode is Anode** → **Fast melt-off**, low-hydrogen electrodes. - ✅ **AC is cheaper** and avoids arc blow; **DC is required for low-hydrogen electrodes** (E7018) and is generally smoother. - ✅ **AWS Electrode Code EXXXX**: E + Tensile strength (ksi) + Position + Coating/Current — Example: **E7018** = 70 ksi, all positions, low-hydrogen DCEP. - ✅ **Five welding positions**: 1G/1F flat, 2G/2F horizontal, 3G/3F vertical, 4G/4F overhead, 5G/6G pipe positions. - ✅ **Process cycle**: Strike → Arc stabilize → Travel → Solidify → **Chip slag** → Brush → Inspect. - ✅ **Electrode diameters**: 1.6, 2.0, 2.5, 3.15, 4.0, 5.0, 6.3 mm. **Length**: usually 300 mm or 450 mm. - ✅ **Slag removal** is mandatory after every pass; slag is chipped with a chipping hammer and brushed with a wire brush. - ✅ **Hydrogen control**: Low-hydrogen electrodes (E7018, E7016) must be stored in **heated ovens** at 60–120 °C to prevent hydrogen-induced cracking. - ✅ **SMAW limitations**: Low deposition rate, electrode must be replaced frequently (every 300–450 mm burn-off), not suitable for reactive metals (Al, Mg, Ti). - ✅ **Major applications**: Structural construction, pipeline welding, shipbuilding, pressure vessel fabrication, heavy equipment repair, civil rebar splicing. - ✅ **Comparison point to remember**: SMAW has the **best portability** of all arc welding processes; it is the **#1 choice for outdoor field work**.

topics.title: Soldering and Brazing and their Applications, Machining Processes: Description and Operations Performed on Lathe
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDEST203 - BASIC MECHANICAL AND CIVIL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Soldering and Brazing and their Applications, Machining Processes: Description and Operations Performed on Lathe

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Soldering — Formal KTU Definition

**Soldering** is a metal joining process in which two or more metal parts are joined together by melting and flowing a **filler metal (solder)** into the joint. The filler metal has a melting point **below 450 °C** and is distributed between the closely fitted surfaces of the joint by capillary action. Crucially, the **base metals are NOT melted**; only the filler metal melts, wets the surfaces, and solidifies to form a metallurgical bond.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The melting point threshold of **450 °C** is the universally accepted industrial boundary. Any filler melting *below* this → **Soldering**. Any filler melting *above* this → **Brazing**. The base metal *never* melts in either process (unlike welding).

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Sticky Glue" Effect**
Think of soldering like joining two pieces of paper using a thin layer of hot-melt glue. The glue (solder) melts at a low temperature, seeps into the gap by capillary action (the same force that pulls water up a thin straw), and hardens to hold the papers together. The paper itself (base metal) is never damaged by heat. The strength of the joint is far less than the base metal — just as glued paper is weaker than a single sheet.

### 1.2 Brazing — Formal KTU Definition

**Brazing** is a metal joining process in which a **filler metal (spelter or brazing rod)** with a melting point **above 450 °C** but **below the melting point of the base metals** is melted and distributed by capillary action between closely fitted surfaces. The base metals remain solid; bonding occurs through metallurgical diffusion and wetting at the interface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Brazing Temperature Range:** Most brazing operations occur between **450 °C and 950 °C**, depending on the filler alloy (e.g., brass at ~870 °C, copper at ~1083 °C, silver brazing alloys at 600–750 °C).

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Cemented Bricks" Effect**
Imagine stacking two bricks and pouring molten cement into the thin gap between them. The bricks (base metal) don't melt, but the cement (brazing filler) hardens into a strong, permanent bond that holds them together. The strength is much higher than glue (soldering) because the cement creates a strong chemical/metallurgical bond.

### 1.3 Lathe — Formal KTU Definition

A **Lathe** is a fundamental machine tool used to perform various machining operations on a workpiece by **rotating the workpiece about a fixed axis** and removing excess material using a stationary (or linearly fed) **single-point cutting tool**. It is the "mother of all machine tools" because almost every rotational part (shafts, pulleys, screws, etc.) is manufactured on it.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Potter's Wheel" Effect**
A lathe works exactly like a traditional potter's wheel. The clay (workpiece) is held on a spinning disc, and the potter (cutting tool) presses a shaping tool against it as it rotates. By controlling the tool's position, the potter (machinist) can shape perfect cylinders, cones, threads, or decorative patterns. In a lathe, the same principle is used to cut metal instead of clay.

### 1.4 Fundamental Comparison at a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Soldering vs Brazing vs Welding (the three thermal joining processes):**

| Feature | Soldering | Brazing | Welding |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Filler melting point** | < 450 °C | > 450 °C | Melts base metal |
| **Base metal melted?** | **No** | **No** | **Yes** |
| **Joint strength** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| **Heat required** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| **Typical filler** | Lead-Tin (Pb-Sn) | Brass, Copper, Silver alloys | Same as base metal (or compatible) |
| **Mechanism** | Capillary + adhesion | Capillary + diffusion | Fusion + solidification |

## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Soldering — Process Decomposition

**Working Principle — Step-by-step**

1. **Surface Preparation:** The mating surfaces of the parts to be joined are mechanically cleaned (using files, sandpaper, wire brushes) and chemically cleaned (using solvents) to remove **oxides, dirt, oil, and grease**. Clean surfaces ensure proper wetting.
2. **Application of Flux:** A chemical cleaning agent called **flux** is applied to the joint area. Flux dissolves existing oxides, prevents re-oxidation during heating, and lowers the surface tension of the molten solder, improving wetting.
3. **Heating:** The joint area is heated using a **soldering iron, soldering gun, torch, or hot plate** to a temperature above the melting point of the solder but well below the base metal's melting point.
4. **Solder Application:** The solder (in wire, bar, or paste form) is brought into contact with the heated joint. It melts and is drawn into the joint gap by **capillary action**.
5. **Cooling and Solidification:** The assembly is allowed to cool naturally. The solder solidifies and forms a permanent metallurgical bond.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Capillary Action?** When the gap between two closely fitted surfaces is very small (typically **0.05 mm to 0.4 mm**), the molten solder "climbs" into the gap against gravity. The narrower the gap (within limits), the higher the capillary rise — this is governed by the **Jurin's Law** (mathematically equivalent to capillary rise in fluids).

**Types of Soldering (Classified by Temperature)**

| Type | Filler | M.P. Range | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Soft soldering** | Pb-Sn (Tin-Lead) | 180 – 250 °C | Electronics, plumbing, sheet metal |
| **Hard soldering (Silver soldering)** | Ag-Cu-Zn alloys | 600 – 850 °C | Stronger joints (actually overlaps with brazing in some classifications) |
| **Rosin-core soldering** | Sn-Pb with rosin flux core | ~190 °C | PCB assembly in electronics |

**Common Soldering Tools**

- **Soldering Iron** — Heated metal tip (copper/iron-plated) used to transfer heat; rated 15W to 200W.
- **Soldering Gun** — Pistol-shaped, instant-heat, transformer-based; used for quick repair work.
- **Soldering Station** — Variable-temperature iron with digital control; used in professional electronics.
- **Hot Air Blower / Reflow Oven** — Used in surface-mount technology (SMT) for PCB soldering.

**Types of Flux Used in Soldering**

| Flux Type | Composition | Best For | Corrosivity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rosin (colophony)** | Pine tree resin | Electronics | Mild, non-corrosive residue |
| **Organic (water-soluble)** | Acids, halides | Copper pipes, plumbing | High, must be cleaned |
| **Inorganic (acid)** | Zinc chloride, HCl | Sheet metal, heavy work | Very high, must be removed |

### 2.2 Brazing — Process Decomposition

**Working Principle — Step-by-step**

1. **Joint Design and Cleaning:** Joints are designed with a small, uniform clearance (typically **0.03 mm to 0.15 mm**). Surfaces are cleaned to remove oxides and contaminants.
2. **Flux Application (or Vacuum/Inert Atmosphere):** Flux is applied, OR brazing is done in a **controlled atmosphere (vacuum furnace brazing, hydrogen atmosphere, or argon shielding)** to prevent oxidation.
3. **Heating above 450 °C:** Heat is supplied using a **torch (oxy-acetylene, oxy-propane), induction coil, resistance heating, furnace, or dip bath**. The entire joint area is brought above the filler's flow point.
4. **Filler Metal Melting and Flow:** The brazing filler (pre-placed in the joint as a ring, wire, paste, or shim) melts and is drawn throughout the joint by capillary action.
5. **Diffusion Bonding:** At the elevated temperature, atomic diffusion occurs across the filler-base interface, creating a strong metallurgical bond.
6. **Cooling:** The joint is allowed to cool (sometimes slowly, to relieve stresses), and the flux residue is removed by washing or pickling.

**Types of Brazing Processes**

| Method | Heat Source | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Torch Brazing** | Oxy-fuel flame (manual) | Plumbers, HVAC, jewelry |
| **Furnace Brazing** | Gas/electric furnace | Mass production, auto parts |
| **Induction Brazing** | High-frequency AC coil | Tools, carbide tips, mass production |
| **Dip Brazing** | Molten salt bath | Aluminium assemblies |
| **Resistance Brazing** | Electric current through joint | Electrical contacts, thin parts |
| **Vacuum Brazing** | Vacuum furnace | Aerospace, medical, reactive metals |

**Common Brazing Filler Metals**

| Filler Alloy | M.P. (°C) | Base Metals Joined | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Brass (Cu-Zn)** | 870 – 950 | Steel, copper | Most common, used in torch brazing |
| **Copper** | 1083 | Steel, tungsten | Requires reducing atmosphere |
| **Silver brazing (BAg series)** | 600 – 780 | Almost all metals | Strongest, used in refrigeration |
| **Aluminium-silicon** | 575 – 630 | Aluminium | Critical for aerospace |
| **Nickel-based** | 900 – 1200 | Stainless steel, superalloys | Aerospace, jet engines |

### 2.3 Lathe — Comprehensive Machine Anatomy

A lathe consists of the following main parts:

| Part | Function |
|---|---|
| **Bed** | Heavy, rigid base (cast iron) on which all other parts are mounted. Provides alignment. |
| **Headstock** | Houses the **spindle**, **chuck**, **gear train**, and **main motor**. Rotates the workpiece. |
| **Spindle** | Hollow rotating shaft that holds the workpiece via the chuck. |
| **Chuck** | Clamping device (3-jaw self-centering, 4-jaw independent) that holds the workpiece. |
| **Tailstock** | Supports the free end of long workpieces. Holds a **dead center** or **drill chuck**. |
| **Carriage** | Slides along the bed. Houses the tool post and saddle. |
| **Cross-slide** | Moves perpendicular to the spindle axis. |
| **Compound Rest** | Mounted on cross-slide; can be rotated to set tool at any angle (for taper turning, threading). |
| **Tool Post / Toolholder** | Clamps the single-point cutting tool. |
| **Feed Rod and Lead Screw** | Power the carriage for threading and automatic feed. |
| **Apron** | Part of the carriage; contains feed mechanisms, clutch, and engagement levers. |
| **Legs / Cabinet** | Supports the bed. Often houses the motor and coolant pump. |

### 2.4 Operations Performed on Lathe

The following operations are essential for KTU 2024:

| Operation | Description | Tool Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Turning** | Reduces the diameter of a cylindrical workpiece. Most common operation. | Turning tool (HSS / carbide) |
| **Facing** | Produces a flat surface perpendicular to the rotational axis. | Facing tool |
| **Plain Turning** | Removing metal from a cylindrical surface in straight line. | Turning tool |
| **Step Turning** | Producing multiple diameters on a single workpiece. | Turning tool, multiple passes |
| **Taper Turning** | Producing a conical surface. | Compound rest / taper turning attachment / form tool |
| **Knurling** | Producing a patterned (diamond / straight / diagonal) roughened surface for grip. | Knurling tool |
| **Thread Cutting** | Producing external (V) threads on a cylindrical surface. | Thread-cutting tool (matched to thread pitch) |
| **Drilling** | Making a hole along the rotational axis using the tailstock drill chuck. | Twist drill |
| **Boring** | Enlarging an already drilled hole to precise dimensions. | Boring bar / boring tool |
| **Reaming** | Finishing a hole to high accuracy and surface finish. | Reamer |
| **Parting / Grooving** | Cutting off a finished workpiece or cutting a narrow groove. | Parting tool (thin blade) |
| **Chamfering** | Beveling the edge of a cylindrical workpiece. | Chamfer tool |
| **Form Turning** | Producing a contoured profile using a specially shaped tool. | Form tool |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Lathe Machining Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table for KTU numerical questions:**

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cutting Speed (V)** | $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ | m/min | Surface speed of workpiece at cutting zone |
| **Spindle Speed (N)** | $N = \dfrac{1000 V}{\pi D}$ | rpm | Rotational speed of spindle |
| **Feed (f)** | $f$ (given) | mm/rev | Distance tool advances per spindle revolution |
| **Feed Rate (Fr)** | $Fr = f \times N$ | mm/min | Linear feed rate of tool |
| **Depth of Cut (d)** | $d = \dfrac{D_1 - D_2}{2}$ | mm | Radial penetration of tool |
| **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** | $MRR = \pi \times D_{avg} \times d \times f \times N$ | mm³/min | Volume of metal removed per minute |
| **Machining Time (Tm)** | $T_m = \dfrac{L}{f \times N}$ | min | Time required for one longitudinal pass |
| **Power (P)** | $P = \dfrac{MRR \times \text{Specific Energy}}{60 \times 10^6}$ | kW | Power consumed during cutting |

> Where:
> - $D$ = Diameter of workpiece (mm)
> - $N$ = Spindle speed (rpm)
> - $L$ = Length of cut (mm)
> - $D_1$ = Initial diameter, $D_2$ = Final diameter
> - $D_{avg} = \dfrac{D_1 + D_2}{2}$ = Average diameter

**Real-world utility of these formulas:**
- **Cutting speed (V):** Used in CNC programming to set spindle RPM and optimize tool life. Different materials have recommended V values: Mild Steel ~ 30 m/min, Aluminium ~ 100 m/min, Cast Iron ~ 25 m/min.
- **MRR:** Used to estimate production time and cost in process planning.
- **Power calculation:** Used to select the machine tool's motor capacity.

## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of Cutting Speed Formula

**Problem Setup:** A cylindrical workpiece of diameter $D$ (in mm) is rotated on a lathe at $N$ revolutions per minute. Determine the surface (cutting) speed $V$ in m/min.

**Step 1 — Circumference of the workpiece**

When the workpiece rotates one full revolution, every point on its surface traces a circular path equal to the circumference.

$$C = \pi D \quad \text{(in mm, since } D \text{ is in mm)}$$

**Step 2 — Distance traveled per minute**

If the workpiece rotates $N$ times per minute, the total linear distance covered by a point on the surface in one minute is:

$$\text{Distance per minute} = N \times \pi D \quad \text{(in mm/min)}$$

**Step 3 — Convert from mm/min to m/min**

Since $1 \text{ m} = 1000 \text{ mm}$, we divide by 1000:

$$V = \frac{N \times \pi D}{1000} \quad \text{(m/min)}$$

**Final Equation (Boxed for KTU reference):**

$$V = \frac{\pi D N}{1000} \text{ m/min}$$

> **Logic Explanation:** This formula says — *"The cutting speed equals the linear speed of the outer surface of the rotating workpiece."* Larger diameter or higher RPM means faster cutting speed.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Material Removal Rate (MRR)

**Setup:** A turning operation removes a cylindrical shell of metal. Initial diameter = $D_1$, Final diameter = $D_2$, Feed = $f$ mm/rev, Spindle speed = $N$ rpm.

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of the removed chip**

The metal removed per revolution is a thin ring of thickness $f$ (longitudinal) and width $d$ (radial). The cross-sectional area of this ring is:

$$A = f \times d = f \times \left(\frac{D_1 - D_2}{2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Volume removed per revolution**

The cross-section is "swept" along the circumference of the **average** diameter $D_{avg} = (D_1 + D_2)/2$ in one revolution.

$$V_{rev} = A \times (\pi \times D_{avg}) = f \times d \times \pi \times D_{avg}$$

**Step 3 — Volume removed per minute**

Multiply by $N$ (revolutions per minute):

$$MRR = \pi \times D_{avg} \times d \times f \times N \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/min)}$$

**Final Equation (Boxed):**

$$MRR = \pi \times D_{avg} \times d \times f \times N \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

> **Logic Explanation:** This formula helps estimate how much metal is "cut away" per minute — critical for production cost and machine tool power sizing.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Machining Time

**Setup:** A turning operation requires a longitudinal cut of length $L$ mm. Feed = $f$ mm/rev. Spindle speed = $N$ rpm.

**Step 1 — Time for one revolution**

Since spindle makes $N$ revolutions per minute, the time for one revolution is:

$$t_{rev} = \frac{1}{N} \text{ min/rev}$$

**Step 2 — Tool advances $f$ mm in one revolution**

In one revolution, the tool moves forward by $f$ mm. To move a distance of $L$ mm, the number of revolutions required is:

$$n = \frac{L}{f}$$

**Step 3 — Total time**

$$T_m = n \times t_{rev} = \frac{L}{f} \times \frac{1}{N}$$

**Final Equation (Boxed):**

$$T_m = \frac{L}{f \times N} \text{ minutes}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — KTU Typical Numerical Problem

**Worked Example (Model Solution Style):**

**Problem:** A mild steel workpiece of diameter 80 mm is to be turned on a lathe to a final diameter of 70 mm in a single pass. The cutting speed is recommended as 30 m/min, and the feed is 0.25 mm/rev. Find:
(a) Spindle speed (N) in rpm
(b) Material Removal Rate (MRR)
(c) Machining time for a 150 mm long cut

**Given Data:**
- $D_1 = 80$ mm, $D_2 = 70$ mm
- $V = 30$ m/min
- $f = 0.25$ mm/rev
- $L = 150$ mm

**Part (a) — Spindle Speed (using average diameter for two-pass calculations)**

Average diameter:
$$D_{avg} = \frac{D_1 + D_2}{2} = \frac{80 + 70}{2} = 75 \text{ mm}$$

Using the cutting speed formula (solved for N):
$$N = \frac{1000 \times V}{\pi \times D_{avg}} = \frac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 75}$$

$$N = \frac{30000}{235.619} \approx 127.3 \text{ rpm}$$

**Part (b) — Material Removal Rate**

Depth of cut:
$$d = \frac{D_1 - D_2}{2} = \frac{80 - 70}{2} = 5 \text{ mm}$$

$$MRR = \pi \times D_{avg} \times d \times f \times N$$

$$MRR = \pi \times 75 \times 5 \times 0.25 \times 127.3$$

$$MRR = 3.1416 \times 75 \times 5 \times 0.25 \times 127.3$$

$$MRR = 3.1416 \times 75 \times 5 \times 0.25 \times 127.3$$

Step-by-step multiplication:
- $75 \times 5 = 375$
- $375 \times 0.25 = 93.75$
- $93.75 \times 127.3 = 11934.375$
- $11934.375 \times 3.1416 \approx 37493.6$

$$MRR \approx 37494 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min} \approx 37.49 \text{ cm}^3\text{/min}$$

**Part (c) — Machining Time**

$$T_m = \frac{L}{f \times N} = \frac{150}{0.25 \times 127.3}$$

$$T_m = \frac{150}{31.825} \approx 4.71 \text{ minutes}$$

> **Examiner's note:** Use the **average diameter** in the cutting speed formula when calculating spindle speed for a multi-pass turning operation. This is a common KTU mistake students make — they incorrectly use $D_1$ (initial diameter).

## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram of Soldering Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Two Metal Parts to Join] --> B[Step 1: Clean Surfaces]
    B --> C[Step 2: Apply Flux]
    C --> D[Step 3: Heat Joint Area]
    D --> E{Is Temp > Solder M.P.?}
    E -->|No| D
    E -->|Yes| F[Step 4: Apply Solder Wire]
    F --> G[Step 5: Capillary Action Draws Molten Solder into Joint]
    G --> H[Step 6: Cool Naturally]
    H --> I[Final: Soldered Joint Forms Metallurgical Bond]
    I --> J[Optional: Clean Flux Residue]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram of Brazing Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Two Metal Parts to Join] --> B[Joint Design with 0.05 to 0.15 mm Clearance]

    D --> E[Step 3: Heat Above 450 C Using Torch / Furnace / Induction]
    E --> F{Temperature Reached?}
    F -->|No| E
    F -->|Yes| G[Step 4: Filler Metal Melts and Flows by Capillarity]
    G --> H[Step 5: Atomic Diffusion at Interface]
    H --> I[Step 6: Cool and Remove Flux Residue]
    I --> J[Final: Strong Brazed Joint]
```

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — With Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q. (a)** [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Modified]
**With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the soldering process. List the functions of flux and state the different types of fluxes used.**

**Model Solution:**

**Soldering Process (Step-by-step explanation – 4 marks):**

1. **Surface Cleaning:** The mating surfaces of the parts to be joined are cleaned mechanically (using emery paper, file, wire brush) and chemically (using solvents) to remove dirt, oil, and oxide layers.
2. **Flux Application:** A suitable flux is applied to the cleaned surfaces to dissolve existing oxides and prevent further oxidation during heating.
3. **Heating:** The joint area is heated using a soldering iron (or torch) to a temperature just above the melting point of the solder.
4. **Solder Application:** The solder wire is brought into contact with the heated joint. It melts and is drawn into the joint gap by capillary action.
5. **Cooling:** The joint is allowed to cool, and the solder solidifies to form a permanent bond.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):**

```
Clean → Apply Flux → Heat → Apply Solder → Cool → Joint
```

**Functions of Flux (1 mark):**

1. **Removes existing oxides** from the metal surfaces chemically.
2. **Prevents re-oxidation** during heating by forming a protective liquid layer.
3. **Reduces surface tension** of the molten solder, improving wetting and spreading.
4. **Improves capillary flow** of the molten solder into the joint.

**Types of Fluxes (1 mark):**

| Type | Use |
|---|---|
| Rosin (non-corrosive) | Electronics |
| Organic (water-soluble) | Copper pipe joints |
| Inorganic (acid, e.g., ZnCl₂) | Heavy sheet metal, plumbing |

---

**Q. (b)** [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Describe any seven lathe operations with the help of neat sketches. (Sketches not required in text answer – describe geometry, tool, and purpose of each.)**

**Model Solution (7 operations × 1 mark each):**

1. **Turning:** Used to reduce the diameter of a cylindrical workpiece. The cutting tool moves longitudinally parallel to the axis, removing a uniform layer of material. Most common lathe operation.

2. **Facing:** Used to produce a flat surface at the end of a workpiece, perpendicular to its axis. The tool moves radially inward from the outer diameter to the center.

3. **Step Turning:** Produces multiple diameters (steps) on a single workpiece. The tool is fed in stages, each producing a different diameter.

4. **Taper Turning:** Produces a conical surface. Achieved by the compound rest method (rotating the compound rest to a half-angle), the taper attachment method, or the form tool method.

5. **Knurling:** Creates a patterned (diamond, straight, or diagonal) roughened surface on the workpiece to provide grip. A knurling tool with hardened rollers is pressed against the rotating workpiece.

6. **Thread Cutting:** Produces helical V-threads on a cylindrical surface. The thread-cutting tool (shaped to match the thread profile) is fed radially, and the carriage is driven by the lead screw synchronized with the spindle rotation.

7. **Drilling:** Creates a hole along the rotational axis. A twist drill is held in the tailstock drill chuck, advanced into the rotating workpiece.

8. **Boring:** Enlarges an already-drilled hole to a precise diameter and improved surface finish. A boring bar (single-point tool) is mounted on the tool post and fed into the rotating workpiece.

9. **Parting (Cut-off):** Cuts off a finished part from the workpiece stock. A thin parting tool is fed radially into the workpiece until it separates.

> **Examiner's note:** Provide a clear one-line purpose + the tool used + the motion of the tool for each operation to score full marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q. (a)** [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the brazing process in detail. Compare brazing with soldering.**

**Model Solution:**

**Brazing Process (5 marks):**

1. **Joint Design:** Joints are designed with a small, uniform clearance (**0.05 to 0.15 mm**). Butt, lap, T-joints, and scarf joints are common. The small gap ensures strong capillary action.

2. **Cleaning:** Surfaces are cleaned mechanically and chemically to remove oxides, oil, and dirt.

3. **Flux Application:** A suitable flux (e.g., borax for brass filler) is applied to prevent oxidation and promote wetting. In vacuum brazing, no flux is needed.

4. **Heating Above 450 °C:** The joint is heated using:
   - Oxy-acetylene / oxy-propane torch
   - Furnace (batch or continuous)
   - Induction coil
   - Resistance heating
   - Dip bath (molten salt)
   - Vacuum furnace (for reactive metals)

5. **Filler Flow:** The filler metal (pre-placed as ring, wire, paste, or shim) melts and flows through the joint by capillary action. Atomic diffusion at the filler-base interface forms a strong metallurgical bond.

6. **Cooling and Cleaning:** The joint is allowed to cool. Flux residue is removed by washing, brushing, or pickling.

**Comparison with Soldering (2 marks — compact table):**

| Feature | Soldering | Brazing |
|---|---|---|
| Filler M.P. | < 450 °C | > 450 °C |
| Strength | Low–Medium | High |
| Heat source | Iron, gun, torch | Torch, furnace, induction |
| Typical filler | Pb-Sn | Brass, Ag, Cu, Al-Si |
| Joint clearance | 0.05 – 0.4 mm | 0.03 – 0.15 mm |
| Common use | Electronics, plumbing | Aerospace, automotive, tools |

---

**Q. (b)** [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**A mild steel shaft of initial diameter 100 mm is to be turned down to 80 mm in one pass at a cutting speed of 30 m/min and a feed of 0.3 mm/rev. Calculate (i) spindle speed, (ii) depth of cut, (iii) MRR, (iv) machining time for a 200 mm cut length.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $D_1 = 100$ mm, $D_2 = 80$ mm
- $V = 30$ m/min
- $f = 0.3$ mm/rev
- $L = 200$ mm

**(i) Spindle Speed (N):**

Average diameter:
$$D_{avg} = \frac{D_1 + D_2}{2} = \frac{100 + 80}{2} = 90 \text{ mm}$$

$$N = \frac{1000 \times V}{\pi \times D_{avg}} = \frac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 90}$$

$$N = \frac{30000}{282.743} \approx 106.1 \text{ rpm}$$

**(ii) Depth of Cut (d):**

$$d = \frac{D_1 - D_2}{2} = \frac{100 - 80}{2} = 10 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Material Removal Rate (MRR):**

$$MRR = \pi \times D_{avg} \times d \times f \times N$$

$$MRR = \pi \times 90 \times 10 \times 0.3 \times 106.1$$

Step-by-step:
- $90 \times 10 = 900$
- $900 \times 0.3 = 270$
- $270 \times 106.1 = 28647$
- $28647 \times \pi = 28647 \times 3.1416 \approx 89990.5$

$$MRR \approx 89990.5 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min} \approx 90 \text{ cm}^3\text{/min}$$

**(iv) Machining Time (Tm):**

$$T_m = \frac{L}{f \times N} = \frac{200}{0.3 \times 106.1}$$

$$T_m = \frac{200}{31.83} \approx 6.28 \text{ minutes}$$

> **Final Answer Summary:**
> - Spindle speed: **106.1 rpm**
> - Depth of cut: **10 mm**
> - MRR: **~89,990 mm³/min (~90 cm³/min)**
> - Machining time: **~6.28 minutes**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:**

1. **Using $D_1$ instead of $D_{avg}$** in the spindle speed formula when there is a change in diameter. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
2. **Forgetting the 1000 factor** in $V = \pi D N / 1000$. The diameters are in mm, but $V$ is in m/min — students often confuse units. **Loss: 1 mark.**
3. **Confusing brazing with welding** — saying base metal melts in brazing. **Loss: 1 mark.**
4. **Writing "soldering uses high temperature"** — soldering is precisely the *low-temperature* process. **Loss: 1 mark.**
5. **Listing lathe operations without describing them** — KTU requires a one-line description of each operation. **Loss: 1 mark per incomplete operation.**
6. **In flux-related questions**, students often write "flux removes dirt" — the precise function is "flux **dissolves oxides and prevents re-oxidation**." **Loss: 1 mark.**
7. **Confusing turning and facing** — turning reduces diameter, facing produces flat end. **Loss: 0.5 mark per mix-up.**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets:**

- [ ] **Soldering** → filler M.P. < **450 °C**; **Brazing** → filler M.P. > **450 °C**; **Welding** → base metal melts.
- [ ] In **both** soldering and brazing, the **base metal does NOT melt** — only the filler melts.
- [ ] **Capillary action** is the governing mechanism for filler flow in both processes.
- [ ] **Flux** dissolves oxides, prevents re-oxidation, and improves wetting.
- [ ] Common solder: **60-40 Pb-Sn (60% tin, 40% lead)**, M.P. ~190 °C.
- [ ] Common brazing filler: **Brass (Cu-Zn)**, M.P. ~870–950 °C.
- [ ] Brazing gives **stronger joints** than soldering because of higher temperature and atomic diffusion.
- [ ] **Lathe** rotates the workpiece; the tool is stationary (or linearly fed) — unlike a drilling machine.
- [ ] Main parts of a lathe: **Bed, Headstock, Tailstock, Carriage, Cross-slide, Compound Rest, Tool Post, Chuck, Lead Screw, Feed Rod**.
- [ ] **Turning** = diameter reduction; **Facing** = flat end surface; **Knurling** = grip pattern; **Drilling** = axial hole; **Boring** = enlarging hole; **Thread cutting** = helical V-grooves.
- [ ] **Cutting Speed:** $V = \pi D N / 1000$ (m/min).
- [ ] **Spindle Speed:** $N = 1000 V / (\pi D)$ (rpm) — use $D_{avg}$ for multi-pass turning.
- [ ] **Feed:** $f$ in mm/rev; **Feed Rate:** $f \times N$ in mm/min.
- [ ] **Depth of Cut:** $d = (D_1 - D_2)/2$ (mm).
- [ ] **MRR:** $\pi \cdot D_{avg} \cdot d \cdot f \cdot N$ (mm³/min).
- [ ] **Machining Time:** $T_m = L / (f \cdot N)$ (minutes).
- [ ] **Power:** $P = \text{MRR} \times \text{Specific Energy} / (60 \times 10^6)$ (kW).
- [ ] The **3-jaw chuck** is self-centering (used for round stock); the **4-jaw chuck** is independent (used for square/rectangular stock).
- [ ] **Tailstock** supports long workpieces and is used for **drilling/boring** operations.
- [ ] The **lead screw** drives the carriage for **thread cutting**; the **feed rod** drives the carriage for **automatic feed**.
- [ ] The **compound rest** is rotated to a half-angle to perform **taper turning** (by compound rest method).
- [ ] **Knurling** does not change dimensions significantly; it is a surface-texturing operation.

---

**End of Notes — KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Output**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drilling Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDEST203 - BASIC MECHANICAL AND CIVIL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Drilling Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Drilling Machine** is a powered machine tool designed to produce cylindrical holes in a workpiece by rotating a *cutting tool* (the **drill bit** or **twist drill**) against a stationary workpiece, or alternatively by feeding the workpiece against a rotating tool. The primary motion is **rotary** (provided by the spindle) and the secondary motion is **linear** (axial feed of the tool into the workpiece).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:**
> A drilling machine is a *hole-making machine tool* in which a multi-point cutting tool (commonly a twist drill) is rotated against a firmly held workpiece to enlarge an existing hole or to originate a fresh hole. It is classified as a *metal-removal* (machining) process governed by the principles of *shear deformation* in the form of a continuous chip.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to pierce a hole through a thick wooden plank using a nail. What do you do? You *press* the nail against the wood and *rotate* it back and forth while pushing. The wood shavings come out and a hole is formed. A drilling machine does *exactly* this — but with far more power, precision, and rigidity. The **spindle** acts like your hand (rotating + pushing the drill), the **drill bit** is the nail, and the **worktable** holds the wooden plank (workpiece) in place.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering analogy:** A carpenter's hand brace and bit is the oldest, simplest form of a drilling machine. The *pedestal drill* and the *radial drill* are industrial-grade mechanical descendants of the same principle.

## 1.3 Significance in Manufacturing

- Drilling is the **most common** hole-making operation in any manufacturing industry — automotive, aerospace, civil construction (rebar), electronics.
- The **standard hole-making tool** is the *twist drill*, standardized in metric (DIN 338, DIN 340) and inch (Jobber) sizes.
- Drilling operations account for roughly **25–40%** of all material-removal operations in a typical machine shop.

> [!TIP]
> **Why a student should care:** Every bolt hole, rivet hole, dowel pin, and fastener clearance in the engineering world originates from a drilling process. Mastering this topic is foundational for *Design of Machine Elements*, *Production Engineering*, and *CAD/CAM* courses later in the B.Tech curriculum.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle (Step-by-Step)

A drilling machine works on the principle of **relative rotary + linear motion** between the tool and the workpiece:

1. **Holding:** The workpiece is rigidly clamped on the *worktable* using a vise, clamps, or a T-slot fixture.
2. **Tool Mounting:** The twist drill is held in the *drill chuck* which is mounted on the rotating *spindle*.
3. **Primary Motion (Cutting Motion):** The *spindle* rotates the drill at a calculated RPM — this is the **cutting speed** $V_c$ in m/min.
