To ensure uniformity and enforce minimum standards, every civil engineering project in India — and specifically in Kerala — must comply with a hierarchy of codal provisions. Three of the most important regulatory frameworks that a student of **GDEST203** must recognize are: 1. **NBC** – National Building Code of India 2. **KBR** – Kerala Building Rules 3. **CRZ Norms** – Coastal Regulation Zone Notification > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (Module 3):** > This topic demands only a *brief discussion of relevance* — meaning the student is expected to know **what** each code is, **who** issues it, **why** it matters, and **how** it governs civil engineering practice. Numerical design is **out of scope** here. --- ## 1.2 The National Building Code of India (NBC) ### Formal Definition The **National Building Code of India (NBC)** is a **comprehensive building code** that provides guidelines for the planning, design, construction, and maintenance of buildings across India. It is published by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** and serves as a **model code** for adoption by all states, local bodies, and construction agencies. The currently operative version is **NBC 2016** (National Building Code of India, 2016), which replaced the earlier 2005 version. ### Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The "Constitution" of Indian Buildings** > Think of the NBC as the **Constitution of Buildings in India**. Just as the Indian Constitution lays down fundamental rights, duties, and directives that every state law must respect, the NBC lays down the **fundamental rules** for safe, sustainable, and uniform construction across the country. Individual state rules (like KBR) act as the "state laws" that must align with this national framework. ### Key Highlights of NBC - **Publisher:** Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) - **First Published:** 1970 - **Current Version:** NBC 2016 - **Coverage:** Structural design, fire safety, plumbing, electrical, HVAC, sustainability, accessibility, and disaster resilience - **Volumes:** Organized into **11 parts / volumes** plus administrative provisions --- ## 1.3 Kerala Building Rules (KBR) ### Formal Definition The **Kerala Building Rules (KBR)** are the **state-level building regulations** framed by the Government of Kerala under the **Kerala Municipality Act, 1994** and the **Kerala Panchayat Act, 1994**. They prescribe rules for building permits, site coverage, Floor Area Ratio (FAR/FSI), setbacks, height restrictions, parking, and occupancy classifications applicable **specifically within the State of Kerala**. The KBR is operationalized through the **Kerala Municipal Building Rules (KMBR)** and the **Kerala Panchayat Building Rules (KPBR)**, periodically amended to address local conditions (e.g., the 2019 amendment incorporating coastal and hilly terrain provisions). ### Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The "State Government Order"** > If NBC is the national Constitution, KBR is like a **State Government Order (GO)**. It customizes national guidelines to Kerala's specific conditions — for instance, the heavy monsoon rainfall, the coastal terrain, the high population density, and the prevalence of laterite/soft soil. Whenever you build a house in Kerala, the **local panchayat or municipality** enforces KBR — not NBC directly. ### Key Highlights of KBR - **Governing Authority:** Local Self Government Department, Government of Kerala - **Applicable To:** All buildings in Kerala (rural panchayats, municipalities, corporations) - **Enforced By:** Local bodies (Panchayat / Municipality / Corporation) - **Key Parameters:** Plot coverage, FAR, setback distances, building height, parking, safety --- ## 1.4 Coastal Regulation Zone (CRZ) Norms ### Formal Definition The **Coastal Regulation Zone (CRZ) Notification** is an **environmental regulation** issued by the **Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEF&CC), Government of India**, under the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**. It governs all activities — including construction — within a **500 m to 2 km** belt from the **High Tide Line (HTL)** along India's coastline, classifying the zone into **CRZ-I, CRZ-II, CRZ-III, and CRZ-IV** with progressively relaxed restrictions. The most recent significant notification is the **CRZ Notification 2019**, which supersedes earlier notifications of 1991 and 2011. ### Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The "Lifeguard Zone" of the Coast** > Imagine the Indian coastline as a swimming pool. The lifeguard paints a **yellow line** beyond which no one is allowed to dive or play roughly. CRZ norms are that **yellow line for construction**. Within 500 m of the high tide line, building is **strictly regulated or banned** to protect the beach, mangroves, coral reefs, and coastal ecosystems from reckless urbanization — a particularly sensitive issue for Kerala with its long coastline and tourism-driven construction pressure. ### Key Highlights of CRZ - **Issued By:** MoEF&CC, Government of India - **Legal Basis:** Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 - **Zone Width:** **500 m** to **2 km** from High Tide Line (HTL) — varies by area - **CRZ Categories:** I, II, III, IV - **Restricted Activities:** New construction, reclamation, mining, discharge of waste --- ## 1.5 Why This Topic is Relevant to a B.Tech Student | Stakeholder | Why These Rules Matter | |---|---| | **Civil Engineer** | Must design buildings that **pass permit approval** and stand safe against disasters | | **Architect** | Must plan layouts conforming to setbacks, FAR, and height limits | | **Environmental Engineer** | Must ensure construction does not damage CRZ or natural drainage | | **Project Manager** | Must anticipate **delays and rejections** if rules are ignored | | **Society at Large** | Rules protect **lives, property, and environment** from poor construction | # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 Hierarchical Relationship of the Three Frameworks The three regulations are **not parallel** — they operate at **different levels of jurisdiction** and **overlap** in application. A civil engineer in Kerala must satisfy all three when applicable. ### Level 1: National Framework (NBC) - Issued by: **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)** - Scope: **All of India** — model code - Mandatory? **Voluntary at national level**, but typically referenced in contracts and adopted by states. ### Level 2: State Framework (KBR) - Issued by: **Government of Kerala** - Scope: **Only Kerala** - Mandatory? **Yes** — enforced through local bodies (Panchayat / Municipality / Corporation). ### Level 3: Environmental / Sectoral Framework (CRZ) - Issued by: **MoEF&CC, Government of India** - Scope: **Coastal belt of all states**, including Kerala's **~590 km coastline** - Mandatory? **Yes** — binding under the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986, with **penal provisions**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hierarchy Principle:** CRZ > KBR > NBC in terms of legal force for coastal projects, because CRZ is rooted in a **central environmental statute**. However, for inland projects in Kerala, **KBR is the operational rulebook**, and NBC is the **reference standard**. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet The following table is the **single most important exam-aid** for this topic. Memorize it for short-answer questions. | Parameter | NBC | KBR | CRZ | |---|---|---|---| | **Full Name** | National Building Code of India | Kerala Building Rules | Coastal Regulation Zone Notification | | **Issued By** | Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) | Govt. of Kerala (LSGD) | MoEF&CC, Govt. of India | | **Legal Basis** | BIS Act, 2016 (model code) | Kerala Municipality Act & Panchayat Act | Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 | | **Latest Version** | NBC 2016 | KMBR/KPBR (with 2019 amendments) | CRZ Notification 2019 | | **Geographic Scope** | Pan-India | State of Kerala only | Coastal belt (500 m – 2 km from HTL) | | **Mandatory?** | Voluntary (model code) | Yes (enforced by local bodies) | Yes (binding) | | **Key Focus** | Structural, fire, MEP, accessibility, sustainability | Building permits, FAR, setbacks, height | Coastal environment protection | | **Penalty for Violation** | No direct penalty | Permit rejection, demolition order | Criminal prosecution, demolition, fine | --- ## 2.3 Detailed Breakdown of Each Regulation ### A. National Building Code (NBC) — Salient Features The NBC is organized into the following broad sections, with the **most relevant for basic civil engineering** highlighted: 1. **Part 0:** Integrated Approach — Prerequisite for applying the code 2. **Part 1:** Definitions and terminology 3. **Part 2:** Administration — **Building permits, inspections** 4. **Part 3:** Development Control Rules and General Building Requirements 5. **Part 4:** Fire and Life Safety — **Critical for safety** 6. **Part 5:** Building Materials 7. **Part 6:** Structural Design (Loads, Foundations, Masonry, Concrete, Steel, Timber) 8. **Part 7:** Constructional Practices and Safety 9. **Part 8:** Building Services (Plumbing, Drainage, Electrical, HVAC) 10. **Part 9:** Landscape and Sustainability 11. **Part 10:** Asset Management and Facility Management 12. **Part 11:** Appendices and Tables > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Whenever a designer is unsure about the **design load, fire resistance, or material specification**, the NBC provides the minimum national benchmark. Most I.S. Codes (IS 456 for concrete, IS 800 for steel, IS 875 for loads) are **referenced by NBC**. ### B. Kerala Building Rules (KBR) — Key Parameters KBR governs a wide range of practical building parameters. The most commonly tested ones in KTU exams are: - **Plot Coverage (Plinth Area Ratio):** Maximum percentage of plot that can be built upon. For residential plots in Kerala, this is typically capped at **50% to 75%** depending on plot size. - **Floor Area Ratio (FAR) / Floor Space Index (FSI):** Ratio of total built-up area to plot area. KBR specifies FAR in the range of **1.5 to 3.0** depending on road width and zone. - **Setbacks:** Mandatory open spaces at the **front, rear, and sides** of a building. For small plots (< 3 cents), reduced setbacks apply under KBR 2019 amendments. - **Building Height:** Restricted based on **road width, airport funnel zone, and coastal CRZ status**. - **Parking:** Minimum number of car parking slots per dwelling unit. - **Staircase, Lift, Ramp, and Refuge Area** requirements. ### C. CRZ Norms — Zone-Wise Classification | CRZ Category | Description | Permitted Construction | |---|---|---| | **CRZ-I** | Ecologically sensitive areas (mangroves, coral reefs, national parks, salt marshes) | **No new construction** allowed | | **CRZ-II** | Developed urban areas within 500 m of HTL | Construction allowed subject to **FAR / Ground Coverage** restrictions | | **CRZ-III** | Rural and undeveloped areas within 500 m of HTL | Limited construction; only for **traditional community needs** | | **CRZ-IV** | Aquatic area between LTL and HTL (sea, tidal water bodies) | **No construction** except for specific defence/port activities | > [!IMPORTANT] > **No Development Zone (NDZ):** A buffer of **50 m to 200 m** from the HTL where **no construction** is permitted at all, regardless of CRZ category. This is the most important KTU-relevance point. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Scenario | Applicable Rule | |---|---| | Designing a 10-storey residential apartment in Kochi city | **KBR** + **NBC** (structural loads) | | Constructing a beach resort at Kovalam | **CRZ** (binding) + **KBR** + **NBC** | | Building a school in an inland Kerala panchayat | **KBR** + **NBC** (CRZ not applicable) | | Reconstructing a house after 2018 Kerala floods | **KBR** (revised) + **NBC 2016** disaster resilience provisions | | Highway construction near coast | **CRZ** clearance mandatory from State Coastal Zone Management Authority (SCZMA) | # Section 3: Analytical Matrix and Step-by-Step Comparative Analysis ## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: The Three Codes Side-by-Side The following matrix is structured to allow a student to **write a 14-mark comparative answer** in the KTU examination. It captures every dimension an examiner typically evaluates. ### Matrix 1: Comparative Analysis | Dimension | NBC | KBR | CRZ | |---|---|---|---| | **Type of Document** | Model building code | State building rules | Environmental regulation | | **Issuing Authority** | BIS (national standards body) | Govt. of Kerala (LSGD) | MoEF&CC (central ministry) | | **Legal Strength** | Advisory | Statutory (state) | Statutory (central, dominant) | | **Applicability Trigger** | All buildings in India | Any building construction in Kerala | Any project within CRZ (coastal India) | | **Primary Concern** | Safety, uniformity, technical standards | Permit mechanism, town planning | Environmental protection, coastline | | **Key Engineering Inputs** | IS Codes (IS 456, 800, 875, etc.) | Setbacks, FAR, height, parking | Setback from HTL, NDZ, zone classification | | **Penalty Mechanism** | None directly | Local body can refuse permit / demolish | Prosecution under EPA 1986, demolition | | **Revision Frequency** | Every ~10 years (1970, 1983, 2005, 2016) | Frequent amendments (e.g., 2019) | Periodic notifications (1991, 2011, 2019) | ### Matrix 2: Application Decision Tree (Step-by-Step Logic) For any building project, a civil engineer must follow this **decision logic** before construction begins: **Step 1: Identify the Location** - Is the project in Kerala? → If YES, KBR applies. - If NO, follow the respective state rules. **Step 2: Check Coastal Proximity** - Measure the distance from the **High Tide Line (HTL)**. - If the project lies within **500 m** of HTL → **CRZ applies**. - If distance > 500 m (or project is inland) → CRZ does **not** apply. **Step 3: Check Building Type and Scale** - For structural design (loads, materials, fire safety) → **NBC / IS Codes** are referenced as technical benchmarks. **Step 4: Apply Rules in Order of Priority** - **Coastal Project:** CRZ (priority 1) → KBR (priority 2) → NBC (priority 3, reference) - **Inland Kerala Project:** KBR (priority 1) → NBC (priority 2, reference) - **Inland Project Outside Kerala:** Respective state rules → NBC (reference) **Step 5: Obtain Necessary Clearances** - Building permit from Panchayat / Municipality / Corporation - Environmental clearance (if CRZ or large project) - Approval from SCZMA (State Coastal Zone Management Authority) for CRZ projects --- ## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of Compliance Logic Since this topic is descriptive, the "derivation" here is the **logical flow of compliance reasoning** a student must articulate in the exam. ### Step A: Establish the Jurisdiction $$ J_{\text{project}} = \{ \text{State} \times \text{Coastal Proximity} \} $$ where: - **State** ∈ {Kerala, Other Indian States} - **Coastal Proximity** = (Distance from HTL) ≤ 500 m → CRZ-applicable **Logic:** Every project must first be classified by **state** and **coastal status** to determine applicable laws. ### Step B: Apply the Hierarchical Filter $$ \text{Applicable Rules} = \begin{cases} \{\text{CRZ}, \text{KBR}, \text{NBC}\}, & \text{if Project in Kerala AND within CRZ} \\ \{\text{KBR}, \text{NBC}\}, & \text{if Project in Kerala AND outside CRZ} \\ \{\text{State Rules}, \text{NBC}\}, & \text{if Project outside Kerala} \end{cases} $$ **Logic:** The set of applicable rules depends on the intersection of state and coastal status. This is the **most exam-relevant formula** for this topic. ### Step C: Compute the Design Constraints For a typical KBR-compliant project, the key geometric constraints are: $$ \text{Plot Coverage (\%)} = \frac{A_{\text{plinth}}}{A_{\text{plot}}} \times 100 $$ $$ \text{FAR (Floor Area Ratio)} = \frac{\text{Total Built-up Area of all Floors}}{A_{\text{plot}}} $$ $$ \text{Setback}_{\text{required}} = f(\text{Road Width}, \text{Plot Size}, \text{Zone}) $$ **Logic:** The KBR stipulates maximum permitted values of plot coverage, FAR, and minimum required setbacks. A building exceeding any of these is **non-compliant** and liable for permit rejection. ### Step D: Verify CRZ Compliance For a coastal project: $$ \text{NDZ distance} = \text{Distance from HTL to Building} $$ $$ \text{NDZ Constraint:} \quad \text{NDZ} \geq \begin{cases} 200 \text{ m} & \text{(CRZ-I, ecologically sensitive)} \\ 100 \text{ m} & \text{(CRZ-II, urban developed)} \\ 50 \text{ m} & \text{(CRZ-III, rural)} \end{cases} $$ **Logic:** The No Development Zone (NDZ) buffer is **non-negotiable** — any construction within NDZ is a violation and liable for **demolition and prosecution** under the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986. --- ## 3.3 Summary of the Derivation Flow > [!NOTE] > **The Compliance Chain for a Coastal Kerala Project:** > > 1. **Check CRZ** → Identify zone (I/II/III/IV), measure HTL distance, verify NDZ. > 2. **Apply KBR** → Verify setbacks, FAR, plot coverage, height, parking. > 3. **Reference NBC / IS Codes** → Structural loads (IS 875), concrete (IS 456), steel (IS 800), fire safety. > 4. **Obtain Approvals** → Local body permit + SCZMA clearance + Environmental clearance (if required). > 5. **Construct and Inspect** → Periodic inspections by local body and structural auditor. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams and Schematics ## 4.1 Hierarchy of Building Regulations (Flow Diagram) The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **legal hierarchy** and **overlapping jurisdiction** of NBC, KBR, and CRZ in India, with focus on Kerala. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Constitution of India] --> B[Environment Protection Act 1986] A --> C[BIS Act 2016] A --> D[Kerala Municipality Act 1994] A --> E[Kerala Panchayat Act 1994] B --> F[CRZ Notification 2019
Issued by MoEFCC] C --> G[National Building Code 2016
Issued by BIS] D --> H[Kerala Building Rules
KMBR and KPBR] E --> H F --> I[Project Compliance
Kerala Coastal Area] G --> I H --> I G --> J[Project Compliance
Kerala Inland Area] H --> J G --> K[Project Compliance
Rest of India] L[State Building Rules] --> K F --> M[Enforcement Body] H --> M M --> N[Local Body
Panchayat or Municipality] F --> O[SCZMA
State Coastal Zone Mgmt Authority] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style H fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style I fill:#F38181,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style J fill:#F38181,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style K fill:#F38181,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 CRZ Zone Classification (Cross-Sectional Schematic) The following diagram illustrates the **cross-section of a typical Kerala coastline** showing the four CRZ zones and the No Development Zone (NDZ). ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Sea] --> B[LTL

KBR and NBC Apply] style A fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style C fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style D fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style E fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#6BCB77,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#4D96FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style H fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Compliance Decision Flowchart for a Kerala Project ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start
New Building Project] --> B{Is project in Kerala?} B -->|NO| C[Apply Respective State Rules
Reference NBC] B -->|YES| D{Is site within 500 m of HTL?} D -->|NO| E[Apply KBR
Reference NBC] D -->|YES| F[CRZ Applies] F --> G[Identify CRZ Category] G --> H{Category?} H -->|CRZ I| I[No Construction Permitted] H -->|CRZ II| J[Restricted Construction
With FAR and NDZ Limits] H -->|CRZ III| K[Limited Construction
For Local Community Only] H -->|CRZ IV| L[No Construction
Except Defence and Ports] J --> M[Obtain SCZMA Clearance] K --> M M --> N[Apply KBR Setbacks and FAR] N --> O[Reference NBC and IS Codes
for Structural Design] E --> O O --> P[Obtain Building Permit
from Local Body] P --> Q[Construction and Inspection] Q --> R[End] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style I fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style L fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style P fill:#6BCB77,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style R fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]` **Briefly explain the National Building Code (NBC) of India and its relevance to civil engineering practice.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **National Building Code of India (NBC)** is a comprehensive model building code published by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)**, with the latest edition being **NBC 2016**. It provides uniform guidelines for the planning, design, construction, and maintenance of buildings across India, covering **structural design, fire safety, building services, materials, sustainability, and accessibility**. **Relevance to civil engineering:** 1. Acts as the **national technical benchmark** for all building design and construction in India. 2. **References Indian Standard (IS) codes** (IS 456, IS 800, IS 875) for structural design, providing engineers with a unified technical language. 3. Ensures **safety, health, and welfare** of building occupants, including provisions for fire, accessibility, and disaster resilience. 4. Serves as the **base document** for state building rules like KBR to formulate their state-specific regulations. 5. Promotes **uniformity** in construction practices across diverse climatic and seismic zones of India. > [!NOTE] > **Mark Distribution (as per KTU 2024):** Definition (1 mark) + Three relevance points (1.5 marks) + Conclusion (0.5 mark). --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]` **What are CRZ norms? Why are they particularly significant for the State of Kerala?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Coastal Regulation Zone (CRZ) norms** are environmental regulations issued by the **Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEF&CC)** under the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**, governing all activities — including construction — within **500 m to 2 km** of the High Tide Line (HTL) along India's coastline. The current CRZ Notification is **2019**. The zone is divided into **four categories**: CRZ-I (ecologically sensitive), CRZ-II (urban developed), CRZ-III (rural), and CRZ-IV (aquatic). A **No Development Zone (NDZ)** of 50–200 m from HTL prohibits construction entirely. **Significance for Kerala:** 1. Kerala has a long coastline of approximately **590 km**, with dense coastal population and heavy tourism pressure. 2. Coastal cities like **Kochi, Kozhikode, Thiruvananthapuram, and Kollam** host major infrastructure projects within CRZ jurisdiction. 3. The 2018 **Kerala floods and subsequent coastal erosion** highlighted the need for strict CRZ enforcement to protect lives and property. 4. Kerala's **mangrove ecosystems and backwaters** are environmentally sensitive and protected under CRZ-I provisions. 5. Beach tourism construction (resorts, villas) in places like Kovalam, Varkala, and Bekal must obtain **SCZMA clearance** under CRZ norms. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern**, Module-level questions carry **14 marks** with **internal choice**. Each question is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Below, **Option A** and **Option B** are provided as the two choices. --- ### Option A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]` **Question A:** Discuss in detail the **National Building Code (NBC) of India** and the **Kerala Building Rules (KBR)**. Compare their scope, issuing authority, legal strength, and relevance to building construction in Kerala. #### Part (a) — National Building Code of India (7 marks) **Model Answer:** **Definition and Issuing Authority:** The **National Building Code of India (NBC)** is the **premier national building code** published by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)**. The current version, **NBC 2016**, supersedes the 2005 edition and provides a comprehensive framework for all aspects of building design and construction across India. It is a **model code** — advisory in nature at the national level but widely adopted. **Scope and Coverage (11 Volumes):** NBC 2016 is organized into **eleven parts**, each addressing a specific domain: 1. Administration and building permits 2. Development control and general building requirements 3. Fire and life safety 4. Building materials specifications 5. Structural design (loads, foundation, masonry, concrete, steel, timber) 6. Constructional practices and safety 7. Building services (plumbing, drainage, electrical, HVAC, lifts) 8. Sustainability, landscape, and asset management **Engineering Relevance:** - Provides **uniform design standards** for loads (IS 875), concrete (IS 456), and steel (IS 800). - Mandates **fire safety, accessibility for the disabled, and disaster resilience** provisions. - Acts as the **technical reference** for all state-level building rules, including KBR. **Conclusion:** The NBC is the **technical backbone of Indian building construction**, ensuring uniformity, safety, and quality. Every civil engineer in India — whether designing a hut in a village or a skyscraper in a metro — relies on the NBC for minimum standards. #### Part (b) — Kerala Building Rules (KBR) (7 marks) **Model Answer:** **Definition and Legal Basis:** The **Kerala Building Rules (KBR)** are state-level regulations framed by the **Government of Kerala (Local Self Government Department)** under the **Kerala Municipality Act, 1994** and the **Kerala Panchayat Act, 1994**. They are operationalized through the **Kerala Municipal Building Rules (KMBR)** and **Kerala Panchayat Building Rules (KPBR)**, with significant amendments in 2019. **Issuing Authority and Enforcement:** Issued by the **LSG Department** and enforced by **local bodies (Panchayats, Municipalities, Corporations)**. Every building construction in Kerala requires a **building permit** from the concerned local body as per KBR. **Key Parameters Governed by KBR:** 1. **Plot Coverage:** Maximum percentage of plot area that can be built upon. 2. **Floor Area Ratio (FAR):** Ratio of total built-up area to plot area — typically 1.5 to 3.0. 3. **Setbacks:** Minimum open spaces at front, rear, and sides — varies with road width and plot size. 4. **Building Height:** Restricted based on road width, airport funnel zone, and CRZ status. 5. **Parking:** Minimum car parking slots per dwelling unit. 6. **Staircase, Lift, Ramp, and Refuge Area** specifications. 7. **Special provisions** for hilly terrain, coastal areas, and flood-prone zones (introduced after 2018 floods). **Engineering Relevance:** - KBR is the **practical rulebook** that a civil engineer in Kerala must follow for permit approval. - It adapts national standards (NBC) to **Kerala-specific conditions** — heavy monsoons, high population density, coastal vulnerability. - Non-compliance leads to **permit rejection, demolition orders, and legal penalties**. **Comparison Table:** | Aspect | NBC | KBR | |---|---|---| | **Issued By** | BIS (national) | Govt. of Kerala (state) | | **Legal Status** | Model code (advisory) | Statutory rules (mandatory) | | **Scope** | All of India | Kerala only | | **Focus** | Technical standards | Permit mechanism + town planning | | **Enforced By** | None directly | Local self-government bodies | **Conclusion:** While the **NBC provides the technical philosophy** of safe building construction in India, the **KBR provides the operational rules** specifically tailored to Kerala. Together, they ensure that every building in Kerala is **technically sound, legally compliant, and contextually appropriate**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):** > Many students **memorize KBR parameters without context** and lose marks. To score full 7 marks, you must: > 1. **State the legal basis** (Kerala Municipality/Panchayat Act) — 1 mark. > 2. **Name the enforcement authority** (local bodies / LSGD) — 1 mark. > 3. **List at least 4-5 KBR parameters** with brief explanation — 3 marks. > 4. **Provide a comparison or concluding statement** linking KBR to NBC — 2 marks. > Merely writing "KBR governs building construction in Kerala" will fetch **at most 2 marks**. Be specific. --- ### Option B `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]` **Question B:** Explain the **Coastal Regulation Zone (CRZ) norms** in detail. Describe the classification of CRZ zones, the concept of the **No Development Zone (NDZ)**, and discuss the relevance of CRZ norms specifically to the **State of Kerala**. #### Part (a) — CRZ Norms and Zone Classification (7 marks) **Model Answer:** **Definition and Legal Basis:** The **Coastal Regulation Zone (CRZ) Notification** is an environmental regulation issued by the **Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEF&CC), Government of India**, under the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**. The first CRZ notification was issued in **1991**, revised in **2011** and most recently in **2019**. It governs all activities — including construction, reclamation, mining, and discharge of effluents — within a defined coastal belt. **Coastal Belt Definition:** The coastal belt extends from the **High Tide Line (HTL)** to a distance of **500 m** on the landward side in most areas, and up to **2 km** in special areas (islands, ecologically sensitive zones). The water area up to **12 nautical miles** in the sea (territorial waters) is also covered. **CRZ Zone Classification:** | Zone | Description | Permitted Construction | |---|---|---| | **CRZ-I** | Ecologically sensitive areas: mangroves, coral reefs, sand dunes, national parks, marine parks, salt marshes, turtle nesting grounds | **No new construction** permitted; only traditional and exploratory activities | | **CRZ-II** | Urban areas already substantially developed up to the shoreline, with civic infrastructure (e.g., Greater Mumbai, Kochi urban) | Construction allowed subject to **FAR (≤ 1.33)** and **Ground Coverage (≤ 60%)** restrictions; no new construction seaward of existing buildings | | **CRZ-III** | Rural and undeveloped areas (villages, agricultural land) | Construction limited to **traditional community needs**, residential dwellings, and small-scale tourism infrastructure on specific conditions | | **CRZ-IV** | Aquatic area between Low Tide Line (LTL) and High Tide Line (HTL), and tidal influenced water bodies | **No construction** permitted except for specific **defence, port, and strategic** activities | **No Development Zone (NDZ):** The NDZ is a critical concept. It is a buffer zone **immediately landward of the HTL** where construction is **strictly prohibited**. The NDZ width is: - **200 m** for CRZ-I (most sensitive) - **100 m** for CRZ-II (urban developed areas) - **50 m** for CRZ-III (rural areas) The NDZ is **non-negotiable** — any construction within this zone is a violation subject to **demolition and prosecution** under the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986. **Enforcement:** The **State Coastal Zone Management Authority (SCZMA)** in each state is the nodal agency for CRZ clearance. For Kerala, the **Kerala State Coastal Zone Management Authority (KSCZMA)** processes CRZ applications. #### Part (b) — Relevance of CRZ Norms to Kerala (7 marks) **Model Answer:** **Kerala's Coastal Profile:** Kerala has a coastline of approximately **590 km** along the Arabian Sea, with **9 coastal districts** and a dense population living within 500 m of the High Tide Line. Major cities like **Kochi, Thiruvananthapuram, Kozhikode, and Kollam** lie within CRZ jurisdiction. **Why CRZ is Particularly Relevant to Kerala:** 1. **High Coastal Population Density:** Kerala is one of the **most densely populated coastal states** in India, with millions of people residing within CRZ limits. Strict regulation is essential to protect lives and livelihoods. 2. **Tourism Pressure:** Kerala's tourism economy is heavily **coastal-dependent** (Kovalam, Varkala, Alleppey, Bekal, Marari). The construction of resorts, beach villas, and tourism infrastructure must comply with CRZ to prevent **reckless coastal commercialization**. 3. **Ecological Significance:** Kerala is home to vital ecosystems protected under **CRZ-I**: - Mangroves of **Kannur and Kasaragod** - Backwater systems of **Vembanad, Ashtamudi, and Kozhikode** - Coral reefs of **Lakshadweep (adjoining Kerala coast)** - Turtle nesting beaches (Kovalam) 4. **Vulnerability to Natural Disasters:** The **2018 Kerala floods** and frequent **coastal erosion, sea-level rise, and cyclones** make CRZ enforcement critical for disaster resilience. 5. **Fisheries and Livelihood:** The traditional **fishing community** depends on coastal access protected by CRZ norms. Illegal construction in CRZ-III affects their livelihood. 6. **Post-2018 Reforms:** After the 2018 floods, the Kerala government, in coordination with **KSCZMA**, tightened CRZ enforcement and introduced **special reconstruction guidelines** for coastal panchayats. **Case Examples:** | Location | Project | CRZ Concern | |---|---|---| | Kochi | Smart City Project | CRZ-II compliance for waterfront development | | Varkala | Cliff resort construction | CRZ-I (geological sensitive) and NDZ issues | | Kovalam | Beach tourism expansion | CRZ-III and NDZ constraints | | Kozhikode | Beach road widening | CRZ clearance from SCZMA required | **Conclusion:** CRZ norms are not merely environmental restrictions — they are **life-saving regulations** for a state like Kerala where the coastline is simultaneously a **livelihood zone, a tourism asset, and a disaster-vulnerable frontier**. Every civil engineer working in Kerala's coastal belt must understand and respect CRZ norms to ensure that development is **sustainable, legal, and disaster-resilient**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):** > Students often write **only definitions** and forget to **link CRZ specifically to Kerala**. To score full 7 marks: > 1. **Mention Kerala's coastline length and coastal districts** — 1 mark. > 2. **Provide at least 3 Kerala-specific examples** (e.g., Kovalam, Varkala, Kochi) — 2 marks. > 3. **Discuss the 2018 floods and post-disaster reforms** — 1 mark. > 4. **Explain the role of KSCZMA** as the state enforcement body — 1 mark. > 5. **Conclude with the engineering-society-livelihood** triple bottom line — 2 marks. > A generic answer on CRZ without Kerala-specific content will **lose at least 3 marks**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 3** ### NBC (National Building Code of India) - Issued by **BIS** (Bureau of Indian Standards) - Current version: **NBC 2016** - Acts as a **model / advisory** code at national level - Covers **11 parts** — from administration to asset management - References Indian Standard codes: **IS 456, IS 800, IS 875** - Primary focus: **structural safety, fire safety, building services, accessibility, sustainability** ### KBR (Kerala Building Rules) - Issued by **Government of Kerala (LSG Department)** - Legal basis: **Kerala Municipality Act 1994** and **Kerala Panchayat Act 1994** - Operationalized through **KMBR and KPBR**, with **2019 amendments** - Enforced by **local bodies (Panchayat / Municipality / Corporation)** - Key parameters: **Plot Coverage, FAR (FSI), Setbacks, Building Height, Parking** - Building **permit mandatory** before construction ### CRZ (Coastal Regulation Zone) Norms - Issued by **MoEF&CC, Government of India** - Legal basis: **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986** - Current version: **CRZ Notification 2019** - Applies within **500 m to 2 km** from High Tide Line (HTL) - **Four zones: CRZ-I, II, III, IV** (with progressively relaxed restrictions) - **No Development Zone (NDZ):** 50–200 m from HTL — construction prohibited - Enforced by **State Coastal Zone Management Authority (KSCZMA in Kerala)** - Violation leads to **demolition and criminal prosecution** ### Hierarchy to Remember - **Coastal Kerala Project:** CRZ (priority 1) → KBR (priority 2) → NBC (reference) - **Inland Kerala Project:** KBR (priority 1) → NBC (reference) - **Rest of India:** Respective State Rules → NBC (reference) ### Kerala-Specific Quick Facts (Frequently Asked) - Kerala coastline length: **~590 km** - Coastal districts: **9** - Post-2018 flood reforms: **KBR amended** for flood resilience and coastal setbacks - KSCZMA headquarters: **Thiruvananthapuram** ### Key Acronyms to Memorize - **NBC** → National Building Code - **BIS** → Bureau of Indian Standards - **KBR** → Kerala Building Rules - **KMBR / KPBR** → Kerala Municipal / Panchayat Building Rules - **CRZ** → Coastal Regulation Zone - **MoEF&CC** → Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change - **HTL / LTL** → High Tide Line / Low Tide Line - **NDZ** → No Development Zone - **SCZMA / KSCZMA** → State / Kerala State Coastal Zone Management Authority - **FAR / FSI** → Floor Area Ratio / Floor Space Index - **LSGD** → Local Self Government Department > [!IMPORTANT] > **One-Line Exam Mantra:** > *"**NBC** is the national model code by **BIS**; **KBR** is the state rulebook by **Govt. of Kerala** enforced by local bodies; **CRZ** is the central environmental law by **MoEF&CC** that protects India's coastline — and all three converge in Kerala, the coastal state with 590 km of coastline."*

topics.title: Environmental Laws and Regulations: Overview of Environmental Laws (Brief Description Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDEST203 - BASIC MECHANICAL AND CIVIL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Environmental Laws and Regulations: Overview of Environmental Laws (Brief Description Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Environmental Laws** are a body of legally binding statutes, rules, regulations, and judicial decisions enacted by competent legislative authorities (Parliament, State Legislatures, or competent regulatory bodies) to **protect human health, preserve ecological balance, prevent pollution of air, water, and soil, and promote sustainable use of natural resources.** In the Indian context, these laws are derived from the constitutional mandate under **Article 48A (Directive Principles of State Policy)** and **Article 51A(g) (Fundamental Duties)** of the Constitution of India, which obligate the State and citizens, respectively, to protect and improve the environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3):**
> The topic demands only a **"Brief Description"** of major Indian Environmental Laws. Students are NOT expected to reproduce lengthy legal sections or case law. The focus is on the **objective, salient features, and the regulating authority** of each Act.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a country as a **shared house** (the "Earth House"). When one roommate (industry/citizen) burns garbage in the corridor, smokes in the hallway, or dumps chemicals in the kitchen sink, the other roommates (neighbours, future generations, ecosystems) suffer. **Environmental Laws are the "House Rules"** drafted, voted upon, and enforced by the "House Committee" (Parliament & Pollution Control Boards) to ensure that no one messes up the shared living space.

### Key Indian Environmental Governing Bodies (Metrics to Remember)

| Regulatory Body | Full Form | Core Function |
|---|---|---|
| **MoEFCC** | Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change | Apex policy formulation body |
| **CPCB** | Central Pollution Control Board | National-level pollution monitoring & standards |
| **SPCB** | State Pollution Control Board | State-level implementation & consent |
| **NGT** | National Green Tribunal | Adjudication of environmental disputes |
| **EIA** | Environmental Impact Assessment | Pre-project environmental clearance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constitutional Backing for Environment Protection in India:**
> - **Article 48A:** "The State shall endeavour to protect and improve the environment and to safeguard the forests and wildlife of the country."
> - **Article 51A(g):** "It shall be the duty of every citizen of India to protect and improve the natural environment including forests, lakes, rivers and wildlife, and to have compassion for living creatures."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Summary

### Classification of Indian Environmental Laws

Indian Environmental Laws can be systematically classified into the following logical categories:

- **A. Water-Related Legislation**
    - Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, **1974**
    - Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Cess Act, **1977**

- **B. Air-Related Legislation**
    - Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, **1981**

- **C. Umbrella / Framework Legislation**
    - Environment (Protection) Act, **1986** (considered the most comprehensive)

- **D. Wildlife & Biodiversity Legislation**
    - Wildlife (Protection) Act, **1972**
    - Forest (Conservation) Act, **1980**
    - Biodiversity Act, **2002**

- **E. Waste Management Rules**
    - Hazardous Waste Management Rules, **1989** (revised 2008/2016)
    - Municipal Solid Waste Management Rules, **2016**
    - Plastic Waste Management Rules, **2016**

- **F. Liability & Institutional Legislation**
    - Public Liability Insurance Act, **1991**
    - National Green Tribunal Act, **2010**

### High-Yield Summary Table (The KTU Board Examiner's Quick Reference)

| S.No. | Year | Name of the Act | Key Objective | Regulating Authority |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **1972** | Wildlife Protection Act | Protect wild animals, birds, plants | Chief Wildlife Warden (MoEFCC) |
| 2 | **1974** | Water (P&CP) Act | Prevent & control water pollution | CPCB / SPCB |
| 3 | **1977** | Water Cess Act | Levy cess on water consumed by industry | CPCB / State Govt. |
| 4 | **1980** | Forest Conservation Act | Restrict diversion of forest land for non-forest use | MoEFCC / State Forest Dept. |
| 5 | **1981** | Air (P&CP) Act | Prevent & control air pollution | CPCB / SPCB |
| 6 | **1986** | Environment Protection Act | Umbrella Act to protect overall environment | MoEFCC / CPCB / SPCB |
| 7 | **1991** | Public Liability Insurance Act | Provide compensation for chemical accidents | Collector / District Magistrate |
| 8 | **2002** | Biological Diversity Act | Conservation of biodiversity, equitable benefit sharing | National Biodiversity Authority (NBA) |
| 9 | **2010** | National Green Tribunal Act | Fast-track environmental dispute resolution | NGT |
| 10 | **2016** | Solid Waste / Plastic Waste Rules | Manage municipal & plastic waste | SPCB / Urban Local Bodies |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Mnemonic – "WAWFEP-BNS" :**
> **W**ater 1974 → **A**ir 1981 → **W**ildlife 1972 → **F**orest 1980 → **E**P Act 1986 → **P**ublic Liability 1991 → **B**iodiversity 2002 → **N**GT 2010 → **S**olid Waste 2016.
> Memorise this sequence by year for the **chronology-based 3-mark questions.**

### Real-World Engineering Utility

For a Civil/Mechanical engineer, these laws directly affect:
- **Project Site Approvals:** Industries must obtain **Consent to Establish (CTE)** and **Consent to Operate (CTO)** from the SPCB under the Water and Air Acts.
- **EIA Clearance:** Major projects (above threshold capacity) need Environmental Clearance from the State EIA Authority under **EIA Notification, 2006** issued under the EP Act 1986.
- **Effluent Treatment:** Every polluting industry MUST install Effluent Treatment Plants (ETP) and Air Pollution Control Devices (APCD) as mandated by the Water Act 1974 and Air Act 1981.
- **Liability:** In case of an industrial accident (e.g., Bhopal-type disaster), the Public Liability Insurance Act 1991 mandates immediate relief to victims.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Detailed Description of Major Environmental Laws

> [!NOTE]
> Since the topic is descriptive, the "derivations" are replaced with **structured logical breakdowns** of each Act, explaining the **Why, What, and How** for each legislation.

### 1. Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974

- **Why enacted?** Realising the severity of river pollution and lack of a national body to enforce water quality, Parliament enacted this as the **first comprehensive environmental law** in independent India.
- **Salient Features:**
    - Established the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** at the national level and **State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs)** at the state level.
    - Defines the powers and functions of CPCB/SPCBs to lay down **effluent standards**, advise the Central Government, coordinate state activities, and resolve disputes.
- **How it works:** No industry can discharge trade effluent or sewage into a stream, well, sewer, or land **without prior consent (Consent to Establish and Consent to Operate)** of the SPCB.

### 2. Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981

- **Why enacted?** To extend pollution control to the **atmosphere**, following the 1972 Stockholm Conference on the Human Environment.
- **Salient Features:**
    - Amends the Water Act 1974 to include "air pollution" in the jurisdiction of CPCB and SPCBs.
    - Empowers SPCBs to declare **"air pollution control areas"** and lay down **emission standards** for industries and automobiles.
- **How it works:** Industries must obtain SPCB consent before establishing or operating any **industrial plant** that emits air pollutants (PM, SO₂, NOₓ, etc.).

### 3. Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 (The Umbrella Act)

- **Why enacted?** Following the **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984)**, the Indian Government passed this Act in the wake of the global **Stockholm Conference** to serve as a comprehensive framework law.
- **Salient Features:**
    - An "Umbrella Act" enabling the Centre to coordinate activities of state and central bodies under Article 253 (read with Entry 13 of the Union List).
    - Empowers the Central Government to **set environmental standards, prescribe procedures for handling hazardous substances, and conduct environmental impact assessments.**
- **How it works:** Most subsidiary rules (EIA Notification 2006, Hazardous Waste Rules, Noise Pollution Rules) are framed **under the powers conferred by this Act.**

### 4. Wildlife (Protection) Act, 1972

- **Why enacted?** To provide a legal framework for the protection of wild animals, birds, and plants, and to establish a network of protected areas.
- **Salient Features:**
    - Prohibits hunting of **specified wild animals** and specifies penalties for poaching.
    - Provides for the creation of **National Parks, Wildlife Sanctuaries, and Reserved Forests.**
- **How it works:** Schedules I to VI list protected species. Schedule I species enjoy the highest level of protection.

### 5. Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980

- **Why enacted?** To check the rapid deforestation caused by diversion of forest land for industrial, mining, or infrastructure projects.
- **Salient Features:**
    - **Prohibits** the use of forest land for non-forest purposes **without prior approval** of the Central Government.
- **How it works:** Any project requiring forest land (e.g., a road, dam, or mine) MUST obtain Forest Clearance; this is handled by the **Forest Advisory Committee (FAC).**

### 6. Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991

- **Why enacted?** After the Bhopal disaster, to ensure victims of industrial accidents receive **immediate, no-fault compensation** without lengthy litigation.
- **Salient Features:**
    - Mandates that every owner of a "hazardous undertaking" must take out an **Insurance Policy** to cover liability arising from an industrial accident.
    - The policy pays compensation up to the **"no-fault" liability** limit.
- **How it works:** The Collector of the district administers the Relief Fund and awards compensation to victims.

### 7. National Green Tribunal Act, 2010

- **Why enacted?** Article 21 (Right to Life) and the **PIL judgment of the Supreme Court** necessitated a dedicated, fast-track quasi-judicial body for environmental matters.
- **Salient Features:**
    - Establishes the **NGT** for the effective and expeditious disposal of cases relating to **environmental protection, conservation of forests and natural resources.**
    - The NGT has **5 seats** (one principal bench at New Delhi + 4 regional benches) with technical and judicial members.
- **How it works:** It adjudicates disputes in a summary manner and orders relief/compensation for environmental damage. Civil courts are **barred** from entertaining cases on the same subject.

### 8. Solid Waste Management Rules, 2016 (and Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016)

- **Why enacted?** To modernise the older 2000 Municipal Solid Waste Rules and address the **escalating plastic pollution crisis.**
- **Salient Features:**
    - Applies to **every urban local body** and waste generator.
    - Introduces the concept of **"Segregation at Source"** (wet/dry/bio-medical waste), extended producer responsibility (EPR), and phasing out of multi-layered plastics.
- **How it works:** Local bodies are responsible for collection, segregation, transportation, and processing of solid waste.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Do NOT confuse the **Water Cess Act, 1977** with the **Water Act, 1974**. The Cess Act is a *revenue-generating* law (imposes a cess to incentivise water treatment), whereas the Water Act is the *regulatory* law.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagram & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Hierarchy of Indian Environmental Regulatory Framework

The Mermaid block below maps the **functional architecture flow** of how the laws, bodies, and enforcement mechanisms interrelate:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Constitution of India Art 48A and 51A g"]:::root
    nodeB["Parliament of India Legislative Authority"]:::level1
    nodeC["Ministry of Environment Forest and Climate Change MoEFCC"]:::level1
    nodeD["Central Pollution Control Board CPCB"]:::level2
    nodeE["State Pollution Control Boards SPCBs"]:::level2
    nodeF["National Green Tribunal NGT Adjudication"]:::level2
    nodeG["Key Statutes"]:::law
    nodeH["Water Act 1974"]:::law
    nodeI["Air Act 1981"]:::law
    nodeJ["Environment Protection Act 1986 Umbrella"]:::law
    nodeK["Wildlife Act 1972"]:::law
    nodeL["Forest Conservation Act 1980"]:::law
    nodeM["NGT Act 2010"]:::law
    nodeN["Rules and Notifications"]:::rule
    nodeO["EIA Notification 2006"]:::rule
    nodeP["Hazardous Waste Rules 2016"]:::rule
    nodeQ["Solid Waste Rules 2016"]:::rule
    nodeR["Plastic Waste Rules 2016"]:::rule
    nodeS["Industries and Project Proponents"]:::enduser
    nodeT["Consent CTE and CTO from SPCB"]:::enduser

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeG --> nodeJ
    nodeG --> nodeK
    nodeG --> nodeL

    classDef root fill:#1f3a68,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef level1 fill:#3a6fb1,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```
