# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Short Answer) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: List any six qualities of a good construction material.**

**Model Answer:**

A good construction material should possess the following qualities:

1. **Strength** — Adequate resistance to compressive, tensile, and shear stresses.
2. **Durability** — Ability to withstand weathering, chemical attack, and abrasion over its service life.
3. **Workability** — Ease of handling, mixing, transporting, placing, and finishing (especially for fresh concrete).
4. **Economy** — Cost-effective in terms of initial cost and life-cycle maintenance.
5. **Availability** — Readily procurable in required quantities at the project site.
6. **Fire Resistance** — Should not lose structural integrity when exposed to high temperatures.

> [!NOTE]
> Additional qualities include dimensional stability, impermeability, aesthetic appeal, and sustainability.

**Marks Distribution:** ½ mark each × 6 = **3 marks**.

---

### Question 2 (Short Answer) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Distinguish between natural and artificial construction materials with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Natural Materials | Artificial Materials |
|:-------|:------------------|:---------------------|
| **Source** | Obtained directly from nature with minimal processing | Manufactured through chemical/physical processes |
| **Examples** | Stone, Timber, Sand, Soil | Cement, Bricks, Concrete, Steel, Glass |
| **Cost** | Generally lower (no manufacturing) | Higher (processing cost involved) |
| **Quality Control** | Variable, depends on natural source | Highly controllable |
| **Environmental Impact** | Often lower embodied energy | Higher, but recyclable |

**Marks Distribution:** Distinction (1 mark) + 4 examples (½ mark each) = **3 marks**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a) Classify construction materials in detail. Add a note on the qualities required for a good building stone. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Classification of Construction Materials:**

Construction materials can be classified on multiple bases:

**1. By Origin:**
- **Natural Materials:** Stone, Timber, Sand, Soil, Clay.
- **Artificial Materials:** Cement, Bricks, Concrete, Steel, Glass, Paints, Plastics, Bitumen.
- **Composite Materials:** RCC, PSC, Plywood, FRP rebars.
- **Specialty Materials:** Self-healing concrete, aerogels, shape-memory alloys.

**2. By Function:**
- **Structural:** Stone, brick, concrete, steel, timber.
- **Binding:** Cement, lime, bitumen.
- **Finishing:** Paints, plaster, tiles, glass.
- **Insulating:** Cork, foam, glass wool.
- **Reinforcing:** TMT bars, FRP.

**3. By Chemical Nature:**
- Organic (timber, plastics, bitumen) vs. Inorganic (stone, cement, steel).

---

**Qualities of a Good Building Stone (Building Stone Requirements):**

A good building stone must possess:

1. **High Compressive Strength** — Generally ≥ 100 N/mm² for heavy engineering works.
2. **Durability** — Should resist weathering, frost, and chemical attack.
3. **Hardness and Toughness** — Resistance to abrasive forces.
4. **Specific Gravity** — Should be ≥ **2.7** for general use.
5. **Low Water Absorption** — Porous stones weather rapidly; absorption ≤ **0.6%** (by weight) is desirable.
6. **Uniform Texture and Color** — For aesthetic consistency.
7. **Free from Defects** — No cracks, veins, cavities, or patches of soft material.
8. **Easy to Quarry, Dress, and Transport** — Economy of construction.
9. **Good Fire Resistance** — Should not disintegrate or split when heated.
10. **Resistant to Atmospheric Action** — Should not be affected by rain, wind, CO₂, SO₂, etc.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** Granite is the strongest natural building stone (compressive strength ≈ 100–250 MPa), while limestone is the weakest (≈ 30–70 MPa). Marble is preferred for decorative work due to its polishability.

**Marks Distribution:** Classification (4 marks) + Qualities of stone (3 marks).

---

**(b) Explain with neat sketches the standard tests conducted on cement in the laboratory. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The following standard tests are conducted on cement as per IS 4031:

**1. Fineness Test (Sieve Method / Air Permeability):**
- **Principle:** Measures particle size of cement.
- **Procedure:** 100 g of cement is placed on **IS Sieve No. 9 (90 microns)** and sieved for 15 minutes. Residue should not exceed **10%** for OPC.
- **Significance:** Finer cement hydrates faster, gains early strength.

**2. Standard Consistency Test (Vicat Apparatus):**
- **Purpose:** Determines the quantity of water required to produce a cement paste of *standard consistency*.
- **Procedure:** Cement paste of varying w/c is prepared; the plunger (10 mm dia, 50 mm long) penetrates to a depth of **33–35 mm** from the top — this is the standard consistency.
- **Significance:** Used to fix water content for setting time and soundness tests.

**3. Initial and Final Setting Time Test:**
- **Initial Setting Time:** Time elapsed between water addition and the needle penetrating to a depth of **33–35 mm** in the paste. **Not less than 30 minutes** (IS 269).
- **Final Setting Time:** Time when the needle does not pierce the paste more than **0.5 mm**. **Not more than 600 minutes (10 hours).**

**4. Soundness Test (Le-Chatelier Method):**
- **Purpose:** Detects unsoundness due to free lime or magnesia.
- **Procedure:** Cement paste is molded in a Le-Chatelier mold, immersed in water at 27 ± 2 °C, and the expansion is measured.
- **Acceptable Limit:** Expansion ≤ **10 mm** for OPC.

**5. Compressive Strength Test:**
- Cement + standard sand (1:3 ratio), w/c = 0.5; cast into **70.6 mm cubes**.
- Tested in compression testing machine at **3, 7, and 28 days**.
- OPC 43 grade requires ≥ **43 N/mm²** at 28 days; OPC 53 requires ≥ **53 N/mm²**.

**6. Tensile Strength Test (Optional):**
- Briquette specimens tested in a standard tensile testing machine.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Soundness is one of the most critical tests — a cement that fails soundness will cause expansion in hardened concrete, leading to cracking and structural failure.

**Marks Distribution:** 1 mark each for 6 tests + 1 mark for conclusion/significance.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: (a) Describe the classification of stones based on their geological origin. Mention the uses of each class with suitable examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Geological Classification of Stones:**

**1. Igneous Rocks (Primary Rocks):**
- **Formation:** Solidified from molten magma or lava.
- **Characteristics:** Very strong, dense, crystalline, high durability, no stratification.
- **Types:**
  - **Intrusive (Plutonic):** Cooled slowly inside earth → coarse-grained (e.g., **Granite, Syenite, Diorite**).
  - **Extrusive (Volcanic):** Cooled rapidly on surface → fine-grained (e.g., **Basalt, Trap**).
- **Uses:** Foundations, bridge piers, columns, flooring, road metal, railway ballast.

**2. Sedimentary Rocks (Secondary Rocks):**
- **Formation:** Deposited in layers (strata) by water, wind, or ice; compacted and cemented over time.
- **Characteristics:** Stratified, porous, easy to dress, fossil-bearing.
- **Types:**
  - **Mechanically formed:** Sandstone, Gravel, Claystone.
  - **Chemically formed:** Limestone, Gypsum, Rock Salt.
  - **Organically formed:** Chalk, Coal, Lignite.
- **Uses:** Ordinary masonry, lime production (limestone), flagstones, ornamental work.

**3. Metamorphic Rocks:**
- **Formation:** Original igneous or sedimentary rocks transformed by heat and pressure.
- **Characteristics:** Dense, crystalline, foliated or non-foliated, very strong.
- **Types:**
  - **Foliated:** Slate, Schist, Gneiss.
  - **Non-foliated:** Marble, Quartzite.
- **Uses:**
  - **Marble:** Decorative flooring, cladding, sculpture.
  - **Slate:** Roofing, paving, electrical switchboards (insulator).
  - **Quartzite:** Flooring, retaining walls, railway ballast.

**Marks Distribution:** 3 classes × (formation + characteristics + 1 example + 1 use) ≈ 7 marks.

---

**(b) Explain the slump cone test for concrete workability in detail with a labeled diagram. Discuss the different types of slump. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Slump Cone Test (IS 1199):**

**Aim:** To determine the **workability** (consistency) of fresh concrete.

**Apparatus:**
- Slump cone: frustum of height **300 mm**, top diameter **100 mm**, bottom diameter **200 mm**.
- Tamping rod: **16 mm dia, 600 mm long**, with a bullet-nosed tip.
- Base plate (non-absorbent), measuring scale, scoop.

**Procedure:**

1. The cone is placed on a smooth, horizontal, non-absorbent base plate.
2. Fresh concrete is filled in **4 equal layers**, each layer tamped with **25 strokes** of the tamping rod (uniformly distributed).
3. After the top layer is rodded, the surface is struck off level with a trowel.
4. The cone is **lifted vertically and slowly** (in 5–10 seconds).
5. The concrete subsides — the **difference in height** between the original cone (300 mm) and the displaced concrete is measured as the **slump value** in mm.

**Slump Values (Typical):**

| Concrete Type | Slump (mm) |
|:--------------|:-----------|
| Very dry (roads) | 0–25 |
| Low workability (foundations) | 25–50 |
| Medium workability (RCC beams/slabs) | 50–100 |
| High workability (columns, thin sections) | 100–175 |

---

**Types of Slump:**

1. **True Slump:** Concrete subsides uniformly, retaining a symmetric shape. → Indicates good workability and cohesive mix. ✅
2. **Shear Slump:** Concrete slides down on one side, leaving a non-symmetric shape. → Indicates a non-cohesive, harsh mix; test should be repeated. ⚠️
3. **Collapse Slump:** Concrete completely collapses, spreading widely. → Indicates very wet, over-workable mix; unsuitable. ❌

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A shear slump is **not acceptable** as a measure of workability. Students often lose 1 mark by stating "the slump is ___ mm" without identifying the slump type. Always state: *"The observed slump is a **true slump** of value X mm."*

**Marks Distribution:** Procedure (3) + Slump values (2) + Types of slump (2).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "setting" with "hardening"** — Setting is the chemical stiffening (gel formation); hardening is strength development.
> 2. **Wrong units** — Always use **N/mm² (MPa)** for strength; **mm** for slump; **%** for water absorption.
> 3. **Skipping the IS code reference** — Examiners award extra marks for citing IS 4031, IS 516, IS 1199, etc.
> 4. **Mixing up rock types** — Slate is **metamorphic**, not sedimentary! Granite is **igneous**, not metamorphic!
> 5. **No diagram for slump test** — Always draw a labeled diagram (even schematic) to score full marks.
> 6. **Abrams' Law without assumptions** — Always state "for the same materials and curing conditions."
> 7. **Confusing "soundness" with "strength"** — Soundness is about *dimensional stability*, not load capacity.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📝

- **Classification Axes:** Origin (Natural/Artificial/Composite/Specialty), Function (Structural/Binding/Finishing/Insulating), Chemical (Organic/Inorganic).
- **Stone Hierarchy (Strength):** Igneous > Metamorphic > Sedimentary (in general).
- **Cement Grades (OPC):** 33, 43, 53 — based on 28-day compressive strength in N/mm².
- **Concrete Grades:** M20, M25, M30 — $f_{ck}$ in N/mm² (cube strength).
- **Setting Time of OPC:** Initial ≥ 30 min, Final ≤ 600 min.
- **Soundness Limit:** ≤ 10 mm expansion (Le-Chatelier, IS 4031 Part 3).
- **Brick 1st Class:** Compressive strength ≥ 3.5 N/mm², water absorption ≤ 20%, ringing sound.
- **Slump Test:** Only **true slump** is acceptable; shear slump = repeat the test.
- **Abrams' Law:** $f_{c}' = A / B^{w/c}$ — strength ↓ as w/c ↑.
- **TMT Steel Grades:** Fe415, Fe500, Fe550 — yield strength in N/mm².
- **Bitumen Grades (Viscosity Grade):** VG-10, VG-30, VG-40 — used in road construction.
- **IS Codes to Memorize:** IS 269 (OPC), IS 4031 (cement tests), IS 516 (concrete strength), IS 1199 (fresh concrete), IS 1786 (TMT bars), IS 1077 (bricks), IS 3495 (brick tests), IS 383 (aggregates).

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"Classify → Qualify → Test → Use."* Every question on this module, regardless of phrasing, funnels back to these four pillars. Master the matrix in Section 3.1 and you master Module 4. 🎯
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cement Concrete: Constituent Materials, Properties, and Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDEST203 - BASIC MECHANICAL AND CIVIL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cement Concrete: Constituent Materials, Properties, and Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Cement Concrete

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cement Concrete** is a composite construction material manufactured by carefully mixing four primary ingredients — **Portland Cement**, **Fine Aggregate (sand)**, **Coarse Aggregate (gravel/crushed stone)**, and **Water** — in predetermined proportions, which hardens (sets and gains strength) over time through a chemical reaction called **hydration**.

Mathematically, the hardened mass may be expressed as a composite system:

$$\text{Concrete} = f(\text{Cement} + \text{Fine Aggregate} + \text{Coarse Aggregate} + \text{Water} \pm \text{Admixtures})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (GDEST203 – Module 4):**
> Students must clearly distinguish between the *ingredients* (raw constituents) and the *properties* (fresh + hardened state behaviour) of cement concrete. Exam questions frequently interlink these two concepts (e.g., "How does water-cement ratio affect workability AND strength?").

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are baking a **cake** in your kitchen.

| Cake Baking Analogy | Civil Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Flour (the binder holding everything) | **Cement** (the binder of concrete) |
| Sugar + milk powder (fills tiny gaps) | **Fine Aggregate / Sand** (fills micro-voids) |
| Chocolate chips or nuts (gives bulk & structure) | **Coarse Aggregate / Gravel** (provides bulk & skeleton) |
| Milk (activates the flour, makes batter pourable) | **Water** (triggers hydration; controls workability) |
| Optional: vanilla essence, baking soda | **Admixtures** (chemical modifiers) |

Just as the **water-to-flour ratio** decides whether your cake is rock-hard or crumbly, the **water-to-cement ratio** decides whether concrete is strong or weak. Too little water → dry, unworkable mix. Too much water → soupy mix, low strength.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Standard Metric:** Concrete's density in normal (ordinary) form is approximately **2400 kg/m³ (or 24 kN/m³)**. This is the most-cited value in KTU numerical problems involving dead load calculations (DL = Volume × Density).

---

## 1.3 Why is Cement Concrete So Widely Used?

1. **High compressive strength** (typically 20–60 MPa for structural grades).
2. **Mouldability** — can be cast into any desired shape before it hardens.
3. **Economical** — ingredients (sand, gravel, water) are locally available almost everywhere.
4. **Durability** — resists weathering, fire, and chemical attack when properly mixed.
5. **Versatility** — combined with steel bars gives **Reinforced Cement Concrete (RCC)**, the backbone of modern infrastructure.

---

## 1.4 Key Vocabulary You Must Know Before Proceeding

| Term | Meaning (KTU-style) |
|---|---|
| **Cement** | A fine grey powder that reacts with water to form a binder (calcium silicate hydrate gel). |
| **Aggregate** | Inert granular material (sand + gravel) embedded in the cement paste. |
| **Hydration** | The chemical reaction between cement compounds and water forming C-S-H gel. |
| **Workability** | Ease with which fresh concrete can be mixed, transported, placed, and compacted. |
| **Setting** | The process of losing plasticity (initial set ≥ 30 min, final set ≤ 600 min for OPC). |
| **Strength** | Ability of hardened concrete to resist load (compressive/tensile/flexural). |
| **Grade of Concrete** | Characteristic compressive strength of 150 mm cube at 28 days (e.g., M20 = 20 MPa). |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four (Plus One) Constituent Materials — A Structured Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Cement (The Active Binder)

- **Function:** Acts as the binding agent. When mixed with water, it forms a paste that hardens and glues aggregates together.
- **Type recommended by IS 456:2000:** **Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC)** of grades **OPC 43** and **OPC 53**.
- **Why OPC?** Provides predictable strength development and is widely tested.
- **Storage tip (exam-favourite):** Cement must be stored in a **dry, moisture-free godown**, on a raised wooden platform, in stacks not exceeding **10–12 bags** high, to prevent air-set (lumps).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** "Why should cement be tested before use?" → Because it absorbs moisture from air and loses strength progressively. A field test called the *float test* or *hand-insertion test* can be performed.

### 2.1.2 Fine Aggregate (Sand)

- **Definition:** Aggregate passing through a **4.75 mm IS sieve** with most particles between 75 µm and 4.75 mm.
- **Types:**
  - **Natural sand** — from river beds (rounded grains, better workability).
  - **Manufactured sand (M-sand)** — crushed rock (angular grains, better interlocking, now IS 383 recommended).
- **Quality requirement:** Should be **clean, sharp, well-graded**, free from silt, clay, organic matter, and salts.
- **Bulking of sand (HIGH-YIELD TOPIC):** Sand increases in volume (bulks) due to moisture films around particles. Maximum bulking occurs at moisture content of **5–8 %** and can be up to **20–30 %**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bulking phenomenon formula:**
> $$\text{Bulking \%} = \frac{V_w - V_d}{V_d} \times 100$$
> where $V_w$ = volume of moist sand, $V_d$ = volume of dry sand. This causes the famous *site mistake* of under-sanding concrete if volumetric batching is used without correction.

### 2.1.3 Coarse Aggregate (Gravel / Crushed Stone)

- **Definition:** Aggregate retained on a **4.75 mm IS sieve**, generally between 10 mm and 40 mm for normal RCC work.
- **Maximum size rules (IS 456):**
  - ≤ **¼ × minimum thickness of member**
  - ≤ **⅕ × minimum clear span** for slabs
  - ≤ **¾ × minimum clear spacing** between reinforcement bars
- **Shape preference:** Angular crushed aggregates give higher bond strength than rounded river gravel.
- **Properties required:** Hard, dense, durable, free from flaky/elongated particles (limit: flaky + elongated ≤ 30–40 %).

### 2.1.4 Water (The Hidden Quality Controller)

- **Function:** (a) Hydrates cement, (b) provides workability to the mix.
- **Potable water** is generally considered suitable for mixing AND curing.
- **Acceptance criteria (IS 456):**
  - pH: not less than **6**
  - Sulphates (as SO₃): ≤ **400 mg/L**
  - Chlorides: ≤ **500 mg/L** for RCC (≤ 2000 mg/L for PCC)
  - Organic solids: ≤ **200 mg/L**
  - Total suspended solids: ≤ **2000 mg/L**
- **Impure water consequences:** Reduces strength by up to **20–30 %**, causes efflorescence, and corrodes steel reinforcement.

### 2.1.5 Admixtures (Optional, Modern-Day Performance Enhancers)

These are chemicals added in small quantities (≤ 5 % by weight of cement) to **modify** properties:

| Admixture Type | Effect | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Plasticizer** | Increases workability without extra water | Lignosulphonates |
| **Superplasticizer** | High-range water reduction (12–30 %) | Polycarboxylates |
| **Accelerator** | Speeds up setting & early strength | CaCl₂ (corrosive to steel!) |
| **Retarder** | Delays setting (useful in hot weather / long transport) | Sugar, Citric acid |
| **Air-entraining agent** | Creates tiny bubbles → freeze-thaw resistance | Vinsol resin |
| **Water-proofing agent** | Reduces permeability | Silicates, stearates |

---

## 2.2 Properties of Fresh Concrete

### 2.2.1 Workability
**Definition (IS 6461):** The property of fresh concrete that determines the ease with which it can be mixed, transported, placed, compacted, and finished **without segregation**.

**Why it matters:** Concrete must be fluid enough to fill the formwork and surround reinforcement, but stiff enough to hold its shape and not segregate.

**Quantitative measurement — The Slump Test (IS 7320):**

$$\text{Slump (mm)} = \text{Height of mould} - \text{Height of subsided concrete}$$
$$\text{Slump} = 300 \text{ mm} - H_{\text{after}}$$

**Slump values for different works:**

| Degree of Workability | Slump (mm) | Suitable For |
|---|---|---|
| Very low | 0 – 25 | Vibrated concrete in roads, pavements |
| Low | 25 – 50 | Mass concrete, lightly reinforced sections |
| Medium | 50 – 100 | Beams, slabs with normal reinforcement |
| High | 100 – 150 | Heavily reinforced sections, tremie concrete |

> [!NOTE]
> **Types of Slump (exam-graphic-favourite):** True slump, Shear slump, Collapse slump. Only *true slump* is acceptable for measurement. *Collapse slump* indicates a too-wet mix.

**Other workability tests (know all 4):**
- **Compaction Factor Test** (IS 5515) → for low-workability mixes; ratio = wt. of partially compacted / wt. of fully compacted.
- **Vee-Bee Consistometer Test** → measures time (in seconds) to remould; lower time = higher workability.
- **Flow Test** → for very wet mixes; % flow = spread diameter increase.

### 2.2.2 Consistency
Closely related to workability; a measure of the fluidity of fresh concrete, often measured by the **slump** or **flow**.

### 2.2.3 Segregation
**Definition:** Separation of constituent materials (heavier coarse aggregate sinks, lighter cement paste floats) due to improper mixing, transport, or placing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Prevention:** Avoid excessive vibration, drop height > 1.5 m, use well-graded aggregates, and adequate cement content.

### 2.2.4 Bleeding
**Definition:** Upward movement of water in fresh concrete after placing, due to settlement of heavier solids. Results in a layer of water on top.
- **Harm:** Weakens top surface, increases permeability, causes laitance.
- **Benefit (slight):** Helps with re-curing of the top surface in dry conditions.

### 2.2.5 Setting Time
- **Initial Setting Time:** ≥ **30 minutes** (gives time to mix, transport, place).
- **Final Setting Time:** ≤ **600 minutes (10 hours)** (so formwork can be removed / finishing done).

---

## 2.3 Properties of Hardened Concrete

### 2.3.1 Compressive Strength
The **single most important mechanical property**.

- **Standard test:** Cube of **150 mm × 150 mm × 150 mm** (or cylinder 150 mm dia × 300 mm).
- **Curing age for design strength:** **28 days**.
- **Characteristic strength:** Strength below which not more than **5 %** of test results are expected to fall.

$$\text{Grade of concrete} = \text{Characteristic compressive strength at 28 days, in MPa}$$

Examples: M20 = 20 MPa, M25 = 25 MPa, M30 = 30 MPa, M40 = 40 MPa.

**Compressive strength formula:**

$$f_c = \frac{P}{A}$$

where $f_c$ = compressive strength (MPa), $P$ = crushing load (N), $A$ = cross-sectional area (mm²).

### 2.3.2 Tensile Strength
Concrete is weak in tension (~10 % of compressive strength).
- **Direct tensile test** is hard → use **Split Tensile Test (Brazilian test)** on cylinder.
- **Modulus of rupture (flexural strength):** $f_{cr} = 0.7 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ (per IS 456 for design).

**IS Code empirical relationship (for normal concrete):**

$$f_{t,\text{split}} = 0.6 \text{ to } 0.7 \cdot \sqrt{f_{ck}}$$

$$f_{cr,\text{flexural}} = 0.7 \cdot \sqrt{f_{ck}}$$

### 2.3.3 Modulus of Elasticity (IS 456:2000)

$$E_c = 5000 \cdot \sqrt{f_{ck}} \quad \text{(in MPa)}$$

This is the **MOST-QUOTED empirical formula** in KTU design questions.

### 2.3.4 Durability
Concrete must resist:
- **Weathering** (freeze-thaw, wet-dry cycles).
- **Chemical attack** (sulphates, chlorides → corrode rebar).
- **Abrasion** (floor surfaces, hydraulic structures).

Minimum cement content (per IS 456) for various exposure conditions ranges from **300 kg/m³** (mild) to **400+ kg/m³** (very severe).

### 2.3.5 Impermeability
Tightly related to w/c ratio. **Lower w/c → lower permeability → more durable.** Target: w/c ≤ 0.45 for severe exposure.

### 2.3.6 Shrinkage & Creep
- **Shrinkage:** Volume reduction on drying (~0.04 % typical).
- **Creep:** Time-dependent increase in strain under sustained load.

---

## 2.4 Types of Cement Concrete

### 2.4.1 Based on Structural Function

| Type | Definition | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Plain Cement Concrete (PCC)** | Concrete **without** any reinforcement. | Foundations, flooring, pavements, base for RCC. |
| **Reinforced Cement Concrete (RCC)** | Concrete reinforced with steel bars/mesh to carry tensile loads. | Beams, columns, slabs, bridges, buildings. |
| **Pre-stressed Concrete** | Concrete in which internal compressive stresses are induced (by tendons) before external load application. | Long-span bridges, railway sleepers, water tanks. |
| **Precast Concrete** | Cast at factory, transported, and assembled at site. | Boundary walls, lintels, staircase units. |
| **Cast-in-situ Concrete** | Poured and cured at the construction site. | Foundations, monolithic structures. |

### 2.4.2 Based on Density

| Type | Density (kg/m³) | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Normal-weight concrete** | 2200 – 2600 | All general construction |
| **Lightweight concrete** | 300 – 1850 | Partition walls, insulation |
| **Heavy-weight concrete** | 3000 – 6400 | Radiation shielding (nuclear plants) |

### 2.4.3 Based on Performance / Special Types

- **Ready-Mix Concrete (RMC):** Batched at a central plant and delivered by agitator trucks. Quality-controlled, used for large projects.
- **High-Strength Concrete (HSC):** $f_{ck} \geq$ 60 MPa, uses superplasticizers + silica fume.
- **High-Performance Concrete (HPC):** Engineered for durability + strength.
- **Self-Compacting Concrete (SCC):** Highly flowable, compacts under its own weight (no vibration needed).
- **Fibre-Reinforced Concrete (FRC):** Contains short discrete fibres (steel, glass, polypropylene) for crack resistance.
- **Pumped Concrete:** Designed for pipeline transport (slump 100–150 mm).
- **Shotcrete / Gunite:** Sprayed pneumatically onto surfaces (tunnel linings, swimming pools).
- **Polymer Concrete:** Binder replaced by polymer resins (chemical-resistant).
- **Geo-polymer Concrete:** Zero-cement; activated fly-ash binder (eco-friendly, KTU high-yield topic).

### 2.4.4 IS Standard Mix Proportions (Nominal Mix – per IS 456)

| Grade of Concrete | Mix Ratio (Cement : Sand : Aggregate) | w/c (max) |
|---|---|---|
| M5 | 1 : 5 : 10 | 0.60 |
| M7.5 | 1 : 4 : 8 | 0.60 |
| M10 | 1 : 3 : 6 | 0.60 |
| M15 | 1 : 2 : 4 | 0.60 |
| M20 | 1 : 1.5 : 3 | 0.55 |
| M25 | 1 : 1 : 2 | 0.50 |

> For M30 and above → **Design Mix is mandatory** (not nominal).

---

## 2.5 The Single Most Important Empirical Law — Abrams' Law

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Duff A. Abrams' Law (1918):** "For a given set of materials, the strength of concrete depends **primarily** and **inversely** on the water-cement ratio."

$$\boxed{f_c = \frac{A}{B^{w/c}}}$$

where $f_c$ = compressive strength, $w/c$ = water-cement ratio, $A, B$ = empirical constants (depend on cement type, age, curing).

**Practical implication (KTU-favourite):**
- Increase in $w/c$ from 0.45 to 0.60 → strength drops by **~30 %**.
- **Rule of thumb:** For durable RCC, $w/c \le 0.45$ (severe exposure) to $0.55$ (moderate).

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_c = P / A$ | Compressive strength, MPa |
| 2 | $f_{cr} = 0.7 \cdot \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | Flexural strength (IS 456), MPa |
| 3 | $f_t = 0.6\text{–}0.7 \cdot \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | Split tensile strength, MPa |
| 4 | $E_c = 5000 \cdot \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | Modulus of Elasticity, MPa |
| 5 | $f_c = A / B^{w/c}$ | Abrams' law |
| 6 | $\text{Bulking \%} = \dfrac{V_w - V_d}{V_d} \times 100$ | Sand bulking |
| 7 | $\text{Density of concrete} \approx 2400 \text{ kg/m}^3$ | Standard unit weight, kg/m³ |
| 8 | $\text{Slump} = 300 \text{ mm} - H_{\text{after}}$ | Slump test |
| 9 | $\text{Compaction Factor} = \dfrac{W_p}{W_f}$ | W_p = partially compacted, W_f = fully compacted |
| 10 | $\text{Absolute Volume} = \dfrac{\text{Mass}}{\text{Specific Gravity} \cdot \rho_w}$ | Mix design by absolute volume |

> **Rule:** $f_{ck}$ is always in **MPa**; remember $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The water-cement ratio governs **almost every** downstream decision in construction:
- Durability of a **coastal bridge** (low w/c = resistant to chloride ingress).
- Impermeability of a **water tank** (low w/c = watertight).
- Pumpability of concrete in **high-rise construction** (specific w/c + superplasticizer).
- Cost optimisation: every 0.01 reduction in w/c above the threshold can cost one extra bag of cement per cubic metre — a **huge cost** for mega-projects.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Quantifying the Effect of Water-Cement Ratio Using Abrams' Law

**Statement of the problem:**
A concrete mix has Abrams' constants $A = 96$ MPa and $B = 4$. Compute and compare the 28-day compressive strengths for two water-cement ratios: 0.40 and 0.60.

### Step 1 — State Abrams' Law
The fundamental relation as established experimentally by Abrams (1918) is:

$$f_c = \frac{A}{B^{w/c}}$$

where $f_c$ is the 28-day compressive strength (MPa), $A$ and $B$ are empirical constants determined for the specific cement-aggregate combination, and $w/c$ is the water-cement ratio by mass.

### Step 2 — Substitute the given constants

$$A = 96 \text{ MPa}, \quad B = 4$$

The general form for any $w/c$ ratio is:

$$f_c = \frac{96}{4^{w/c}}$$

### Step 3 — Compute $f_c$ for $w/c = 0.40$

$$f_{c1} = \frac{96}{4^{0.40}}$$

First compute $4^{0.40}$:

$$4^{0.40} = e^{0.40 \cdot \ln 4} = e^{0.40 \cdot 1.3863} = e^{0.5545} = 1.7411$$

Therefore:

$$f_{c1} = \frac{96}{1.7411} = 55.14 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 4 — Compute $f_c$ for $w/c = 0.60$

$$f_{c2} = \frac{96}{4^{0.60}}$$

Compute $4^{0.60}$:

$$4^{0.60} = e^{0.60 \cdot \ln 4} = e^{0.60 \cdot 1.3863} = e^{0.8318} = 2.2974$$

Therefore:

$$f_{c2} = \frac{96}{2.2974} = 41.79 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 5 — Compare and interpret

$$\Delta f_c = f_{c1} - f_{c2} = 55.14 - 41.79 = 13.35 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\% \text{Drop} = \frac{\Delta f_c}{f_{c1}} \times 100 = \frac{13.35}{55.14} \times 100 = 24.21 \text{ \%}$$

**Interpretation:** Increasing the $w/c$ from 0.40 to 0.60 — a modest 50 % increase in water — causes a **24 % drop in strength**. This is *exactly* why site engineers must control $w/c$ within ±0.02 of design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mix Proportion by Absolute Volume Method (IS 10262 — Simplified)

**Statement of the problem:**
Design a mix for **M20 grade** concrete (1 m³) using the **absolute volume method** (BIS simplified approach). Given:
- Target mean strength $f_{ck,target} = 26.6$ MPa
- Specific gravity of cement $G_c = 3.15$
- Specific gravity of sand $G_s = 2.65$
- Specific gravity of coarse aggregate $G_{ca} = 2.70$
- Water absorption of aggregates $\approx 0$ % (assumed SSD)
- Free water-cement ratio $w/c = 0.50$ (IS 456 max for M20)
- Water content $W = 186$ kg/m³ (for 20 mm aggregate, slump 25–50 mm)
- Sand as % of total aggregate (entrapped air considered) $\approx 35$ %

### Step 1 — Calculate cement content
Cement content follows directly from water content and $w/c$:

$$\text{Cement content} = \frac{W}{w/c} = \frac{186}{0.50} = 372 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

Check IS 456 minimum cement content: for mild exposure, minimum = 300 kg/m³.
$372 \text{ kg/m}^3 \ge 300 \text{ kg/m}^3$ → **OK, safe**.

### Step 2 — Compute the absolute volume of cement paste (water + cement)

The absolute volume of any material is given by:

$$V_i = \frac{M_i}{G_i \cdot \rho_w}$$

where $M_i$ is mass (kg), $G_i$ is specific gravity, and $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³ is the density of water.

**Volume of cement:**

$$V_c = \frac{372}{3.15 \cdot 1000} = \frac{372}{3150} = 0.1181 \text{ m}^3$$

**Volume of water:**

$$V_w = \frac{186}{1.00 \cdot 1000} = 0.1860 \text{ m}^3$$

**Volume of cement + water (paste):**

$$V_{\text{paste}} = 0.1181 + 0.1860 = 0.3041 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 3 — Compute volume of total aggregate
The remaining volume (after accounting for paste and entrapped air) is occupied by aggregates.

Assume entrapped air content for 20 mm aggregate = **2 %** of total volume = 0.02 m³.

Therefore:

$$V_{\text{agg,total}} = 1 - V_{\text{paste}} - V_{\text{air}} = 1 - 0.3041 - 0.02 = 0.6759 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 4 — Split into fine and coarse aggregate (using 35 % sand)
The proportion of sand (fine aggregate) to total aggregate by absolute volume is given as 0.35.

$$V_{\text{sand}} = 0.35 \times 0.6759 = 0.2366 \text{ m}^3$$

$$V_{\text{CA}} = 0.65 \times 0.6759 = 0.4393 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 5 — Convert volumes back to masses
Apply the absolute volume equation in reverse:

**Mass of sand:**

$$M_{\text{sand}} = V_{\text{sand}} \cdot G_s \cdot \rho_w = 0.2366 \times 2.65 \times 1000 = 627.0 \text{ kg}$$

**Mass of coarse aggregate:**

$$M_{\text{CA}} = V_{\text{CA}} \cdot G_{ca} \cdot \rho_w = 0.4393 \times 2.70 \times 1000 = 1186.1 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 6 — Final mix proportion by weight

$$\text{Cement : Sand : Coarse Aggregate} = 372 : 627 : 1186$$

Dividing through by 372:

$$\text{Mix ratio} = 1 : 1.69 : 3.19 \quad (w/c = 0.50)$$

### Step 7 — Per-bag (50 kg) calculation
A site-friendly expression is the quantity per **one bag of cement**:

$$\text{Cement} = 50 \text{ kg} = 1 \text{ bag}$$

$$\text{Sand} = 627 \cdot \frac{50}{372} = 84.27 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{Coarse Aggregate} = 1186 \cdot \frac{50}{372} = 159.4 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{Water} = 0.50 \times 50 = 25 \text{ litres}$$

> **For 1 m³ of M20 concrete** you need ≈ **7.44 bags of cement** + 627 kg sand + 1186 kg gravel + 186 L water.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Modulus of Elasticity from IS 456 Empirical Formula

**Statement of the problem:**
For M30 grade concrete, compute:
(a) Modulus of Elasticity $E_c$
(b) Split tensile strength
(c) Flexural (modulus of rupture) strength

Given: $f_{ck} = 30$ MPa (characteristic compressive strength).

### Part (a): Modulus of Elasticity

IS 456:2000, Clause 6.2.3.1 gives the empirical formula:

$$E_c = 5000 \cdot \sqrt{f_{ck}} \text{ MPa}$$

Substituting $f_{ck} = 30$ MPa:

$$E_c = 5000 \cdot \sqrt{30} = 5000 \cdot 5.4772 = 27386 \text{ MPa} \approx 27.39 \text{ GPa}$$

### Part (b): Split Tensile Strength

IS 5816 empirical relation:

$$f_{ct} = 0.6 \cdot \sqrt{f_{ck}}$$

$$f_{ct} = 0.6 \cdot \sqrt{30} = 0.6 \cdot 5.4772 = 3.286 \text{ MPa}$$

### Part (c): Flexural Strength (Modulus of Rupture)

IS 456 design relation:

$$f_{cr} = 0.7 \cdot \sqrt{f_{ck}}$$

$$f_{cr} = 0.7 \cdot \sqrt{30} = 0.7 \cdot 5.4772 = 3.834 \text{ MPa}$$

### Summary Table for M30

| Property | Formula | Value (MPa) | % of $f_{ck}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $f_{ck}$ | — | 30 | 100 % |
| $E_c$ | $5000 \cdot \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | 27386 (≈ 27.4 GPa) | — |
| $f_{ct}$ (split tensile) | $0.6 \cdot \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | 3.29 | 10.96 % |
| $f_{cr}$ (flexural) | $0.7 \cdot \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | 3.83 | 12.78 % |

> **General rule (for KTU viva):**
> $f_{ct} \approx 0.10 \cdot f_{ck}$ and $f_{cr} \approx 0.13 \cdot f_{ck}$ for normal-weight concrete.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Sand Bulking — Field Correction for Volume Batching

**Statement of the problem:**
A site engineer has a sand stockpile with moisture content = 6 %, and bulking is 20 % at this moisture. If the design requires **0.50 m³ of dry sand**, how much *moist* (bulked) sand should be measured in a volume box?

### Step 1 — Apply the bulking formula
The standard bulking formula is:

$$\text{Bulking \%} = \frac{V_{\text{moist}} - V_{\text{dry}}}{V_{\text{dry}}} \times 100$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the known bulking
Given Bulking % = 20 %, and the dry volume we need is $V_{\text{dry}} = 0.50 \text{ m}^3$:

$$20 = \frac{V_{\text{moist}} - 0.50}{0.50} \times 100$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $V_{\text{moist}}$

$$V_{\text{moist}} - 0.50 = 0.20 \times 0.50 = 0.10$$

$$V_{\text{moist}} = 0.50 + 0.10 = 0.60 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation
The engineer must actually measure **0.60 m³ of moist (bulked) sand** in the gauge box to deliver the design quantity of 0.50 m³ of dry sand. If the correction is ignored, the concrete will be **sand-deficient**, leading to:
- Harsh, unworkable mix
- Honeycombing
- Reduced strength and durability

> [!NOTE]
> **Field practice:** Most modern sites now use **weigh-batching** rather than volume-batching, eliminating bulking errors.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — How Does Each Constituent Affect Each Property?

| Constituent | Workability | Strength | Durability | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **↑ Cement** | ↑ (more paste lubricates) | ↑ (up to optimum) | ↑ | ↑↑ |
| **↑ Water** | ↑↑ (most powerful) | ↓↓ (via w/c) | ↓↓ | — (cheap but costly in strength loss) |
| **↑ Fine aggregate** | ↑ (up to a point) | ↓ beyond optimum | ↓ | slight ↑ |
| **↑ Coarse aggregate** | ↓ (rough texture) | ↑ (up to 65–70 % of vol.) | ↑ (less paste = less shrinkage) | slight ↑ |
| **Admixtures (plasticizer)** | ↑↑ without extra water | ↑↑ (lower w/c) | ↑ | ↑ (but saves cement) |

> This matrix is a **favourite viva cross-question topic** at KTU.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"List the four essential constituents of cement concrete. Mention the function of any two of them."**

**Model Answer:**

The four essential constituents of cement concrete are:

1. **Cement** (acts as the binding agent; reacts with water to form C-S-H gel that binds aggregates).
2. **Fine Aggregate (Sand)** (fills the micro-voids between coarse aggregates, contributes to workability and density).
3. **Coarse Aggregate (Gravel/Crushed stone)** (provides the structural skeleton, reduces shrinkage, reduces cost).
4. **Water** (triggers cement hydration and provides workability to the fresh mix).

Optionally a fifth — **Admixtures** — may be added to modify properties.

> **Examiner tip (3 marks):** Mention all 4 constituents clearly + state the function of 2 with one-line description. Avoid writing a long paragraph; bullet points fetch better marks.

---

### Question A2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**"What is Abrams' Law? Write the relationship."**

**Model Answer:**

**Abrams' Law (1918)** states that, *for a given set of materials, the compressive strength of concrete is inversely related to the water-cement ratio.* Strength decreases exponentially as the w/c ratio increases.

**Mathematical relationship:**

$$f_c = \frac{A}{B^{w/c}}$$

where $f_c$ is the 28-day compressive strength (MPa), $w/c$ is the water-cement ratio by mass, and $A, B$ are empirical constants depending on cement type, aggregate, age, and curing conditions.

**Significance:** This law is the foundation of mix design — a small change in w/c causes a large change in strength, which is why the w/c ratio is the most strictly controlled parameter on any construction site.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various **constituent materials of cement concrete** along with their **desirable properties and IS code requirements**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Describe the **properties of fresh and hardened concrete** with reference to IS 456 standards. (7 Marks)

---

### Question B — 14 Marks **[Alternative Choice for Internal Option]**

**(a)** With the help of a **flow diagram**, describe the **manufacturing process of cement concrete** from material selection to curing. List the **grades of concrete** as per IS 456. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Explain the **types of cement concrete** (PCC, RCC, Pre-stressed, RMC, HSC, SCC, Lightweight, FRC, Geo-polymer) and state the **field applications** of each. (7 Marks)

---

## 5.3 Model Solution — Question A(a) (7 Marks)

**Answer:**

**1. Cement (IS 269 / IS 8112 / IS 12269):**
- **Function:** Binding agent; forms C-S-H gel on hydration.
- **Desirable Properties:** Fineness (≥ 225 m²/kg for OPC 33, higher for OPC 43/53), setting time (initial ≥ 30 min, final ≤ 10 h), compressive strength (43 or 53 MPa at 28 days), soundness (≤ 10 mm Le-Chatelier expansion), low alkali content.
- **Storage:** Dry godown, raised platform, ≤ 12 bags high.

**2. Fine Aggregate (IS 383):**
- **Function:** Fills voids, contributes to workability, reduces cost.
- **Desirable Properties:** Clean, well-graded, sharp particles; silt + clay ≤ 5 % by weight; no organic matter; free from salts.
- **Special consideration:** Bulking of sand must be considered in volume batching.

**3. Coarse Aggregate (IS 383):**
- **Function:** Bulk, structural skeleton, reduces shrinkage.
- **Desirable Properties:** Hard, dense, durable, well-graded, angular, free from flaky/elongated particles.
- **Size rules (IS 456):** Max size ≤ ¼ × slab thickness, ≤ ⅕ × span (for slabs), ≤ ¾ × clear cover between bars.

**4. Water (IS 456, Cl. 5.4):**
- **Function:** Hydration + workability.
- **Quality criteria:** Potable; pH ≥ 6; sulphates (as SO₃) ≤ 400 mg/L; chlorides ≤ 500 mg/L (RCC) / 2000 mg/L (PCC); total dissolved solids ≤ 2000 mg/L.

**5. Admixtures (IS 9103):**
- **Function:** Modify fresh/hardened properties; used in small quantities (≤ 5 %).
- **Types:** Plasticizers, superplasticizers, accelerators, retarders, air-entrainers, water-proofing agents.

**Conclusion:** The *quality of each constituent directly governs the strength, durability, and economy* of the resulting concrete. Compromising on any one ingredient leads to poor structural performance.

---

## 5.4 Model Solution — Question A(b) (7 Marks)

**Answer:**

### A. Properties of Fresh Concrete

| Property | Definition | Test / Standard |
|---|---|---|
| **Workability** | Ease of mixing, placing, compacting | Slump Test (IS 7320), Compaction Factor (IS 5515), Vee-Bee |
| **Consistency** | Fluidity / wetness | Slump cone |
| **Segregation** | Separation of constituents | Visual |
| **Bleeding** | Water rising to surface | Visual |
| **Setting Time** | Initial ≥ 30 min, Final ≤ 600 min | Vicat apparatus (IS 4031) |
| **Air Content** | Entrapped air (1–2 %) | Pressure method |

### B. Properties of Hardened Concrete (IS 456)

1. **Compressive Strength:** $f_{ck}$ at 28 days on 150 mm cube. Grading: M5, M7.5, …, M80.
2. **Tensile Strength:** $\approx 10\%$ of $f_{ck}$; measured via split cylinder (Brazilian) test, $f_{ct} = 0.6\sqrt{f_{ck}}$.
3. **Flexural Strength (Modulus of Rupture):** $f_{cr} = 0.7 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$; measured on 100×100×500 mm beam under 2-point loading.
4. **Modulus of Elasticity:** $E_c = 5000 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ (IS 456, Cl. 6.2.3.1).
5. **Durability:** Resistance to weathering, chemical attack, abrasion. Minimum cement content (300–400+ kg/m³) and max w/c ratio are prescribed.
6. **Impermeability:** Inversely proportional to w/c; for watertight structures, $w/c \le 0.45$.
7. **Shrinkage:** Drying shrinkage ≈ 0.04 %; restrained shrinkage causes cracking.
8. **Creep:** Time-dependent increase in strain under sustained load; increases with w/c and age at loading.

---

## 5.5 Model Solution — Question B(a) (7 Marks)

**Answer:**

**Stages of concrete production:**

1. **Material Selection & Inspection:** Cement tested for freshness, aggregates for grading, water for potability.
2. **Batching:** Weigh-batching preferred over volume-batching (eliminates bulking error).
3. **Mixing:** Drum or pan-type mixer; 1–3 minutes; until uniform colour/ consistency.
4. **Transport:** Wheelbarrows, chutes, transit mixers, or concrete pumps.
5. **Placing & Compaction:** Concrete placed before initial set; compacted with vibrators to remove entrapped air.
6. **Finishing:** Screeding, floating, trowelling for smooth surface.
7. **Curing:** Spraying water, ponding, or wet gunny bags for at least **7 days** (or 14 days for severe exposure) to ensure continued hydration.
8. **Testing:** Cube samples cast and tested at 7 and 28 days for compressive strength.

**Grades of Concrete (IS 456:2000):** M5, M7.5, M10, M15, M20, M25, M30, M35, M40, M45, M50, M55, M60, M65, M70, M75, M80 (with nominal mix permitted up to M25, design mix mandatory from M30 onwards).

---

## 5.6 Model Solution — Question B(b) (7 Marks)

**Answer:**

| Type | Brief Description | Field Application |
|---|---|---|
| **PCC** | Plain concrete without steel. | Foundations, floor bedding, kerbs, pavements. |
| **RCC** | Concrete with steel reinforcement. | Buildings, bridges, water tanks, beams, columns, slabs. |
| **Pre-stressed Concrete** | Concrete pre-compressed using tendons (pre- or post-tensioning). | Long-span bridges, flyovers, railway sleepers, water pipes, nuclear containment. |
| **Ready-Mix Concrete (RMC)** | Batched in plant, delivered by agitator trucks. | High-rise, large infra, time-constrained projects. |
| **High-Strength Concrete (HSC)** | $f_{ck} \ge 60$ MPa; uses superplasticizers + mineral admixtures. | High-rise columns, offshore structures, heavy-duty floors. |
| **Self-Compacting Concrete (SCC)** | Highly flowable, compacts under its own weight. | Heavily reinforced sections, congested zones, tunnels. |
| **Lightweight Concrete** | Made with light aggregates (pumice, expanded clay, perlite) or foaming agents. | Partition walls, roof insulation, long-span floors. |
| **Fibre-Reinforced Concrete (FRC)** | Contains short discrete fibres (steel, glass, polypropylene). | Industrial floors, tunnel linings, blast-resistant structures. |
| **Geo-polymer Concrete** | Zero-cement binder from fly-ash activated with alkaline solution. | Sustainable / green construction, marine environments. |
| **Precast Concrete** | Cast in factory, transported and assembled. | Boundary walls, staircase units, lintels, paving blocks. |
| **Shotcrete / Gunite** | Pneumatically sprayed concrete. | Tunnel linings, swimming pools, retaining wall repairs. |

---

## 5.7 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Where KTU Students Lose Marks in This Topic:**

1. **Confusing "M20 grade" with 20 N/mm² = 20 MPa — correct ✓ — but writing it as "20 N/m²" or "20 kN/m²"** — this shows a units-misconception and forfeits the unit mark. Always write **MPa or N/mm²** for concrete strength.
2. **Listing constituents without stating their *function* —** you get partial marks; the *function* is what fetches the full mark allocation.
3. **Forgetting IS code references** — examiners expect at least one IS code citation (e.g., IS 456, IS 383, IS 9103, IS 10262). It signals syllabus-oriented knowledge.
4. **Mixing up the water-cement ratio effect on workability vs strength** — Workability **increases** with w/c, but strength **decreases**. Students often write both in the same direction.
5. **Forgetting to convert slump, moisture, or specific gravity units** — a numerical answer without unit conversion in the final step is treated as incomplete.
6. **In Abrams' law problems, forgetting that the *constants* $A$ and $B$ are given in the question** — students often invent a relation, losing method marks.
7. **In mix design, omitting the *absolute volume method* equation** $V = M / (G \cdot \rho_w)$ — examiners expect it explicitly.
8. **Not distinguishing between *bulking of sand* and *shrinkage of concrete*** — these are entirely different phenomena and frequently confused.

---

## 5.8 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Save this page for the night before the exam.**

✅ **Concrete = Cement + Sand + Coarse Aggregate + Water (+ Admixtures)** — 4 essential, 1 optional.

✅ **Water is the *enemy* of strength but the *friend* of workability** — w/c governs both inversely.

✅ **Abrams' Law:** $f_c = A / B^{w/c}$ — empirical, foundational, high-yield.

✅ **Standard unit weight of PCC/RCC:** $2400 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 24 \text{ kN/m}^3$.

✅ **Characteristic strength is the 28-day cube strength below which ≤ 5 % of test results fall.**

✅ **IS 456:2000 — the bible for concrete in India.** Always quote clause numbers.

✅ **Modulus of Elasticity:** $E_c = 5000 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ MPa — *the* most-quoted formula.

✅ **Flexural strength:** $f_{cr} = 0.7 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$; **Split tensile:** $f_{ct} = 0.6 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$.

✅ **Slump test** is for workability; **Vicat test** is for cement setting time; **Compression test** is for cube strength.

✅ **Bulking of sand** = moisture in fine aggregate increases its apparent volume (max ~20–30 % at 5–8 % moisture); correct in volume batching.

✅ **Nominal mix** is allowed up to M25; **Design mix** is mandatory from M30 onwards.

✅ **Segregation = separation of ingredients; Bleeding = upward water movement; Honeycombing = voids due to poor compaction.**

✅ **PCC has no steel; RCC has steel; PSC has pre-tensioned tendons** — three major categories to remember.

✅ **RMC** = Ready-Mix Concrete; **SCC** = Self-Compacting Concrete; **HSC** = High-Strength Concrete; **FRC** = Fibre-Reinforced Concrete; **HPC** = High-Performance Concrete.

✅ **Maximum w/c ratio** (IS 456) for M20 = 0.55, M25 = 0.50, M30 and above = 0.45 (with exposure conditions).

✅ **Mix design method (IS 10262)** is based on **absolute volume principle** — the total absolute volume of all ingredients = 1 m³ (minus air).

✅ **Minimum curing period = 7 days** (or 10 days for OPC with mineral admixtures; 14 days for severe exposure).

✅ **Cement setting time:** Initial ≥ 30 min, Final ≤ 600 min (per IS 269/8112/12269).

✅ **Aggregate size rules (IS 456):** ≤ ¼ slab thickness, ≤ ⅕ span, ≤ ¾ bar spacing.

✅ **Aggregate silt + clay limit ≤ 5 %** for fine aggregate (IS 383).

✅ **Water quality limits:** pH ≥ 6, SO₃ ≤ 400 mg/L, Cl⁻ ≤ 500 mg/L (RCC).

✅ **Memorize the slump values table** for various works (very low 0–25 mm, low 25–50 mm, medium 50–100 mm, high 100–150 mm).

✅ **Admixture dose ≤ 5 % of cement weight** (IS 9103).

✅ **Geo-polymer concrete = green, zero-OPC binder, fly-ash + alkaline activator** — KTU's modern viva-favourite.

✅ **In every numerical problem, write the unit.** Lose the unit, lose the mark.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steel: Structural Steel Sections and Steel Reinforcements - Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDEST203 - BASIC MECHANICAL AND CIVIL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Steel: Structural Steel Sections and Steel Reinforcements - Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Structural Steel — Formal Definition
**Structural steel** refers to a category of steel construction materials that are manufactured in standardized cross-sectional shapes, grades, and sizes for use in load-bearing applications such as beams, columns, trusses, and frames. In India, these sections are standardized by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** under **IS 808:1989 (Hot Rolled Steel Sections)** and are produced by passing heated steel billets through a series of rollers (hot-rolling process).

### 1.2 Steel Reinforcement — Formal Definition
**Steel reinforcement** (rebar) refers to steel bars, wires, or mesh embedded in concrete to compensate for concrete's low tensile strength. These reinforcements are governed by **IS 1786:2008 (High Strength Deformed Steel Bars and Wires for Concrete Reinforcement)** and **IS 432:1982 (Mild Steel and Medium Tensile Steel Bars)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must be able to identify, sketch, and state applications of standard rolled steel sections (I, H, T, C, L, hollow) and differentiate between mild steel, HYSD, and TMT reinforcement bars.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy
Think of a building as a **human body**:
- **Structural steel sections** are like the **skeleton (bones)** — they form the rigid framework that bears all vertical and lateral loads. Without bones, the body collapses.
- **Steel reinforcements in concrete** are like the **hidden metal threads in reinforced concrete** — concrete is strong in compression (like our bones under squatting) but weak in tension (like trying to pull apart a chalk stick). Steel rebars take up all the tensile forces, just like hidden ropes in a brittle material.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric:** India uses the **Metric System** throughout — all sections are designated in **millimeters (mm)** for dimensions and **kg/m** (kilogram per meter) for mass per unit length.

### 1.4 Why Steel?
- **High Strength-to-Weight Ratio:** Tensile strength ranges from **250 MPa (Fe 250)** to **550 MPa (Fe 550)**.
- **Ductility:** Allows plastic deformation before failure, warning before collapse.
- **Uniformity & Reliability:** Factory-produced with consistent properties.
- **Speed of Construction:** Prefabricated and bolted/welded on site.
- **Recyclability:** 100% recyclable without loss of quality.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Structural Steel Sections
Structural steel sections are broadly classified as:

| Category | Description | Common Designation (IS 808) |
|----------|-------------|-----------------------------|
| **Rolled Sections** | Hot-rolled into standardized shapes | ISMB, ISLB, ISHB, ISMC, ISLC, ISA, IST |
| **Built-up Sections** | Fabricated by welding/plating rolled pieces | Plate girders, Box girders |
| **Hollow Sections** | Tubular sections (circular, square, rectangular) | CHS, SHS, RHS |
| **Plates & Sheets** | Flat rolled products of varying thickness | MS Plates, Chequered plates |

### 2.2 Detailed Types of Rolled Steel Sections

**(a) I-Sections (Universal Beams/Columns)** — Cross-section resembles the letter 'I' or 'H'.
- **ISMB** — Indian Standard Medium-weight Beam
- **ISLB** — Indian Standard Light-weight Beam
- **ISJB** — Indian Standard Junior Beam
- **ISWB** — Indian Standard Wide-flange Beam
- **ISHB** — Indian Standard Heavy-weight Beam

> [!NOTE]
> **Designation Example:** **ISMB 500** means an Indian Standard Medium-weight Beam of depth **500 mm**.

**(b) Channel Sections (C-Sections)** — Shaped like the letter 'C'.
- **ISMC** — Indian Standard Medium-weight Channel
- **ISLC** — Indian Standard Light-weight Channel
- Used in purlins, built-up columns, and as stiffeners.

**(c) Angle Sections (L-Sections)** — Right-angled L-shape.
- **ISA (Equal Angle)** — both legs of equal length, e.g., ISA 50×50×6 (50 mm leg × 6 mm thickness).
- **ISA (Unequal Angle)** — different leg lengths, e.g., ISA 75×50×6.

**(d) T-Sections** — T-shaped cross-section.
- **IST** — Indian Standard T-section
- Used as flange components in built-up sections.

**(e) Hollow Sections**
- **CHS** — Circular Hollow Section
- **SHS** — Square Hollow Section
- **RHS** — Rectangular Hollow Section

### 2.3 Types of Steel Reinforcement (Rebar)

**(a) Mild Steel Bars (MS Bars) — Fe 250 Grade**
- Yield strength: **250 N/mm²**
- Plain round surface, smooth.
- Used in small residential projects.
- Governed by **IS 432 Part 1**.

**(b) High Yield Strength Deformed Bars (HYSD Bars)**
- Grades: **Fe 415, Fe 500, Fe 550**
- Yield strength: **415 / 500 / 550 N/mm²**
- Surface has ribs/lugs for better bond with concrete.
- Governed by **IS 1786:2008**.

**(c) Thermo Mechanically Treated (TMT) Bars**
- Modern variant of HYSD with superior properties.
- Manufactured by quenching hot bars in water (martensite formation) followed by self-tempering.
- Grades: **Fe 415, Fe 500, Fe 550, Fe 600**.
- Best ductility, weldability, and seismic resistance.

**(d) Corrosion Resistant Steel (CRS) Bars**
- Coated with **epoxy** or made from **weathering steel** (e.g., **Fe 500D, Fe 550D**).
- Used in coastal and chemically aggressive environments.

**(e) Welded Wire Fabric / Mesh**
- Welded grid of cold-drawn wires (typically 4–12 mm diameter).
- Used in slabs, walls, and pavement reinforcement.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Sectional Properties)

> [!NOTE]
> For all formulas, the unit of length is **mm**, area in **mm²**, moment of inertia in **mm⁴**, and section modulus in **mm³**.

| Section Type | Cross-Sectional Area (A) | Moment of Inertia (I) | Section Modulus (Z = I/y_max) |
|--------------|--------------------------|----------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Solid Rectangle** (b × d) | $A = b \times d$ | $I_{xx} = \dfrac{b d^{3}}{12}$, $I_{yy} = \dfrac{d b^{3}}{12}$ | $Z_{xx} = \dfrac{b d^{2}}{6}$ |
| **Solid Circle** (diameter d) | $A = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}$ | $I = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{64}$ | $Z = \dfrac{\pi d^{3}}{32}$ |
| **Hollow Circle** (OD D, ID d) | $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^{2} - d^{2})$ | $I = \dfrac{\pi}{64}(D^{4} - d^{4})$ | $Z = \dfrac{\pi}{32}\left(\dfrac{D^{4} - d^{4}}{D}\right)$ |
| **I-Section (overall B × D, flange tf, web tw)** | $A = 2(B \cdot t_f) + (D - 2t_f) \cdot t_w$ | $I_{xx} = \dfrac{B D^{3}}{12} - \dfrac{(B - t_w)(D - 2t_f)^{3}}{12}$ | $Z_{xx} = \dfrac{I_{xx}}{(D/2)}$ |
| **T-Section** (split into flange + web) | $A_f + A_w$ | $I = I_f + I_w + A_f h_f^{2} + A_w h_w^{2}$ | $Z = I / y_{max}$ |
| **Angle Section** (Equal angle L × t) | $A = 2Lt - t^{2}$ | $I_{xx} = I_{yy} = \dfrac{t(L-t)^{3}}{12} + \dfrac{L \cdot t^{3}}{12}$ | $Z_{xx} = \dfrac{I_{xx}}{c_x}$ |

**Universal Relationships:**

$$r = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}}$$

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}}$$

$$\text{Weight per meter (kg/m)} = \frac{A \, (\text{mm}^2) \times 0.00785}{1}$$

Where **0.00785 kg/mm³** is the density of steel (**7850 kg/m³**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Section modulus (Z) directly determines the **bending capacity** of a beam. A higher Z means the section can resist more bending moment: $M_{allowable} = \sigma_{allowable} \times Z$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Moment of Inertia of an I-Section (Built-up from Rectangles)

An I-section can be visualized as **a large rectangle of width B and depth D, minus two side rectangles** representing the cut-out portions on either side of the web.

**Step 1:** Define geometry.
- Overall depth = $D$
- Overall flange width = $B$
- Flange thickness = $t_f$
- Web thickness = $t_w$
- Inner cut-out depth = $D - 2t_f$
- Inner cut-out width = $B - t_w$

**Step 2:** Apply the parallel axis theorem. The neutral axis passes through the centroid of the I-section, which by symmetry lies at the mid-depth ($D/2$).

**Step 3:** Write the moment of inertia of the **full solid rectangle**:

$$I_{solid} = \frac{B \cdot D^{3}}{12}$$

**Step 4:** Write the moment of inertia of the **two cut-out portions** combined. Since the two cut-outs are symmetric about the neutral axis, no parallel axis shift is needed (their centroids coincide with the neutral axis).

$$I_{cutout} = 2 \times \frac{\left(\dfrac{B - t_w}{2}\right)(D - 2t_f)^{3}}{12} = \frac{(B - t_w)(D - 2t_f)^{3}}{12}$$

**Step 5:** Subtract the cut-outs from the solid rectangle:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{xx} &= I_{solid} - I_{cutout} \\
I_{xx} &= \frac{B D^{3}}{12} - \frac{(B - t_w)(D - 2t_f)^{3}}{12} \\
I_{xx} &= \frac{1}{12}\left[B D^{3} - (B - t_w)(D - 2t_f)^{3}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6:** Section Modulus:

$$Z_{xx} = \frac{I_{xx}}{y_{max}} = \frac{I_{xx}}{D/2} = \frac{2 I_{xx}}{D}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Position of Neutral Axis for an Unequal T-Section

For a T-section, the neutral axis is **NOT** at the mid-depth because the flange and web have different widths.

**Step 1:** Split the T-section into two rectangles.
- **Flange:** width = $b_f$, thickness = $t_f$, area $A_f = b_f \cdot t_f$
- **Web:** height = $h_w$, thickness = $t_w$, area $A_w = t_w \cdot h_w$

**Step 2:** Measure distances from the **top edge** (extreme top fibre) of the T-section.
- Distance to centroid of flange: $y_f = t_f / 2$
- Distance to centroid of web: $y_w = t_f + h_w / 2$

**Step 3:** Compute the centroid (neutral axis location) from the top fibre:

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{y} &= \frac{A_f \cdot y_f + A_w \cdot y_w}{A_f + A_w} \\
\bar{y} &= \frac{(b_f t_f)(t_f/2) + (t_w h_w)(t_f + h_w/2)}{b_f t_f + t_w h_w}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Compute moment of inertia using the **parallel axis theorem** for each rectangle about the NA:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{xx} &= I_f + A_f \cdot (\bar{y} - y_f)^{2} + I_w + A_w \cdot (y_w - \bar{y})^{2} \\
I_{xx} &= \frac{b_f t_f^{3}}{12} + A_f(\bar{y} - t_f/2)^{2} + \frac{t_w h_w^{3}}{12} + A_w(t_f + h_w/2 - \bar{y})^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Determine maximum distances from NA to extreme fibres:
- Top fibre distance: $y_{top} = \bar{y}$
- Bottom fibre distance: $y_{bot} = t_f + h_w - \bar{y}$

**Step 6:** Effective section modulus uses the **larger** of $y_{top}$ and $y_{bot}$:

$$Z_{eff} = \frac{I_{xx}}{\max(y_{top},\ y_{bot})}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: Weight of Steel Reinforcement

**Problem:** Calculate the weight of **12 m** of **TMT Fe 500 bar** of diameter **16 mm**. Steel density = **7850 kg/m³**.

**Step 1:** Cross-sectional area:

$$A = \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (16)^{2}}{4} = 201.06 \ \text{mm}^{2}$$

**Step 2:** Volume of the bar:

$$V = A \times L = 201.06 \times (12 \times 1000) = 2{,}412{,}742 \ \text{mm}^{3}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to m³ and multiply by density:

$$V = 2{,}412{,}742 \times 10^{-9} = 0.002413 \ \text{m}^{3}$$

$$\text{Mass} = \rho \times V = 7850 \times 0.002413 = 18.94 \ \text{kg}$$

**Step 4:** Use the shortcut formula for verification:

$$W = d^{2} \times L / 162 = \frac{16^{2} \times 12}{162} = \frac{3072}{162} = 18.96 \ \text{kg}$$

(Both methods agree within rounding. The constant **1/162** comes from $\pi/4 \times 7850 \times 10^{-6}$.)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always show both the standard formula and the derived shortcut in numerical problems for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
