<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any six standard structural steel sections and state their BIS designation.*

**Model Answer (6 points):**
1. **I-Sections** — Designated as **ISMB** (Medium-weight Beam), **ISLB** (Light-weight Beam), **ISHB** (Heavy-weight Beam). Example: ISMB 500.
2. **Channel Sections** — Designated as **ISMC** (Medium-weight Channel), **ISLC** (Light-weight Channel). Example: ISMC 100.
3. **Angle Sections** — Designated as **ISA** (Indian Standard Angle). Example: ISA 50×50×6 (equal angle), ISA 75×50×6 (unequal angle).
4. **T-Sections** — Designated as **IST**. Example: IST 100.
5. **Hollow Sections** — Designated as **CHS, SHS, RHS**. Example: SHS 100×100×5.
6. **Plates** — Designated by thickness, e.g., MS plate 10 mm thick.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Differentiate between Mild Steel (MS) bars and TMT bars in three points.*

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Mild Steel Bars (Fe 250) | TMT Bars (Fe 500) |
|----------|--------------------------|-------------------|
| **Yield Strength** | 250 N/mm² | 500 N/mm² |
| **Surface** | Plain smooth | Ribbed for better bond |
| **Ductility** | High (more elongation) | Moderate to high (controlled) |
| **Manufacturing** | Simple hot rolling | Quenching + self-tempering |
| **Weldability** | Excellent | Good (Fe 500D is best) |
| **Application** | Small residential RCC | High-rise, bridges, seismic zones |

---

### Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse the designation of beams and channels (ISMB vs ISMC).
> - **Do NOT** forget to mention the **governing IS code** for each section.
> - **Do NOT** skip neat sketches — at least **2 marks** are reserved for diagrams in any 14-mark question.
> - In numericals, **always** carry the unit (kg/m, N/mm², mm) till the final answer.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Module 4]

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain **any five** types of rolled steel sections used in construction. State their standard BIS designation and one typical application for each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. I-Section (ISMB, ISLB, ISHB)**
- *Sketch:* A cross-section shaped like the letter 'I' with two horizontal flanges and a vertical web.
- *Designation:* ISMB 500 (depth 500 mm, medium-weight beam).
- *Application:* Used as **flexural members** in buildings, bridges, and industrial structures as beams and girders.
- *Property:* Maximum moment of inertia about the major axis (xx) per unit area → high bending efficiency.

**2. Channel Section (ISMC, ISLC)**
- *Sketch:* C-shaped cross-section with one web and two equal flanges.
- *Designation:* ISMC 75.
- *Application:* Used as **purlins** in roof trusses, **stiffeners** in plate girders, and **chords** in light trusses.

**3. Angle Section (ISA)**
- *Sketch:* L-shaped cross-section.
- *Designation:* ISA 50×50×6 (equal), ISA 75×50×8 (unequal).
- *Application:* Used in **truss members**, **bracing systems**, **lattice towers**, and as **connecting members** in built-up sections.

**4. T-Section (IST)**
- *Sketch:* T-shaped cross-section (flange + web).
- *Designation:* IST 100.
- *Application:* Used as **flange component** in built-up plate girders, in **roof framing**, and in **reinforced concrete as embedded steel**.

**5. Hollow Section (CHS/SHS/RHS)**
- *Sketch:* Square, rectangular, or circular tube cross-section.
- *Designation:* SHS 100×100×5, RHS 200×100×6, CHS 200×6.
- *Application:* Used as **columns**, **architectural exposed members**, **handrails**, and in **offshore structures** due to equal buckling resistance about both axes.

**6. Plates (MS Plate)**
- *Sketch:* Flat rectangular rolled product.
- *Designation:* MS Plate 10 mm thick.
- *Application:* Used in **base plates**, **gusset plates**, **cover plates**, and in fabricating **plate girders**.

---

**(b)** Explain the **types of steel reinforcements** used in RCC construction. Compare **HYSD bars and TMT bars** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Types of Steel Reinforcements:**

1. **Mild Steel (MS) Bars — Fe 250 Grade**
   - Yield strength: **250 N/mm²** (IS 432 Part 1).
   - Plain round surface, smooth.
   - Use: Small residential buildings, slabs of low spans.

2. **High Yield Strength Deformed (HYSD) Bars — Fe 415, Fe 500, Fe 550**
   - Yield strength: **415, 500, 550 N/mm²** (IS 1786:2008).
   - Cold-twisted deformed bars with ribs/lugs.
   - Better bond strength with concrete.
   - Use: Columns, beams, slabs, footings.

3. **Thermo Mechanically Treated (TMT) Bars**
   - Latest generation of HYSD bars.
   - Manufactured by **quenching** hot bars in a water box followed by **self-tempering** by residual core heat.
   - Outer layer: **hard martensite** (high strength) | Core: **ductile ferrite-pearlite** (high ductility).
   - Use: Multi-storey buildings, bridges, seismic zones.

4. **Corrosion Resistant Steel (CRS) Bars**
   - Epoxy-coated or made from weathering steel composition.
   - Use: Coastal structures, marine environments, chemical plants.

5. **Welded Wire Fabric (WWF) / Mesh**
   - Welded grid of cold-drawn wires.
   - Use: Slabs, walls, road pavements, shell structures.

**Comparison: HYSD Bars vs TMT Bars**

| Property | HYSD Bars | TMT Bars |
|----------|-----------|----------|
| **Manufacturing** | Cold-twisting of hot-rolled bars (introduces residual stress) | Quenching + self-tempering (no residual stress) |
| **Surface** | Ribs from cold twisting | Uniform ribs, sharper pattern |
| **Strength** | Fe 415 / Fe 500 | Fe 415 / Fe 500 / Fe 550 / Fe 600 |
| **Ductility** | Moderate | Superior (Fe 500D has 16% elongation) |
| **Weldability** | Poor (residual stress at weld) | Excellent (low carbon equivalent) |
| **Fatigue Resistance** | Moderate | High (uniform microstructure) |
| **Seismic Performance** | Acceptable | Excellent (preferred in seismic zones) |
| **Cost** | Lower | Slightly higher, but cost-effective |
| **Corrosion Resistance** | Standard | Better due to uniform microstructure |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice]

**(a)** With a **neat sketch**, explain the cross-section of an **I-section** and a **Channel section**. Derive the expression for the **moment of inertia** of an I-section about its major axis. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**I-Section:** Cross-section looks like the capital letter 'I' (or 'H'). It has two horizontal flanges of width **B** and thickness **t_f**, and a vertical web of thickness **t_w** and overall depth **D**.

**Channel Section:** Cross-section looks like the letter 'C'. It has one web and two flanges of equal width.

**Derivation of $I_{xx}$ for I-Section:**

Consider the I-section as a **big rectangle of width B and depth D** from which **two rectangles of width $(B - t_w)/2$ and depth $(D - 2t_f)$** are cut out from either side of the web.

Moment of inertia of the full solid rectangle about its centroidal x-axis:

$$I_{solid} = \frac{B D^{3}}{12}$$

Moment of inertia of the two cut-outs (each symmetric about the x-axis, so no parallel axis shift):

$$I_{cutout} = 2 \times \frac{\left(\dfrac{B - t_w}{2}\right)(D - 2t_f)^{3}}{12} = \frac{(B - t_w)(D - 2t_f)^{3}}{12}$$

By the principle of superposition:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{xx} &= I_{solid} - I_{cutout} \\
I_{xx} &= \frac{B D^{3}}{12} - \frac{(B - t_w)(D - 2t_f)^{3}}{12} \\
I_{xx} &= \frac{1}{12}\left[B D^{3} - (B - t_w)(D - 2t_f)^{3}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

Section modulus:

$$Z_{xx} = \frac{I_{xx}}{(D/2)} = \frac{2 I_{xx}}{D}$$

---

**(b)** Calculate the **weight per metre (kg/m)** of an **ISA 75 × 50 × 6 unequal angle**. Also find its **moment of inertia** about the major axis. Given: cross-sectional area of the section = **7.34 cm²**, centroid distance from outer fibre = **2.56 cm** (xx axis), and **2.07 cm** (yy axis). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Section: ISA 75 × 50 × 6 (Unequal angle, 75 mm × 50 mm, 6 mm thick)
- Area, $A = 7.34 \ \text{cm}^{2} = 734 \ \text{mm}^{2}$
- Centroid distance, $c_x = 2.56 \ \text{cm}$, $c_y = 2.07 \ \text{cm}$

**Step 1: Weight per metre**

$$W = A \times 0.00785 = 734 \times 0.00785 = 5.76 \ \text{kg/m}$$

**Step 2: Radius of gyration (standard table value for ISA 75×50×6)**

From IS 808 steel table:
- $r_{xx} = 2.32 \ \text{cm}$
- $r_{yy} = 1.46 \ \text{cm}$

**Step 3: Moment of inertia using $I = A r^{2}$**

For the major axis (xx):

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{xx} &= A \cdot r_{xx}^{2} \\
I_{xx} &= 7.34 \times (2.32)^{2} \\
I_{xx} &= 7.34 \times 5.3824 \\
I_{xx} &= 39.51 \ \text{cm}^{4} = 39.51 \times 10^{4} \ \text{mm}^{4}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Section modulus**

$$Z_{xx} = \frac{I_{xx}}{c_x} = \frac{39.51}{2.56} = 15.43 \ \text{cm}^{3} = 15.43 \times 10^{3} \ \text{mm}^{3}$$

**Final Answers:**
- Weight per metre = **5.76 kg/m**
- $I_{xx} = 39.51 \ \text{cm}^{4}$
- $Z_{xx} = 15.43 \ \text{cm}^{3}$

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Structural Steel Sections** follow **IS 808:1989** and come in **I, H, T, C, L, hollow** shapes.
- **I-Sections (ISMB, ISLB, ISHB)** are the most efficient flexural members — designation number = depth in mm.
- **Channels (ISMC, ISLC)** and **Angles (ISA)** are used for secondary members, purlins, and bracing.
- **Hollow Sections (CHS/SHS/RHS)** offer aesthetic appeal and **equal buckling resistance** about both axes.
- **Steel Reinforcement** is governed by **IS 1786:2008 (HYSD/TMT)** and **IS 432:1982 (MS bars)**.
- **Grades:** Fe 250 (MS), Fe 415, Fe 500, Fe 550, Fe 600 — number denotes yield strength in **N/mm²** (MPa).
- **TMT bars** are manufactured by **quenching + self-tempering** — best for **seismic zones**.
- **Weight formula:** $W \, (\text{kg/m}) = d^{2} / 162$ for rebars.
- **Section Modulus (Z)** = $I / y_{max}$ — direct measure of bending strength.
- **Radius of Gyration (r)** = $\sqrt{I/A}$ — governs column buckling.
- **Always state the governing IS code** in exam answers for full marks.
- **Neat sketches** are mandatory — they carry 1–2 marks on their own in 7-mark sub-questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Environmental Systems and Sustainable Practices: Pollution Control Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDEST203 - BASIC MECHANICAL AND CIVIL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Environmental Systems and Sustainable Practices: Pollution Control Technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Pollution Control Technologies** refer to the integrated set of physical, chemical, and biological engineering systems, processes, and devices designed to prevent, reduce, or eliminate the discharge of harmful contaminants (pollutants) into the natural environment—air, water, and soil—thereby safeguarding public health and ecological balance.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (GDEST203)**, pollution control engineering is treated as an **interdisciplinary applied science** that combines fluid mechanics (for scrubbers and cyclones), thermodynamics (for incinerators), mass transfer (for absorbers), and microbiology (for biological wastewater treatment). The discipline operates on the principle of the **Polluter Pays Principle (PPP)** and adheres to standards prescribed by the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** of India and the **Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC)**.

The three primary domains of pollution control technology are:

1. **Air Pollution Control (APC)** – Managing particulate matter (PM), SOx, NOx, CO, VOCs, and CO₂.
2. **Water Pollution Control (WPC)** – Treating municipal and industrial wastewater before discharge.
3. **Solid Waste & Noise Pollution Management** – Sanitary landfills, incineration, acoustic dampening.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Human Body Analogy:** Think of the Earth's environment as a giant living body. Just as our body has a **liver (filters toxins)**, **lungs (exchange gases)**, and **kidneys (flush out dissolved waste)**, modern pollution control technology provides artificial "organs" for industrial society. An **electrostatic precipitator** acts like the alveoli in lungs capturing dust; a **sewage treatment plant** functions like kidneys filtering urea and ammonia; an **incinerator** is the industrial equivalent of metabolic oxidation.

A simpler everyday analogy: A **water purifier (RO system)** in your kitchen is a miniature, scaled-down version of a municipal water treatment plant. It uses **sedimentation** (mud settles), **filtration** (activated carbon catches chlorine), and **reverse osmosis** (semi-permeable membrane rejects dissolved salts). The same physical principles—just applied at gigalitre scale for a city.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Indicators

> [!NOTE]
> The following **bolded metrics** are universally used in KTU textbook problems and CPCB notifications:

- **PM2.5 and PM10** – Particulate Matter of aerodynamic diameter ≤ 2.5 µm and ≤ 10 µm. The NAAQS (National Ambient Air Quality Standard) 24-hour limit for **PM2.5 = 60 µg/m³** and for **PM10 = 100 µg/m³**.
- **AQI (Air Quality Index)** – A composite number (0–500) computed from pollutant concentrations.
- **BOD₅ (5-day Biochemical Oxygen Demand)** at **20°C** – the standard metric for organic water pollution, measured in **mg/L**.
- **COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand)** – measures total oxidizable matter, always ≥ BOD.
- **TDS (Total Dissolved Solids)** – drinking water limit **≤ 500 mg/L** (BIS 10500).
- **dB (Decibel)** – logarithmic unit for sound; permissible industrial limit is **75 dB(A)** during day and **70 dB(A)** at night.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Favourite:** Examiners frequently test the difference between **BOD and COD**, and the **stages of wastewater treatment (Primary → Secondary → Tertiary)**. Memorize the order of unit operations!

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Pollution Control Technologies

Pollution control technologies are classified along the **waste stream** they target and the **phase** in which the pollutant exists.

### 2.1.1 Air Pollution Control (APC) Technologies

Air pollutants exist in two physical phases that demand fundamentally different removal mechanisms:

**A. Particulate Control Devices (handle solid/liquid aerosols):**

- **Gravitational Settling Chambers** – simplest; use gravity. Efficiency low (~50%) for fine particles.
- **Cyclone Separators** – centrifugal force; efficiency high (>90%) for particles **> 10 µm**.
- **Wet Scrubbers** (Venturi, Spray Tower) – liquid droplets capture particulates. Also absorb gases.
- **Electrostatic Precipitators (ESP)** – high-voltage corona discharge charges particles, collected on plates. Efficiency **> 99%** even for sub-micron dust. Used in thermal power plants (coal fly ash).
- **Fabric Filters (Baghouse)** – woven fabric filters; efficiency **> 99.9%** for PM2.5.
- **HEPA Filters** – High-Efficiency Particulate Air filters for cleanrooms and hospitals.

**B. Gaseous Control Devices (handle molecular pollutants):**

- **Absorption** – gas dissolves into a liquid (e.g., SO₂ in lime/limestone slurry → gypsum in FGD).
- **Adsorption** – gas adheres to a solid surface (e.g., activated carbon adsorbs VOCs, mercury).
- **Incineration / Thermal Oxidation** – combustion at 700–1000°C destroys VOCs and CO.
- **Catalytic Converters** – Pt/Pd/Rh catalysts in automobiles convert CO → CO₂, NOx → N₂, HC → CO₂ + H₂O.
- **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** – injects NH₃/urea to reduce NOx in power plant flue gas.

### 2.1.2 Water Pollution Control (WPC) Technologies

Municipal wastewater treatment follows a staged protocol:

| Stage | Name | Unit Operations | Purpose | Removal Target |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary** | Physical | Screening, Grit chamber, Primary Clarifier | Remove settleable solids & floating debris | 50–70% TSS, 25–40% BOD |
| **Secondary** | Biological | Activated Sludge Process, Trickling Filter, Oxidation Pond, SBR | Biodegrade dissolved organics using microbes | 85–95% BOD |
| **Tertiary** | Advanced | Filtration, Chlorination, UV, RO, Nutrient Removal (N & P) | Polishing & disinfection | 99% pathogens, <1 mg/L nitrate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Wastewater vs Municipal Wastewater:** Industrial effluents (from textile, tannery, pharma) often contain non-biodegradable toxic compounds that resist conventional biological treatment, requiring **Tertiary treatment** such as **Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs)**, **Membrane Bioreactors (MBR)**, or **Electrocoagulation**.

### 2.1.3 Solid Waste Management Hierarchy

The **5R Principle** (preferred order):

1. **Refuse** – Don't generate waste.
2. **Reduce** – Minimize generation.
3. **Reuse** – Use again for same/different purpose.
4. **Recycle** – Reprocess into new raw material.
5. **Recover** – Energy recovery via incineration, RDF, biogasification (most preferred disposal).

Final disposal options: **Sanitary Landfill** (engineered, lined, leachate-controlled) and **Incineration** (with energy recovery).

### 2.1.4 Noise Pollution Control

Three engineering approaches:
- **Source control** – Quieter machinery, mufflers, vibration dampers.
- **Path control** – Acoustic barriers, green belts, building insulation.
- **Receiver control** – Ear protection, time-of-day restrictions.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important study artifact** for KTU Board Exams. Print this and revise before every test.

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Expression | Variables & Units | Application Domain |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Stokes' Law (Terminal Settling Velocity)** | $V_s = \dfrac{g \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f) \cdot d^2}{18 \cdot \mu}$ | $V_s$ = settling velocity (m/s); $g = 9.81$ m/s²; $\rho_p, \rho_f$ = particle, fluid density (kg/m³); $d$ = particle diameter (m); $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s) | Cyclones, ESP, dust collectors |
| 2 | **BOD First-Order Kinetics (Streeter-Phelps)** | $L_t = L_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$ | $L_t$ = BOD remaining at time $t$ (mg/L); $L_0$ = ultimate BOD; $k$ = deoxygenation constant (day⁻¹) | River self-purification, WWTP design |
| 3 | **Collection Efficiency (Particulate Control)** | $\eta = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$ | $C_{in}, C_{out}$ = inlet/outlet concentrations | APC device performance |
| 4 | **Sound Intensity Level** | $L_I = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | $I$ = intensity (W/m²); $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m² (reference) | Noise pollution |
| 5 | **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)** | $L_P = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)$ | $P$ = sound pressure (Pa); $P_0 = 20 \times 10^{-6}$ Pa | Industrial noise |
| 6 | **Overall SPL (multiple sources)** | $L_{total} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\sum 10^{L_i / 10}\right)$ | $L_i$ = individual SPL values (dB) | Combining noise sources |
| 7 | **COD/BOD Ratio** | $\text{COD} \geq \text{BOD}$ always | For domestic sewage: COD ≈ 2 × BOD | Waste characterization |
| 8 | **Population Equivalent** | $PE = \dfrac{\text{Total BOD load (kg/day)}}{0.08 \text{ kg/person/day}}$ | BOD per capita = 0.08 kg/day | Industrial discharge equivalence |
| 9 | **Mixing Ratio (Scrubbers)** | $L/G = \dfrac{\text{liquid flow}}{\text{gas flow}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | Wet scrubber design |
| 10 | **Reynolds Number (Air Duct)** | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D_h}{\mu}$ | $D_h$ = hydraulic diameter (m); $v$ = velocity (m/s) | Duct flow regime |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Boards:** *"Stokes Saves Slow, Sand-size Stuff"* — Stokes' law is valid only for **laminar flow** ($Re_p < 1$) and small particles ($d < 100$ µm). For larger particles, Newton's Law applies.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Thermal Power Plants**: ESP removes fly ash before flue gas enters the FGD (Flue Gas Desulfurization) wet scrubber that captures SO₂. Combined efficiency > 99.5% for PM and > 95% for SO₂.
- **Cement Industry**: Baghouse filters control cement kiln dust emissions.
- **Municipal Corporations**: Sewage Treatment Plants (STPs) using Activated Sludge Process treat 100–500 MLD (Million Litres per Day).
- **Smart Cities Mission**: Uses IoT-based Air Quality Monitoring Stations (AAQMS) and SCADA-controlled STPs.
- **Automotive Industry**: BS-VI norms mandate catalytic converters with lambda sensors, drastically cutting urban NOx and CO.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Stokes' Law (Terminal Settling Velocity of a Particle)

**Problem Context:** A spherical particle falling through a viscous fluid accelerates until the net force becomes zero. We need to find the steady-state (terminal) velocity $V_s$.

### Step 1: Force Balance on the Falling Particle

Three forces act on the particle:
1. **Weight (downward):** $F_W = m \cdot g = \rho_p \cdot V_{particle} \cdot g = \rho_p \cdot \left(\dfrac{\pi d^3}{6}\right) \cdot g$
2. **Buoyancy (upward):** $F_B = \rho_f \cdot \left(\dfrac{\pi d^3}{6}\right) \cdot g$
3. **Drag (opposing motion, upward when falling):** $F_D = C_D \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{\rho_f \cdot V_s^2}{2}$

where $C_D$ = drag coefficient, $A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$ = projected area of sphere.

### Step 2: Apply Net Force = 0 at Terminal Velocity

$$\sum F = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_W = F_B + F_D$$

Substituting:

$$\rho_p \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6} \cdot g = \rho_f \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6} \cdot g + C_D \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_f \cdot V_s^2}{2}$$

### Step 3: Simplify the Equation

Cancel $\pi$ on both sides and divide by $d^2$:

$$\rho_p \cdot \dfrac{d \cdot g}{6} = \rho_f \cdot \dfrac{d \cdot g}{6} + C_D \cdot \dfrac{\rho_f \cdot V_s^2}{8}$$

Isolate the term containing $V_s$:

$$C_D \cdot \dfrac{\rho_f \cdot V_s^2}{8} = \dfrac{d \cdot g}{6} \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f)$$

Rearrange:

$$V_s^2 = \dfrac{4 \cdot d \cdot g \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{3 \cdot C_D \cdot \rho_f}$$

### Step 4: Apply the Laminar Flow Drag Coefficient

For **laminar flow** around a sphere ($Re_p < 1$), experimental data (Stokes, 1851) gives:

$$C_D = \dfrac{24}{Re_p} = \dfrac{24 \cdot \mu}{\rho_f \cdot V_s \cdot d}$$

### Step 5: Substitute $C_D$ and Solve for $V_s$

$$V_s^2 = \dfrac{4 \cdot d \cdot g \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{3 \cdot \left(\dfrac{24 \cdot \mu}{\rho_f \cdot V_s \cdot d}\right) \cdot \rho_f}$$

The $\rho_f$ in numerator and denominator cancel:

$$V_s^2 = \dfrac{4 \cdot d \cdot g \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f) \cdot \rho_f \cdot V_s \cdot d}{3 \cdot 24 \cdot \mu \cdot \rho_f}$$

$$V_s^2 = \dfrac{4 \cdot d^2 \cdot g \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f) \cdot V_s}{72 \cdot \mu}$$

Divide both sides by $V_s$ (assuming $V_s \neq 0$):

$$V_s = \dfrac{4 \cdot d^2 \cdot g \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{72 \cdot \mu} = \dfrac{d^2 \cdot g \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \cdot \mu}$$

### Step 6: Final Result (Stokes' Law)

$$\boxed{V_s = \dfrac{g \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f) \cdot d^2}{18 \cdot \mu}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $d^2$ and not $d^3$?** The $d^3$ in weight cancels the $d^2$ in drag area, leaving $d^2$. This is why **doubling the particle diameter quadruples** the settling velocity—an extremely useful design insight for clarifiers.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: BOD Decay Kinetics

**Problem:** A municipal wastewater sample has an initial ultimate BOD $L_0 = 300$ mg/L. The deoxygenation constant is $k = 0.23$ day⁻¹ (typical for domestic sewage at 20°C). Calculate:
(a) The 5-day BOD ($BOD_5$).
(b) The BOD remaining after 8 days.
(c) The BOD exerted in the period between day 5 and day 8.

### Part (a): $BOD_5$ Calculation

The Streeter-Phelps equation for **oxygen demand exerted** up to time $t$ is:

$$Y_t = L_0 \cdot (1 - e^{-k \cdot t})$$

Substitute $t = 5$ days, $L_0 = 300$ mg/L, $k = 0.23$ day⁻¹:

$$Y_5 = 300 \cdot (1 - e^{-0.23 \cdot 5})$$

$$Y_5 = 300 \cdot (1 - e^{-1.15})$$

$$e^{-1.15} = 0.3166$$

$$Y_5 = 300 \cdot (1 - 0.3166) = 300 \cdot 0.6834 = 205.0 \text{ mg/L}$$

$$\boxed{BOD_5 = 205.0 \text{ mg/L}}$$

### Part (b): BOD Remaining After 8 Days

The **BOD remaining (unexerted)** is:

$$L_t = L_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t} = 300 \cdot e^{-0.23 \cdot 8} = 300 \cdot e^{-1.84}$$

$$e^{-1.84} = 0.1588$$

$$\boxed{L_8 = 47.6 \text{ mg/L}}$$

### Part (c): BOD Exerted Between Day 5 and Day 8

$$\Delta BOD = Y_8 - Y_5$$

First compute $Y_8$:

$$Y_8 = 300 \cdot (1 - e^{-1.84}) = 300 \cdot (1 - 0.1588) = 300 \cdot 0.8412 = 252.4 \text{ mg/L}$$

Then:

$$\Delta BOD = 252.4 - 205.0 = 47.4 \text{ mg/L}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta BOD_{5 \to 8} = 47.4 \text{ mg/L}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check:** The BOD remaining after 8 days (47.6 mg/L) and the BOD exerted between days 5–8 (47.4 mg/L) should be nearly equal. This is **NOT a coincidence** — it confirms internal consistency because $L_8$ represents the *unoxidized* portion, while $\Delta BOD$ represents the *newly oxidized* portion, and they must be equivalent to each other (with small rounding differences).

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: APC Devices

| Device | Principle | Removal Efficiency | Pressure Drop (mm H₂O) | Capital Cost | Operating Cost | Best Particle Size |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Settling Chamber | Gravity | 50% (coarse) | ~5 | Very Low | Very Low | > 50 µm |
| Cyclone | Centrifugal | 70–95% | 50–150 | Low | Low | 5–50 µm |
| Wet Scrubber | Inertial impaction with droplets | 80–95% | 50–250 | Medium | High (water) | 1–10 µm |
| ESP | Electrostatic | 99%+ | 10–25 | High | Medium | 0.1–10 µm |
| Baghouse | Filtration | 99.9% | 100–200 | Medium | Medium (filter change) | 0.1–5 µm |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2-Mark Question Frequency:** Examiners often ask *"Why ESP is preferred over baghouse in thermal power plants?"* Answer: ESP handles **high-temperature (up to 400°C)** flue gas, requires **low pressure drop**, and has **low operating cost** despite high capital cost. Bag filter cannot tolerate such temperatures.
