# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]**

**Q: Differentiate between BOD and COD. Why is COD always greater than or equal to BOD?**

**Model Answer:**

**BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand):**
- Measures the amount of **dissolved oxygen** consumed by **aerobic microorganisms** while biologically oxidizing **biodegradable organic matter** over a standard period (usually 5 days at 20°C).
- Only detects **biodegradable** components.
- Standard test duration: 5 days → $BOD_5$.
- Typical value for domestic sewage: 200–300 mg/L.

**COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand):**
- Measures the oxygen equivalent of **all chemically oxidizable matter** (both biodegradable and non-biodegradable) when refluxed with strong oxidant (potassium dichromate, $K_2Cr_2O_7$ in $H_2SO_4$).
- Test duration: only 2–3 hours.
- Typical value for domestic sewage: 400–600 mg/L.

**Why COD ≥ BOD always:**
- COD oxidizes **all organics** (biodegradable + non-biodegradable + some inorganics like sulfides), while BOD oxidizes **only the biodegradable fraction**.
- Hence, the chemical oxidation captures a *larger* amount of pollutants, so $\text{COD} \geq \text{BOD}$.
- The **ratio COD/BOD** is a key indicator of biodegradability:
  - Ratio ≈ 2 → easily biodegradable (domestic sewage).
  - Ratio > 3 → difficult to biodegrade (industrial effluent requiring tertiary treatment).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (1 mark loss):** Students often write "COD is faster" without explaining *why* the numerical value is higher. Examiners want the **chemical-vs-biological oxidizer** distinction.

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]**

**Q: List the various types of air pollution control devices for particulate matter with their working principles.**

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. No. | Device | Working Principle | Typical Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Settling Chamber** | Gravity causes large particles to settle; relies on Stokes' law. | 50% for > 50 µm |
| 2 | **Cyclone Separator** | Centrifugal force throws particles to outer wall; clean air spirals upward. | 70–95% for 5–50 µm |
| 3 | **Wet Scrubber (Venturi)** | High-velocity gas jet atomizes liquid; droplets capture particulates by impaction. | 80–95% for 1–10 µm |
| 4 | **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP)** | Corona discharge charges particles; collected on oppositely charged plates. | 99%+ for sub-micron |
| 5 | **Baghouse / Fabric Filter** | Particles trapped on fabric surface as gas passes through filter media. | 99.9% for 0.1–5 µm |
| 6 | **HEPA Filter** | High-density fiber mat with diffusion + interception mechanisms. | 99.97% for 0.3 µm |

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners award full 3 marks only if you **briefly state the principle** for each device, not just list the names.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working of an **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP)** with a neat sketch. State any **four** advantages and **two** limitations.

#### Model Solution:

**Working Principle of ESP:**

An ESP removes particulate matter from flue gas using the **principle of electrostatic attraction**. The process involves three stages:

1. **Charging (Ionization):** A high negative voltage (20–100 kV DC) is applied to the discharge electrode (thin wire), creating a **corona discharge**. Free electrons attach to passing dust particles, giving them a **net negative charge**.

2. **Collection (Migration):** The charged particles migrate through the electric field toward the **collecting electrode plates** (positively charged or grounded). The migration velocity is given by:

$$\omega = \dfrac{q \cdot E}{3 \cdot \pi \cdot \mu \cdot d_p}$$

where $\omega$ = drift velocity, $q$ = charge on particle, $E$ = electric field strength, $d_p$ = particle diameter, $\mu$ = gas viscosity.

3. **Removal (Rapping):** Periodically, **rappers** strike the collecting plates, causing the dust layer to dislodge and fall into **hoppers** below. The collected fly ash is then conveyed to ash silos for disposal or reuse (cement, brick manufacture).

**Types:** Wire-plate ESP (most common), Wire-pipe ESP (for small flow rates).

**Advantages (any four):**
1. Collection efficiency exceeds **99%**, even for sub-micron PM2.5 particles.
2. Very **low pressure drop** (~10–25 mm H₂O), saving fan power.
3. Can handle **high-temperature gas** (up to 400°C) — ideal for power plants.
4. Low **operating cost** after installation; no moving parts in gas stream.
5. Can handle large **gas flow rates** (millions of m³/hr).
6. Dry collection — no water pollution issue.

**Limitations (any two):**
1. **High capital cost** and requires skilled maintenance.
2. **Cannot remove gaseous pollutants** like SO₂, NOx — needs FGD separately.
3. Performance drops if dust has **high electrical resistivity** (>10¹¹ Ω·cm).
4. Space requirement is large compared to baghouse.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A wastewater treatment plant receives a flow of $Q = 50$ MLD with $BOD_5 = 220$ mg/L. The deoxygenation constant is $k = 0.25$ day⁻¹ at 20°C. Calculate:
1. The **ultimate BOD** ($L_0$).
2. The **BOD remaining** after 7 days.
3. The **mass of oxygen** (in kg/day) required by the aeration tank if 60% of the ultimate BOD is to be removed in secondary treatment.

#### Model Solution:

**Given:**
- $Q = 50$ MLD = 50 × 10⁶ L/day
- $BOD_5 = 220$ mg/L
- $k = 0.25$ day⁻¹

**1. Ultimate BOD ($L_0$):**

The relationship between $BOD_5$ and $L_0$:

$$BOD_5 = L_0 \cdot (1 - e^{-k \cdot 5})$$

$$220 = L_0 \cdot (1 - e^{-0.25 \cdot 5}) = L_0 \cdot (1 - e^{-1.25})$$

$$e^{-1.25} = 0.2865$$

$$220 = L_0 \cdot (1 - 0.2865) = L_0 \cdot 0.7135$$

$$L_0 = \dfrac{220}{0.7135} = 308.3 \text{ mg/L}$$

$$\boxed{L_0 \approx 308.3 \text{ mg/L}}$$

**2. BOD remaining after 7 days:**

$$L_7 = L_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot 7} = 308.3 \cdot e^{-0.25 \cdot 7} = 308.3 \cdot e^{-1.75}$$

$$e^{-1.75} = 0.1738$$

$$\boxed{L_7 = 53.6 \text{ mg/L}}$$

**3. Mass of oxygen required:**

Oxygen demand removed in secondary treatment = 60% of $L_0$:

$$\Delta L = 0.60 \cdot L_0 = 0.60 \cdot 308.3 = 185.0 \text{ mg/L}$$

Mass of oxygen per day:

$$M = Q \cdot \Delta L = 50 \times 10^6 \text{ L/day} \cdot 185.0 \text{ mg/L}$$

$$M = 9.25 \times 10^9 \text{ mg/day} = 9.25 \times 10^6 \text{ g/day}$$

$$M = 9.25 \times 10^3 \text{ kg/day}$$

$$\boxed{M_{O_2} = 9250 \text{ kg/day}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (2–3 marks loss):** Many students forget to **convert MLD → L/day** and use mg/L directly with MLD, producing numerically wrong answers. Always show unit conversion explicitly. Also, $e^{-0.25 \times 5} = e^{-1.25}$ — students sometimes compute $e^{-1.25}$ as $0.882$ which is wrong (it should be $0.2865$). Memorize: $e^{-1} \approx 0.368$, $e^{-2} \approx 0.135$, $e^{-1.25} \approx 0.2865$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a **neat flow diagram**, describe the **municipal sewage treatment plant (STP)** explaining the functions of the **primary, secondary, and tertiary** stages. State the typical **percentage removals** at each stage.

#### Model Solution:

**Diagram (to be drawn on answer script):**

```
Raw Sewage → Bar Screen → Grit Chamber → Primary Clarifier → Activated
Sludge Tank → Secondary Clarifier → Sand Filter → Chlorination → Treated
Effluent to River
```

**Stage 1: Preliminary & Primary Treatment (Physical)**

- **Bar Screens:** Remove large floating objects (rags, sticks, plastics) by manual/mechanical raking. Opening: 15–50 mm.
- **Grit Chamber:** Removes inorganic heavy particles (sand, gravel) by gravity; flow velocity maintained at 0.25–0.30 m/s to prevent organic carryover.
- **Primary Clarifier:** Large settling tank (detention time 2–3 hours) where 50–70% of suspended solids and 25–40% of BOD settle as **primary sludge**. Floating scum is skimmed off.
- **Removal:** TSS ≈ 50–70%, BOD ≈ 25–40%, no nutrient removal.

**Stage 2: Secondary Treatment (Biological)**

- **Activated Sludge Process (ASP):** Aerated tank where air diffusers supply oxygen; heterotrophic bacteria oxidize dissolved organics into $CO_2$ + $H_2O$ + new biomass. Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids (MLSS) maintained at 2000–4000 mg/L.
- **Secondary Clarifier:** Settles the biological flocs; clarified water flows to tertiary stage; settled sludge is partly **Returned Activated Sludge (RAS)** to aeration tank and partly **Waste Activated Sludge (WAS)** to sludge handling.
- **Removal:** BOD 85–95%, TSS 80–95%, some nitrification.

**Stage 3: Tertiary Treatment (Advanced/Polishing)**

- **Filtration (Rapid Sand Filter):** Removes residual suspended solids.
- **Disinfection:** Chlorination (Cl₂ dose 5–15 mg/L, contact time 30 min) or UV irradiation to kill pathogens (coliforms < 1000 MPN/100 mL).
- **Nutrient Removal (if required):** Nitrification-Denitrification (biological), or chemical precipitation of phosphorus using alum/lime.
- **Removal:** Pathogens 99.9%, residual BOD < 10 mg/L, TSS < 5 mg/L.

**Overall Plant Removal Efficiency:** BOD 95–99%, TSS 95–99%, Coliforms 99.99%.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A community generates **2,00,000 litres/day** of wastewater with an average **BOD of 250 mg/L**. The proposed STP uses primary + secondary treatment achieving **95% BOD removal**. Calculate:
1. The **BOD of the treated effluent** (mg/L).
2. The **mass of BOD removed per day** (kg/day).
3. The **population equivalent** of this discharge (assuming per capita BOD = 0.08 kg/day).

#### Model Solution:

**Given:**
- $Q = 2,00,000$ L/day = $2 \times 10^5$ L/day
- $BOD_{in} = 250$ mg/L
- Removal efficiency $\eta = 95\%$
- Per capita BOD = 0.08 kg/day

**1. BOD of treated effluent:**

$$BOD_{out} = BOD_{in} \cdot (1 - \eta) = 250 \cdot (1 - 0.95) = 250 \cdot 0.05$$

$$\boxed{BOD_{out} = 12.5 \text{ mg/L}}$$

This is well below the CPCB standard of 30 mg/L for discharge into surface water. ✓

**2. Mass of BOD removed per day:**

$$\Delta BOD = BOD_{in} \cdot \eta = 250 \cdot 0.95 = 237.5 \text{ mg/L}$$

Total mass removed per day:

$$M = Q \cdot \Delta BOD = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ L/day} \cdot 237.5 \text{ mg/L}$$

$$M = 4.75 \times 10^7 \text{ mg/day} = 47.5 \times 10^3 \text{ g/day}$$

$$\boxed{M_{removed} = 47.5 \text{ kg/day}}$$

**3. Population Equivalent (PE):**

Total BOD load in raw wastewater (before treatment):

$$\text{Load} = Q \cdot BOD_{in} = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ L/day} \cdot 250 \text{ mg/L}$$

$$\text{Load} = 5 \times 10^7 \text{ mg/day} = 50 \text{ kg/day}$$

Population equivalent:

$$PE = \dfrac{\text{Total BOD load}}{\text{Per capita BOD}} = \dfrac{50 \text{ kg/day}}{0.08 \text{ kg/person/day}}$$

$$\boxed{PE = 625 \text{ persons}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Common 2-mark loss):** In PE problems, use the **raw wastewater BOD load** (not the *removed* load). Some students mistakenly use $47.5$ kg/day as the load, leading to $PE = 594$, which is **wrong** by ~5%. The PE concept compares the *strength* of the industrial/communal discharge to that of domestic sewage from a known population, hence the **influent** load must be used.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this and revise 10 minutes before your exam.**

- [x] **Stokes' Law:** $V_s = \dfrac{g(\rho_p - \rho_f)d^2}{18\mu}$ — valid only for **laminar flow ($Re_p < 1$)** and **fine particles**.
- [x] **BOD-COD Order:** Always $\text{COD} \geq \text{BOD}$; ratio > 3 indicates **non-biodegradable** industrial effluent.
- [x] **Streeter-Phelps BOD Equation:** $L_t = L_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$ (remaining); $Y_t = L_0(1 - e^{-kt})$ (exerted).
- [x] **Primary vs Secondary vs Tertiary:** Physical → Biological → Chemical/Disinfection. Memorize the **order**.
- [x] **APC Device Selection Rule:** Coarse particles (>50 µm) → settling/cyclone; Fine (<10 µm) → ESP/baghouse; Toxic gases → scrubber/SCR.
- [x] **5R Solid Waste Hierarchy:** Refuse → Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Recover (in that exact priority order).
- [x] **NAAQS Limits:** PM2.5 = 60 µg/m³, PM10 = 100 µg/m³ (24-hr), SO₂ = 80 µg/m³, NO₂ = 80 µg/m³.
- [x] **BIS Drinking Water:** TDS ≤ 500 mg/L, pH 6.5–8.5, turbidity ≤ 1 NTU.
- [x] **Noise Limits:** Industrial 75 dB(A) day / 70 dB(A) night; commercial 65/55; residential 55/45.
- [x] **SPL Combination Formula:** $L_{total} = 10 \log_{10}(\sum 10^{L_i/10})$ — **always** use this for multiple noise sources; do **not** arithmetically add dB values.
- [x] **BS-VI Auto Norms:** Mandatory OBD (On-Board Diagnostics), catalytic converter, SCR for diesel.
- [x] **CPCB Standards for Sewage Discharge:** BOD ≤ 30 mg/L, TSS ≤ 100 mg/L, Fecal Coliform ≤ 1000 MPN/100 mL.
- [x] **Population Equivalent Formula:** $PE = \dfrac{\text{Total BOD load (kg/day)}}{0.08}$ — use **influent** load only.
- [x] **Stokes' Law Unit Trap:** $V_s$ is in **m/s** only if all inputs are in SI; $d$ must be in **metres**, not mm or µm.
- [x] **Efficiency of ESP:** Migration velocity $\omega$ is directly proportional to electric field $E$ and charge $q$, inversely to particle diameter.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** In numerical questions, **always write units explicitly**. Show **Stokes' law / BOD equation substitution step-by-step**. Partial marks are awarded for the *correct setup* even if the final arithmetic slips. **Diagrams carry marks** — in descriptive 7-mark questions, a labelled flow diagram can fetch 2–3 marks even if your text is imperfect.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sustainable Development and Renewable Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDEST203 - BASIC MECHANICAL AND CIVIL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sustainable Development and Renewable Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Sustainable Development – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Brundtland Commission, 1987)**
> *"Sustainable Development is the development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs."*
> — **World Commission on Environment and Development (WCED), Our Common Future Report, 1987**

In simple KTU-board terminology, **Sustainable Development** is a balanced approach to economic growth, social well-being, and environmental protection, ensuring that natural resources are used at a rate at which they can be replenished naturally.

### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy – The "Earth as a Bank Account"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine Earth is a **bank account**. If you withdraw money (use resources) faster than the bank pays interest (natural replenishment), you will eventually go bankrupt (ecological collapse). Sustainable development means living off the **interest**, not the **principal**.

- **Principal** = Stock of natural resources (fossil fuels, forests, clean water).
- **Interest** = Renewable flow of resources (solar radiation, wind, tides).
- **Sustainable** = Withdrawal $\leq$ Interest rate.
- **Unsustainable** = Withdrawal $>$ Interest rate (deforestation, over-mining).

---

## 1.2 The Three Pillars (Triple Bottom Line)

> [!NOTE]
> Sustainable development rests on **three interconnected pillars**. For KTU exams, this is a **3-mark sure-shot question**.

| Pillar | Focus | Engineering Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Environmental** | Ecology, resources, pollution | Solar panels, rainwater harvesting |
| **Social** | Equity, health, education | Affordable housing, clean cooking fuel |
| **Economic** | Growth, employment, industry | Green jobs, GDP from renewable sector |

A project is **truly sustainable only if it is sustainable in ALL three pillars simultaneously**.

---

## 1.3 Renewable Energy – Core Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Renewable Energy** is energy collected from resources which are naturally replenished on a human time-scale, such as **sunlight, wind, rain, tides, waves, and geothermal heat**.

The opposite is **Non-Renewable Energy** (coal, petroleum, natural gas, nuclear) — which exists in fixed quantities and takes **millions of years** to form.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (KTU):** Global renewable capacity in 2024 is approximately **444 GW** added in a single year (IRENA data). India targets **500 GW of non-fossil capacity by 2030**.

### 1.3.1 Real-World Analogy – "The Self-Filling Bucket"

Think of energy as water in a bucket:
- **Non-renewable** = Pouring water from a sealed tank (eventually empty).
- **Renewable** = A bucket kept under a running tap (the sun keeps "refilling" the source every day).

---

## 1.4 Why Study This? (KTU Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Module 4 of GDEST203 is **directly linked to United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (UN-SDGs)**. KTU frequently tests:
> 1. Definition of Sustainable Development (3 marks).
> 2. List of renewable energy sources with examples (3 marks).
> 3. Working principle of Solar PV / Wind / Tidal systems (14 marks).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Energy Sources

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Board frequently asks: *"Classify energy sources with two examples each."* (Module Internal choice, 7 marks)

### 2.1.1 Primary vs Secondary Energy
- **Primary Energy**: Found in nature (coal, sunlight, crude oil, wind).
- **Secondary Energy**: Derived from primary (electricity from coal/dam, hydrogen from water electrolysis).

### 2.1.2 Conventional vs Non-Conventional
- **Conventional (Traditional)**: Firewood, coal, petroleum, natural gas.
- **Non-Conventional (Modern Renewable)**: Solar, wind, tidal, geothermal, biomass, hydrogen.

### 2.1.3 Renewable vs Non-Renewable
- **Renewable**: Replenished naturally (sun, wind, biomass, hydro, geothermal, tidal).
- **Non-Renewable**: Finite stock (fossil fuels, uranium).

---

## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Major Renewable Sources

### A. Solar Energy

> [!NOTE]
> **Source of Energy:** Nuclear fusion of hydrogen into helium in the Sun's core.
> **Solar Constant (Gsc):** $\mathbf{1367 \; W/m^2}$ (energy received at the outer atmosphere, perpendicular to sun rays).
> **At Earth's surface (AM 1.5):** Approximately **1000 W/m²** (used as standard test condition for PV panels).

**Two Principal Technologies:**
1. **Photovoltaic (PV) Cells** – Direct conversion of sunlight to electricity using semiconductors (Silicon-based).
2. **Solar Thermal** – Sunlight heats a fluid (water, oil, molten salt) which produces steam to drive a turbine.

### B. Wind Energy

> [!NOTE]
> **Source:** Differential heating of Earth's surface by the sun creates pressure gradients, causing air movement.
> **Key Parameter:** Power in wind is proportional to the **cube of wind speed** — doubling wind speed gives **8× more power**.

### C. Biomass Energy

> [!NOTE]
> **Source:** Organic matter (agricultural residue, wood, animal waste, municipal solid waste).
> **Conversion routes:** Direct combustion, biogas (anaerobic digestion), bioethanol/biodiesel (fermentation, transesterification).

### D. Geothermal Energy

> [!NOTE]
> **Source:** Heat from Earth's core (radioactive decay of U, Th, K).
> **Types:** Dry steam, flash steam, binary cycle plants.
> **India's Potential:** Puga Valley (Ladakh), Manikaran (Himachal Pradesh), Surajkund (Haryana).

### E. Tidal & Wave Energy

> **[!NOTE]**
> **Source:** Gravitational pull of the Moon and Sun on ocean waters.
> **Tidal Range** required for economic generation: $\geq \mathbf{5 \; m}$.
> **India's potential site:** Gulf of Kutch and Gulf of Cambay (tidal range 5–8 m).

### F. Hydroelectric Energy

> **[!NOTE]**
> **Source:** Potential energy of stored water at height.
> **Two main types:**
> - **Large Hydro (>25 MW):** Major dams.
> - **Small Hydro (<25 MW):** Run-of-river projects.

### G. Hydrogen Energy (Future Fuel)

> **[!NOTE]**
> **Source:** Electrolysis of water using renewable electricity, producing **Green Hydrogen**.
> **Key property:** Combustion product is **water only** (zero CO₂ emission at point of use).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following table — these are the most repeated equations in KTU university exams for this module.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Application |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Power in Wind | $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \, \rho \, A \, v^{3} \, C_{p}$ | $\rho$ = air density (kg/m³), $A$ = swept area (m²), $v$ = wind speed (m/s), $C_p$ = power coefficient (max **0.593**, Betz limit) | Wind turbine output |
| 2 | Solar Cell Output | $P_{solar} = \eta \cdot I_{solar} \cdot A_{panel}$ | $\eta$ = efficiency (0.15–0.22 for Si), $I$ = irradiance (W/m²), $A$ = area (m²) | PV array sizing |
| 3 | Hydroelectric Power | $P_{hydro} = \rho \, g \, Q \, H \, \eta$ | $\rho$ = water density (1000 kg/m³), $g = 9.81 \; m/s^2$, $Q$ = discharge (m³/s), $H$ = head (m), $\eta$ = efficiency (~0.85) | Dam-based plants |
| 4 | Tidal Power | $P_{tidal} = \frac{1}{2} \, \rho \, A \, v^{3} \, C_{p}$ | Same form as wind; $\rho$ = seawater density (~1025 kg/m³) | Tidal stream turbines |
| 5 | Wave Power | $P_{wave} \approx \frac{\rho \, g^{2}}{64 \, \pi} \, H_{s}^{2} \, T_{e}$ | $H_s$ = significant wave height, $T_e$ = wave period | Oscillating water columns |
| 6 | Geothermal Power | $P_{geo} = \dot{m} \, c_{p} \, (T_{hot} - T_{cold})$ | $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate, $c_p$ = specific heat | Flash/binary plants |
| 7 | Energy Payback Time | $EPBT = \frac{E_{manufacture}}{E_{annual,generated}}$ | Years | Compare renewable systems |
| 8 | Carbon Footprint | $CF = \sum E_{i} \cdot EF_{i}$ | $E_i$ = energy use, $EF_i$ = emission factor (kg CO₂/kWh) | Sustainability audit |
| 9 | Energy Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{E_{useful}}{E_{input}} \times 100$ | Percentage | Performance rating |
| 10 | Capacity Factor | $CF = \frac{Actual \; energy}{Installed \; capacity \times 8760}$ | Ratio (0–1) | Solar: 0.15–0.25; Wind: 0.20–0.40 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** For wind/tidal formulas, students often forget **Betz limit** ($C_p \leq 0.593$). Writing $C_p = 1$ in the exam costs marks. Always use $C_p = 0.35$ to $0.45$ for real turbines.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Civil Engineering** | Green building design, rainwater harvesting, low-carbon concrete. |
| **Mechanical Engineering** | Wind turbine blade design, solar thermal receivers, heat exchangers. |
| **Electrical Engineering** | Grid integration of solar/wind, MPPT controllers, battery storage. |
| **Architecture** | Passive solar building orientation, daylighting design. |
| **Urban Planning** | Smart cities, transit-oriented development, waste-to-energy plants. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivations are **favourite KTU 14-mark questions**. Walk through every step slowly; the examiner awards marks for **logical flow**, not just final answer.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Power Available in Wind (Wind Turbine Energy Equation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is asked almost every year under "Explain with neat sketch the working of a wind energy conversion system" (14 marks).

### Step 1: Mass of Air Flowing Through Swept Area per Second

Consider a wind turbine with rotor diameter $D$, sweeping an area:

$$A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$$

In one second, air moving at velocity $v$ covers a length equal to $v$ metres. Hence the volume of air passing per second is $A \cdot v$.

Multiplying by the density of air $\rho$, the **mass flow rate** is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \, A \, v$$

### Step 2: Kinetic Energy of This Air Mass

Kinetic energy of a moving body of mass $m$ and velocity $v$ is:

$$KE = \frac{1}{2} m v^{2}$$

Substituting the mass flow rate for the energy **per second** (i.e., power in the wind):

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} v^{2} = \frac{1}{2} (\rho A v) v^{2}$$

$$\boxed{P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}}$$

### Step 3: Actual Power Extracted (Betz's Law)

A real turbine cannot extract 100 % of the wind's kinetic energy. The fraction extracted is the **power coefficient** $C_p$. German physicist **Albert Betz (1919)** proved that the theoretical maximum value of $C_p$ is:

$$C_{p,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593 \; (59.3\%)$$

Therefore, the **actual mechanical power available at the turbine shaft** is:

$$\boxed{P_{turbine} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3} C_{p} \quad \text{with} \quad C_{p} \leq 0.593}$$

### Step 4: Generator Output (After Gearbox & Generator Losses)

Including mechanical efficiency $\eta_m$ and generator efficiency $\eta_g$:

$$P_{electrical} = P_{turbine} \cdot \eta_{m} \cdot \eta_{g} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3} C_{p} \eta_{m} \eta_{g}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Hydroelectric Power from a Dam

> **[!NOTE]**
> Standard 7-mark sub-question: *"Derive an expression for the power developed by a hydroelectric plant."*

### Step 1: Potential Energy of Water Stored at Height

Water of mass $m$ stored at height $H$ above the turbine possesses gravitational potential energy:

$$E_{PE} = m \, g \, H$$

### Step 2: Mass Flow Rate from Discharge

If water flows at volume rate $Q$ (m³/s) and density is $\rho$ (kg/m³), the mass flow rate is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \, Q$$

### Step 3: Theoretical Power (Assuming 100 % Conversion)

$$P_{ideal} = \dot{m} \, g \, H = \rho \, Q \, g \, H$$

### Step 4: Actual Electrical Output (Including Turbine & Generator Efficiency)

$$\boxed{P_{electrical} = \rho \, g \, Q \, H \, \eta_{overall}}$$

For a typical plant:
- $\rho = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^{3}$
- $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^{2}$
- $\eta_{overall} \approx 0.85$ (turbine × generator)

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
A small hydro plant has head $H = 25 \; m$ and discharge $Q = 10 \; m^{3}/s$. With $\eta = 0.85$:

$$P = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 10 \times 25 \times 0.85$$

$$P = 2{,}084{,}625 \; W \approx 2.08 \; \text{MW}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Renewable Sources

> **[!NOTE]**
> The following table is **mandatory for 14-mark comparison questions**.

| Parameter | Solar PV | Wind | Hydro | Biomass | Geothermal | Tidal |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Source** | Sunlight | Wind | Stored water | Organic matter | Earth heat | Ocean tides |
| **Availability** | Daytime, weather dependent | 24h, weather dependent | Continuous | Continuous | 24h, continuous | Cyclic (12h 25m) |
| **Capacity Factor** | 0.15–0.25 | 0.20–0.40 | 0.40–0.60 | 0.60–0.80 | 0.70–0.95 | 0.20–0.35 |
| **Initial Cost** | High | High | Very High | Medium | High | Very High |
| **CO₂ Emission (g/kWh)** | 40–50 | 10–20 | 4–13 | 25–50 | 45 | 15–25 |
| **Land Footprint** | Large | Medium | Very Large (with reservoir) | Medium | Small | Small |
| **Maturity (TRL)** | 9 (Commercial) | 9 (Commercial) | 9 (Commercial) | 8 | 7–8 | 5–6 |
| **Best Location (India)** | Rajasthan, Gujarat | Tamil Nadu, Gujarat | Himalayas, Western Ghats | Punjab, UP | Ladakh, HP | Gujarat (Kutch) |
| **Energy Payback (yrs)** | 1–2 | 0.5–1 | 0.2–0.5 | 1–2 | 1–2 | 3–5 |
| **Key Limitation** | Intermittency | Intermittency | Ecological impact | Land for crops | Location-specific | Few sites, costly |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write "Tidal energy works 24 hours". **Wrong** — Tidal cycles are **semi-diurnal** (~12 h 25 min) with spring and neap variations. Power is available in bursts, not continuously.

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Energy Payback Time (EPBT) Calculation

> **[!NOTE]**
> Often asked as a 3-mark numerical: *"A solar PV system requires 1500 kWh to manufacture and produces 1200 kWh/year. Find the EPBT."*

### Step 1: Write the Formula

$$EPBT = \frac{\text{Embodied Energy (manufacture)}}{\text{Annual Energy Output}}$$

### Step 2: Substitute Values

$$EPBT = \frac{1500 \; \text{kWh}}{1200 \; \text{kWh/year}}$$

### Step 3: Compute

$$EPBT = 1.25 \; \text{years}$$

### Step 4: Interpret

The system recovers the energy used in its manufacture in **1.25 years**, then produces **net clean energy** for its remaining 20–25 year lifetime.

> [!NOTE]
> **Benchmark values for KTU reference:**
> - Coal plant EPBT: continuous (never pays back CO₂).
> - Solar PV EPBT: 1–2 years.
> - Wind turbine EPBT: 0.5–1 year.
> - Nuclear plant EPBT: 5–10 years.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Solar PV Array Sizing

**Question (KTU 14-mark type):** *Design a solar PV system for a house that consumes 10 kWh/day. Assume average sun-hours = 5 h/day, PV efficiency = 18 %, derating factor = 0.77, battery autonomy = 1 day, battery efficiency = 0.85, DoD = 0.8, system voltage = 48 V.*

### Step 1: Total Daily Energy Required

$$E_{daily} = 10 \; \text{kWh/day}$$

### Step 2: Energy Required from PV Array (Accounting for Battery Losses)

$$E_{PV} = \frac{E_{daily}}{\eta_{battery} \times DoD} = \frac{10}{0.85 \times 0.8} = 14.71 \; \text{kWh/day}$$

### Step 3: PV Array Wattage Required

$$P_{PV} = \frac{E_{PV}}{Sun-hours \times Derating} = \frac{14700}{5 \times 0.77} = 3818 \; W \approx 3.82 \; \text{kWp}$$

### Step 4: PV Array Area

$$A = \frac{P_{PV}}{\eta \times 1000 \; W/m^{2}} = \frac{3818}{0.18 \times 1000} = 21.2 \; m^{2}$$

### Step 5: Battery Bank Capacity

$$C_{battery} = \frac{E_{daily} \times Autonomy}{V_{system} \times DoD} = \frac{10000 \times 1}{48 \times 0.8} = 260.4 \; \text{Ah}$$

**Final Design:** ~3.82 kWp PV array, ~22 m² roof area, 48 V / 260 Ah battery bank.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> **[!NOTE]**
> KTU 14-mark questions require **neat labelled sketches**. The following Mermaid diagrams provide the block-level flow that you must redraw by hand in the exam.

---

## 4.1 Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS) – Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Natural Wind Kinetic Energy] --> A2[Wind Turbine Rotor Blades]
    A2 --> A3[Aerodynamic Conversion: KE to Mechanical Rotation]
    A3 --> A4[Gearbox: Steps Up RPM from Rotor to Generator]
    A4 --> A5[Generator: Mechanical to Electrical AC]
    A5 --> A6[Power Conditioning Unit: Rectifier and Inverter]
    A6 --> A7[Transformer: Steps Up Voltage for Grid]
    A7 --> A8[Electrical Grid or Local Load Bank]
    A8 --> A9[Net Metering and Revenue Meter]

    B1[Pitch Control System] --> A2
    B2[Yaw Drive Motor] --> A2
    C1[Anemometer Input] --> A3
    C1 --> B1

    style A1 fill:#cce5ff
    style A8 fill:#d4edda
    style A9 fill:#fff3cd
```

**Key Labels You Must Add by Hand in Exam:**
1. Rotor diameter $D$ and swept area $A = \pi D^{2}/4$.
2. Wind velocity $v$ (m/s).
3. Power coefficient $C_p$ and Betz limit 0.593.
4. Hub height, nacelle, tail vane (for horizontal axis) or Darrieus/Savonius (for vertical axis).

---

## 4.2 Solar PV System – Standalone Off-Grid Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Sunlight: Irradiance 1000 W per m squared] --> S2[PV Array: Silicon Cells DC Output]
    S2 --> S3[Charge Controller with MPPT Algorithm]
    S3 --> S4[Battery Bank: Deep Cycle Lead Acid or Lithium Ion]
    S3 --> S5[DC Bus: 12V 24V 48V]
    S5 --> S6[DC Loads: LED Lights Fans]
    S5 --> S7[Inverter: DC to AC Conversion]
    S7 --> S8[AC Loads: Refrigerator Television]
    S8 --> S9[Net Meter if Grid Tied]

    style S1 fill:#fff3cd
    style S2 fill:#cce5ff
    style S4 fill:#f8d7da
    style S7 fill:#d4edda
```

**Working Principle (for 14-mark answer):**
- PV cells generate DC electricity through the **photovoltaic effect** (photons knock electrons from the valence band to conduction band in a p-n junction).
- Charge controller prevents battery overcharge/discharge.
- Inverter converts DC to AC (50 Hz, 230 V in India).

---

## 4.3 Hydroelectric Power Plant – Schematic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H1[Reservoir: Water Stored at Head H] --> H2[Penstock: High Pressure Pipeline]
    H2 --> H3[Control Valves and Trash Racks]
    H3 --> H4[Pelton or Francis or Kaplan Turbine]
    H4 --> H5[Generator Coupled to Turbine Shaft]
    H5 --> H6[Step Up Transformer]
    H6 --> H7[Transmission Lines to Grid]
    H4 --> H8[Tailrace: Water Discharged Back to River]
    H8 --> H1

    style H1 fill:#cce5ff
    H4:::turbine
    classDef turbine fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:2px
```

**Turbine Selection Rule (memorize for exam):**
- **Pelton Wheel** → High head, low discharge (mountain regions).
- **Francis Turbine** → Medium head, medium discharge.
- **Kaplan Turbine** → Low head, high discharge (large dams, tidal barrages).

---

## 4.4 Biomass Energy Conversion Pathways

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BM1[Biomass Feedstock: Agricultural Waste Wood Manure] --> BM2{Conversion Process Selection}

    BM2 -->|Combustion| BM3[Direct Burning: Produces Steam]
    BM3 --> BM4[Steam Turbine Generates Electricity]

    BM2 -->|Anaerobic Digestion| BM5[Biogas Plant: Produces Methane CH4]
    BM5 --> BM6[Biogas for Cooking or Engine Generator]

    BM2 -->|Fermentation| BM7[Bioethanol Plant: Sugars to Alcohol]
    BM7 --> BM8[Bioethanol Blended with Petrol E10 E20]

    BM2 -->|Transesterification| BM9[Biodiesel Plant: Oils to Methyl Esters]
    BM9 --> BM10[Biodiesel for Diesel Engines]

    style BM1 fill:#d4edda
    style BM3 fill:#fff3cd
    style BM5 fill:#f8d7da
    style BM7 fill:#cce5ff
```

---

## 4.5 Tidal Power Plant – Working Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[High Tide: Sea Water Enters Basin] --> T2[Sluice Gate Opens]
    T2 --> T3[Barrage Reservoir Fills]
    T3 --> T4[Low Tide: Sluice Gate Closes]
    T4 --> T5[Water Released Through Turbine]
    T5 --> T6[Bulb Turbine Coupled to Generator]
    T6 --> T7[Electrical Output to Grid]

    T1 -.->|Reverse Cycle| T8[Low Tide Generation: Sea Below Basin Level]
    T8 --> T5

    style T1 fill:#cce5ff
    style T5 fill:#fff3cd
    style T6 fill:#d4edda
```

**Three Generation Modes (commonly asked in viva):**
1. **Single Basin – Single Effect**: Generate only at ebb tide.
2. **Single Basin – Double Effect**: Generate at both flood and ebb.
3. **Double Basin**: Continuous generation (rare, expensive).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> **[!NOTE]**
> Question pattern follows the **2024 Scheme KTU End Semester Evaluation (ESE)** format. Each question below is tagged with the relevant **Course Outcome (CO)** and **Bloom's Level**.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define sustainable development. List its three pillars.**

**Model Answer:**

> Sustainable development, as defined by the **Brundtland Commission Report (1987)**, is *"the development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs."*

The **three pillars** (Triple Bottom Line) are:

| Pillar | Focus |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Environmental Sustainability** | Conservation of natural resources, biodiversity, pollution control |
| **Social Sustainability** | Equity, health, education, community well-being |
| **Economic Sustainability** | Growth, employment, profitability |

A development project is sustainable only when it is viable in **all three dimensions** simultaneously.

> **[CO Mapping: CO4 | Bloom Level: Remember/Understand]**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between renewable and non-renewable energy sources. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Renewable Energy | Non-Renewable Energy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | Sources replenished naturally in human time-scale | Finite stock; takes millions of years to form |
| **Availability** | Continuous / cyclic | Limited, depleting |
| **Environmental Impact** | Low carbon, low pollution | High CO₂, pollution, climate change |
| **Examples** | Solar, Wind, Hydro, Biomass, Tidal, Geothermal, Hydrogen | Coal, Petroleum, Natural Gas, Nuclear (Uranium) |
| **Initial Cost** | Generally high | Generally lower for thermal plants |
| **Operating Cost** | Near zero (sun, wind are free) | Fuel cost is significant |

> **[CO Mapping: CO4 | Bloom Level: Understand]**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[!NOTE]**
> KTU ESE pattern: **Choice between two questions**, each with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Explain the working principle of a wind energy conversion system (WECS) with a neat sketch. Derive the power available in the wind.

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**
Wind energy is the kinetic energy of moving air masses caused by **uneven heating of the Earth's surface by the sun**. A wind energy conversion system converts this kinetic energy into electrical energy through the following stages:

1. **Rotor (Blades)**: As wind strikes the aerodynamically shaped blades, it creates a pressure difference (lift and drag), causing the rotor to rotate.
2. **Hub and Shaft**: The hub connects the blades to the low-speed shaft, transmitting mechanical rotation.
3. **Gearbox**: Steps up the slow rotor RPM (15–20 rpm) to the high speed required by the generator (1500 rpm for 50 Hz, 4-pole).
4. **Generator**: Converts mechanical rotation into electrical energy (synchronous or asynchronous/induction type).
5. **Yaw and Pitch Control**: Yaw aligns the rotor with wind direction; pitch control adjusts blade angle to optimize power and protect against storms.
6. **Power Conditioning**: Rectifier-inverter units condition the electrical output before feeding to the grid.

**Derivation of Power in Wind:**
Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the complete derivation. Final expression:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3} C_{p}, \quad C_{p,max} = 0.593 \; (\text{Betz limit})$$

**Diagram:** Draw the block diagram from **Section 4.1** of these notes (neat, labelled, in pencil).

> **[CO Mapping: CO4 | Bloom Level: Apply/Analyse | Marks: 4 sketch + 3 derivation]**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: A wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 40 m. Wind speed is 12 m/s, air density is 1.226 kg/m³, and overall efficiency (combined $C_p$ and mechanical) is 35 %. Calculate (i) the swept area, (ii) the total power in the wind, and (iii) the actual electrical power output.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Swept Area:**
$$A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (40)^{2}}{4} = 1256.6 \; m^{2}$$

**(ii) Total Power in Wind:**
$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3} = 0.5 \times 1.226 \times 1256.6 \times (12)^{3}$$

Computing step by step:
$$P_{wind} = 0.5 \times 1.226 \times 1256.6 \times 1728$$

$$P_{wind} = 1{,}331{,}065 \; W \approx 1.33 \; \text{MW}$$

**(iii) Actual Electrical Power Output:**
$$P_{actual} = P_{wind} \times \eta_{overall} = 1.33 \times 0.35 = 0.466 \; \text{MW} \approx 466 \; \text{kW}$$

**Result:** The turbine can deliver approximately **466 kW** to the grid.

> **[CO Mapping: CO4 | Bloom Level: Apply | Marks: 7]**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: With a neat diagram, explain the working of a solar photovoltaic (PV) system for power generation. What is the photovoltaic effect?

**Model Solution:**

**Photovoltaic Effect:**
The **photovoltaic effect** is the generation of a voltage and electric current in a material upon exposure to light. When photons of sufficient energy strike a **p-n junction** semiconductor (typically silicon), they transfer energy to valence electrons, knocking them into the conduction band. This creates **electron-hole pairs** that are separated by the junction's electric field, producing a DC voltage across the cell.

**Working of a Solar PV System:**

1. **PV Array (Module)**: Multiple silicon cells (mono-crystalline, poly-crystalline, or thin-film) connected in series-parallel to obtain the required voltage and current.
2. **Charge Controller**: Prevents overcharging of batteries; modern units use **MPPT (Maximum Power Point Tracking)** algorithms to extract maximum power under varying irradiance.
3. **Battery Bank**: Stores DC energy for use during night/cloudy periods (typically lead-acid or lithium-ion).
4. **Inverter**: Converts DC to AC (sine wave, 50 Hz) to run AC appliances or feed the grid.
5. **Mounting Structure**: Fixed-tilt or single-axis / dual-axis trackers to maximize solar capture.

**Diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.2** of these notes. Sketch should include: Sun → PV array → Charge controller → Battery + Inverter → AC load/grid.

**Key Equations:**
- Cell voltage (open circuit): ~0.6 V for silicon.
- Standard Test Conditions: $1000 \; W/m^{2}$ irradiance, $25^{\circ}C$ cell temperature, AM 1.5 spectrum.
- Module efficiency: 15–22 % for commercial silicon panels.

> **[CO Mapping: CO4 | Bloom Level: Understand/Apply | Marks: 5 diagram + 2 explanation]**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: A small hydroelectric plant has a head of 30 m and a discharge of 5 m³/s. The turbine-generator overall efficiency is 85 %. Calculate (i) the power available in the water and (ii) the electrical power generated. If the plant operates 18 hours a day, find the daily energy output.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $H = 30 \; m$, $Q = 5 \; m^{3}/s$, $\eta = 0.85$, $\rho = 1000 \; kg/m^{3}$, $g = 9.81 \; m/s^{2}$.

**(i) Power Available in Water:**
$$P_{water} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 5 \times 30$$

$$P_{water} = 1{,}471{,}500 \; W = 1.4715 \; \text{MW}$$

**(ii) Electrical Power Generated:**
$$P_{electrical} = P_{water} \times \eta = 1.4715 \times 0.85$$

$$P_{electrical} = 1.2508 \; \text{MW} \approx 1.25 \; \text{MW}$$

**(iii) Daily Energy Output (18 h operation):**
$$E_{daily} = P_{electrical} \times t = 1.2508 \times 18$$

$$E_{daily} = 22.51 \; \text{MWh/day}$$

**Result:** The plant generates approximately **1.25 MW of power** and **22.51 MWh of energy per day**.

> **[CO Mapping: CO4 | Bloom Level: Apply | Marks: 7]**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (per actual KTU board examiner feedback):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers (always write W, kW, MW, m, m³/s).
> 2. **Wrong sign or wrong exponent** in $v^{3}$ calculation for wind power — common cube-of-12 mistake.
> 3. **Not stating Betz limit** when writing $C_p$ — examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing tidal and wave** energy — Tidal uses gravitational pull; Wave uses wind-induced surface oscillation.
> 5. **Skipping the sketch** in 14-mark questions — KTU awards **3–4 marks** purely for neat, labelled diagram.
> 6. **Writing "solar energy is renewable" without naming the conversion route** (PV or thermal) — vague answers lose 2 marks.
> 7. **No mention of efficiency / losses** in numerical derivations — examiner expects at least one sentence on $\eta$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **[!NOTE]**
> Final-day revision guide — read this **30 minutes before the exam**.

### Must-Know Definitions
- [ ] **Sustainable Development** (Brundtland 1987) — verbatim definition worth 2 marks.
- [ ] **Three Pillars** — Environmental, Social, Economic.
- [ ] **Renewable Energy** — naturally replenished within human timescale.
- [ ] **Photovoltaic Effect** — light → electricity in p-n junction.
- [ ] **Betz Limit** — maximum 59.3 % of wind energy extractable.
- [ ] **Solar Constant** — 1367 W/m² (at top of atmosphere).
- [ ] **Standard Test Conditions (STC)** — 1000 W/m², 25 °C, AM 1.5.

### Must-Know Formulas
- [ ] $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3} C_{p}$
- [ ] $P_{hydro} = \rho g Q H \eta$
- [ ] $P_{solar} = \eta \cdot I \cdot A$
- [ ] $EPBT = E_{manufacture} / E_{annual}$
- [ ] $\text{Capacity Factor} = \text{Actual energy} / (\text{Capacity} \times 8760)$

### Must-Know Comparisons
- [ ] Pelton (high head) vs Francis (medium) vs Kaplan (low head).
- [ ] Tidal (gravitational) vs Wave (wind-induced) — don't mix up.
- [ ] PV (direct conversion, no moving parts) vs Solar Thermal (uses turbine).

### Must-Know Indian Facts
- [ ] **Largest solar park**: Bhadla Solar Park, Rajasthan (2.25 GW).
- [ ] **Largest wind farm state**: Tamil Nadu (Muppandal).
- [ ] **First tidal plant**: Vizhinjam, Kerala (experimental).
- [ ] **National Hydrogen Mission**: Launched 2021, target 5 MMT by 2030.
- [ ] **Panchamrit承诺 (Panchamrit Pledge)** at COP-26: 500 GW non-fossil, 50 % renewable share, net zero by 2070.

### Must-Know UN-SDGs Linked
- [ ] **SDG 7**: Affordable and Clean Energy.
- [ ] **SDG 11**: Sustainable Cities and Communities.
- [ ] **SDG 13**: Climate Action.

### Final Exam Tips
- [ ] Always start 14-mark answers with a **labelled diagram** (3–4 marks secured upfront).
- [ ] In numericals, write **Given Data → Formula → Substitution → Final Answer with Units**.
- [ ] Use **Indian examples** (NTPC, Suzlon, Adani Green, SECI) for full marks in descriptive parts.
- [ ] End your 14-mark answer with a **2-line conclusion** on sustainability impact (impresses examiner).

> **Best of luck for your KTU ESE exam! Prepare the diagrams neatly, memorize the formulas, and structure every numerical in the Given–Formula–Substitution–Answer format. You will score full marks. 🌱⚡**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Climate Change and Mitigation Strategies (Brief Description Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDEST203 - BASIC MECHANICAL AND CIVIL ENGINEERING
> - **Course Code:** GDEST203
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basics of Climate Change and Mitigation Strategies (Brief Description Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Climate Change** refers to a long-term, persistent shift in the statistical distribution of weather patterns over decades to millions of years. In the contemporary engineering and policy context (UNFCCC, IPCC framework), it specifically denotes the **anthropogenic (human-induced) global warming** caused by the accumulation of Greenhouse Gases (GHGs) in the Earth's atmosphere, leading to measurable alterations in global temperature, precipitation, sea level, and cryospheric systems.

**Key Associated Terms (KTU Board Standard Vocabulary):**

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Greenhouse Effect** | The natural process by which atmospheric gases absorb and re-emit infrared radiation, warming the Earth's surface. |
| **Global Warming Potential (GWP)** | A factor describing the radiative forcing impact of one unit mass of a given GHG relative to CO₂ over a specified time horizon (usually 100 years). |
| **Carbon Footprint** | The total set of GHG emissions caused directly and indirectly by an individual, organization, event, or product, expressed as **CO₂ equivalent (CO₂e)**. |
| **Mitigation** | Human intervention to reduce the sources of, or enhance the sinks for, greenhouse gases. |
| **Adaptation** | Adjustment in natural or human systems in response to actual or expected climatic stimuli or their effects, which moderates harm or exploits beneficial opportunities. |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Blanket" Analogy for the Greenhouse Effect**
> Imagine the Earth as a sleeping person under a **blanket** (the atmosphere). The blanket traps body heat and keeps the sleeper warm. Now, if you keep adding more and thicker blankets (more CO₂, CH₄, N₂O), the person gets hotter and hotter — uncomfortable and eventually in danger. **Climate change mitigation** is like removing the extra blankets; **adaptation** is like learning to sleep with a fan on while keeping them.

Another simple way to visualize it:
- **Sunlight** (shortwave radiation) passes through the atmosphere and warms the Earth's surface.
- The warm Earth emits **heat** (longwave infrared radiation) back toward space.
- **Greenhouse gases** absorb and re-radiate this heat in all directions, including back toward Earth.
- More GHGs = more heat retained = **global temperature rise**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Climate Metrics (Bold Highlights for Board Exams):**
> - **Global Mean Surface Temperature Anomaly (GMSTA):** Baseline reference period is **1850–1900** (pre-industrial). Current anomaly (2023–2024): approximately **+1.45 °C** above pre-industrial levels.
> - **Atmospheric CO₂ concentration (2024):** approximately **422 ppm** (parts per million) — highest in at least **800,000 years** (pre-industrial level: ~280 ppm).
> - **Global Warming Potential (GWP-100):** CO₂ = **1**, CH₄ = **28–30**, N₂O = **265–273**, SF₆ = **23,500**, HFCs (e.g., HFC-134a) ≈ **1,300–1,430**.
> - **Paris Agreement Target:** Hold global average temperature increase to **well below 2 °C** above pre-industrial levels, and pursue efforts to limit it to **1.5 °C**.
> - **Carbon Dioxide Equivalent (CO₂e):** Standardized unit for comparing emissions of different GHGs based on their GWP.

### 1.4 Why Civil & Mechanical Engineers Must Study This

> [!NOTE]
> Climate change is not a "biology topic." It directly affects the **design, construction, and operation** of all engineering infrastructure:
> - **Civil Engineers:** Sea-level rise affects coastal structures; increased rainfall intensity alters stormwater and flood design; thermal stress affects concrete and asphalt.
> - **Mechanical Engineers:** HVAC load calculations change; refrigeration transitions to low-GWP refrigerants; industrial energy efficiency becomes a design constraint.
> - **Both:** Energy-efficient building codes, sustainable materials, and renewable energy systems are core to the **UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)** — particularly **SDG 7 (Affordable and Clean Energy)**, **SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities)**, and **SDG 13 (Climate Action)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Science of Climate Change — Structured Logic Flow

**Step 1: Solar Energy In**
- The Earth receives shortwave solar radiation (~**1,361 W/m²** at the top of the atmosphere — the **Solar Constant**).
- About **30%** is reflected back to space (Earth's **albedo**).
- The remaining **~70%** is absorbed by the surface and atmosphere.

**Step 2: Earth's Energy Balance**
- The warm Earth re-emits longwave infrared (IR) radiation.
- Under natural equilibrium: **Incoming Solar Radiation = Outgoing Terrestrial Radiation**, keeping the global mean surface temperature at approximately **+15 °C** (without greenhouse effect, it would be approximately **−18 °C**).

**Step 3: Greenhouse Gas Forcing**
- GHGs (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, H₂O vapor, O₃, CFCs) absorb outgoing IR and re-radiate it.
- This adds a **net energy surplus** to the Earth system = **Radiative Forcing** (measured in W/m²).
- Since the pre-industrial era, total anthropogenic radiative forcing is approximately **+2.72 W/m²** (IPCC AR6).

**Step 4: Climate System Response**
- The surplus energy manifests as: warmer oceans, melting ice, warmer atmosphere, changing wind/rain patterns.
- Some heat is stored in the deep ocean — this **delays** the full warming impact (thermal inertia).

**Step 5: Cumulative Emissions Matter**
- Climate warming is roughly **linearly proportional to total cumulative CO₂ emissions** (IPCC AR6).
- This is why **net-zero targets** (not just annual reductions) are critical.

### 2.2 Major Greenhouse Gases — KTU High-Yield Comparison Table

| GHG | Primary Anthropogenic Source | Atmospheric Lifetime | GWP-100 | Share of Global Warming Contribution |
|-----|------------------------------|----------------------|---------|-------------------------------------|
| **CO₂** (Carbon Dioxide) | Fossil fuel combustion, cement, deforestation | 300–1,000 years (variable) | **1** | ~**65–66%** |
| **CH₄** (Methane) | Livestock, rice paddies, landfills, natural gas leakage | ~12 years | **28–30** | ~**16%** |
| **N₂O** (Nitrous Oxide) | Fertilizers, industrial processes, biomass burning | ~109 years | **265–273** | ~**6%** |
| **HFCs** (Hydrofluorocarbons) | Refrigerants, air conditioning | 1–270 years | 12 – 14,800 | ~**2%** |
| **PFCs / SF₆** | Aluminum smelting, electronics, switchgear | 2,600 – 50,000 years | 7,390 – 23,500 | <1% (very potent) |
| **Water Vapour (H₂O)** | Natural evaporation (feedback, not direct emission) | Days | Not defined | Amplifies other GHGs |

> [!NOTE]
> **CO₂ equivalent formula:** $E_{CO_2e} = \sum_i (m_i \times GWP_i)$ where $m_i$ is the mass of GHG $i$ emitted.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Engineering Application |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------|-------------------------|
| 1 | **CO₂ Equivalent (CO₂e)** | $CO_2e = \sum (m_i \times GWP_i)$ | kg CO₂e | Carbon accounting, LCA |
| 2 | **Stefan-Boltzmann Law (Earth's emission)** | $E = \sigma T^4$ | W/m² | Energy balance estimation |
| 3 | **Radiative Forcing (ΔF)** | $\Delta F = 5.35 \times \ln(C/C_0)$ | W/m² | For CO₂, where C = current, C₀ = pre-industrial |
| 4 | **Climate Sensitivity (TCR / ECS)** | $\Delta T_{2x} \approx 1.5 - 4.5\ °C$ | °C | Temperature response to doubled CO₂ |
| 5 | **Cumulative Carbon Budget (1.5 °C)** | ~**500 GtCO₂** remaining (as of 2023, 50% likelihood) | GtCO₂ | Determines net-zero timeline |
| 6 | **Atmospheric CO₂ doubling time** | Doubled from pre-industrial 280 → 560 ppm around **2060** | years | Long-term planning |
| 7 | **Cement's CO₂ contribution** | ~0.9 t CO₂ per ton of cement (limestone calcination + fuel) | t CO₂/t | Sustainable materials selection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for Top 4 GHGs:** **"Clever Monkeys Never Pollute"** → **C**O₂, **M**ethane (CH₄), **N**₂O, **P**FCs/SF₆.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Energy Audit & Efficiency:** Building Energy Management Systems (BEMS) reduce operational CO₂ by 30–50%.
- **Net-Zero Buildings:** Use the equation **Operational Energy = On-site Renewable Generation** for net-zero operation.
- **Refrigerant Transition:** KTU emphasizes replacement of HFC-134a (GWP ~1,430) with R-1234yf (GWP < 1) in automotive AC.
- **Sustainable Concrete:** Use of **Supplementary Cementitious Materials (SCMs)** like fly ash (15–30%), slag, and calcined clay to lower cement content.
- **Renewable Energy Systems:** Solar PV, wind, small-hydro replace fossil generation — directly displacing CO₂.

### 2.5 Causes of Climate Change (Two Categories)

**A. Natural Causes (Pre-Industrial Drivers)**
1. **Volcanic eruptions** — emit SO₂ aerosols (short-term cooling) and CO₂.
2. **Solar irradiance variations** — ~11-year sunspot cycle (~0.1% variation, minor effect).
3. **Earth's orbital variations** (Milankovitch cycles) — drive ice ages over 10,000–100,000-year timescales.
4. **Ocean-atmosphere oscillations** (El Niño/La Niña — ENSO) — short-term variability.

**B. Anthropogenic (Human-Induced) Causes — Dominant Since 1850**
1. **Burning of fossil fuels** (coal, oil, natural gas) — largest single source of CO₂.
2. **Deforestation & land-use change** — reduces the **carbon sink** capacity of forests.
3. **Industrial processes** — cement, steel, chemicals (N₂O, PFCs).
4. **Agriculture** — livestock (CH₄ enteric fermentation), rice paddies (CH₄), N-fertilizers (N₂O).
5. **Waste management** — landfills emit CH₄; wastewater emits CH₄ and N₂O.
6. **Fluorinated gases** — refrigerants, aerosols, switchgear.

> [!NOTE]
> **IPCC AR6 (2021) Statement:** "It is unequivocal that human influence has warmed the atmosphere, ocean, and land."

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework, Impacts & Mitigation Pathways

### 3.1 Impacts of Climate Change (Engineering-Relevant)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU often asks students to list impacts in a tabular format. Memorize the **5 physical impact categories** and connect each to **engineering consequences.**

| Impact Category | Specific Change | Engineering Consequence |
|-----------------|------------------|--------------------------|
| **Temperature** | Global mean +1.1–1.5 °C; heatwaves 5–10× more frequent | Higher HVAC load; thermal stress on rails, bridges, pavements |
| **Precipitation** | Wetter wet regions, drier dry regions; intense rainfall events | Urban flooding; dam spillway redesign; stormwater capacity upgrade |
| **Sea Level Rise** | +3.4 mm/yr (satellite altimetry); projected +0.3–1.0 m by 2100 | Coastal infrastructure at risk; foundation corrosion; saltwater intrusion |
| **Cryosphere** | Arctic sea ice declining ~13%/decade; glacier retreat | Hydropower variability; GLOFs (Glacial Lake Outburst Floods) in Himalayas |
| **Extreme Events** | More frequent cyclones, droughts, wildfires | Higher structural design loads; insurance costs; disaster-resilient codes |

### 3.2 Climate Change Mitigation Strategies — Detailed Engineering Framework

**Mitigation = "Stop the problem at the source"** (reduce emissions or enhance sinks).

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 module expects a **brief description** of mitigation strategies. Below is a comprehensive, exam-ready structured classification.

#### A. **Energy Sector Mitigation** (Largest CO₂ source ~73% of global emissions)

1. **Renewable Energy Deployment**
   - Solar Photovoltaic (PV), Concentrated Solar Power (CSP), Onshore & Offshore Wind, Small Hydro, Geothermal, Biomass, Tidal/Wave.
   - LCOE (Levelized Cost of Energy) of solar PV has fallen from **$0.378/kWh (2010)** to **$0.044/kWh (2024)** — competitive with coal.

2. **Energy Efficiency**
   - ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code) in India; **ECBC+** and **SuperECBC** for net-ready buildings.
   - **BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency)** star ratings for appliances.
   - **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade)** scheme for industrial units.

3. **Fuel Switching**
   - From coal to natural gas (transitional, since CH₄ is also a GHG).
   - Hydrogen economy: **Green H₂** (electrolysis powered by renewables) is the long-term fuel switch.

4. **Phase-out of Unabated Fossil Fuels**
   - Coal phase-down commitment under COP26 Glasgow Climate Pact (2021).
   - Carbon Capture, Utilization, and Storage (CCUS) for residual emissions.

#### B. **Transport Sector Mitigation** (~14% of global emissions)

1. **Electric Vehicles (EVs)** — Zero tailpipe emissions (well-to-wheel emissions depend on grid mix).
2. **Public Transport & Non-Motorized Transport (NMT)** — Metro, BRT, cycling, walking.
3. **Sustainable Aviation Fuels (SAF)**, hydrogen fuel cell vehicles.
4. **Fuel efficiency standards** — Corporate Average Fuel Economy (CAFE) norms.

#### C. **Industrial & Building Sector Mitigation**

1. **Green Buildings** — LEED, GRIHA, IGBC ratings; passive design, daylighting, cool roofs.
2. **Sustainable Materials** — Low-carbon cement (LC³), geopolymer concrete, recycled steel, mass timber (CLT).
3. **Circular Economy** — Reduce, Reuse, Recycle; industrial symbiosis.
4. **Process Electrification** — Replace fossil-fuel furnaces with electric/induction heating.

#### D. **Land Use, Agriculture & Forestry (AFOLU) Mitigation**

1. **Afforestation & Reforestation** — Carbon sequestration in biomass.
2. **REDD+** (Reducing Emissions from Deforestation and Forest Degradation).
3. **Climate-smart agriculture** — Alternate wetting & drying (AWD) in rice reduces CH₄ by 30–70%.
4. **Biochar** application to soils.

#### E. **Carbon Dioxide Removal (CDR) — Negative Emissions**

1. **Direct Air Capture (DAC)** with storage.
2. **Ocean alkalinization**.
3. **Enhanced weathering** (olivine, basalt).
4. **BECCS** (Bioenergy with Carbon Capture and Storage).

### 3.3 Adaptation Strategies (Brief)

> [!NOTE]
> **Adaptation ≠ Mitigation.** Adaptation accepts that some change is already locked in and focuses on **coping with impacts**.

| Sector | Adaptation Measure |
|--------|--------------------|
| **Water** | Rainwater harvesting, drip irrigation, watershed management |
| **Agriculture** | Drought-resistant crop varieties, crop diversification, agroforestry |
| **Coastal** | Sea walls, mangroves, climate-resilient housing, managed retreat |
| **Urban** | Heat action plans, urban green cover, cool roofs, permeable pavements |
| **Health** | Disease surveillance, vector control, early warning systems |
| **Infrastructure** | Updated design codes for rainfall, temperature, and sea level |

### 3.4 International Policy Framework (KTU Frequently Tested)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Major Milestones — Memorize Year + Outcome:**

1. **UNFCCC (1992, Rio Earth Summit)** — Framework Convention on Climate Change; **"common but differentiated responsibilities."**
2. **Kyoto Protocol (1997, in force 2005)** — Binding emission reduction targets for **Annex I (developed)** countries. Introduced **Clean Development Mechanism (CDM), Joint Implementation (JI), Emissions Trading.**
3. **Copenhagen Accord (2009)** — Acknowledged 2 °C limit; non-binding pledges.
4. **Paris Agreement (2015, in force 2016)** — 196 parties; **NDCs (Nationally Determined Contributions)**; 1.5 °C–2 °C goal; **Global Stocktake** every 5 years.
5. **Glasgow Climate Pact (COP26, 2021)** — Coal phase-down, methane reduction (Global Methane Pledge).
6. **COP27 (2022, Sharm el-Sheikh)** — **Loss and Damage Fund** established.
7. **COP28 (2023, Dubai)** — First call to "transition away from fossil fuels"; Triple Renewables (3× capacity by 2030); Double Efficiency (2× by 2030).
8. **COP29 (2024, Baku)** — Climate finance goal: **$300 billion/year by 2035** (New Collective Quantified Goal — NCQG).

### 3.5 India's Climate Action (KTU-Specific)

> [!NOTE]
> **India-specific initiatives — High Board Exam Weightage:**

| Initiative | Description | Target / Year |
|------------|-------------|----------------|
| **Panchamrit (5-point pledge)** PM announcement, COP26 | 500 GW non-fossil capacity; 50% renewable mix; 45% emissions intensity reduction; net-zero by **2070** | 2030 / 2070 |
| **National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC, 2008)** | 8 national missions incl. Solar, Energy Efficiency, Sustainable Habitat, Water | Ongoing |
| **FAME India** (Faster Adoption of EVs) | Subsidies for EV purchase, charging infra | Phase II |
| **UJALA** | LED distribution — saved ~9,500 MW peak demand | ~37 crore LEDs distributed |
| **PAT Scheme** | Industrial energy efficiency trading | BEE |
| **India's Updated NDC (2022)** | 45% emissions intensity reduction (vs 2005); 50% non-fossil installed capacity by 2030; net-zero by 2070 | 2030 / 2070 |
| **Green Hydrogen Mission (2023)** | 5 MMT production by 2030; ₹19,744 cr outlay | 2030 |
| **PM-PRANAM** | Protect & Restore Natural Habitats; promote alternative fertilizers | 2023 |
| **LiFE (Lifestyle for Environment)** | Demand-side, behaviour-led sustainability (PM vision) | 2022 |
| **Bharat Stage VI** | Vehicular emission norms aligned with Euro 6 | 2020 onwards |
| **PM Surya Ghar: Muft Bijli Yojana (2024)** | Rooftop solar for 1 crore households | 2026 |

### 3.6 Numerical Example (For Board Practice)

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** A small industry emits 500 tonnes of CO₂, 10 tonnes of CH₄, and 2 tonnes of N₂O in a year. Calculate its total CO₂-equivalent emissions. (Use GWP-100: CO₂ = 1, CH₄ = 28, N₂O = 273)

**Step 1:** List all masses and GWPs.

| GHG | Mass (t) | GWP | CO₂e (t) |
|-----|----------|-----|----------|
| CO₂ | 500 | 1 | 500 × 1 = 500 |
| CH₄ | 10 | 28 | 10 × 28 = 280 |
| N₂O | 2 | 273 | 2 × 273 = 546 |

**Step 2:** Apply the CO₂e summation formula.

$$E_{CO_2e} = \sum_i (m_i \times GWP_i)$$

$$E_{CO_2e} = 500 + 280 + 546 = 1{,}326 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{e per year}$$

**Step 3:** State the interpretation.

The industry's total annual GHG footprint is **1,326 t CO₂e** — primarily driven by N₂O despite its small mass, because of its high GWP. **Engineering takeaway:** Targeting the high-GWP gas first yields maximum mitigation impact per unit effort.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model]**
**Define the term "Global Warming Potential (GWP)" and explain why CH₄ has a higher GWP than CO₂ even though CO₂ is more abundant.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Global Warming Potential (GWP) is a unitless factor that expresses the cumulative radiative forcing impact of 1 kg of a given greenhouse gas over a specified time horizon (usually 100 years) relative to that of 1 kg of CO₂.
>
> **Numerical GWP-100 values (1 mark):** CO₂ = 1; CH₄ ≈ 28–30.
>
> **Reason for CH₄'s higher GWP per kg (1 mark):** Methane's molecular structure is more efficient at absorbing infrared radiation per unit mass than CO₂. Although CO₂ is more abundant in the atmosphere and has a much longer atmospheric lifetime (hundreds to thousands of years) compared to CH₄ (~12 years), on a per-molecule and per-100-year basis, CH₄ traps significantly more heat.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model]**
**List any three differences between climate change "mitigation" and "adaptation" strategies with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Mitigation | Adaptation |
|--------|------------|------------|
| **Goal (1 mark)** | Reduce/prevent GHG emissions at source | Cope with already-occurred or unavoidable climate impacts |
| **Timeframe (1 mark)** | Long-term, preventive | Short- to medium-term, responsive |
| **Example (1 mark)** | Installing solar PV to replace coal-fired power | Building a seawall to protect against sea-level rise |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> For 14-mark questions, students commonly **lose 2–3 marks** by:
> 1. **Listing without explanation** — merely bullet-pointing strategies without describing how they mitigate emissions.
> 2. **Mixing mitigation and adaptation** — examiners deduct marks if asked specifically for one and you describe the other.
> 3. **Forgetting India's context** — when discussing global strategies, anchor at least 2–3 points to Indian initiatives (Panchamrit, ECBC, FAME, Green Hydrogen Mission) to score full marks.
> 4. **Missing units & numerical examples** — board examiners reward concrete numbers (e.g., GWP values, GtCO₂ budgets, % targets).

---

**Q3 (A). [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model] — Choice A (14 Marks)**
**(a) Explain the greenhouse effect with a neat diagram. Discuss the major greenhouse gases and their relative contributions to global warming. (7 marks)**
**(b) Describe the various climate change mitigation strategies in the energy sector, with specific reference to India's initiatives. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) The Greenhouse Effect (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The greenhouse effect is the process by which greenhouse gases in the Earth's atmosphere absorb and re-emit longwave infrared radiation emitted by the Earth's surface, thereby warming the lower atmosphere. It is a natural phenomenon, but **human activities have intensified it**, leading to **enhanced greenhouse effect** and global warming.

**Process (3 marks):**
1. The Sun emits shortwave radiation that passes through the atmosphere and is absorbed by the Earth's surface.
2. The warmed Earth emits longwave infrared (IR) radiation back toward space.
3. Greenhouse gases (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, H₂O vapour, O₃, HFCs, SF₆) absorb this outgoing IR and re-radiate it in all directions — including back toward the surface.
4. This traps additional energy in the lower atmosphere, raising the global mean surface temperature.

**Major GHGs and Relative Contributions (3 marks):**

| GHG | Contribution to Global Warming (IPCC AR6) | Major Source |
|-----|--------------------------------------------|--------------|
| CO₂ | ~**65%** | Fossil fuel combustion, cement, deforestation |
| CH₄ | ~**16%** | Livestock, landfills, gas leaks |
| N₂O | ~**6%** | Fertilizers, biomass burning |
| Halocarbons (CFCs, HFCs, SF₆) | ~**13% combined** | Refrigerants, switchgear |

**Diagram (sketch expected — see Section 4.1 Mermaid).** Use a clear incoming/outgoing arrow representation.

---

**(b) Energy Sector Mitigation Strategies with India's Initiatives (7 marks):**

**1. Renewable Energy Deployment (2 marks):**
- Solar, wind, hydro, biomass, geothermal, tidal.
- Solar PV LCOE has dropped from **$0.378/kWh (2010)** to **~$0.044/kWh (2024)**.
- **India's PM Surya Ghar: Muft Bijli Yojana (2024)** — rooftop solar for 1 crore households.
- India's **PM-KUSUM** scheme — solar pumps for farmers.
- Cumulative installed RE capacity (India, mid-2024) ≈ **190 GW** (target: 500 GW non-fossil by 2030).

**2. Energy Efficiency in Buildings & Industry (2 marks):**
- **ECBC / ECBC+ / SuperECBC** (Energy Conservation Building Code by BEE).
- **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade)** scheme — 130+ energy-intensive industries.
- **UJALA** (LED distribution) — saved ~9,500 MW peak demand.
- **Standards & Labelling** programme for appliances.

**3. Green Hydrogen & Storage (1.5 marks):**
- **National Green Hydrogen Mission (2023)** — 5 MMT production target by 2030, ₹19,744 crore outlay.
- Green H₂ produced via electrolysis using renewable electricity.
- Applications: steel, ammonia, refineries, long-duration storage, heavy transport.

**4. Grid Modernization & Storage (1 mark):**
- **One Nation One Grid** — integrated national power grid.
- Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS), pumped hydro storage.
- Green Energy Corridors for renewable evacuation.

**5. Phasing Down Unabated Fossil Fuels (0.5 mark):**
- Coal phase-down commitment under **Glasgow Climate Pact (COP26)**.
- Just transition for coal-dependent regions; supercritical / ultra-supercritical plants as transitional technology.

---

**Q3 (B). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model] — Choice B (14 Marks) — Alternative**
**(a) What is climate change? Distinguish between natural and anthropogenic causes. (7 marks)**
**(b) Describe the key mitigation strategies in the transport and industrial sectors, with examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definition and Causes (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Climate change refers to long-term, statistically significant shifts in the average state of the climate system, encompassing changes in temperature, precipitation, sea level, ice cover, and weather patterns. As per the IPCC, **anthropogenic climate change** is unequivocally caused by human activities, primarily through the emission of greenhouse gases from fossil fuel combustion, industrial processes, agriculture, and land-use changes.

**Natural Causes (2.5 marks):**
1. **Volcanic eruptions** — emit SO₂ aerosols (causing short-term cooling) and CO₂.
2. **Variations in solar irradiance** — 11-year sunspot cycle causes minor fluctuations (~0.1%).
3. **Milankovitch cycles** — orbital eccentricity, axial tilt, and precession changes over 10,000–100,000 years drive ice age cycles.
4. **ENSO** (El Niño Southern Oscillation) and **NAO** (North Atlantic Oscillation) — natural ocean-atmosphere variability.

**Anthropogenic Causes (2.5 marks):**
1. **Burning of fossil fuels (coal, oil, natural gas)** — largest single source of CO₂ (~73% of global emissions).
2. **Deforestation & land-use change** — reduces forest carbon sink capacity (accounting for ~10–11% of emissions).
3. **Industrial processes** — cement (CO₂), steel, chemicals (N₂O, PFCs).
4. **Agriculture** — livestock (enteric CH₄), rice paddies (CH₄), nitrogen fertilizers (N₂O).
5. **Waste management** — landfills emit CH₄; wastewater treatment emits N₂O and CH₄.
6. **Fluorinated gases** — refrigerants, aerosols, switchgear (HFCs, PFCs, SF₆) — very high GWP.

> **IPCC AR6 Conclusion:** "Human influence has warmed the atmosphere, ocean, and land. Widespread and rapid changes have occurred in the atmosphere, ocean, cryosphere, and biosphere."

---

**(b) Mitigation Strategies in Transport and Industry (7 marks):**

**1. Transport Sector Mitigation (3.5 marks):**

**(i) Electric Vehicles (EVs):**
- Zero tailpipe emissions; lifecycle emissions depend on grid mix.
- India's **FAME India Phase II** — subsidies for EV purchase and charging infrastructure.
- Battery swapping policy (2022), PM E-Drive scheme.

**(ii) Public & Non-Motorized Transport:**
- Metro rail, BRT, suburban rail, electric buses.
- NMT (cycling, walking) infrastructure; TOD (Transit-Oriented Development).
- **India:** Metro projects in 20+ cities; *MetroNeo*, *Metrolite* for tier-2/3 cities.

**(iii) Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF), Hydrogen, and Efficiency:**
- SAF from biomass/algae; bio-CNG; hydrogen fuel cells for heavy vehicles.
- CAE norms, BS-VI standards (India) for vehicular emissions.

**2. Industrial Sector Mitigation (3.5 marks):**

**(i) Process Electrification & Fuel Switching:**
- Replace fossil-fired furnaces with induction, electric arc, or resistance heating.
- Hydrogen-based direct reduced iron (DRI) for green steel.

**(ii) Material Substitution & Efficiency:**
- **Low-carbon cement** — LC³ (Limestone Calcined Clay Cement) replaces 50% of clinker.
- **Geopolymer concrete**, supplementary cementitious materials (SCMs) like fly ash (15–30%), GGBS, calcined clay.
- **Recycled steel** via Electric Arc Furnace (EAF) — 75% less CO₂ vs. blast furnace.

**(iii) Circular Economy & Waste Heat Recovery:**
- Industrial symbiosis, material recycling, design for disassembly.
- **Combined Heat & Power (CHP)** and waste heat recovery systems.

**(iv) Carbon Capture, Utilization, and Storage (CCUS):**
- Capturing CO₂ at source (cement, steel, refinery) and storing it underground or using it in enhanced oil recovery, mineralization, or synthetic fuel synthesis.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick-Revision Masterlist — Memorize Before the Exam:**

- [ ] **Climate change = anthropogenic global warming**, primarily from **fossil fuel CO₂ (~65%)**.
- [ ] **Pre-industrial CO₂: ~280 ppm; 2024: ~422 ppm** — highest in 800,000 years.
- [ ] **Top 4 GHGs (descending impact):** CO₂ > CH₄ > N₂O > Halocarbons.
- [ ] **GWP-100: CO₂ = 1; CH₄ ≈ 28; N₂O ≈ 273; SF₆ ≈ 23,500.**
- [ ] **CO₂e = Σ (mass × GWP)** — fundamental carbon accounting formula.
- [ ] **Paris Agreement target:** well below 2 °C, pursue 1.5 °C.
- [ ] **India's NDC targets (updated 2022):**
  - 45% emissions intensity reduction by 2030 (vs 2005).
  - 50% non-fossil installed power capacity by 2030.
  - Net-Zero by **2070**.
- [ ] **Panchamrit (PM, COP26, Glasgow, 2021):** 5-point climate pledge, including net-zero by 2070.
- [ ] **Key Indian Missions:** National Solar Mission, ECBC, PAT, FAME, UJALA, Green Hydrogen Mission (5 MMT by 2030), PM-PRANAM, PM Surya Ghar.
- [ ] **Mitigation vs Adaptation:** Mitigation = reduce emissions at source; Adaptation = cope with impacts.
- [ ] **COP timeline (remember years + outcomes):** UNFCCC 1992 → Kyoto 1997 → Copenhagen 2009 → Paris 2015 → Glasgow 2021 → COP27 (Loss & Damage) 2022 → COP28 (transition away from fossil fuels) 2023 → COP29 (NCQG finance) 2024.
- [ ] **Mitigation hierarchy in energy:** Energy efficiency → Renewables → Fuel switching (H₂) → CCUS for residuals.
- [ ] **Engineering relevance:** Cool roofs, ECBC, EV transition, low-carbon concrete, refrigerant transition (HFC-134a → R-1234yf), green H₂.
- [ ] **Cement CO₂ footprint:** ~0.9 t CO₂ per ton of cement — drives demand for LC³ and geopolymer.
- [ ] **Climate sensitivity range:** ECS = 1.5–4.5 °C for doubled CO₂ (IPCC).
- [ ] **Remaining carbon budget for 1.5 °C (50% likelihood, 2023):** ~500 GtCO₂.
- [ ] **Key acronyms to know:** UNFCCC, IPCC, GWP, CO₂e, NDC, CDR, BECCS, DAC, REDD+, ECBC, BEE, BESS, FAME, AFOLU, CCUS, LC³.
